Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَوْفُوا۟ بِٱلْعُقُودِ ۚ أُحِلَّتْ لَكُم بَهِيمَةُ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ إِلَّا مَا يُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ غَيْرَ مُحِلِّى ٱلصَّيْدِ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ مَا يُرِيدُ (١)
O you who have faith! Keep your agreements. You are permitted animals of grazing livestock, except what is [now] announced to you, disallowing game while you are in pilgrim sanctity. Indeed Allah decrees whatever He desires. (1)

Contents of this Surah

This Surah is among the Medinan Surahs and contains 120 verses. It is said to have been revealed after Surah Al-Fath, and according to a narration, the entirety of this Surah was revealed during the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada') between Mecca and Medina [1]. This Surah contains a series of Islamic teachings and beliefs, as well as a series of religious rulings and duties.

In the first part, it refers to the issue of Wilayah (guardianship) and leadership after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the Christian doctrine of the Trinity, and parts of the issues related to the Day of Judgment and Resurrection, and the questioning of the Prophets regarding their nations.

In the second part, it addresses the issue of fulfilling covenants, social justice, bearing witness with justice, and the prohibition of killing (and accordingly, the story of the children of Adam and the murder of Abel by Cain). It also provides explanations regarding parts of lawful and unlawful foods, and some of the rulings of Wudu (ablution) and Tayammum.

It is named "Surah Al-Ma'idah" (The Table) because the story of the descent of the Ma'idah [2] for the disciples of Christ is mentioned in verse 114 of this Surah.

[^1]: Al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 116. It should be noted that a Surah being "Medinan" means it was revealed after the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله migration from Mecca, even if the revelation did not occur within Medina itself.

[^2]: "Ma'idah" originally refers to a tray or table that has food upon it.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيمَةُ الْأَنْعامِ إِلاَّ ما يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ غَيْرَ مُحِلِّي الصَّيْدِ وَ أَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ ما يُرِيدُ (1)

Commentary

The Necessity of Fulfilling Covenants and Pledges

As understood from Islamic narrations and the words of great commentators, this Surah is the last Surah (or among the last Surahs) revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that Hazrat Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام said: Surah Al-Ma'idah was revealed two or three months before the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [1]. The fact that some narrations state this Surah is "abrogating" (nasikh) and not "abrogated" (mansukh) also refers to this same point.

This statement does not contradict what we mentioned in the second volume of this Tafsir under verse 281 of Surah Al-Baqarah—that according to narrations, the aforementioned verse was the last verse revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—because here the discussion is about a Surah, while there the discussion was about a single verse! In this Surah—due to this specific timing—emphasis is placed on a series of Islamic concepts, the final religious programs, the issue of the leadership of the Ummah, and the succession of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Perhaps it is for this reason that it begins with the issue of the necessity of fulfilling covenants and pledges, stating in the first sentence: "O you who have believed, fulfill [your] contracts."

(يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَوْفُوا بِالْعُقُودِ).

This is to obligate the believers to the covenants they previously made with Allah or those referred to in this Surah. It is exactly like a traveler who, in the final moments of departure, emphasizes to his relatives and followers not to forget his recommendations and advice and to remain faithful to the promises and agreements he made with them.

It should be noted that "Uqud" (عقود) is the plural of "Aqd" (عقد), which originally means "bringing the ends of something together firmly." For this reason, tying the two ends of a rope or two ropes together is called "Aqd." Subsequently, this physical meaning was transferred to the spiritual concept, and every kind of covenant and pledge is called an "Aqd." However, according to the clarification of a group of jurists and commentators, "Aqd" has a more restricted meaning than "Ahd" (عهد), as "Aqd" refers to covenants that possess sufficient firmness, not just any pledge. If in some "narrations" and "expressions of commentators," "Aqd" and "Ahd" are used synonymously, it does not contradict what we have said, as the intention was a general explanation of these two words, not a detailed distinction.

Given that "Al-Uqud"—in technical terms—is a "plural defined by the definite article (alif and lam)" and signifies generality, and the sentence is also completely absolute, the above verse is evidence for the obligation of fulfilling all covenants that are firmly established between individuals, or between individuals and Allah. Thus, it encompasses all divine and human covenants, as well as political, economic, social, commercial, marital, and similar agreements. It has a completely broad concept that oversees all aspects of human life, including belief and action—from innate and monotheistic covenants to the agreements people make with one another regarding various life issues.

In the commentary "Ruh al-Ma'ani," it is quoted from Raghib that "Aqd," considering the status of the parties, is of three types: sometimes the contract is between Allah and the servant, sometimes between a person and himself, and sometimes between him and other human beings [1]. (Of course, all three types of contracts have two sides; however, when one makes a covenant with oneself, he considers himself as two persons who are the parties to the covenant).

In any case, the concept of the verse is so broad that it also includes the covenants and pledges that Muslims make with non-Muslims.

There are points in the verse that must be noted:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 430. It should be noted that instructions regarding Wudu, Tayammum, and the like in this Surah do not contradict its being the last Surah, as many of these instructions were for repetition and emphasis; hence, we see some of these same rulings in Surah An-Nisa as well.

  1. This verse is among those cited in Islamic legal discussions throughout jurisprudence, and an important jurisprudential rule, "The Principle of Necessity in Contracts" (Asalat al-Luzum fi al-Uqud), is derived from it. This means that any covenant or treaty concluded between two people regarding objects or actions is mandatory to execute. Even—as a group of researchers believe—it includes types of transactions, partnerships, and contracts that exist in our era but did not exist previously, or those that will arise among rational people in the future, provided they are based on correct standards; this verse validates all of them (of course, considering the general criteria that Islam has established for contracts).

However, arguing from this verse as a jurisprudential rule does not mean it does not include the divine covenants made between "servants" and "Allah," or issues related to the leadership and guidance of the Ummah for which the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took pledges from the people. Rather, the verse has a broad concept that encompasses all these matters.

It is also necessary to remind that the necessity of fulfilling bilateral covenants lasts as long as they have not been violated by one side. But if they are violated by one side, the other side is not obligated to remain faithful, as it has lost the essence of a contract and covenant.

2. The Importance of Fulfilling Covenants and Pledges

The issue of fulfilling covenants and pledges raised in the verse under discussion is one of the most fundamental conditions of collective life. Without it, no social cooperation is possible, and by losing it, humanity would practically lose social life and its effects. For this reason, extraordinary emphasis has been placed on this issue in Islamic sources, and perhaps few things are as widespread, because without it, chaos and the loss of public trust—which is the greatest social calamity—would emerge among mankind.

In Nahj al-Balagha, in the command to Malik al-Ashtar, we read:

"For there is nothing among the obligations of Allah upon which people are more united—despite their differing desires and scattered opinions—than the honoring of fulfillment of covenants. Even the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance respected covenants among themselves, apart from the Muslims, because they had tasted the bitter consequences of treachery" [1]. It is also narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, that he said:

"Indeed, Allah does not accept except righteous deeds, and Allah does not accept except the fulfillment of conditions and covenants" [2]. And it is narrated from the Great Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"There is no religion for one who has no [regard for his] covenant" [3]. For this reason, the subject of fulfilling covenants is one of those matters where there is no difference among humans; whether the party to the covenant is a Muslim or a non-Muslim. In technical terms, it is among "human rights," not just the rights of religious brothers.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"There are three things for which Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, has granted no one a concession: returning a trust to the righteous and the wicked alike, fulfilling a covenant to the righteous and the wicked alike, and kindness to parents whether they are righteous or wicked!" [1]. Even in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is stated that if someone undertakes a covenant through a gesture, he must fulfill it:

"If any of the Muslims signals or gestures to any of the polytheists and he [the polytheist] relies on that, then he is in [a state of] security" [2].

Then, following the command to fulfill covenants—which includes all divine rulings and pledges—a series of Islamic rulings are stated, the first of which is the lawfulness of the meat of certain animals, stating: "Lawful to you are the animals of grazing livestock (or their fetuses)" (أُحِلَّتْ لَكُمْ بَهِيمَةُ الْأَنْعامِ‌).

"An'am" (انعام) is the plural of "Na'am" (نعم), meaning camels, cows, and sheep [3]. "Bahimah" (بهيمة) is from the root "Bahmah" (بهمة) which originally means "solid stone." Anything difficult to perceive is called "Mubham" (ambiguous), and all animals that do not possess speech are called "Bahimah" because their sounds contain ambiguity. However, this word is usually used only for four-legged animals and does not include predators or birds.

And since the "fetus" of animals also possesses a kind of ambiguity, it is also called "Bahimah."

Therefore, the lawfulness of "Bahimat al-An'am" either means the lawfulness of all four-legged livestock (with the exception of what is mentioned later in the verse) or it means the lawfulness of the offspring within the bellies of animals whose meat is lawful (offspring whose creation is complete and hair or wool has grown on their bodies) [1]. Since the lawfulness of animals like camels, cows, and sheep was already known to the people before this verse, the verse might be referring to the lawfulness of their fetuses.

However, what seems closer to the meaning of the verse is that it has a broad meaning: it states both the lawfulness of these types of animals and their "fetuses." If the ruling on these animals was known previously, it is repeated here as a preamble for the subsequent exceptions.

From what we have said in the commentary of this sentence, it becomes clear that the connection of this ruling with the general principle of the necessity of fulfilling covenants is that this "general principle" emphasizes divine rulings, which are a type of covenant between God and His servants. Then, following that, a number of rulings are stated, of which the lawfulness of the meat of some animals and the prohibition of others is one.

Then, at the end of the verse, two cases are excepted from the ruling of the lawfulness of the meat of livestock, stating: "Except that which is recited to you [as prohibited]" (إِلَّا ما يُتْلى‌ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

And with the exception of the state of Ihram (for performing the rituals of Hajj or Umrah, during which hunting is forbidden) (غَيْرَ مُحِلِّي الصَّيْدِ وَ أَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ). And finally, it states: "Indeed, Allah ordains what He intends," meaning because He is aware of everything and the Owner of everything, He legislates whatever ruling is in the best interest of His servants and as wisdom dictates (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ ما يُرِيدُ).

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, p. 162. [^2]: Mustadrak al-Wasa'il, Vol. 2, p. 250. [^3]: "Na'am," if used in the singular, means a camel, but if it means the plural, it includes camels, cows, and sheep (Mufradat al-Raghib, root n-'-m).

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحِلُّوا۟ شَعَـٰٓئِرَ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا ٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ وَلَا ٱلْهَدْىَ وَلَا ٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ وَلَآ ءَآمِّينَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَٰنًا ۚ وَإِذَا حَلَلْتُمْ فَٱصْطَادُوا۟ ۚ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ أَن صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ ٱلْمَسْجِدِ ٱلْحَرَامِ أَن تَعْتَدُوا۟ ۘ وَتَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ (٢)
O you who have faith! Do not violate Allah’s sacraments, neither the sacred month, nor the offering, nor the necklaces, nor those bound for the Sacred House, who seek their Lord’s grace and [His] pleasure. But when you emerge from pilgrim sanctity, you may hunt for game. Ill feeling for a people should not lead you, because they barred you from [access to] the Sacred Mosque, to transgress. Cooperate in piety and Godwariness, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is severe in retribution. (2)

Commentary

Eight Commands in One Verse

In this verse, several important Islamic instructions are mentioned, among the final commands revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. All or most of them pertain to the Hajj and the pilgrimage to the House of Allah:

1- First, addressing the believers, He says: "Do not violate the symbols of Allah, nor treat their sanctity as permissible."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُحِلُّوا شَعَائِرَ اللَّهِ)

There is much discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by "symbols of Allah" (Sha’a’ir Allah). However, considering the other parts of this verse and its year of revelation (the 10th year of the Hijra), which was the year of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Farewell Pilgrimage (Hujjat al-Wada'), it appears that "symbols" refers to the "rituals and programs of Hajj" which Muslims are duty-bound to respect. Evidence for this interpretation is that in the Quran, the word "Sha’a’ir" is usually used in the context of Hajj ceremonies. [^1]

2- "Observe the sanctity of the sacred months and refrain from fighting during these months." (وَلَا الشَّهْرَ الْحَرَامَ)

3- "Do not treat as permissible the sacrificial animals brought for Hajj, whether they are unmarked (Hady) [^2] or marked with collars (Qala'id) [^3]; let them reach the place of sacrifice and be offered there." (وَلَا الْهَدْيَ وَلَا الْقَلَائِدَ)

4- All pilgrims to the House of Allah must enjoy complete freedom in this great Islamic ceremony. There is no distinction in this regard among tribes, individuals, races, or languages. Therefore, "You must not harass those who set out for the Sacred House seeking the bounty of their Lord, His pleasure, or even commercial profit—whether they are your friends or enemies. As long as they are Muslims and pilgrims of the House of Allah, they possess immunity."

(وَلَا آمِّينَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرَامَ يَبْتَغُونَ فَضْلًا مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ وَرِضْوَانًا)

Some commentators and jurists believe the above sentence is general and even includes non-Muslims; meaning, if polytheists come with the intention of visiting the House of Allah, they should not be harassed. However, considering that in Surah At-Tawbah (known to have been revealed in the 9th year of Hijra), verse 28 commands the prevention of polytheists from entering Al-Masjid al-Haram, and given that Surah Al-Ma'idah was revealed at the end of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life in the 10th year of Hijra—and according to both Shia and Sunni narrations, none of its rulings have been abrogated—such an interpretation is incorrect. The truth is that the above ruling is specific to Muslims.

5- The prohibition of hunting is limited to the time of Ihram; therefore, "When you have come out of Ihram for Hajj or Umrah, hunting is permissible for you."

(وَإِذَا حَلَلْتُمْ فَاصْطَادُوا)

6- If a group of idolaters during the Age of Ignorance (during the event of Hudaybiyyah) obstructed your pilgrimage to the House of Allah and prevented you from performing the rituals, "This event should not cause you, after their conversion to Islam, to revive old grudges and prevent them from visiting the House of Allah."

(وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ أَنْ صَدُّوكُمْ عَنِ الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ أَنْ تَعْتَدُوا) [^1]

Although this ruling was revealed regarding the pilgrimage to the House of Allah, a general law is derived from it: a Muslim must not be "spiteful" or revive past events in their mind to seek revenge. Given that one of the causes of hypocrisy and division in any society is this very issue, the importance of this Islamic instruction to prevent the flames of hypocrisy among Muslims—especially on the eve of the sunset of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life—becomes even clearer.

7- Then, to complete the previous discussion, He says: "Instead of joining hands to take revenge on your former enemies and current friends, you must join hands in unity for goodness and piety, and not cooperate in sin and transgression."

(وَتَعَاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَلَا تَعَاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوَانِ)

8- At the end of the verse, to reinforce and emphasize the preceding rulings, He says: "Observe piety and fear the opposition of Allah's command, for Allah's punishment and retribution are severe."

(وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ ۖ إِنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقَابِ)

[^1]: Al-Baqarah: 158, and Al-Hajj: 32 and 36. [^2]: Hady is the plural of Hadiyyah, meaning the livestock "offered" as a sacrifice to the House of Allah. [^3]: Qala'id is the plural of Qaladah, meaning something placed around the neck of a person or animal; here it refers to the livestock marked for sacrifice in Hajj ceremonies. [^1]: What is understood from the words of linguists and commentators is that Jurm (rhyming with Garm) originally means cutting unsuitable fruit from a tree, and subsequently refers to any unpleasant act or forcing someone into an unpleasant task. Therefore, La yajrimannakum here means La yahmilannakum, i.e., "let it not incite you" to a wrongful act.

The Necessity of Cooperation in Goodness

What is mentioned in the above verse regarding cooperation is a general Islamic principle that encompasses all social, legal, moral, and political issues. According to this principle, Muslims are duty-bound to cooperate in good deeds, but cooperation in false goals, incorrect actions, and oppression is absolutely forbidden, even if the perpetrator is a close friend or brother.

This Islamic law is exactly the opposite of the law that governed the Arab Age of Ignorance—and even today's ignorance—which says: "Support your brother, whether he is the oppressor or the oppressed!"

In those days, if individuals from one tribe attacked another, the rest of the tribe would rise to support them without investigating whether the attack was just or oppressive. This principle also governs today's international relations; often, allied countries or those with shared interests support one another in major global issues without observing the principle of justice or distinguishing between the oppressor and the oppressed.

Islam has drawn a line of cancellation over this ignorant law and commands that the cooperation of Muslims with one another must "only" be in good deeds and useful, constructive programs, not in sin, oppression, and transgression.

It is noteworthy that "righteousness" (Birr) and "piety" (Taqwa) are mentioned together in the above verse; one has a positive aspect referring to useful deeds, and the other has a negative aspect referring to the prevention of wrongful acts. Thus, cooperation must take place both in inviting toward goodness and in combating evil.

In Islamic jurisprudence, this law is utilized in legal matters, and certain transactions and commercial contracts that involve aiding sin are prohibited, such as selling grapes to wine factories, selling weapons to the enemies of truth and justice, or renting a place of business for illegitimate transactions and acts contrary to Sharia (of course, these rulings have conditions explained in books of jurisprudence).

If this principle were revived in Islamic societies and people cooperated with those taking positive and constructive steps—regardless of personal, racial, or family ties—and refrained from cooperating with oppressors and transgressors in any group or class, many social disorders would be rectified.

Similarly, on an international scale, if the governments of the world did not cooperate with the aggressor—whoever it may be—transgression, aggression, colonialism, and exploitation would be eradicated from the world.

However, when we see some of them rising to support aggressors and oppressors, explicitly admitting that shared interests invite them to this support, we should not expect a better situation than this.

In Islamic narrations, there are many emphases on this issue; as an example, we point to a few parts:

1- It is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "When the Day of Resurrection arrives, a caller will cry out: 'Where are the oppressors? And the helpers of the oppressors? And those who made themselves like the oppressors?'—even those who sharpened a pen for them or placed silk-wadding [^1] in an inkwell for them—they will all be placed in a chest of iron and then cast into Hell." [^2]

2- In a narration from Safwan al-Jammal, a companion of Imam al-Kadhim عليه السلام, he says: I visited the Imam, and he said: "O Safwan! All your deeds are good except one!" I said: "May I be sacrificed for you, which one?" He said: "That you rent your camels to this man, meaning Harun!" I said: "By Allah, I do not rent them to him for paths of vanity, play, or forbidden hunting; I only provide them for this path, the path to Mecca. Furthermore, I do not go with the camels myself; I send some of my children and relatives with them." He said: "O Safwan! Do you receive rent from them?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Do you wish for them to remain alive and in power until they pay you your rent?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Whoever loves their survival is one of them, and whoever is one of them will be in the Fire of Hell." Safwan says: "I went immediately and sold all my camels." This reached Harun, who sent for me and said: "Safwan! I heard you sold your camels?" I said: "Yes." He said: "Why?" I said: "I have grown old, and my children and relatives cannot manage them." He said: "It is not so! It is not so! I know who gave you this command; yes, Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام commanded you so." I said: "What do I have to do with Musa ibn Ja'far?" Harun said: "Drop this talk! By Allah, were it not for your good past record, I would have ordered your head to be struck off!" [^1]

In another Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said to Ali عليه السلام: "O Ali, ten groups of this Ummah have disbelieved in Allah, the Most High, the Great... [one of them is] the seller of weapons to the people of war [enemies of Islam who are at war with them]." [^2]

[^1]: Liqah in Arabic refers to the piece of cloth or silk-cotton placed in an inkwell to absorb the ink and prevent it from spilling. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 12, p. 131. [^1]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 12, pp. 131-132. [^2]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 12, p. 71.

Ayah

حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْمَيْتَةُ وَٱلدَّمُ وَلَحْمُ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ وَمَآ أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ ٱللَّهِ بِهِۦ وَٱلْمُنْخَنِقَةُ وَٱلْمَوْقُوذَةُ وَٱلْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ وَٱلنَّطِيحَةُ وَمَآ أَكَلَ ٱلسَّبُعُ إِلَّا مَا ذَكَّيْتُمْ وَمَا ذُبِحَ عَلَى ٱلنُّصُبِ وَأَن تَسْتَقْسِمُوا۟ بِٱلْأَزْلَـٰمِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ ۗ ٱلْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن دِينِكُمْ فَلَا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَٱخْشَوْنِ ۚ ٱلْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِى وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمَ دِينًا ۚ فَمَنِ ٱضْطُرَّ فِى مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لِّإِثْمٍ ۙ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣)
You are prohibited carrion, blood, the flesh of swine, and what has been offered to other than Allah, and the animal strangled or beaten to death, and that which dies by falling or is gored to death, and that which is mangled by a beast of prey—barring that which you may purify —and what is sacrificed on stone altars [to idols], and that you should divide by raffling with arrows. All that is transgression. Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me. Today I have perfected your religion for you, and I have completed My blessing upon you, and I have approved Islam as your religion. But should anyone be compelled by hunger, without inclining to sin, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (3)

Commentary

At the beginning of this Surah, reference was made to the lawfulness of the meat of quadrupeds, with the exception of what would follow. The verse under discussion is, in fact, those very promised exceptions. In this verse, the prohibition of eleven things is decreed, some of which have also been mentioned in other verses of the Quran, and their repetition serves as an emphasis.

First, He says: "Forbidden to you is carrion" (حُرِّمَتْ عَلَيْكُمُ الْمَيْتَةُ).

And also blood (وَ الدَّمُ‌).

And the flesh of swine (وَ لَحْمُ الْخِنْزِيرِ).

And animals slaughtered in the name of idols, according to the custom of the Age of Ignorance, or generally in any name other than Allah's (وَ ما أُهِلَّ لِغَيْرِ اللَّهِ بِهِ‌).

Regarding the prohibition of these four things and their philosophy, we have provided sufficient explanation in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 427 onwards.

Furthermore, animals that have been strangled are forbidden, whether they died by themselves, by a trap, or by human hands (as was common during the Age of Ignorance, where they would sometimes squeeze an animal between two pieces of wood or two stiff branches of a tree until it died so they could use its meat) (وَ الْمُنْخَنِقَةُ).

In some narrations, it is reported that the Magians were particularly committed to killing animals by strangulation and then consuming their meat; therefore, the verse may also be addressing their practice [1]. And animals killed by a violent blow or torture, or those that die from disease (وَ الْمَوْقُوذَةُ) [1]. In the commentary of al-Qurtubi, it is mentioned that it was customary among the Arabs to beat certain animals for the sake of idols until they died, considering it a form of worship! [2]. And animals that die by falling from a height (وَ الْمُتَرَدِّيَةُ), and animals killed by being gored by horns (وَ النَّطِيحَةُ).

And animals killed by the attack of predators (وَ ما أَكَلَ السَّبُعُ‌).

One philosophy behind the prohibition of these five types of meat may be that the blood does not exit them sufficiently. As long as the main veins of the neck are not severed, the blood will not drain adequately, and we know that blood is a center for various microbes. Upon the death of the animal, the blood becomes infected before anything else. In other words, such meats possess a kind of toxicity and are not considered healthy, especially if the animal dies due to torture, disease, or being chased by a predator, which increases the toxicity. Additionally, the spiritual aspect of slaughter—achieved by mentioning the name of Allah and facing the Qibla—is absent in all of these.

However, if these animals are reached before they expire and are slaughtered according to Islamic rites, allowing sufficient blood to drain, they will be lawful. Thus, following the prohibition of the above cases, He says: "except what you [are able to] slaughter [before its death]" (إِلَّا ما ذَكَّيْتُمْ‌).

Some commentators have suggested that this exception refers only to the last category, namely "what has been eaten by predators," but most commentators believe it refers to all the aforementioned categories, and this view is closer to the truth.

A question may arise: why are these cases mentioned when "carrion" (maytah) was mentioned at the beginning of the verse? Are they not all included in the concept of "carrion"?

In response, we say: from a jurisprudential and Sharia perspective, "carrion" has a broad meaning, and any animal not slaughtered according to Sharia methods falls under it. However, linguistically, "carrion" usually refers to an animal that dies on its own. Therefore, the above cases are not included in the linguistic definition of carrion, or at least there is a possibility they are not, hence the need for clarification.

During the Age of Ignorance, idolaters had erected stones around the Kaaba that had no specific shape or form; these were called Nusub. They would sacrifice before them and smear their blood on them. The difference between them and idols was that idols always had specific shapes and forms, while Nusub did not. Islam prohibited such meat in the above verse, saying: "and those sacrificed on stone altars" (وَ ما ذُبِحَ عَلَى النُّصُبِ‌).

It is clear that the prohibition of this type of meat is of a moral and spiritual nature rather than a physical or material one. In reality, it is one of the categories of "what is dedicated to other than Allah," which was explicitly mentioned due to its prevalence among the pagan Arabs.

Another type of animal prohibited in the verse is those slaughtered and distributed through "games of chance." This was done by ten people betting together; they would buy and slaughter an animal, then place ten arrows—seven marked as "winners" and three as "losers"—into a special bag. They would draw them in the name of each of the ten people. Whoever drew a winning arrow would take a portion of the meat without paying anything, but the three who received the losing arrows had to each pay one-third of the animal's price without receiving any meat. These arrows were called Azlam (plural of Zalam). Islam prohibited eating this meat, not because the meat itself was inherently forbidden, but because it involved gambling and games of chance, saying: "and [forbidden is] that you seek decision through divining arrows" (وَ أَنْ تَسْتَقْسِمُوا بِالْأَزْلامِ‌).

It is clear that the prohibition of gambling and the like is not limited to animal meat; rather, it is forbidden in whatever form it takes, as it encompasses all the harms of "uncalculated social activities" and superstitious programs.

Finally, to further emphasize their prohibition, He says: "That is grave disobedience" (ذلِكُمْ فِسْقٌ‌) [1].

Moderation in the Consumption of Meat

What can be inferred from the sum of the above discussions and other Islamic sources is that Islam's approach to the utilization of meat—like its other instructions—follows a path of complete moderation. It is neither like the people of the Age of Ignorance who ate lizard meat, carrion, blood, and the like, nor like many Westerners today who do not even refrain from eating crab meat and worms, nor like the Hindus who absolutely forbid eating meat. Rather, it has permitted the meat of animals that have clean nutrition and are not naturally loathsome, striking a line through both extremist and negligent methods, and establishing conditions for the use of meat as follows:

  1. The animals used for meat must be herbivores. This is because the meat of carnivores is often unhealthy and a source of various diseases due to their consumption of carrion and contaminated meat, unlike herbivorous quadrupeds which usually consume clean and healthy food. Furthermore, as we previously mentioned under verse 72 of Surah Al-Baqarah, every animal transfers its traits through its meat to those who consume it. Therefore, feeding on the meat of predatory animals strengthens the traits of cruelty and predation in humans. It is for this same reason that in Islam, Jallal animals—those that feed on filth—are prohibited.

  2. The animals used for meat must not be naturally loathsome.

  3. They must not produce harm to the human body or soul.

  4. Animals sacrificed in the path of polytheism and idol worship, and their like, are prohibited because they are spiritually impure.

  5. A series of instructions have been issued in Islam regarding the method of slaughtering animals, each of which, in turn, has a hygienic or moral effect.

After stating the above rulings, two profound sentences appear in the verse under discussion. First, it says: "This day those who disbelieve have despaired of [defeating] your religion; so fear them not, but fear Me." (الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَ اخْشَوْنِ‌).

And then it says: "This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion." (الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَ أَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي وَ رَضِيتُ لَكُمُ الْإِسْلامَ دِيناً).

Which Day is the Day of the Perfection of Religion?

A significant discussion arises here: what is meant by "This Day" (Al-Yawm) which is repeated in the two sentences above? That is, which day is it that these "four aspects" converged: the disbelievers despaired, the religion was perfected, Allah's favor was completed, and Allah accepted the faith of Islam as the final religion for the people of the world?

There is much debate among commentators here, but what is beyond doubt is that such a day must be a very important day in the history of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, not a simple, ordinary, or routine day. Such immense significance for an ordinary day makes no sense. Hence, it is mentioned in some narrations that some Jews and Christians, upon hearing this verse, said that if such a verse had been revealed in our scriptures, we would have made that day a day of celebration [1]. Now, based on clues, signs, the history of the revelation of this verse and this Surah, the biography of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and narrations reached from various Islamic sources, we must find this important day.

Does it refer to the day the above rulings regarding lawful and forbidden meats were revealed? Certainly not, because the revelation of these rulings does not carry such immense importance. It is not the cause of the perfection of religion, as these were not the last rulings revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, evidenced by the fact that we encounter other rulings later in this Surah. Furthermore, the revelation of these rulings does not cause the despair of the disbelievers. What causes the despair of the disbelievers is the provision of a solid support for the future of Islam. In other words, the revelation of these rulings and their like has little impact on the morale of the disbelievers; they have no particular sensitivity toward whether certain meats are lawful or forbidden.

Does it refer to the Day of Arafah during the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله final pilgrimage?! (As a group of commentators have suggested). The answer to this is also negative, as the aforementioned signs do not apply to that day either. No specific event occurred on that day that would cause the despair of the disbelievers. If the reference is to the large gathering of Muslims, they were already in the service of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Mecca before the Day of Arafah. And if the reference is to the revelation of the above rulings on that day, as we said, that was not something terrifying for the disbelievers.

And does it refer to the day of the Conquest of Mecca (as some have suggested), even though the date of this Surah's revelation was long after the Conquest of Mecca?! Or does it refer to the day of the revelation of the verses of Surah Bara'ah, which was also long before the revelation of this Surah?! Most strange of all is the suggestion by some that this day is the day of the emergence of Islam or the appointment (Bi'thah) of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, despite them having no connection to the day of this verse's revelation, with many years separating them.

Therefore, none of the six aforementioned possibilities are consistent with the contents of the verse.

Here, there is another possibility that all Shia commentators have recorded in their books, supported by numerous narrations, and which is perfectly consistent with the contents of the verse:

It refers to the Day of Ghadir Khum, the day when the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله officially appointed the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, as his successor. It was on that day that the disbelievers were submerged in waves of despair, for they expected the faith of Islam to be dependent on a person, and that with the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, affairs would return to their former state and Islam would gradually be dismantled. However, when they observed that a man who was peerless among Muslims in terms of knowledge, piety, power, and justice after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was chosen as the Prophet's successor and allegiance was taken from the people for him, despair and hopelessness regarding the future of Islam overcame them, and they realized it was a deep-rooted and enduring faith.

It was on this day that the faith of Islam reached its final perfection, for without the appointment of a successor for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and without clarifying the future state of the Muslims, this faith would not have reached final perfection.

It was on that day that Allah's favor was completed by the appointment of a worthy leader like Ali عليه السلام for the future of the people.

And it was also on that day that Islam, with the completion of its programs, was accepted by Allah as the final religion (thus, the four aspects converged in it).

Furthermore, the following clues also support this interpretation:

A- It is noteworthy that in the commentaries of Fakhr Razi, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and al-Manar, it is narrated under this verse that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not live more than eighty-one days after the revelation of this verse. Given that the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Sunni narrations and even in some Shia narrations (such as what al-Kulayni narrated in the famous book al-Kafi) was on the 12th of Rabi' al-Awwal, we conclude that the day of the verse's revelation was exactly the 18th of Dhu al-Hijjah [1].

B- In numerous narrations reported through well-known Sunni and Shia channels, it is explicitly stated that the above noble verse was revealed on the Day of Ghadir Khum following the communication of the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام. Among them:

  1. The famous Sunni scholar Ibn Jarir al-Tabari, in the book al-Wilayah, narrates from the famous companion Zayd ibn Arqam that this verse was revealed on the Day of Ghadir Khum regarding Ali عليه السلام.

  2. Hafiz Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani, in the book Ma Nazala min al-Quran fi Ali عليه السلام, narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri (the famous companion) that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله introduced Ali عليه السلام to the people at Ghadir Khum as the authority (Wilayah), and the people had not dispersed until the verse "This day I have perfected for you..." was revealed. At this moment, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Allah is Greatest (Allahu Akbar) for the perfection of religion, the completion of favor, and the pleasure of the Lord with my message and the Wilayah of Ali عليه السلام after me." Then he said: "Whomsoever I am his master (mawla), Ali is his master. O Allah! Befriend him who befriends him, and be the enemy of him who is his enemy; help him who helps him, and forsake him who forsakes him."

  3. Khatib al-Baghdadi, in his Tarikh, narrates from Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that after the event of Ghadir Khum and the covenant of Ali's عليه السلام Wilayah, and the statement of Umar ibn al-Khattab: "Congratulations, O son of Abu Talib! You have become my master and the master of every Muslim." The verse "This day I have perfected for you your religion" was revealed [1]. In the precious book al-Ghadir, in addition to the three aforementioned narrations, thirteen other narrations are cited in this regard.

In the book Ihqaq al-Haq, from the second volume of Tafsir Ibn Kathir, page 14, and from Maqtal al-Khwarizmi, page 47, the revelation of this verse regarding the story of Ghadir is narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

In Tafsir al-Burhan and Nur al-Thaqalayn, ten narrations from various channels are also cited stating that this verse was revealed regarding Ali عليه السلام or the Day of Ghadir Khum, the citation of all of which would require a separate treatise [2]. The late Allamah Sayyid Sharaf al-Din says in the book al-Muraja'at: "The revelation of this verse on the Day of Ghadir is mentioned in authentic narrations reported from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, and the Sunnis have narrated six hadiths with different chains from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard, which are explicit about the revelation of the verse in this event" [3].

From what we have said above, it becomes clear that: the reports describing the revelation of the above verse during the event of Ghadir are not in the category of "solitary reports" (khabar al-wahid) that could be ignored by weakening some of their chains; rather, they are reports that, if not mutawatir (frequently narrated), are at least mustafid (widely known), and are recorded in famous Islamic sources. Although some biased Sunni scholars, such as al-Alusi in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, have tried to consign the rest to oblivion by weakening the chain of only one of these reports, labeling them as fabricated and incorrect because the narration did not suit their taste; or like the author of Tafsir al-Manar, who provided a simple interpretation of the verse without making the slightest reference to these narrations, perhaps finding himself in a deadlock where mentioning and weakening the narrations would be contrary to fairness, and accepting them would be contrary to his taste!

An interesting point to note here is that the Quran says in Surah An-Nur, verse 55: "Allah has promised those who have believed among you and done righteous deeds that He will surely grant them succession [to authority] upon the earth just as He granted it to those before them and that He will surely establish for them [therein] their religion which He has preferred for them and that He will surely substitute for them, after their fear, security..."

In this verse, Allah says: He will establish on the earth the religion He has "preferred" (irtada) for them. Given that Surah An-Nur was revealed before Surah Al-Ma'idah, and considering the phrase "and have approved (raditu) for you Islam as religion" in the verse under discussion, which was revealed regarding the Wilayah of Ali عليه السلام, we conclude that Islam will become established and deep-rooted on earth only when it is coupled with Wilayah. For this is the very Islam that God has "preferred" and promised to establish and strengthen. In clearer terms, Islam becomes global only if it is not separated from the issue of the Wilayah of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام.

Another point derived from combining the "verse of Surah An-Nur" with the "verse under discussion" is that in the verse of Surah An-Nur, three promises are given to the believers: first, succession on earth; second, security and peace for the worship of the Lord; and third, the establishment of the religion that is pleasing to Allah.

These three promises were fulfilled on the Day of Ghadir Khum with the revelation of the verse "This day I have perfected for you your religion..." because the perfect example of a person with faith and righteous deeds, namely Ali عليه السلام, was appointed as the Prophet's successor. According to the phrase "This day those who disbelieve have despaired of your religion," the Muslims were placed in relative peace and security, and according to "and have approved for you Islam as religion," the religion pleasing to the Lord was established among the Muslims.

Of course, this interpretation does not contradict the narrations stating that the verse of Surah An-Nur was revealed concerning the status of al-Mahdi عليه السلام, because "those who believe among you..." has a broad meaning, one instance of which was realized on the Day of Ghadir Khum, and then it will be realized on a much broader and more universal scale at the time of the uprising of al-Mahdi عليه السلام. (Therefore, the word "the earth" in the verse does not mean the entire globe, but has a broad meaning that can refer to the entire globe or a part of it, as is also understood from its usage in the Quran). (Reflect on this).

A Necessary Question

The only question remaining regarding the verse is: first, according to the aforementioned documents and the documents that will come under the verse "O Messenger, announce that which has been revealed to you," both are related to the event of "Ghadir," so why is there a distance between them in the Quran?! One is verse 3 of Surah Al-Ma'idah and the other is verse 67 of the same Surah.

Second, this part of the verse related to the Ghadir event is attached to matters concerning lawful and forbidden meats, and there seems to be little correlation between the two [1].

In response, it must be said

First, we know that the verses of the Quran, as well as its Surahs, were not collected according to the chronological order of revelation. Rather, many Surahs revealed in Medina contain verses revealed in Mecca, and conversely, we see Medinan verses interspersed within Meccan Surahs. Given this fact, the separation of these two verses from each other in the Quran is not surprising (of course, the arrangement of the verses of each Surah was only by the command of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله). Yes, if the verses had been collected according to the date of revelation, this objection would be valid.

Second, it is possible that placing the verse related to "Ghadir" amidst the rulings concerning lawful and forbidden foods was for protection against distortion, deletion, and alteration. It often happens that to preserve a precious object, it is mixed with simple matters so that it attracts less attention (Reflect on this).

The events that occurred in the final hours of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, and the explicit opposition by some individuals to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله writing a will—to the extent that they even accused the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (God forbid!) of delirium and illness and speaking incoherent words—the details of which are recorded in famous Islamic books of both Sunnis and Shias [1], are a vivid witness that some individuals had a specific sensitivity regarding the issue of the Caliphate and the succession of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and recognized no boundaries in denying it!

Did such conditions not necessitate that, to preserve the documents related to the Caliphate and deliver them to future generations, such precautions be taken and they be mixed with simple matters that would less attract the attention of fierce opponents?!

Beyond this—as we have learned—the documents related to the revelation of the verse "This day I have perfected for you" regarding "Ghadir" and the issue of the Prophet's succession are not narrated only in Shia books for such an objection to be directed at the Shia; rather, they appear in many Sunni books as well, and this hadith has been narrated through multiple channels from three famous companions.

At the end of the verse, it returns once more to the issues of forbidden meats and states the ruling for the case of necessity, saying: "But whoever is forced by severe hunger with no inclination to sin—then indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful," and He does not burden His servants with hardship in times of necessity nor does He punish them. (فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِي مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجانِفٍ لِإِثْمٍ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

Makhmassah is derived from the root khams, meaning "recession" or "hollowness," and it also refers to extreme hunger that causes the stomach to become hollow, whether during a famine or a personal predicament.

Ghayra mutajanifin li-ithm means having no inclination toward sin. This either serves as an emphasis on the concept of necessity, or it is intended to mean that in times of necessity, one should not exceed in eating forbidden meat, nor consider it lawful, or that one should not have brought about the necessity themselves, or that one should not encounter such necessity during a journey undertaken for a sinful act. It is possible that all these meanings are intended by this phrase. (For more explanation in this regard, refer to Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 430 and 431.)

It is also in Sahih Muslim in Part 2, p. 14, at the end of "Wills," as well as in other books as cited by the late Sharaf al-Din in al-Muraja'at under the title "The Calamity of Thursday."

Ayah

يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ مَاذَآ أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ ۖ قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ ۙ وَمَا عَلَّمْتُم مِّنَ ٱلْجَوَارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ فَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّآ أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱسْمَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٤)
They ask you as to what is lawful to them. Say, ‘All the good things are lawful to you.’ As for what you have taught hunting dogs [to catch], teaching them out of what Allah has taught you, eat of what they catch for you and mention Allah’s Name over it, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is swift at reckoning. (4)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the above verse, several occasions of revelation have been mentioned, the most appropriate of which is the following:

Zayd al-Khayr and 'Adi ibn Hatim, who were two companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, came to him and said: "We are a people who hunt with dogs and predatory birds. Our hunting dogs catch wild animals whose meat is permissible (halal). Some of them reach us alive, and we slaughter them, but some are killed by the dogs, and we do not find the opportunity to slaughter them. Given that we know Allah has forbidden carrion (dead meat) to us, what is our duty?" The above verse was revealed and provided them with the answer. [^1]

Commentary

Permissible Hunting

Following the rulings regarding permissible and forbidden meats mentioned in the previous two verses, this verse refers to another part of them. As an answer to a question asked in this regard, it states: "They ask you what has been made lawful for them" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ ما ذا أُحِلَّ لَهُمْ).

Then, it commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to first tell them that every pure thing is lawful for you (قُلْ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّباتُ).

This means that everything Islam has prohibited falls under the category of "khaba'ith" (vile/impure things).

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Volume 3, under the verse being discussed.

Divine laws never prohibit a pure entity that was naturally created for the use and benefit of humanity; the system of "Legislation" (Tashri') is everywhere in harmony with the system of "Creation" (Takwin).

Then, turning to hunted animals, it says: "And [lawful for you is] what you have taught the predatory animals, training them as hounds, teaching them from what Allah has taught you" (وَ ما عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوارِحِ مُكَلِّبِينَ تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ). [^1] The word jawarikh is originally derived from the root jarh, which sometimes means "acquisition" (earning) and sometimes means "wound." For this reason, predatory animals—whether birds or others—are called jarihah (plural: jawarikh), meaning an animal that wounds its prey or an animal that earns for its owner.

If the limbs of the body are called jawarikh, it is because a human performs tasks and makes acquisitions through them.

In this way, the phrase "وَ ما عَلَّمْتُمْ مِنَ الْجَوارِحِ" (what you have taught of the predatory animals) includes all animals trained for hunting. However, the constraint "mukallibin," which means "trainers of hunting dogs" and is derived from the root kalb (dog), restricts it to hunting dogs. For this reason, it does not include hunting with animals other than dogs, such as hunting with hawks and the like. Consequently, in Shi'a jurisprudence, hunting is "only" permissible by means of hunting dogs, although a group of Sunni scholars and commentators consider all of them permissible and have interpreted the term "mukallibin" in a broad sense that is not exclusive to dogs. However, as we mentioned, the original root of this word restricts its concept to the training of hunting dogs.

Of course, if other hunting animals kill a prey, but we slaughter it according to Sharia rites before it dies, it is lawful.

Furthermore, the phrase "تُعَلِّمُونَهُنَّ مِمَّا عَلَّمَكُمُ اللَّهُ" (teaching them from what Allah has taught you) points to several matters: First, the training of such animals must be continuous; if they forget their training and tear an animal like a stray dog, the meat of that prey will not be lawful (because the verb tu'allimunahunna is in the present continuous tense, which denotes continuity).

Second, the education and training of the dog must be according to correct principles compatible with the concept of "what Allah has taught you." Third, the source of all sciences, however simple or small, is from Allah, and we have no knowledge without His teaching.

Additionally, it should be noted that the purpose of training hunting dogs is that they be trained to move at their owners' command and return at their command. It is also necessary to mention that if the animal caught by the dogs is found alive, it must be slaughtered according to Islamic rites; but if it dies before they reach it, it is lawful, even though it was not slaughtered.

Then, at the end of the verse, it points to two other conditions for the lawfulness of such prey, saying: "Eat of what they have caught for you" (فَكُلُوا مِمَّا أَمْسَكْنَ عَلَيْكُمْ).

Therefore, if hunting dogs are accustomed to eating part of their prey and leaving the rest, such prey is not lawful and falls under the category of "what wild beasts have eaten" mentioned in the previous verse. In reality, such a dog is neither truly trained, nor is what it kept an instance of "for you" ('alaykum);

[^1]: At the beginning of this phrase, there is an omission and an implied word; originally it was "and the prey of what you have taught..." based on the evidence of the phrase "Eat of what they have caught for you" (reflect on this).

rather, it hunted for itself. (However, some jurists, based on narrations found in hadith sources, do not consider this a condition, the details of which are found in jurisprudence). In short, they must be trained such that they do not eat their prey.

The other condition is that when the hunting dog is released, you must mention the name of Allah (وَ اذْكُرُوا اسْمَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْهِ).

And finally, to ensure the observance of all these instructions, it says: "And fear Allah, for Allah is swift in account" (وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَرِيعُ الْحِسابِ). [^1]

Ayah

ٱلْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتُ ۖ وَطَعَامُ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ حِلٌّ لَّكُمْ وَطَعَامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَّهُمْ ۖ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُحْصَنَـٰتُ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ إِذَآ ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَّ مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسَـٰفِحِينَ وَلَا مُتَّخِذِىٓ أَخْدَانٍ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُۥ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٥)
Today all the good things have been made lawful to you—the food of those who were given the Book is lawful to you, and your food is lawful to them—and the chaste ones from among faithful women, and chaste women of those who were given the Book before you, when you have given them their dowries, in wedlock, not in license, nor taking paramours. Should anyone renounce his faith, his work shall fail and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter. (5)

Commentary

Consuming the Food of the People of the Book and Marrying Them

In this verse, which complements the preceding verses, it first states: "This day [all] good things have been made lawful to you. The food of those who were given the Scripture is lawful for you and your food is lawful for them."

(الْيَوْمَ أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ الطَّيِّباتُ وَ طَعامُ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ حِلٌّ لَكُمْ وَ طَعامُكُمْ حِلٌّ لَهُمْ‌).

There are several points here that must be noted:

1- According to a group of commentators, the meaning of "Today" (اليوم) is the Day of Arafah, and according to some, it refers to the period after the conquest of Khaybar. However, it is not unlikely that it refers to the Day of Ghadir Khum and the complete victory of Islam over the disbelievers (an explanation of this will be provided shortly).

2- The mention of "good things" (طيبات) being lawful, despite them being lawful before this day as well, serves as a preamble to mentioning the ruling regarding the "food of the People of the Book."

3- What is meant by the "food of the People of the Book" which is deemed lawful in this verse?

Most Sunni commentators and scholars believe it includes every type of food, whether it be the meat of animals slaughtered by their own hands or otherwise. However, the overwhelming majority of Shi'a commentators and jurists are of the opinion that it refers to things other than meat slaughtered by them; only a small number of Shi'a scholars follow the first view.

Numerous narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام emphasize that "food" in this verse refers to things other than the slaughtered animals of the People of the Book.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the above verse that he said:

عنى بطعامهم هاهنا الحبوب و الفاكهة غير الذبائح التي يذبحون فانهم لا يذكرون اسم اللَّه عليها

"What is meant by their food here is grains and fruits, not the animals they slaughter, for they do not mention the name of Allah over them." [^1]

There are several other narrations mentioned in Volume 16 of Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Chapter 51 of the Chapters on Foods and Drinks, page 371. A careful examination of the preceding verses shows that the second interpretation (interpreting food as other than slaughtered meat) is closer to the truth. As Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام pointed out in the aforementioned narration, the People of the Book generally do not observe the conditions of Islamic slaughter; they neither mention the name of Allah nor face the animal toward the Qibla, nor are they committed to observing other conditions. How could it be possible for such an animal to be explicitly forbidden in the previous verses and then deemed lawful in this verse?

Here, several questions arise:

First, if "food" refers to items other than meat, these were already lawful. Was there any prohibition against buying wheat or other grains from the People of the Book before the revelation of this verse, while trade and commerce always existed between Muslims and them?

The answer to this question becomes clear by considering a fundamental point in the interpretation of the verse: the verse was revealed at a time when Islam had gained dominance over the Arabian Peninsula and had established its presence and authority throughout the region, such that the enemies of Islam had despaired of defeating the Muslims. At this stage, the restrictions—

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 16, p. 291.

—that previously existed regarding the social interaction of Muslims with disbelievers, which had made visiting them, hosting them, or being hosted by them prohibited, needed to be removed. Therefore, the verse was revealed, declaring that today, now that you have consolidated your position and do not fear danger from them, the restrictions regarding social interaction with them have been eased. You may go to their banquets, you may host them, and you may also take wives from them (each with the conditions that will be mentioned).

It should be noted that those who do not consider the People of the Book to be [ritually] pure state that one can only share food with them if their food is of the non-moist variety, or if moist, it has not come into contact with their hands. However, those researchers who believe in the [ritual] purity of the People of the Book say that sharing food with them is permissible provided their food is not prepared from meat they have slaughtered and there is no certainty of secondary impurity (becoming impure through things like wine or beer and the like).

In short, the above verse is primarily concerned with lifting previous restrictions regarding social interaction with the People of the Book. The evidence for this is His statement: "and your food is lawful for them," meaning there is no objection to hosting them. Furthermore, immediately in the following verse, the ruling on marrying women from the People of the Book is stated. It is obvious that only a government that has complete control over its environment and has no fear of the enemy can grant such latitude to its subjects. Such conditions were effectively realized on the Day of Ghadir Khum, or according to some, on the Day of Arafah during the Farewell Pilgrimage, or after the conquest of Khaybar—though the Day of Ghadir Khum seems most appropriate for this subject in every respect.

Another objection mentioned in Tafsir al-Manar regarding the interpretation of the above verse is that the word "food" (طعام) in many verses of the Quran refers to any kind of—

—food and even includes meats. How is it possible for it to be limited to grains, fruits, and the like in the above verse? He then writes: "I raised this objection in a gathering where a group of Shi'ites were present (and no one had an answer for it)."

In our view, the answer to the above objection is also clear. We do not deny that "food" has a broad meaning, but the previous verses that discussed meats—specifically prohibiting the meat of animals over which the name of Allah was not mentioned at the time of slaughter—specify this broad concept and limit it to things other than meat. We know that any general (عام) or absolute (مطلق) term is subject to specification (تخصيص) and qualification (تقييد). We also know that the People of the Book are not committed to mentioning the name of Allah over the slaughtered animal; moreover, they certainly do not observe the other conditions mentioned in the "Sunnah."

In the book Kanz al-Irfan, in the commentary of this verse, another objection is pointed out, the summary of which is: "Good things" (طيبات) is a broad concept and is technically "general," while "the food of those who were given the Scripture" is "specific." Usually, mentioning the specific after the general must have a particular point, which is not clear here. He then expresses hope that Allah will solve this scholarly problem for him. [^1]

Considering what was mentioned above, the answer to this objection also becomes clear: mentioning the lawfulness of "good things" is actually a preamble to stating the removal of restrictions on social mixing with the People of the Book. In effect, the verse says: "Every pure thing has been made lawful for you; for this reason, the food of the People of the Book (where it is pure) is also lawful for you, and the restrictions you previously had in social interaction with them have been reduced in light of the victories you have achieved today." (Reflect on this).

[^1]: Kanz al-Irfan, Vol. 2, p. 312.

Marriage with Non-Muslim Women

After stating the lawfulness of the food of the People of the Book, the above verse speaks about marriage with chaste women from among the Muslims and the People of the Book, saying: "And [lawful in marriage are] chaste women from among the believers and chaste women from among those who were given the Scripture before you, when you have given them their due compensation."

(وَ الْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِناتِ وَ الْمُحْصَناتُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ إِذا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَ‌).

On the condition that it is through legitimate marriage, not in the form of open fornication, nor by taking secret lovers.

(مُحْصِنِينَ غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ وَ لا مُتَّخِذِي أَخْدانٍ‌) [^1]

In truth, this part of the verse also reduces the restrictions that existed regarding the marriage of Muslims with non-Muslims and permits marriage with women of the People of the Book under certain conditions.

However, as to whether marriage with the People of the Book is permissible in any form—whether it be permanent or temporary marriage—or whether only temporary marriage is permissible, there is a difference of opinion among Islamic jurists.

Sunni scholars make no distinction between these two types of marriage and believe the above verse is general. However, among Shi'a jurists, a group believes that the verse exclusively describes temporary marriage, and some narrations transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام—

[^1]: As we mentioned in Volume 3 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 25 of Surah An-Nisa, "akhdan" is the plural of "khidn," which originally means friend or companion, but is usually used for a secret lover of the opposite sex in an illegitimate capacity.

—in this regard support this view.

There are clues in the verse that may support this opinion. First, He says: "when you have given them their due compensation" (إِذا آتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ أُجُورَهُنَ). It is true that the word "ajr" (compensation/wage) is used for both the "dowry of a permanent contract" and the "dowry of a temporary marriage," but it is more frequently mentioned in the context of temporary marriage—meaning it has a greater affinity with it. Secondly, the expression "not committing fornication nor taking secret lovers" (غَيْرَ مُسافِحِينَ وَ لا مُتَّخِذِي أَخْدانٍ) is also more consistent with temporary marriage, because permanent marriage bears no resemblance to the issue of fornication or choosing an illegitimate secret lover such that it would need to be forbidden. However, sometimes ignorant and uninformed individuals mistake temporary marriage for fornication or choosing a secret lover. Above all, these exact expressions are seen in verse 25 of Surah An-Nisa, and we know that verse is about temporary marriage.

Nevertheless, another group of jurists considers marriage with the People of the Book to be absolutely permissible and does not find the above clues sufficient to restrict the verse, and they also argue based on some narrations in this regard (further details on this should be studied in books of jurisprudence).

It should not go unmentioned that in today's world, where many Ignorant (Jahili) customs have been revived in various forms, the idea has emerged that choosing a female or male "friend" for single individuals is unobjectionable—not only in a secret manner, as existed in the pre-Islamic era of Ignorance, but openly as well! In fact, today's world has gone a step beyond the era of Ignorance in sexual pollution and licentiousness; for if at that time they only considered choosing a secret lover permissible, these people consider it permissible openly as well and even boast about it with the utmost audacity. This disgraceful custom, which is considered an open and scandalous indecency—

—is one of the ominous "souvenirs" transferred from the West to the East and has become the source of many misfortunes and crimes.

It is also necessary to mention this point: regarding the food of the People of the Book, permission has been given both to eat from their food (under the conditions mentioned) and to feed them. However, regarding marriage, only taking a wife from them is permitted; Muslim women are by no means permitted to marry men from the People of the Book. The philosophy behind this is self-evident, as women, due to having more delicate emotions, may more quickly accept the beliefs of their husbands than men!

And since the aforementioned facilities regarding social interaction with the People of the Book and marrying their women might be misused by some, leading them consciously or unconsciously toward them, at the end of the verse, Allah warns the Muslims, saying:

"And whoever denies the faith—his work has become worthless, and he, in the Hereafter, will be among the losers."

(وَ مَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالْإِيمانِ فَقَدْ حَبِطَ عَمَلُهُ وَ هُوَ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنَ الْخاسرِينَ‌).

This is an indication that these facilities, in addition to creating ease in your life, should be a cause for the influence and expansion of Islam among outsiders, not that you should fall under their influence and abandon your own religion, in which case your punishment will be very severe and heavy. [^1]

In the interpretation of this part of the verse, considering some narrations and the circumstances of revelation that have been narrated, there is another possibility: that some Muslims, after the revelation of the above verse and the ruling on the lawfulness of the food of the People of the Book and their women, were—

[^1]: Regarding "habt" (nullification of deeds), refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 69, under verse 217 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

—reluctant to accept such a ruling. The Quran warns them that if they have any objection to such a ruling revealed by Allah and deny it, their deeds will go to waste and they will be among the losers.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ فَٱغْسِلُوا۟ وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْمَرَافِقِ وَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِرُءُوسِكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى ٱلْكَعْبَيْنِ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُمْ جُنُبًا فَٱطَّهَّرُوا۟ ۚ وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰٓ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَآءَ أَحَدٌ مِّنكُم مِّنَ ٱلْغَآئِطِ أَوْ لَـٰمَسْتُمُ ٱلنِّسَآءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا۟ مَآءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا۟ صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَٱمْسَحُوا۟ بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُم مِّنْهُ ۚ مَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْ حَرَجٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُۥ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٦)
O you who have faith! When you stand up for prayer, wash your faces and your hands up to the elbows, and wipe a part of your heads and your feet, up to the ankles. If you are junub, purify yourselves. But if you are sick, or on a journey, or any of you has come from the toilet, or you have touched women, and you cannot find water, then make tayammum with clean ground and wipe a part of your faces and your hands with it. Allah does not desire to put you to hardship, but He desires to purify you, and to complete His blessing upon you so that you may give thanks. (6)

Commentary

Purification of Body and Soul

In the preceding verses, various discussions regarding "physical wholesome things and material blessings" were presented. In this verse, reference is made to the "wholesome things of the soul" and that which causes the purification of the human spirit. A significant portion of the rulings on Wudu (ablution), Ghusl (ritual bath), and Tayammum (dry ablution)—which result in spiritual clarity—are explained. First, addressing the believers, the rulings of Wudu are stated as follows:

"O you who have believed, when you rise to [perform] prayer [^1], wash your faces and your forearms to the elbows and wipe over [a portion of] your heads and [wash/wipe] your feet to the ankles."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلَاةِ فَاغْسِلُوا وُجُوهَكُمْ وَأَيْدِيَكُمْ إِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ وَامْسَحُوا بِرُؤُسِكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ إِلَى الْكَعْبَيْنِ)

[^1]: In several narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, it is mentioned that the phrase "qumtum" (you rise) here refers to rising from sleep. A careful examination of the verse's content supports this meaning, because in the subsequent sentences, while explaining the ruling of Tayammum, He says: "أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ" (or one of you comes from the place of relieving himself)... If the beginning of the verse explained the ruling for all those who are technically without Wudu, then conjoining this phrase—especially using "or" (aw)—would not align with the apparent meaning of the verse, as that too is included under the status of being without Wudu. However, if the beginning of the verse exclusively describes the state of those waking from sleep (referring only to the "hadath of sleep"), then this phrase becomes perfectly meaningful (reflect upon this).

In the verse, the boundaries of the face that must be washed in Wudu are not detailed, but they are explained in the narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, who described the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله Wudu in detail:

1- The boundary of the face in length is from the hairline to the chin, and in width, it is what falls between the middle finger and the thumb. This is, in fact, an explanation of the meaning understood from the word "wajh" (face) in common usage, as the face is that part with which a person "confronts" (muwajahah) another upon meeting.

2- The boundary of the arm to be washed in Wudu is mentioned as "to the elbows," because "marafıq" is the plural of "mirfaq," meaning "elbow." Since saying "wash your hands" might lead one to think of washing only up to the wrists—as this portion is most frequently washed—to remove this misconception, He says: wash up to the elbows (إِلَى الْمَرَافِقِ).

With this explanation, it becomes clear that the word "ila" (to/until) in the above verse is only to define the extent of washing, not the method of washing, as some have mistakenly assumed, imagining that the verse says: you must wash the arms from the fingertips toward the elbow (as is common among a group of Sunnis).

To clarify, this is exactly like ordering a worker to paint a room's wall "from the floor to one meter." Obviously, the intention is not that the wall must be painted from bottom to top, but rather that this specific amount must be painted—no more and no less.

Therefore, only the portion of the arm that must be washed is mentioned in the verse, while the method is found in the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which has reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt, and that is washing from the elbow toward the fingertips.

It should be noted that the "elbow" itself must also be washed in Wudu, because in such cases, technically, "the limit is included in the limited" (al-ghayah dakhilah fil-mughayya); meaning the boundary is included in the ruling of the area being bounded. [^1]

3- The letter "ba" in "bi-ru'usikum" (بِرُؤُسِكُمْ), according to explicit narrations and the statements of some linguists, denotes "partiality" (tab'id). That is, wipe "a part" of the head. In our narrations, this area is limited to the front quarter of the head, and a portion of this quarter—however small—must be wiped with the hand. Therefore, the practice common among some Sunni sects of wiping the entire head and even the ears does not align with the meaning of the verse.

4- The placement of "arjulakum" (your feet) alongside "ru'usikum" (your heads) is evidence that the feet must also be wiped, not washed. (If we observe that "arjulakum" is recited with a fatha on the letter lam, it is because it is conjoined to the position of "bi-ru'usikum," not conjoined to "wujuhakum"). [^2]

5- "Ka'b" linguistically means the protrusion on the top of the feet and also means the joint—the point where the shin bone connects to the foot bone. [^3]

[^1]: Sibawayh, the famous scholar of Arabic linguistics, says: Whenever what follows "ila" and what precedes it are of the same genus, what follows is included in the ruling; if they are of two different genera, it is excluded. (For example, if it is said "fast until the last hour of the day," it means the last hour is also included in the fast; but if it is said "fast until the beginning of the night," it means the beginning of the night is not included). (Al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 223).

[^2]: There is no doubt that there is a relatively large distance between "wujuhakum" and "arjulakum," making conjunction to the former seem unlikely. Furthermore, a large number of reciters have read "arjulakum" with a kasra.

[^3]: In the Qamus, "ka'b" is mentioned with three meanings: "the protrusion on the back of the foot," "the joint," and "the ankles" (malleoli) on either side of the foot. However, with the explanation provided in the Sunnah, it is certain that the side ankles are not intended. As for whether it means the protruding bone on the back of the foot or the joint, there is no consensus among jurists; in any case, precaution dictates wiping up to the joint.

Then, He proceeds to explain the ruling of Ghusl, stating: "And if you are in a state of Janabah (ritual impurity), then purify yourselves." (وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ جُنُباً فَاطَّهَّرُوا).

It is clear that the phrase "fattahharu" (purify yourselves) refers to washing the entire body, because if washing a specific limb were required, it would have been named. Therefore, when He says "wash yourselves," it implies washing the whole body. A similar expression appears in Surah An-Nisa, verse 43: "حَتَّىٰ تَغْتَسِلُوا" (until you have washed [your whole body]).

"Junub"—as we noted in the third volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 43 of Surah An-Nisa—is a verbal noun used as an "active participle" (ism fa'il), originally meaning "one who moves away." Its root "janabah" means "distance" or "remoteness." A person in this state is called "junub" because they must, in that state, stay away from prayer, staying in a mosque, and the like. This word (junub) is applied to singular, plural, masculine, and feminine alike. The term "jar junub" for a distant neighbor is used for the same reason. It is possible that from the fact that the Quran says to perform Ghusl for prayer if you are "junub," it can be inferred that Ghusl of Janabah also replaces Wudu.

Then, He explains the ruling of Tayammum, saying: "And if you have risen from sleep and intend to pray and are ill or on a journey, or if one of you comes from the place of relieving himself, or you have had sexual contact with women and do not find water, then perform Tayammum with clean earth."

(وَإِنْ كُنْتُمْ مَرْضَىٰ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ فَلَمْ تَجِدُوا مَاءً فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيداً طَيِّباً).

A point to be noted is that the phrase "أَوْ جَاءَ أَحَدٌ مِنْكُمْ مِنَ الْغَائِطِ" (or one of you comes from the place of relieving himself) and the phrase "أَوْ لَامَسْتُمُ النِّسَاءَ" (or you have touched women)—as we previously mentioned—are conjoined to the beginning of the verse, i.e., the phrase "إِذَا قُمْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلَاةِ" (when you rise for prayer). In fact, the beginning of the verse refers to the issue of sleep, and the end of the verse refers to two other categories of causes for Wudu or Ghusl.
