If we were to conjoin these two phrases to "on a journey" (ala safarin), two problems would arise in the verse: first, returning from relieving oneself cannot be a counterpart to illness or travel, and thus we would be forced to take "aw" (or) to mean "wa" (and)—as some commentators have said—which is completely contrary to the apparent meaning. Furthermore, mentioning specifically the relief of nature among the causes of Wudu would be without [apparent] reason. However, if we interpret the verse as we have stated, neither of these two problems arises (reflect upon this). (Although we, like many commentators, mentioned "aw" as meaning "and" in volume three under verse 43 of An-Nisa, what is stated here seems closer to the truth).

Another matter is that in this verse, the issue of Janabah is mentioned twice, which may be for emphasis. It is also possible that the word "junub" refers to Janabah via nocturnal emission (wet dream), while "or you have touched women" is a metaphor for Janabah through sexual intercourse. If we interpret "rising" in the verse as rising from sleep (as narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام and for which there is an indication in the verse itself), it would support this meaning (reflect upon this).

Then, He briefly explains the method of Tayammum, saying: "wipe your faces and hands with it."

(فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ مِنْهُ).

It is clear that the intention is not to pick up some soil and rub it on the face and hands, but rather to wipe the face and hands after striking the hands upon clean earth. However, some jurists, because of the word "minhu" (from it), have said that at least a minimal amount of dust must stick to the hand. [^1]

The only thing remaining here is the meaning of "sa'idan tayyiban": many linguists have mentioned two meanings for "sa'id": one is "soil/dust" and the other is "everything that covers the surface of the earth," including soil, sand, stone, etc. This very subject has caused a difference of opinion among jurists regarding what is permissible for Tayammum—whether it is only permissible on soil or if stone and sand and the like are also sufficient. However, considering the linguistic root of the word "sa'id," which is "su'ud" (to ascend/be on top), the second meaning is closer to the mind.

"Tayyib" refers to things that are agreeable to human nature and is applied to many subjects in the Quran (the good land, good dwellings, a good wind, a good life, etc.). Anything pure is also called "tayyib" because human nature instinctively dislikes impure things. From this, it becomes clear that the earth for Tayammum must be completely pure and clean.

This point has been repeatedly emphasized in the narrations that have reached us from the leaders of Islam. In one narration, we read:

"The Commander of the Faithful (Ali) عليه السلام forbade a man from performing Tayammum with dust from the tracks of a road." [^2]

It is noteworthy that although "Tayammum" in the Quran and Hadith refers to this specific Islamic duty, linguistically it means "to intend." In fact, the Quran says: when you wish to perform Tayammum, you must intend to choose a piece of pure earth from among various pieces of land and perform Tayammum upon it—a piece that, according to the concept of "sa'id" (from the root of su'ud), is situated on the surface of the earth and is exposed to rainfall, sunlight, and the blowing of winds. It is clear that such earth, which has not been underfoot and possesses these qualities, is not only not unhygienic to use but, as we explained in volume three under verse 43 of Surah An-Nisa, according to the testimony of scientists, it has significant germ-killing effects!

The Philosophy of Wudu and Tayammum

The philosophy of "Tayammum" was discussed sufficiently in volume three. As for the philosophy of "Wudu," there is no doubt that Wudu has two clear benefits: a hygienic benefit and a moral/spiritual benefit. From a hygienic perspective, washing the face and hands five times—or at least three times—a day has a significant effect on the cleanliness of the body. Wiping the head and the back of the feet, the condition of which is water reaching the hair or skin, causes us to keep these parts clean as well. As we will mention in the philosophy of Ghusl, the contact of water with the skin has a specific effect on the balance of the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems.

From a moral and spiritual perspective, because it is performed with the intention of seeking nearness to God (qurbat) and for His sake, it has an educational effect. Especially since its metaphorical meaning is "I walk in the path of Your obedience from head to toe," it reinforces this moral and spiritual philosophy.

In a narration from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read:

"Wudu was commanded and worship begins with it so that the servant may be pure when standing before the Compeller (al-Jabbar), during his intimate conversation with Him, obedient to Him in what He commanded, clean from impurities and filth, in addition to what it contains of removing lethargy, dispelling drowsiness, and purifying the heart for standing before the Compeller." [^1]

From the explanations we will give regarding the philosophy of Ghusl, the philosophy of Wudu will also become clearer.

The Philosophy of Ghusl

Some ask: Why does Islam command washing the entire body upon becoming "junub" when only a specific organ becomes soiled? Is there a difference between urinating and the discharge of semen such that in one, only the local area must be washed, and in the other, the whole body?

This question has a brief answer and a detailed answer:

The brief answer is that the discharge of semen from a human is not a localized action (like urination and other wastes), because its effect appears throughout the entire body. All the cells of the body fall into a specific state of weakness following its discharge, and this itself is a sign of its impact on all parts of the body. To explain:

According to scientific research, there are two chains of autonomic nerves in the human body that control all bodily activities: the "sympathetic nerves" and the "parasympathetic nerves." These two sets of nerves are spread throughout the human body and around all internal and external systems and organs. The duty of the sympathetic nerves is to "accelerate" and activate the various systems of the body, while the duty of the parasympathetic nerves is to "decelerate" their activity. In fact, one plays the role of the "accelerator" of a car and the other the role of the "brake." From the balance of the activity of these two sets of autonomic nerves, the body's systems work in a balanced manner.

Sometimes processes occur in the body that upset this balance, including the phenomenon of "orgasm" (the peak of sexual pleasure), which usually occurs concurrently with the discharge of semen.

At this time, the parasympathetic nervous system (the braking nerves) takes precedence over the sympathetic nerves (the stimulating nerves), and the balance is upset in a negative way.

It has also been proven that among the things that can stimulate the sympathetic nerves and restore the lost balance is the contact of water with the body. Since the effect of "orgasm" is tangibly seen across all organs of the body and the balance of these two sets of nerves is upset throughout the body, it is commanded that after sexual intercourse or the discharge of semen, the entire body be washed with water. In the light of its life-giving effect, complete balance is restored between these two sets of nerves throughout the entire body. [^1]

Of course, the benefit of Ghusl is not limited to this; rather, performing Ghusl is also a form of worship and adoration, the moral effects of which cannot be denied. For this reason, if the body is washed without the intention (niyyah) of seeking nearness to God and obeying His command, the Ghusl is not valid. In reality, during the discharge of semen or sexual intercourse, both the soul and the body are affected: the soul is drawn toward material desires, and the body toward lethargy and stagnation. Ghusl of Janabah, which is both a washing of the body and—because it is performed with the intention of nearness—a washing of the soul, has a dual effect simultaneously on the body and spirit. it drives the soul toward God and spirituality, and the body toward purity, vitality, and activity.

Beyond all this, the obligation of Ghusl of Janabah is an Islamic requirement to keep the body clean and observe hygiene throughout life. Many people neglect their own cleanliness, but this Islamic ruling forces them to wash themselves at various intervals and keep the body pure. This matter is not exclusive to people of past ages; in our own time, there are many who, for various reasons, neglect the cleanliness and hygiene of the body. (Of course, this ruling exists as a general and universal law, even including someone who has just washed their body).

The combination of the three aforementioned aspects clarifies why one must perform Ghusl and wash the entire body upon the discharge of semen (whether asleep or awake) and also after sexual intercourse (even if semen is not discharged).

At the end of the verse, to clarify that there was no harshness intended in the previous commands, but rather they were all legislated for significant benefits, He says: "Allah does not intend to make difficulty for you, but He intends to purify you and complete His favor upon you that you may be grateful."

(مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ حَرَجٍ وَلَكِنْ يُرِيدُ لِيُطَهِّرَكُمْ وَلِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ).

In fact, the above sentences once again emphasize the reality that all divine commands and Islamic programs are established for the sake of the people and to protect their interests, and no other goal was involved. Allah wants to provide both spiritual and physical purity for the people through these commands.

Additionally, it should be noted that the phrase "مَا يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ لِيَجْعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ حَرَجٍ" (Allah does not intend to place any hardship upon you), although mentioned following the rulings related to Ghusl, Wudu, and Tayammum, states a general law: that divine rulings are never in the form of an exhausting or unbearable task. Therefore, if we observe that certain obligations take on a burdensome form for some individuals and become unbearable, that ruling—based on this very verse—is exempted and dropped for them. For example, if fasting becomes burdensome for individuals such as the elderly or the weak and their like, fasting is not obligatory for them based on this verse.

Of course, it should not be forgotten that some commands are inherently difficult, and those difficulties must be endured for the sake of the important interests involved, such as the ruling of Jihad against the enemies of truth.

This general law in Islamic jurisprudence, under the title of the rule of "La Haraj" (No Hardship), is an essential principle cited by jurists in various chapters, and they have derived many rulings from it.

[^1]: And the fact that we see in a report narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام that he said: "Janabah comes out from the entire body, and therefore it is obligatory to purify the entire body" (Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 1, p. 466) seems to refer to this very subject.

Ayah

وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَمِيثَـٰقَهُ ٱلَّذِى وَاثَقَكُم بِهِۦٓ إِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٧)
Remember Allah’s blessing upon you and His covenant with which He has bound you when you said, ‘We hear and obey.’ And be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah knows best what is in the breasts. (7)

Commentary

Divine Covenants

In connection with the discussion in the previous verse regarding a portion of Islamic rulings and the completion of Divine favors, this verse once again draws the attention of Muslims to the importance of the endless blessings of Allah—the most significant of which are the blessings of faith, Islam, and guidance—stating:

"Remember the favor of Allah upon you."

(وَ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ)

Although "favor" (نعمت) is singular here, it carries a generic sense, and the genus is used here in a universal meaning; thus, it encompasses all favors.

Of course, there is also the possibility in the verse that it refers specifically to the blessing of Islam, which was briefly mentioned in the preceding verse where it says: "and that He may complete His favor upon you" (وَ لِيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكُمْ).

And what favor could be greater than that, under the shadow of Islam, all kinds of bounties, honors, and opportunities were bestowed upon the Muslims? A people who were previously completely scattered, ignorant, astray, bloodthirsty, corrupt, and corrupters became an organized, united, and knowledgeable community with abundant material and spiritual resources.

Then, reminding them of the covenant they made with God, it says: "And do not forget the covenant which He firmly bound you with, when you said: 'We heard and we obeyed.'"

(وَ مِيثاقَهُ الَّذِي واثَقَكُمْ بِهِ إِذْ قُلْتُمْ سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا)

Regarding which covenant is intended here, two possibilities exist: First, the covenant that Muslims made at the beginning of Islam at "Hudaybiyyah," or "the Farewell Pilgrimage," or "Aqaba," or the implicit covenant all Muslims make with Allah upon the mere acceptance of Islam. Second, the covenant that every person has made with their Creator by the dictate of nature (fitrah) and creation, which is sometimes referred to as the "World of Alast" or "Alam al-Dharr."

To explain: At the time of human creation, Allah endowed man with remarkable talents and placed countless bounties at his disposal, including the capacity to study the secrets of creation and recognize the Lord through them, as well as the intellect, intelligence, and perception by which he may recognize His prophets and implement their instructions. By granting these talents, Allah has "practically" taken a covenant from them not to let these talents go to waste and to utilize them in the correct path. Human beings, in turn, through the "language of their state and potential," cried out: "We heard and we obeyed" (سَمِعْنا وَ أَطَعْنا).

This covenant is the most extensive, firmest, and most universal covenant that Allah has taken from His servants, and it is the same one that Ali عليه السلام referred to in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha, saying:

"ليستادوهم ميثاق فطرته"

"The Prophets were sent to call the people to fulfill the covenant of their nature (fitrah)."

Naturally, this broad covenant encompasses all religious matters as well. [^1]

[^1]: Further explanation regarding this subject and why it is called "Alam al-Dharr" will come, God willing, in the commentary of Surah Al-A'raf, verse 172.

There is no obstacle to the verse referring to all ontological (takwini) and legislative (tashri'i) covenants (covenants Allah took by the dictate of nature or those the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took from Muslims at various stages). From here, it becomes clear that the narration stating that the "covenant" refers to the pledge the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله took during the Farewell Pilgrimage regarding the Wilayah (guardianship) of Ali عليه السلام [^1] is consistent with what we mentioned above. For we have repeatedly stated that the interpretations appearing under verses in such cases refer to one of the clearest instances (masadiq), not in the sense of exclusivity.

Additionally, it should be noted that "Mithaq" (covenant) originally comes from the root "wathaqah" or "wuthuq," meaning to bind or fasten something firmly with a rope or the like. Later, it was applied to any action that brings peace of mind. Since a pledge or covenant is like a knot tied between two people or two groups and provides mental tranquility, it is called "Mithaq."

At the end of the verse, to emphasize this point, it says: "And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts."

(وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ)

The expression "bi-dhat al-sudur" (بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ), which is a combination of "dhat" (meaning the essence or reality) and "sudur" (meaning breasts/chests), indicates that Allah is aware of the most subtle secrets hidden in the depths of the human soul, of which no one but He is aware. As for how emotions, feelings, intentions, and decisions are attributed to the heart and the interior of the chest, we have discussed this in detail on page 54 of the first volume.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 454.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ كُونُوا۟ قَوَّٰمِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَدَآءَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۖ وَلَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَـَٔانُ قَوْمٍ عَلَىٰٓ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا۟ ۚ ٱعْدِلُوا۟ هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٨)
O you who have faith! Be maintainers, as witnesses for the sake of Allah, of justice, and ill feeling for a people should never lead you to be unfair. Be fair; that is nearer to Godwariness, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do. (8)
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۙ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (٩)
Allah has promised those who have faith and do righteous deeds forgiveness and a great reward. (9)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (١٠)
As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of hell. (10)

Commentary

An Urgent Call to Justice

This verse calls for the establishment of justice, and a similar verse with slight differences appeared in Surah An-Nisa, verse 135. First, addressing the believers, it says:

"O you who have believed, be persistently standing firm for Allah, witnesses in justice."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا كُونُوا قَوَّامِينَ لِلَّهِ شُهَدَاءَ بِالْقِسْطِ).

Then, it points to one of the factors of deviation from justice, warning Muslims as follows: "Ethnic hatreds, enmities, and personal score-settling must not prevent the execution of justice or lead to the violation of others' rights, for justice is higher than all of these."

(وَ لَا يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ شَنَآنُ قَوْمٍ عَلَىٰ أَلَّا تَعْدِلُوا).

Once again, due to the importance of the subject, it emphasizes the issue of justice, stating:

"Be just; that is nearer to righteousness."

(اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ).

And since justice is the most important pillar of piety and righteousness, it adds for the third time as an emphasis: "And fear Allah; indeed, Allah is Acquainted with what you do."

(وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ).

The differences between this verse and the one in Surah An-Nisa are from several aspects:

First, in the verse of An-Nisa, the call is to stand firm for justice and to bear witness for Allah; however, here the call is to stand firm for Allah and to bear witness to truth and justice. Perhaps this difference is because in the verse of An-Nisa, the goal was for testimonies to be for Allah rather than for relatives and kin, but here, since the discussion involves enemies, the expression used is witnessing to justice and equity—meaning not to oppression and tyranny.

Second, in Surah An-Nisa, one factor of deviation from justice was mentioned, and here another factor is pointed out. There, it was groundless excessive love, and here, it is groundless excessive hatred. Yet both are encompassed within the subject of following whims and desires, which appeared in Surah An-Nisa with the phrase: “So follow not [personal] inclination, lest you not be just.” In fact, following whims is a broader source of oppression and tyranny, because sometimes oppression occurs for the sake of self-indulgence and protecting personal interests, not because of love or hatred for others. Therefore, the real root of deviation from justice is following whims, as stated in the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام:

“As for following whims, it bars one from the truth.” [^1]

Justice: A Vital Pillar of Islam

Few issues in Islam carry as much importance as justice, for the concept of "Justice" ('Adl), like the concept of "Monotheism" (Tawhid), has spread its roots throughout all the principles and branches of Islam. That is to say, just as no intellectual, practical, individual, social, moral, or legal issue is separate from the reality of Monotheism and Oneness, we shall likewise find none of them devoid of the spirit of "Justice."

Therefore, it is not surprising that "Justice" is recognized as one of the principles of the school of thought (Usul al-Madhhab) and one of the intellectual foundations of Muslims. Although the justice that is part of the principles of the school is one of the attributes of God and is included within the knowledge of God—which is the first of the principles of religion—distinguishing it as a separate principle is very meaningful. For this reason, in the social discussions of Islam, no principle has been emphasized as much as justice.

Observing the following hadiths as examples is sufficient to understand the importance of this subject:

  1. The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله says: “Beware of oppression, for oppression with Allah is the darknesses of the Day of Resurrection.” [^1] We know that every good and blessing lies in light, and darkness is the source of every kind of non-existence and loss.

  2. “By justice, the heavens and the earth were established.” [^2] This expression is the most eloquent description possible regarding justice. It means that not only is the limited life of man on this earthly sphere impossible without justice, but the entire universe, the heavens, and the earth are all maintained in the light of justice, the balance of forces, and the placement of everything in its appropriate position. If they were to deviate from these principles for a single moment, even by the width of a needle's tip, they would head toward annihilation.

We read a similar theme in another famous hadith which states: “A kingdom may endure with disbelief, but it will not endure with oppression.” This is because tyranny is something whose effect is swift and immediate in this very life. Observing the wars, anxieties, distresses, political, social, and moral chaos, and economic crises in today's world also proves this reality well.

However, what must be fully noted is that Islam does not merely "recommend justice"; rather, more importantly, it demands the "execution of justice." Reciting these verses and narrations only from pulpits, writing them in books, or mentioning them within speeches alone will not cure the pain of injustice, discrimination, and social corruption in Islamic society. Rather, the greatness of these commands will become apparent on the day they are implemented in the very fabric of Muslim life.

Then, in the following verse—according to the Quranic tradition of mentioning general principles and laws after specific rulings for emphasis and completion—it states to emphasize the issue of executing justice and witnessing to the truth: "Allah has promised those who believe and do righteous deeds [that] for them there is forgiveness and great reward."

(وَعَدَ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ).

And in contrast: "But those who disbelieve and deny Our signs—those are the companions of Hellfire."

(وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ).

It is noteworthy that forgiveness and a great reward are mentioned as a divine promise in the verse, saying: “Allah has promised...” but the punishment of Hell is stated as the result of the action, saying: “Those who possess such deeds shall have such a fate.” This is, in fact, an allusion to the issue of Allah's grace and mercy regarding the rewards of the next world, which in no way equal the insignificant deeds of man, just as the punishments of that world are not for revenge but are the result of man's own actions.

Furthermore, the expression "Companions of the Hellfire" (Ashab al-Jahim), considering that Ashab means companions and constant associates, is evidence that they will be constant associates of Hell. However, this verse alone cannot be proof for the issue of "eternity" (khulud) as mentioned in Tafsir al-Tibyan, Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir Fakhr Razi, because companionship may be permanent or it may last for a duration and then cease. For instance, the expression "Companions of the Ship" (Ashab al-Safinah) mentioned in the Quran regarding the passengers of Noah's ark was not permanent. Of course, there is no doubt that disbelievers remain eternally in Hell, but that is not discussed in the above verse; rather, it is derived from other verses. [^1]

[^1]: This hadith is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the book Safinat al-Bihar under the entry "Hawa," and from Ali عليه السلام in Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 42. [^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, entry "Zulm." [^2]: This hadith is narrated from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (Tafsir al-Safi, under verse 7 of Surah Ar-Rahman).

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَن يَبْسُطُوٓا۟ إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنكُمْ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١١)
O you who have faith! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you when a people set out to extend their hands against you, but He withheld their hands from you, and be wary of Allah, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (11)

Commentary

Following the reminder of divine blessings in the preceding verses, this verse once again addresses the Muslims and reminds them of another part of His blessings, so that in gratitude for them, they may strive in obeying Allah’s command and implementing the principles of justice. It says: "O you who have believed, remember the favor of Allah upon you when a people determined to extend their hands against you [to harm you], but He withheld their hands from you."

(يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ هَمَّ قَوْمٌ أَنْ يَبْسُطُوا إِلَيْكُمْ أَيْدِيَهُمْ فَكَفَّ أَيْدِيَهُمْ عَنْكُمْ‌).

Allah repeatedly reminds the Muslims in the verses of the Quran of various blessings and His favors upon them, in order to strengthen the spirit of faith within them and to arouse a sense of gratitude and steadfastness in the face of difficulties; the above verse is one such instance.

However, regarding which specific event this verse refers to, there is much discussion among the commentators. Some consider it an allusion to the thwarting of the danger posed by the Jews of "Bani al-Nadir," who had plotted to destroy the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims in Medina. Others consider it a reference to the story of "Batn Nakhl" which occurred during the events of "Hudaybiyyah" in the sixth year of the Hijra, where a group of Meccan polytheists led by "Khalid bin Walid" decided to attack the Muslims during the Asr prayer, but the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became aware of this conspiracy and their plan was foiled by performing the shortened "Prayer of Fear" (Salat al-Khawf). Others still consider it a reference to other incidents from the eventful life of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Muslims.

Some commentators also believe that it refers to all the incidents that occurred throughout the history of Islam. This interpretation—if we set aside the word "people" (قوم), which is indefinite and suggests a specific group—is the best of all interpretations. In any case, this verse draws the attention of the Muslims to the dangers that could have potentially erased their name from the pages of history forever, and warns them that in appreciation of these blessings, they should adopt Taqwa (God-wariness) and rely upon Allah. They should know that if they are pious, they will not be left alone in life, and that unseen hand which has always been their protector will continue to support them.

(وَ اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ‌).

It is clear that the meaning of Tawakkul (reliance on Allah) is not that a person should shirk their responsibilities or surrender to events under the pretext of leaving their affairs to Allah. Rather, it means that while employing all one's power and strength, one should, firstly, be mindful that whatever they possess is not from themselves but from another source, thereby killing the spirit of pride and vanity within. Secondly, one should never fear the magnitude of events and difficulties nor become despondent, knowing they have a support whose power is the highest of all powers.

Furthermore, considering that in this verse the command for Taqwa is given first, followed by the mention of the issue of Tawakkul, it is understood that Allah’s support encompasses those who are pious.

It should be noted that "Taqwa" is derived from the root "wiqayah," meaning self-restraint and preventing oneself from factors of evil and corruption.

Ayah

۞ وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمُ ٱثْنَىْ عَشَرَ نَقِيبًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱللَّهُ إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ ۖ لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتَيْتُمُ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَءَامَنتُم بِرُسُلِى وَعَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ وَأَقْرَضْتُمُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًا لَّأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنكُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِكُمْ وَلَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۚ فَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَوَآءَ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (١٢)
Certainly Allah took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs. And Allah said, ‘I am with you! Surely, if you maintain the prayer and give the zakat and have faith in My apostles and support them and lend Allah a good loan, I will surely absolve you of your misdeeds, and I will surely admit you into gardens with streams running in them. But whoever of you disbelieves after that has certainly strayed from the right way.’ (12)

Commentary

From its beginning, this Surah has referred to the issue of fulfilling covenants, and this theme has been repeated on various occasions, including in the verse above. Perhaps one philosophy behind this continuous and repeated emphasis on the issue of fulfilling covenants and the condemnation of breaking them is to highlight the importance of the Covenant of Ghadir, which will be mentioned in verse 67.

At the beginning of the verse under discussion, it states: "We took a covenant from the Children of Israel that they would act according to Our commands, and following this covenant, We chose twelve leaders and supervisors for them, so that each would take charge of one of the twelve tribes of the Children of Israel."

(وَ لَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثاقَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَ بَعَثْنا مِنْهُمُ اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ نَقِيباً)

The word "Naqib" (نقيب) is originally derived from the root "Naqb" (نقب), which means wide openings, especially underground passages. A chief or leader of a group is called a Naqib because he is aware of the group's secrets, as if he has created a passage (naqb) among them and become informed of their condition. Sometimes, Naqib refers to someone who is not the head of the group but merely their representative and a means of identifying them. If the virtues of individuals are called "Manaqib" (مناقب), it is because they must be discovered through investigation and curiosity.

Some commentators have interpreted Naqib in the above verse solely as one who is aware and informed of secrets, but this meaning seems very unlikely. This is because what is gathered from history and Hadith is that each of the Nuqaba (plural of Naqib) of the Children of Israel was the supervisor of his own tribe. In the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that:

“The Nuqaba of the Children of Israel were the ministers of Moses and became prophets after that.” [^1]

In the accounts of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that on the Night of Aqaba, when the people of Medina came to the place of Aqaba to invite him, he commanded them to choose twelve "Nuqaba" from among themselves, equal to the number of the Nuqaba of the Children of Israel. Their duty was certainly the leadership of the group, not merely reporting information. [^2] It is noteworthy that in numerous narrations transmitted through Sunni sources, reference is made to the twelve caliphs and successors of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and their number is introduced as being equal to the number of the Nuqaba of the Children of Israel. We mention some of them here:

  1. The famous Imam of the Sunnis, Ahmad ibn Hanbal, narrates in his Musnad from "Masruq," who says: I asked Abdullah ibn Mas'ud how many people would rule over this Ummah. Ibn Mas'ud replied:

“We asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله the same question, and he replied: Twelve, like the number of the Nuqaba of the Children of Israel.” [^1]

  1. In the history of "Ibn Asakir," it is narrated from Ibn Mas'ud that he said: We asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله how many caliphs would rule over this Ummah. He said:

“The number of caliphs after me will be the number of the Nuqaba of Moses.” [^2]

  1. In Muntakhab Kanz al-Ummal, it is narrated from Jabir ibn Samurah that: "Twelve caliphs will rule over this Ummah, according to the number of the Nuqaba of the Children of Israel." [^3] A similar Hadith is also narrated in Yanabi' al-Mawaddah, page 445, and in the book al-Bidayah wa al-Nihayah, volume 6, page 247.

Then, the verse describes God's promise to the Children of Israel, stating that Allah said to them:

"I will be with you and I will support you" (وَ قالَ اللَّهُ إِنِّي مَعَكُمْ).

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 6, p. 78. [^2]: Safinat al-Bihar (N-Q-B). [^1]: Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 1, p. 398, Egypt edition, 1313 AH. [^2]: Fayd al-Qadir Sharh al-Jami' al-Saghir, Vol. 2, p. 459. [^3]: Muntakhab Kanz al-Ummal in the margins of Musnad Ahmad, Vol. 5, p. 312.

However, this is subject to several conditions:

  1. On the condition that you establish prayer (لَئِنْ أَقَمْتُمُ الصَّلاةَ).
  2. And pay your Zakat (وَ آتَيْتُمُ الزَّكاةَ).
  3. Believe in My messengers and assist them (وَ آمَنْتُمْ بِرُسُلِي وَ عَزَّرْتُمُوهُمْ). [^1]
  4. Furthermore, do not withhold from recommended spending, which is a kind of "goodly loan" to Allah (وَ أَقْرَضْتُمُ اللَّهَ قَرْضاً حَسَناً).

If you fulfill this covenant, I will remit your past sins and evil deeds (لَأُكَفِّرَنَّ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ).

And I will admit you into gardens of Paradise beneath whose trees rivers flow.

(وَ لَأُدْخِلَنَّكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ)

But those who choose the path of disbelief, denial, and disobedience will certainly have strayed from the straight path.

(فَمَنْ كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ مِنْكُمْ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ سَواءَ السَّبِيلِ)

Regarding why spending is called "giving a loan to Allah" in the Holy Quran, we have provided the necessary explanation in Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 2, page 160.

Here, a question remains: why were "prayer and Zakat" mentioned before belief in Moses عليه السلام, even though belief in him precedes action?

[^1]: Azzartumuhum (عزرتموهم) is from the root Ta'zir, meaning to prevent and to assist. If some Islamic punishments are called Ta'zir, it is because they are actually a form of help to the sinners, preventing them from further sin. This shows that Islamic punishments are not for revenge but are educational in nature, hence the name Ta'zir.

A group of commentators have answered this question by stating that "Messengers" (رسل) in the above verse refers to the prophets who came after Moses, not Moses himself. Therefore, it is a command regarding the future, which can follow prayer and Zakat. It is also possible that "Messengers" refers to those very Nuqaba of the Children of Israel, for whom a covenant of loyalty was taken from the Children of Israel (in Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, we read that some earlier commentators suggested the Nuqaba of the Children of Israel were messengers of Allah, and this possibility supports what we mentioned above).

Ayah

فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَـٰقَهُمْ لَعَنَّـٰهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا قُلُوبَهُمْ قَـٰسِيَةً ۖ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ عَن مَّوَاضِعِهِۦ ۙ وَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَلَا تَزَالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلَىٰ خَآئِنَةٍ مِّنْهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّنْهُمْ ۖ فَٱعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَٱصْفَحْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (١٣)
Then, because of their breaking their covenant We cursed them and made their hearts hard: they pervert words from their meanings, and have forgotten a part of what they were reminded. You will not cease to learn of some of their treachery, excepting a few of them. Yet excuse them and forbear. Indeed Allah loves the virtuous. (13)

Commentary

Following the discussion regarding God's covenant with the Children of Israel in the previous verse, this verse points to their breach of covenant and the consequences of this betrayal, stating: "Because they broke their covenant, We cursed them and removed them from Our mercy, and We made their hearts hard and heavy."

(فَبِما نَقْضِهِمْ مِيثاقَهُمْ لَعَنَّاهُمْ وَ جَعَلْنا قُلُوبَهُمْ قاسِيَةً) [^1] In reality, they were punished with these two penalties for the crime of breaking the covenant: they were distanced from Allah's mercy, and their thoughts and hearts became petrified and inflexible.

Then, it describes the effects of this hard-heartedness as follows: "They distort words and take them out of their proper context and path."

(يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ عَنْ مَواضِعِهِ‌).

And also: "They have forgotten a significant portion of what they were reminded of."

(وَ نَسُوا حَظًّا مِمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ‌).

It is not unlikely that the portion they forgot refers to the signs and characteristics of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله which have been mentioned in other verses of the Quran. It is also possible that this sentence refers to the fact that we know the Torah was lost throughout history; subsequently, a group of Jewish scholars undertook to write it, and naturally, many parts were lost, and some parts were distorted or forgotten. What reached them was only a part of the actual book of Moses عليه السلام, which was mixed with many superstitions, and they sometimes forgot even this remaining portion.

[^1]: L'an (لعن) linguistically means "to expel and distance," and when attributed to Allah, it means depriving someone of His mercy. Qasiyah (قاسية) is derived from qasawah (قساوت), which originally refers to hard stones; by extension, it is applied to those who show no flexibility toward truths.

Then He adds: "Every day you discover a new betrayal from them, except for a group of them who are free from these crimes and are in the minority."

(وَ لا تَزالُ تَطَّلِعُ عَلى‌ خائِنَةٍ [^1] مِنْهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِنْهُمْ‌).

And at the end, He commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to "pardon them and overlook [their misdeeds], for Allah loves the doers of good."

(فَاعْفُ عَنْهُمْ وَ اصْفَحْ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

Does this mean he should overlook the past sins of this righteous minority? Or the unrighteous majority? The apparent meaning of the verse strengthens the second possibility, because the righteous minority committed no betrayal to require pardon. However, certainly, this forgiveness and pardon pertain to the harms they inflicted upon the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, not regarding the objective and fundamental issues of Islam, where forgiveness has no place.

The Distortions of the Jews

From the collection of verses in the Holy Quran regarding the distortions (tahrif) by the Jews, it is understood that they engaged in various types of distortion of their Heavenly Book. Sometimes their distortion was semantic (ma'nawi); that is, they interpreted the phrases revealed in their Heavenly Book contrary to their true meaning. They preserved the original words but altered their meanings. Sometimes they engaged in verbal distortion (lafzi); out of mockery, instead of saying sami'na wa ata'na (we heard and we obeyed), they would say: sami'na wa 'asayna! (we heard and we disobeyed!). And sometimes they resorted to concealing a portion of the divine verses; they would reveal what agreed with their desires and hide what was contrary to their desires. Sometimes, even in the presence of the Heavenly Book, to deceive people, they would place their hand over a portion of it so that the other party could not read it, as will be mentioned under verse 41 of Surah Al-Ma'idah in the story of "Ibn Suriya."

Does Allah Make Anyone Hard-hearted?

In the verse under discussion, we read that Allah attributes the hard-heartedness of a group of Jews to Himself, and we know that this hard-heartedness and lack of flexibility toward the truth becomes the source of deviations and sins. Here, the question arises: since Allah is the agent of this act, how are these individuals responsible for their actions, and is this not a form of predestination (jabr)?

By carefully examining various verses of the Quran and even the verse under discussion, it becomes clear that in many cases, individuals are deprived of Allah's grace and guidance due to their own wrongful acts. In reality, their actions become the source of a series of intellectual and moral deviations, from whose consequences they sometimes cannot distance themselves at all. However, since the effect of every cause is by Allah's command, such effects are attributed to Allah in the Quran. For example, in the verse under discussion, He says: because they broke the covenant, We made their hearts hard and inflexible. And in verse 27 of Surah Ibrahim, He says: وَ يُضِلُّ اللَّهُ الظَّالِمِينَ‌ (Allah leaves the wrongdoers to stray). And in verse 77 of Surah At-Tawbah, regarding some of the covenant-breakers, He says:

فَأَعْقَبَهُمْ نِفاقاً فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ يَلْقَوْنَهُ بِما أَخْلَفُوا اللَّهَ ما وَعَدُوهُ‌ وَ بِما كانُوا يَكْذِبُونَ‌:

"So He caused hypocrisy to follow in their hearts until the Day they shall meet Him, because they broke their promise to Allah and because they used to lie." Similar expressions are abundant in the Quran.

It is clear that these evil effects, which originate from the person's own actions, are in no way inconsistent with the spirit of choice and free will, because the prerequisites were provided by the individuals themselves, and they stepped into this valley consciously. These are the mandatory products of their own deeds. This is exactly like someone who intentionally consumes alcoholic beverages and commits crimes while intoxicated. It is true that in the state of intoxication he has no self-control, but because he consciously provided the prerequisites and knew that such acts might occur during intoxication, he will be responsible for his actions. If it were said here: "Because they drank wine, We took away their intellect and threw them into crimes due to their actions," would there be any problem with this expression, or would its meaning be predestination?

In summary: all guidance and misguidance and the like attributed to Allah in the Quran are certainly due to the prerequisites and actions previously performed by the servants, following which they became deserving of guidance or misguidance. Otherwise, Allah's justice and wisdom would never allow Him to guide one to the right path and leave another wandering in misguidance without cause. [^1]

[^1]: Khainah (خائِنَة), although an active participle, here carries an infinitive meaning and is equivalent to khiyanat (betrayal). This has precedents in Arabic literature where an active participle takes an infinitive meaning, such as afiyah and khatiyah. There is also the possibility that khainah is an adjective for a group (ta'ifah) which is implied.

[^1]: In the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 51, we have provided further explanations on this matter.

Ayah

وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا۟ حَظًّا مِّمَّا ذُكِّرُوا۟ بِهِۦ فَأَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَاوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَسَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (١٤)
Also from those who say, ‘We are Christians,’ We took their pledge; but they forgot a part of what they were reminded. So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection, and soon Allah will inform them concerning what they had been doing. (14)

Commentary

Eternal Enemies

In the previous verse, the discussion concerned the breach of covenant by the Children of Israel, and in this verse, it refers to the breach of covenant by the Christians, stating: "A group of those who claim to be Christians, despite the fact that We had taken a covenant of loyalty from them, resorted to breaking the covenant and consigned to oblivion a portion of the instructions that had been given to them."

(وَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّا نَصارى‌ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَهُمْ فَنَسُوا حَظًّا مِمَّا ذُكِّرُوا بِهِ‌)

Indeed, they too had entered into a covenant with Allah not to deviate from the truth of Monotheism (Tawhid), not to consign Divine instructions to oblivion, and not to conceal the signs of the final Prophet. However, they also met the same fate as the Jews, with the difference that:

Regarding the Jews, the Quran says that only a few of them were pure and appreciative of the truth, but regarding the Christians, it says a group of them deviated. From this expression, it becomes clear that the deviants among the Jews were more numerous than the deviants among the Christians.

The history of the current Gospels states that all of them were written many years after Christ (peace be upon him) and by the hands of certain Christians; for this reason, they contain manifest contradictions. This indicates that they had completely forgotten significant portions of the verses of the Gospel (Injil). The existence of obvious superstitions in the current Gospels—such as the story of Christ making wine [^1], which contradicts the judgment of reason and even some verses of the Torah and the current Gospels, and the story of Mary Magdalene [^2] and the like—also clarifies this reality.

Furthermore, it should be noted that "Nasara" (نصارى) is the plural of "Nasrani" (نصرانى). Regarding the reason for naming Christians by this name, various possibilities have been suggested. First, it is because Jesus (peace be upon him) was raised in the city of "Nazareth" during his childhood. It has also been suggested that it is derived from "Nasran," which is the name of a village for which the "Nasara" had a special affection. It is also possible that this name was chosen because when Christ (peace be upon him) sought helpers and companions from the people, they responded to his call, as the Quran says:

كَما قالَ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ لِلْحَوارِيِّينَ مَنْ أَنْصارِي إِلَى اللَّهِ قالَ الْحَوارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنْصارُ اللَّهِ‌ [^3]

And since a group of them did not act upon their word and were merely claimants of helping Christ (peace be upon him), the Quran says in the verse under discussion: وَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّا نَصارى‌:

"From those who claimed, 'We are the helpers of Jesus (peace be upon him),'" but they were not truthful in this claim.

Then, the Quran describes the consequence of the Christians' actions such that, as a penalty for their deeds, We cast enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection.

(فَأَغْرَيْنا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَ الْبَغْضاءَ إِلى‌ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ)

And their other punishment, which is mentioned in the final sentence of the verse, is that "In the future, Allah will inform them of the results of their deeds, and they will practically see it with their own eyes."

(وَ سَوْفَ يُنَبِّئُهُمُ اللَّهُ بِما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ‌)

Here, several points should be noted:

1- The word "Aghrayna" (اغرينا) is originally from the root "Ighra" (اغراء), meaning to glue or attach something, and was subsequently used to mean encouraging and compelling someone toward a task, because it causes individuals to be linked to specific goals. Therefore, the concept of the above verse is that the breach of covenant by the Christians and their wrongdoings caused the factors of enmity and hatred and the seeds of hypocrisy and discord to be sown among them (for we know that the effects of existential and natural causes are attributed to Allah). Even now, the numerous conflicts that exist between Christian nations, which have so far been the source of two World Wars, and the groupings accompanied by enmity and hatred among them continue. Furthermore, religious differences and enmities among the various sects of Christianity are so great that they continue to kill one another even now.

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the meaning is the continuation of enmity and hatred between the Jews and Christians until the end of the world, but the apparent meaning of the verse is the emergence of enmity among the Christians themselves [^1]. Perhaps it does not need mentioning that this painful end is not exclusive to Christians; if Muslims also follow their path, they will meet the same fate.

2- "Adawah" (عداوت - enmity) originally comes from the root "Adw" (عدو), meaning transgression, and "Baghdha" (بغضاء - hatred) is from the root "Bughdh" (بغض), meaning detestation of something. The difference between these two words might be that "Bughdh" is mostly "internal/heart-based," while "Adawah" is "practical/action-based," or at least encompasses both action and heart.

3- From the above verse, it appears that the Christian group as followers of a religion (or both Jews and Christians) will exist in the world until the end of time. Here, a question arises: Islamic reports suggest that after the appearance of the Mahdi (peace be upon him), there will be no more than one religion throughout the world, and that is the religion of Islam. How can these two be reconciled?

However, it is possible that Christianity (or both Christianity and Judaism) will remain in the world as a very weak minority even in the era of the Mahdi, for we know that even in that era, the free will of humans is not abolished and religion does not take on a compulsory aspect, even though the overwhelming majority of the people of the world will find the path of truth and embrace it, and most importantly, the government on earth will be an Islamic government.

[^1]: Gospel of John - Chapter 2, verses 2 to 12.

[^2]: Gospel of Luke - Chapter 7, verses 36 to 47.

[^3]: Surah As-Saff - Verse 14.

[^1]: Therefore, the pronoun "among them" (بينهم) refers back to the Christians mentioned at the beginning of the verse.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيرًا مِّمَّا كُنتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ ۚ قَدْ جَآءَكُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَـٰبٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٥)
O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying for you much of what you used to hide of the Book, and excusing many [an offense of yours]. Certainly, there has come to you a light from Allah, and a manifest Book. (15)
يَهْدِى بِهِ ٱللَّهُ مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ رِضْوَٰنَهُۥ سُبُلَ ٱلسَّلَـٰمِ وَيُخْرِجُهُم مِّنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِۦ وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (١٦)
With it Allah guides those who follow [the course of] His pleasure to the ways of peace, and brings them out from darkness into light by His will, and guides them to a straight path. (16)

Commentary

Following the verses that discussed the Jews and Christians and their breaches of covenant, this verse addresses the People of the Book in general and invites them toward Islam—an Islam that purifies their divine religion from superstitions and guides them toward the Straight Path, a path far removed from any deviation or crookedness.

First, it says: "O People of the Book, Our Messenger has come to you, to make clear to you much of the truths of the Divine Books that you used to conceal, while at the same time, he overlooks many of them that do not need to be mentioned and pertain to past eras."

(يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ قَدْ جَاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ كَثِيراً مِمَّا كُنْتُمْ تُخْفُونَ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ وَيَعْفُو عَنْ كَثِيرٍ)

From this phrase, it is understood that the People of the Book concealed many truths, but the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله has explained what is currently needed by the people of the world (such as clarifying the truth of Monotheism, the purity of the Prophets from the unworthy attributions given to them in the Old and New Testaments, and the rulings on the prohibition of usury, wine, and the like). However, he has refrained from mentioning some truths that pertained to previous nations and past times, the mention of which would have no significant effect on the upbringing of current peoples.

Then, referring to the importance and greatness of the Majestic Quran and its profound effects on the guidance and education of humanity, it says: "From Allah, a Light and a Clear Book have come to you."

(قَدْ جَاءَكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ نُورٌ وَكِتَابٌ مُبِينٌ)

"The very Light by which Allah guides those who seek His pleasure to the ways of peace."

(يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلامِ)

And furthermore, "He brings them out of various darknesses (the darkness of polytheism, the darkness of ignorance, the darkness of division and hypocrisy...) toward the light of Monotheism, knowledge, and unity."

(وَيُخْرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُمَاتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ)

And above all, "He guides them to a Straight Path in which there is no crookedness in terms of belief or practical programs."

(وَيَهْدِيهِمْ إِلَى صِرَاطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ)

Regarding whether the "Light" in the first verse refers to the person of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله or the Majestic Quran, two opinions are found among commentators. However, observing various verses in the Majestic Quran that liken the "Quran" to "Light" shows that "Light" in the above verse means the Quran. Therefore, the conjunction of "Clear Book" to it is an explanatory conjunction. In Surah Al-A'raf, verse 157, we read:

فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ: "So those who believe in him, honor him, assist him, and follow the Light which has been sent down with him—it is they who will be successful."

And in Surah At-Taghabun, verse 8, we read:

فَآمِنُوا بِاللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَالنُّورِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلْنَا: "So believe in Allah and His Messenger and the Light which We have sent down."

There are also numerous other verses, whereas the application of the word "Light" in the Quran to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is not seen.

Furthermore, the singular form of the pronoun "به" (by it) in the following verse also confirms that "Light and a Clear Book" refer to a single reality.

Of course, in several narrations, we read that "Light" has been interpreted as the person of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, or all the Imams علیهم السلام. However, it is clear that this interpretation is of the nature of mentioning the "inner meanings" (butun) of the verses. As we know, the Quran, in addition to its apparent meaning, has inner meanings referred to as the "depths of the Quran." The reason we say it is clear that these interpretations relate to the inner meanings of the Quran is that at that time, the Imams did not yet exist for the People of the Book to be invited to believe in them.

Another point is that the second verse gives glad tidings to those who step onto the path of seeking Allah's pleasure, that in the light of the Quran, three great blessings will be granted to them: First, guidance to the "ways of peace"—peace of the individual, peace of society, peace of the soul and spirit, peace of the family, and peace of morality (and these all have a practical aspect).

Second, bringing them out of the darknesses of disbelief and irreligion toward the light of faith, which has a doctrinal aspect.

All of these are achieved through the shortest and nearest path, which is the "Straight Path," mentioned in the third phrase.

However, all these blessings are the portion of those who approach with submission and truth-seeking and are instances of "those who follow His pleasure" (مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَهُ). As for the hypocrites, the stubborn, and those who are enemies of the truth, they will derive no benefit, as other verses of the Quran testify.

Moreover, all these effects stem from the definitive Will of Allah, which is indicated by the phrase "by His permission" (بِإِذْنِهِ).

Ayah

لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قُلْ فَمَن يَمْلِكُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا إِنْ أَرَادَ أَن يُهْلِكَ ٱلْمَسِيحَ ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَأُمَّهُۥ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۚ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٧)
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ Say, ‘Who can avail anything against Allah should He wish to destroy the Messiah, son of Mary, and his mother, and everyone upon the earth?’ To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them. He creates whatever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (17)

Commentary

How Can the Messiah Be God?!

To complete the previous discussions, this verse strongly attacks the claim of the divinity of the Messiah عليه السلام, considering it a manifest form of disbelief (kufr), and states: "Certainly they have disbelieved who say: 'Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.'"

(لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ‌).

To clarify the meaning of this sentence, we must understand that Christians hold several baseless claims regarding God. First is the belief in a Trinity, which verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa' refers to and invalidates:

"Do not say, 'Three'; desist—it is better for you. Indeed, Allah is but one God." [^1]

Another is that they consider the God who created the universe to be one of the three gods, calling Him "God the Father." [^2] The Quran also invalidates this belief in verse 73 of Surah Al-Ma'idah: (لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ وَ ما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا إِلهٌ واحِدٌ) "Certainly they have disbelieved who say, 'Allah is the third of three.' And there is no god except one God," the commentary of which will soon follow, Allah willing.

Furthermore, they claim that the three gods, despite being truly distinct, are one—an idea sometimes expressed as "Unity in Trinity." This is precisely what is referred to in the above verse: they say God is the Messiah, son of Mary, and the Messiah, son of Mary, is God! They claim these two, along with the Holy Spirit, form a single true unit while simultaneously being three distinct essences!! Therefore, each aspect of the Trinity—which is the greatest deviation in Christianity—is discussed and strongly refuted in various verses of the Quran (for more details on the invalidity of the doctrine of the Trinity, please refer to the commentary on verse 171 of Surah An-Nisa' in this same volume).

From what has been stated above, it becomes clear that some commentators, such as Fakhr al-Razi, encountered difficulties in understanding the above verse. They imagined that none of the Christians explicitly express the belief in the "union" of God and the Messiah. This was due to a lack of sufficient familiarity with Christian texts; otherwise, existing Christian sources explicitly state the issue of "Unity in Trinity." Perhaps such books were not available to the likes of Fakhr al-Razi at that time.

Then, to invalidate the belief in the divinity of the Messiah عليه السلام, the Quran says: "If Allah willed to destroy the Messiah and his mother Mary and everyone on earth, who could prevent Him?"

(قُلْ فَمَنْ يَمْلِكُ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً إِنْ أَرادَ أَنْ يُهْلِكَ الْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَ أُمَّهُ وَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً).

This points to the fact that the Messiah عليه السلام, like his mother Mary and like all other human beings, was nothing more than a human. Consequently, in terms of being a created being, he is in the same category as all other creatures. For this reason, mortality and non-existence are applicable to his essence. How can such a being, for whom non-existence is conceivable, be the Eternal and Everlasting God?!

In other words, if the Messiah عليه السلام were God, the Creator of the world would not be able to destroy him, and thus His power would be limited. Such a being cannot be God, for God's power, like His essence, is limitless (reflect on this).

The repetition of the phrase "the Messiah, son of Mary" in this verse is perhaps to point to the reality that you yourselves admit the Messiah عليه السلام was the son of Mary and was born of a mother. One day he was a fetus, another day a newborn infant, and he gradually grew and matured. Is it possible for God to be contained in a small environment like a mother's womb, undergo all these changes and transformations, and be in need of a mother during the fetal and nursing stages?!

It is noteworthy that the above verse, besides mentioning Jesus عليه السلام, specifically names his mother with the words "and his mother" (wa ummahu), thereby distinguishing the mother of the Messiah عليه السلام from the rest of the people on earth. This expression may be because Christians also worship his mother during their devotions. Even now, among the statues in churches before which they bow and worship is the statue of Mary. This point is also mentioned in verse 116 of Surah Al-Ma'idah:

وَ إِذْ قالَ اللَّهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ أَ أَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَ أُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ‌:

"And [beware the Day] when Allah will say, 'O Jesus, Son of Mary, did you say to the people, "Take me and my mother as two gods besides Allah"?'"

At the end of the verse, a response is given to those who take the birth of the Messiah without a father as evidence of his divinity, stating: "To Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. He creates what He wills (whether a human without a father or mother like Adam, or a human from a father and mother like ordinary people, or only from a mother like the Messiah; this diversity in creation is proof of His power and proof of nothing else), and Allah is over all things competent."

(وَ لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

[^1]: The commentary on this verse was provided earlier in this volume.

[^2]: In Christian sources, we read: "And God the Father is the Creator of all beings through the Son!" (Bible Dictionary, p. 345). We also read: "God means the self-existent one, and it is the name of the Creator of all creatures and the Ruler of all beings; He is an infinite and eternal Spirit, unchanging in existence, wisdom, power, justice, and dignity in various forms" (Bible Dictionary, p. 344).

Ayah

وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ نَحْنُ أَبْنَـٰٓؤُا۟ ٱللَّهِ وَأَحِبَّـٰٓؤُهُۥ ۚ قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُم بِذُنُوبِكُم ۖ بَلْ أَنتُم بَشَرٌ مِّمَّنْ خَلَقَ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٨)
The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are Allah’s children and His beloved ones.’ Say, ‘Then why does He punish you for your sins?’ No, you are humans from among His creatures. He forgives whomever He wishes, and punishes whomever He wishes, and to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, and toward Him is the return. (18)

Commentary

In completion of the previous discussions regarding the Jews and the Christians, this verse points to one of the baseless claims and imaginary privileges they held, stating:

"The Jews and the Christians say, 'We are the children of Allah and His beloved ones'"! (وَ قالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَ النَّصارى‌ نَحْنُ أَبْناءُ اللَّهِ وَ أَحِبَّاؤُهُ‌). This is not the only imaginary privilege they claimed for themselves; rather, the verses of the Quran have repeatedly pointed to such claims of theirs. In verse 111 of Surah Al-Baqarah, it mentions and refutes their claim that no one shall enter Paradise except them, and that Paradise is exclusive to the Jews and Christians. In verse 80 of Surah Al-Baqarah, it mentions the claim of the Jews that the fire of Hell will not touch them except for a few numbered days, and rebukes them. In the above verse, it refers to the imaginary claim of being the children of Allah and His special beloved ones.

There is no doubt that they did not consider themselves literally the children of Allah—only the Christians consider Jesus to be the literal son of God and state this explicitly [^1]—but their purpose in choosing this name and title for themselves was to say they have a special relationship with God. It was as if whoever belonged to their race or was part of their community would automatically become one of the friends and the group of "children of God" without having performed any righteous deeds! [^2] However, we know that the Quran combats all these imaginary privileges and considers the merit of every human being to lie only in his faith, righteous deeds, and piety. Therefore, in the above verse, to refute this claim, it says: "Say, 'Why then does He punish you for your sins?'" (قُلْ فَلِمَ يُعَذِّبُكُمْ بِذُنُوبِكُمْ‌).

That is to say, you yourselves admit that you will be punished at least for a short period; this punishment of sinners is a sign that your claim of an extraordinary connection with God—to the extent that you consider yourselves His friends or rather His children—is a baseless claim. Furthermore, your history shows that you have been afflicted with a series of divine punishments and penalties even in this world, and this is another proof of the invalidity of your claim.

[^1]: Christian sources state: "The term 'Son of God' is one of the titles of our Savior and Redeemer, which is not applied to any other person unless it is clear from the context that the literal Son of God is not intended!" (Dictionary of the Holy Bible, p. 345).

[^2]: Recently, a group in our environment began a type of Christian missionary activity and named themselves "The Group of the Children of God!"

Then, to emphasize the matter, it adds: "Nay, you are but human beings from among those He has created" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ بَشَرٌ مِمَّنْ خَلَقَ‌).

And this is a general law that: "He forgives whom He wills (and deems worthy) and punishes whom He wills (and deems deserving)." (يَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

Beyond this, everyone is a creation of Allah and His servant and property; therefore, giving someone the name "child of God" is not logical.

(وَ لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما) "And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them."

"And to Him is the final return" (وَ إِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ).

Here a question arises: where did the Jews and Christians claim to be the children of God (even if "children" here is meant metaphorically rather than literally)?

In response to this question, it should be noted that in the current Gospels, this expression is frequently seen. For instance, in the Gospel of John, Chapter 8, verse 41 onwards, we read from the tongue of Jesus addressing the Jews: "You do the deeds of your father." They (the Jews) said to him: "We were not born of fornication; we have one Father—God!" Jesus said to them, "If God were your Father, you would love me." In Islamic narrations, we also read in a Hadith from Ibn Abbas that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله invited a group of Jews to Islam and warned them of Allah's punishment. They said: "How can you frighten us with Allah's punishment while we are the children of Allah and His beloved ones!" [^1] In the commentary of Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed, a Hadith similar to the one above is narrated, stating that a group of Jews, in response to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله threat of divine punishment, said: "Do not threaten us, for we are the children of Allah and His beloved ones; if He becomes angry with us, it is like the anger a person has toward his own child—meaning this anger will soon subside!"

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi, Vol. 11, p. 192.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلَىٰ فَتْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلرُّسُلِ أَن تَقُولُوا۟ مَا جَآءَنَا مِنۢ بَشِيرٍ وَلَا نَذِيرٍ ۖ فَقَدْ جَآءَكُم بَشِيرٌ وَنَذِيرٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٩)
O People of the Book! Certainly Our Apostle has come to you, clarifying [the Divine teachings] for you after a gap in [the appearance of] the apostles, lest you should say, ‘There did not come to us any bearer of good news nor any warner.’ Certainly, there has come to you a bearer of good news and a warner. And Allah has power over all things. (19)

Commentary

Once again, in this verse, the address is directed toward the People of the Book: "O People of the Book, O Jews and Christians! Our Messenger has come to you and has clarified the truths for you during an era when a fatrah (interval) and gap had occurred between the Divine Prophets, lest you say, 'No bringer of glad tidings or warner came to us from Allah.'"

(يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ قَدْ جَاءَكُمْ رَسُولُنَا يُبَيِّنُ لَكُمْ عَلَىٰ فَتْرَةٍ مِنَ الرُّسُلِ أَنْ تَقُولُوا مَا جَاءَنَا مِنْ بَشِيرٍ وَلَا نَذِيرٍ)

Indeed, the "bringer of glad tidings" (bashir) and "warner" (nadhir)—meaning the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, who gives glad tidings of Divine mercy and reward to the believers and righteous, and warns the faithless and sinful of Divine punishments—has come to you (فَقَدْ جَاءَكُمْ بَشِيرٌ وَنَذِيرٌ).

"Fatrah" originally means stillness and calm, and it is also applied to the interval between two movements, two efforts, or two uprisings and revolutions.

Since there were prophets and messengers in the interval between Moses عليه السلام and Christ عليه السلام, but it was not the same between Christ and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the Quran has named this period the era of "fatrah min al-rusul" (an interval between messengers). We know that there was a gap of approximately six hundred years between the era of Christ and the mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [1]. However, according to what is alluded to in the Quran (in Surah Ya-Sin, verse 14) and according to what Islamic commentators have stated, at least three messengers came between these two Prophets, and some consider their number to be four. Nevertheless, there was an interval between the passing of those messengers and the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, and for this reason, it is referred to in the Quran as the period of "fatrah."

Question

It might be asked here: according to our belief, human society is never for a single moment devoid of a representative of God and His messengers; how is it possible for such a fatrah to exist?

Answer

It must be noted that the Quran says 'ala fatratin min al-rusul, meaning there were no messengers (Rusul) during this period, but there is no obstacle to the existence of their successors (Awsiya).

To put it more clearly, "Messengers" were those who engaged in extensive and far-reaching propagation, giving glad tidings and warnings to the people, breaking the silence and stagnation of societies, and making their voices heard by everyone. However, their successors did not all have such a mission; sometimes they might even live secretly among the people due to a series of social factors.

Ali عليه السلام says in one of his statements in Nahj al-Balagha:

اللهم بلى لا تخلو الارض من قائم لله بحجة اما ظاهرا مشهورا او خائفا مغمورا لئلا تبطل حجج الله و بيناته يحفظ الله بهم حججه و بيناته حتى يودعوها نظرائهم و يزرعوها فى قلوب اشباههم.

"O Allah! Yes, the earth shall never be devoid of one who stands for Allah with a proof (hujjah), whether he be manifest and famous or fearful and hidden, so that the proofs and clear signs of Allah are not nullified (and so they are protected from distortion and tampering)... Through them, Allah preserves His proofs and clear signs until they entrust them to their peers and sow their seeds in the hearts of those like them..." [1]

It is clear that when revolutionary messengers and wave-creating propagators are not present within society, superstitions, satanic whisperings, distortions, and ignorance of Divine teachings gradually spread. It is at this point that some might imagine the existing situation to be an excuse or pretext to escape their responsibilities. At such a time, Allah cuts off these excuses and pretexts by means of Heavenly men.

And at the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah is Powerful over all things" (وَاللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ).

That is, appointing Prophets and raising their successors to spread the call of Truth is simple and easy for His power.

[1]: Some consider the interval between these two great Prophets to be less than six hundred years and some more than six hundred years. According to some, the interval between the birth of Christ عليه السلام and the Hijrah of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله is six hundred and twenty-one years and one hundred and ninety-five days by the Roman calendar (Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 4, footnote p. 154, written by the late Sha'rani).

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 147.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنۢبِيَآءَ وَجَعَلَكُم مُّلُوكًا وَءَاتَىٰكُم مَّا لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَدًا مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٠)
When Moses said to his people, ‘O my people, remember Allah’s blessing upon you when He appointed prophets among you, and made you kings, and gave you what none of the nations were given. (20)
يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْأَرْضَ ٱلْمُقَدَّسَةَ ٱلَّتِى كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَرْتَدُّوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِكُمْ فَتَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (٢١)
O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you, and do not turn your backs, or you will become losers.’ (21)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّ فِيهَا قَوْمًا جَبَّارِينَ وَإِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَا حَتَّىٰ يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّا دَٰخِلُونَ (٢٢)
They said, ‘O Moses, there are a tyrannical people in it. We will not enter it until they leave it. But once they leave it, we will go in.’ (22)
قَالَ رَجُلَانِ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمَا ٱدْخُلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْبَابَ فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غَـٰلِبُونَ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٢٣)
Said two men from among those who were Godfearing and whom Allah had blessed: ‘Go at them by the gate! For once, you have entered it, you will be victors. Put your trust in Allah, should you be faithful.’ (23)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَن نَّدْخُلَهَآ أَبَدًا مَّا دَامُوا۟ فِيهَا ۖ فَٱذْهَبْ أَنتَ وَرَبُّكَ فَقَـٰتِلَآ إِنَّا هَـٰهُنَا قَـٰعِدُونَ (٢٤)
They said, ‘O Moses, we will never enter it so long as they remain in it. Go ahead, you and your Lord, and fight! We will be sitting right here.’ (24)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى لَآ أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِى وَأَخِى ۖ فَٱفْرُقْ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٥)
He said, ‘My Lord! I have no power over [anyone] except myself and my brother, so part us from the transgressing lot.’ (25)
قَالَ فَإِنَّهَا مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ ۛ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً ۛ يَتِيهُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (٢٦)
He said, ‘It shall be forbidden them for forty years: they shall wander about in the earth. So do not grieve for the transgressing lot.’ (26)

Commentary

Bani Israel and the Holy Land

In these verses, to revive the spirit of gratitude within the Jews and to awaken their consciences regarding the errors they committed in the past—so they might think of making amends—it first says: "Remember when Moses said to his followers: O Bani Israel! Do not forget the favors that Allah has bestowed upon you."

(وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِقَوْمِهِ يا قَوْمِ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ‌).

It is clear that the phrase "نعمت الله" (the favor of Allah) encompasses all the blessings and gifts of the Lord, but following this, it points to three significant categories of them.

First is the blessing of the existence of numerous prophets and leaders among them, which was the greatest divine gift to them (إِذْ جَعَلَ فِيكُمْ أَنْبِياءَ), to the extent that it is said during the time of Moses bin Imran alone, there were seventy prophets, and all seventy who accompanied him to Mount Tur were counted among the prophets.

It was in the light of this blessing that they were delivered from the terrifying valley of polytheism, idol-worship, and calf-worship, and were saved from various superstitions, delusions, vices, and impurities; this was the greatest spiritual blessing for them.

Then, it points to the greatest material blessing, which in turn serves as a prelude to spiritual blessings, saying: "He made you masters of your own lives, property, and existence" (وَ جَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكاً).

For Bani Israel had been for many years in the chains of captivity and slavery under Pharaoh and his people, possessing no "authority" of their own, and were treated like captive animals. Allah, by the blessing of the uprising of Moses bin Imran, broke the chains of slavery and colonialism from their hands and feet and made them masters of their own existence and lives.

Some have imagined that "Muluk" (kings) here refers to "monarchs and kings" who rose from among the Bani Israel.

However, we know that Bani Israel possessed government and sovereignty for only a short period, and furthermore, only some of them reached such a position, whereas the verse says: "And He made you kings" (وَ جَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكاً), meaning "Allah gave all of you this status." From this, it becomes clear that the meaning of the verse is what we stated above.

Beyond this, "Mulk" (with a fatha on the Mim) in linguistics refers both to a sovereign/ruler and to one who is an owner and has authority over something [^1]. In a Hadith in the Tafsir Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

[^1]: In lexicons we read: "The King (al-Malik) is he who possesses sovereignty (al-mulk), and 'al-mulk' is everything that a human owns and disposes of—or—grandeur and authority."

"When one of the Bani Israel possessed a wife, a servant, and a mount, he was called a king (Malik)" [^1]. And at the end of the verse, it refers generally to the important and prominent blessings that had not been given to anyone else at that time, saying: "He gave you what He had not given to any among the worlds."

(وَ آتاكُمْ ما لَمْ يُؤْتِ أَحَداً مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ‌). These diverse blessings were many, including the miraculous rescue from the clutches of the Pharaohs, the parting of the sea for them, and the provision of special food called Manna and Salwa, the details of which were discussed in Volume 1 under verse 57 of Surah Al-Baqarah, and similar favors.

Then, in the following verse, it describes the event of Bani Israel's entry into the Holy Land: "Moses said to his people: Enter the Holy Land which Allah has ordained for you, and do not fear the difficulties of entering it, nor withhold sacrifice; if you turn your backs on this command, you will suffer loss."
