(يا قَوْمِ ادْخُلُوا الْأَرْضَ الْمُقَدَّسَةَ الَّتِي كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَ لا تَرْتَدُّوا عَلى‌ أَدْبارِكُمْ فَتَنْقَلِبُوا خاسِرِينَ‌).

Regarding which location the "Holy Land" (al-Ard al-Muqaddasah) mentioned in the verse refers to, commentators have discussed much. Some consider it the land of Jerusalem (Bayt al-Maqdis), some Syria (Sham), and others Jordan, Palestine, or the land of Tur. However, it is not unlikely that the Holy Land refers to the entire region of the Levant (Shamat), which is consistent with all these possibilities, as this region—by the testimony of history—has been the cradle of divine prophets and the land where great religions emerged. Throughout history, it was for long periods the center of monotheism and the dissemination of prophetic teachings, and for this reason, the name "Holy Land" was chosen for it, although sometimes the name is applied specifically to the area of Jerusalem (as explained in Volume 1, page 183).

From the phrase "Allah has ordained for you" (كَتَبَ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ), it is understood that Allah had decreed that Bani Israel should live in this Holy Land in peace and prosperity (on the condition that they purify it from the filth of polytheism and idol-worship and do not deviate from the teachings of the prophets). But if they did not implement this command, heavy losses would afflict them.

Therefore, if we observe that the generation of Bani Israel to whom this verse was addressed ultimately did not succeed in entering this Holy Land, but rather remained wandering in the wilderness for forty years, and their future generation achieved this success, it does not contradict the phrase "Allah has ordained for you." This is because this decree was conditional upon certain requirements which they did not fulfill, as is understood from the subsequent verses.

However, Bani Israel's response to this proposal by Moses—as is the way of weak, cowardly, and ignorant individuals who wish for all victories to be provided through coincidences or miracles, wanting the "morsel to be placed in their mouths"—was: "O Moses! You know that in this land lives a powerful and tyrannical people, and we will never set foot in it until they vacate this land and leave; when they depart, we will obey your command and step into this Holy Land."

(قالُوا يا مُوسى‌ إِنَّ فِيها قَوْماً جَبَّارِينَ‌ [^1] وَ إِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها حَتَّى يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنْها فَإِنَّا داخِلُونَ‌).

[^1]: It should be noted that the word "Jabbar" (tyrant/mighty) originally comes from the root "Jabr," meaning to repair something accompanied by pressure or force; hence, setting a broken bone is called "Jabr." Later, it was applied to any type of compensation on one hand, and any type of force and dominance on the other. (If the name "Jabbar" is applied to Allah, it is either because of His dominance over all things or because He rectifies every being in need of rectification).

This response from Bani Israel clearly shows what a sinister effect Pharaonic colonialism had left on their generation over many years. The word "Lan" (never), which usually signifies "eternal negation," indicates the deep terror this group felt toward engaging in a struggle to liberate and purify the Holy Land.

But Bani Israel was supposed to acquire the Holy Land through sacrifice, effort, striving, and Jihad. If, hypothetically, contrary to divine tradition, all enemies were annihilated by a miracle without any action, and Bani Israel inherited this prosperous and vast region without toil or trouble, they would have remained incapable of managing it. They would have shown no interest in preserving something they had not worked for, nor would they have possessed the readiness or worthiness for such a task.

Furthermore, the "tyrannical people" (Qawman Jabbarin) mentioned in this verse—as understood from histories—were the "Amalekites" [^1], who had large and tall statures. Sometimes exaggerations and legends have been made regarding their height, and ridiculous claims unsupported by any scientific evidence are seen regarding them, especially concerning "Og" (Uj), in fabricated histories mixed with superstitions. It appears that such legends, which have also found their way into some Islamic books, are fabrications of the Bani Israel, usually referred to as "Isra'iliyyat." Evidence for this is that in the text of the current Torah, examples of these legends are seen. In the Book of Numbers, at the end of Chapter 13, we read: Regarding the land which (the Israelite spies) had scouted, they brought a bad report to the Bani Israel, saying:

"The land through which we have gone as spies is a land that devours its inhabitants, and all the people whom we saw in it are men of great stature. There we saw the giants (the descendants of Anak)... and we were like grasshoppers in our own sight, and so we were in their sight!" Then the Quran says that at this moment, two men from among the believers, in whose hearts the fear of Allah resided—and for this reason were included in His great blessings and had blended the spirit of steadfastness and courage with foresight and social and military awareness—stood up to defend Moses' عليه السلام proposal and said to Bani Israel:

"Enter upon them through the gate; for when you enter it (and place them before a fait accompli), you will be victorious."

(قالَ رَجُلانِ مِنَ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهما ادْخُلُوا عَلَيْهِمُ الْبابَ فَإِذا دَخَلْتُمُوهُ فَإِنَّكُمْ غالِبُونَ‌) "But in any case, you must seek help from the spirit of faith and rely upon Allah to reach this goal."

(وَ عَلَى اللَّهِ فَتَوَكَّلُوا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Regarding who these two men were, most commentators have written that they were "Joshua son of Nun" and "Caleb son of Jephunneh," who were considered among the twelve leaders (Nuqaba) of Bani Israel whom we previously mentioned [^1].

[^1]: The Amalekites were a people of Semitic race who lived in the north of the Arabian Peninsula near the Sinai Desert. They attacked Egypt and held it for a long time; their rule lasted about 500 years (from 2213 BC to 1703 BC). (Encyclopedia of Farid Wajdi, Vol. 6, p. 232, 3rd edition).

[^1]: From the first chapter of the Book of Deuteronomy in the current Torah, it is also understood that the names of these two men were "Joshua" and "Caleb."

In the interpretation of the phrase "from those who fear" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ), although several possibilities have been given, it is clear that the apparent meaning of the phrase is that those two men were among the individuals who feared Allah, and for this reason, they had no fear of anyone other than Allah. The phrase "Allah had bestowed His favor upon them" (أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِما) also testifies to this meaning; for what blessing is greater than for a human to fear only Allah and none other?

Here the question arises: how did these two men know that if Bani Israel entered the city with a surprise attack, the Amalekites would retreat?

Perhaps it was because, in addition to their confidence in the promise of Moses bin Imran regarding conquest and victory, they knew there is a general rule in all wars: if an attacking force can reach the enemy's main center—that is, fight him in his own home—they will usually be victorious [^1]. Furthermore, the "Amalekites," as we know, had large bodies (though we denied the legendary aspects of this); it is clear that such a population can perform better in open desert battlefields, but in the twists and turns of city alleys, they have less readiness for hand-to-hand combat. Above all, as it is said, despite their large stature, they were cowardly individuals who would be quickly intimidated by a surprise attack. The combination of these factors led those two men to guarantee the victory of Bani Israel in such an attack.

But Bani Israel accepted none of these proposals, and because of the weakness and abjectness that had nested in their souls and spirits, they explicitly addressed Moses,

[^1]: In Nahj al-Balagha, in the Sermon of Jihad, this military tactic is also mentioned: "By Allah, no people were ever attacked in the heart of their homes except that they were humiliated." (Sermon 27, Nahj al-Balagha).

saying: "As long as they are in this land, we will never, ever enter it. You and your Lord, who promised you victory, go and fight the Amalekites; when you are victorious, let us know; we are sitting right here!" (قالُوا يا مُوسى‌ إِنَّا لَنْ نَدْخُلَها أَبَداً ما دامُوا فِيها فَاذْهَبْ أَنْتَ وَ رَبُّكَ فَقاتِلا إِنَّا هاهُنا قاعِدُونَ‌).

This verse shows that Bani Israel had taken their insolence toward their Prophet to the maximum; for firstly, with the word "Lan" (never) and "Abadan" (ever), they expressed their explicit opposition, and secondly, with the phrase "You and your Lord go and fight, we are sitting here," they actually belittled Moses عليه السلام and his promises, and they did not even pay attention to the proposal of those two godly men, perhaps not even giving them the slightest answer.

Interestingly, the current Torah also brings significant parts of this story in the fourteenth chapter of the Book of Numbers, where it says: And all the children of Israel grumbled against Moses and Aaron, and the whole congregation said to them: "If only we had died in the land of Egypt! Or if only we had died in this wilderness! Why has the Lord brought us to this land to fall by the sword, that our wives and children should become plunder?..." Then Moses and Aaron fell on their faces before all the assembly of the congregation of the children of Israel. And Joshua the son of Nun and Caleb the son of Jephunneh, who were among those who had spied out the land, tore their clothes...

In the next verse, we read that Moses became completely despondent of the people and turned to prayer, requesting his separation from them with these words: "My Lord! I have no power except over myself and my brother. O Allah! Part us from the people of the transgressors and rebels so they may see the result of their actions and be reformed."

(قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ إِلَّا نَفْسِي وَ أَخِي فَافْرُقْ بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ‌).

What the Bani Israel did—namely, the explicit rejection of their Prophet's command—was on the verge of disbelief (Kufr). If we see the Quran labeling them as "Fasiq" (transgressor), it is because "Fasiq" has a broad meaning and includes any type of departure from the custom of servitude and worship of Allah. Hence, regarding Satan, we also read: "He transgressed (Fasaqa) against the command of his Lord" [^1]. It is also necessary to mention that from the phrase "from those who fear" (مِنَ الَّذِينَ يَخافُونَ) in the previous verses, it is understood that there was a minority among the Bani Israel who feared Allah, and Joshua and Caleb were considered among them. However, here we see Moses mentioning only himself and his brother Aaron, making no reference to them. Perhaps this is because Aaron was both Moses' successor and the most prominent individual of Bani Israel after Moses, and thus he mentioned his name specifically.

Finally, Moses' عليه السلام prayer was answered, and Bani Israel reaped the sinister result of their actions, for it was revealed to Moses from Allah: "This population shall be forbidden from entering this Holy Land, which was full of various material and spiritual blessings, for forty years."

(قالَ فَإِنَّها مُحَرَّمَةٌ عَلَيْهِمْ أَرْبَعِينَ سَنَةً).

Furthermore, during these forty years, they must wander in the deserts (يَتِيهُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ‌) [^2]. Then He says to Moses: "Whatever befalls this population during this time is just; never be grieved over them because of this fate."

[^1]: Surah Al-Kahf, verse 50.

[^2]: "Yatihuna" comes from the root "Tih," meaning wandering. Later, this name (Tih) was given to the desert in which Bani Israel wandered, and this desert, as we mentioned in Volume 1 of this Tafsir, page 177, was part of the Sinai Desert.

(فَلا تَأْسَ عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ‌).

The final sentence is perhaps because after the decree of the punishment of wandering for forty years in the deserts was issued, Moses' emotions were stirred, and perhaps—as stated in the current Torah—he requested pardon and forgiveness from the threshold of Allah for them. But he was soon answered that they deserve such a fate, not pardon, for they were, as the Quran says: transgressing, rebellious, and defiant individuals, and for anyone who is such, such a fate is certain.

It should be noted that this forty-year deprivation was never for the sake of revenge (just as none of the divine punishments are; rather, they are either constructive or the result of the action itself). In truth, it had a philosophy: Bani Israel had lived for many years under the blows of Pharaonic colonialism, and the residues of this era had nested in their souls in the form of inferiority complexes, self-deprecation, and a sense of humiliation and deficiency. They were not ready to wash their spirits and souls in a short time under the guidance of a great leader like Moses عليه السلام and to prepare themselves with a swift leap for a new life accompanied by honor, power, and pride. What they said to Moses عليه السلام regarding the refusal to undertake a liberating Jihad in the Holy Lands was clear evidence of this reality.

Therefore, they had to remain wandering in the deserts for many years so that the existing generation, which was a weak and incapable generation, would gradually pass away. A new generation would be raised in the environment of the wilderness, in an atmosphere of freedom and liberty, in the embrace of divine teachings, and yet amidst difficulties and hardships that grant strength and power to the human spirit and body, so that they could undertake such a Jihad and establish the government of truth in the Holy Lands!

Ayahs

۞ وَٱتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ٱبْنَىْ ءَادَمَ بِٱلْحَقِّ إِذْ قَرَّبَا قُرْبَانًا فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِمَا وَلَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرِ قَالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ ۖ قَالَ إِنَّمَا يَتَقَبَّلُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٢٧)
Relate to them truly the account of Adam’s two sons. When the two of them offered an offering, it was accepted from one of them and not accepted from the other. [One of them] said, ‘Surely I will kill you.’ [The other one] said, ‘Allah accepts only from the Godwary. (27)
لَئِنۢ بَسَطتَ إِلَىَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِى مَآ أَنَا۠ بِبَاسِطٍ يَدِىَ إِلَيْكَ لِأَقْتُلَكَ ۖ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ ٱللَّهَ رَبَّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٨)
Even if you extend your hand toward me to kill me, I will not extend my hand toward you to kill you. Indeed, I fear Allah, the Lord of all the worlds. (28)
إِنِّىٓ أُرِيدُ أَن تَبُوٓأَ بِإِثْمِى وَإِثْمِكَ فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٢٩)
I desire that you earn [the burden of] my sin and your sin, to become one of the inmates of the Fire, and such is the requital of the wrongdoers.’ (29)

Commentary

The First Murder on Earth!

In these verses, the story of the sons of Adam and the murder of one by the other is detailed. The connection between this and the preceding verses regarding the Children of Israel might be that the motive behind many of the transgressions of the Children of Israel was the issue of "envy" (hasad). Through these verses, Allah warns them of how bitter and deadly the consequences of envy can be—to the extent that it causes a brother to stain his hands with the blood of his own brother! First, He says: "O Prophet! Recite to them the story of the two sons of Adam in truth."

(وَ اتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ ابْنَيْ آدَمَ بِالْحَقِ‌)

The mention of the phrase "in truth" (bi-l-haqq) may be an indication that the aforementioned story in the "Old Testament" (Torah) has been mixed with myths, whereas what appears in the Quran is the exact reality of what occurred. There is no doubt that "Adam" here refers to the well-known Adam, the father of the current human generations. The suggestion by some that it refers to a person named "Adam" from a tribe of the Children of Israel is baseless, as this word appears repeatedly in the Holy Quran with the same primary meaning. If it were to carry a different meaning here, a contextual clue would have been necessary. However, the verse "Because of that..." (min ajli dhalika), the commentary of which will follow shortly, can never serve as a clue for such a meaning, as we shall explain.

Then, the verse proceeds to detail the story, saying: "When they both offered a sacrifice [to God], it was accepted from one of them but was not accepted from the other."

(إِذْ قَرَّبا قُرْباناً فَتُقُبِّلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِما وَ لَمْ يُتَقَبَّلْ مِنَ الْآخَرِ)

This very issue caused the brother whose deed was not accepted to threaten the other with murder, "swearing: I will surely kill you!" (قالَ لَأَقْتُلَنَّكَ). But the second brother advised him, saying that if such an event has occurred, it is not my fault; rather, the fault lies with you, as your deed was not accompanied by piety and God-wariness, and "Allah only accepts from the righteous [the pious]."

(قالَ إِنَّما يَتَقَبَّلُ اللَّهُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ‌)

He then added: Even "if you stretch out your hand against me to kill me, I shall not stretch out my hand against you to kill you."

(لَئِنْ بَسَطْتَ إِلَيَّ يَدَكَ لِتَقْتُلَنِي ما أَنَا بِباسِطٍ يَدِيَ إِلَيْكَ لِأَقْتُلَكَ‌)

"For I fear Allah, the Lord of the worlds, and I will never stain my hands with such a sin" (إِنِّي أَخافُ اللَّهَ رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

Furthermore, I do not wish to bear the burden of another's sin, "but I want you to bear my sin and your own sin" (for if you truly carry out this threat, the burden of my past sins will also fall upon your shoulders, because you have deprived me of the right to life and must pay the penalty for it; and since you have no righteous deeds, you must take on my sins!) (إِنِّي أُرِيدُ أَنْ تَبُوءَ [^1]بِإِثْمِي وَ إِثْمِكَ‌).

And certainly, by accepting this great responsibility, "you will be among the companions of the Fire, and that is the recompense of the wrongdoers."

(فَتَكُونَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ النَّارِ وَ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الظَّالِمِينَ‌)

Key Points

Several points should be noted here:

  1. In the Holy Quran, the names of Adam's sons are not mentioned, neither here nor elsewhere. However, according to what has reached us in Islamic narrations, the name of one was "Habil" (Abel) and the other was "Qabil" (Cain). In the Book of Genesis of the Torah, Chapter 4, the names are mentioned as "Cain" and "Abel." As the famous commentator Abu al-Futuh al-Razi states, there are several variations for each name: the first was "Habil," "Habel," or "Haben," and the other was "Qabil," "Qabin," "Qabel," "Qaban," or "Qabun." In any case, the difference between Islamic narrations and the text of the Torah regarding the name "Qabil" relates to linguistic variations and is not a significant matter.

What is surprising, however, is that a Christian scholar cited this as a criticism against the Quran, asking why the Quran says "Qabil" instead of "Cain"! This is while, firstly, such linguistic variations and even differences in names are frequent—for instance, the Torah calls "Ibrahim" "Abraham," while the Quran calls him "Ibrahim."

Secondly, the names Habil and Qabil are fundamentally not in the Quran; they appear only in Islamic narrations. [^2]

  1. We know that "Qurban" (sacrifice) refers to anything that causes one to draw near to the Lord. However, the Quran does not mention the specific act the two brothers performed. Yet, according to some Islamic narrations and what is in the Torah (Genesis, Chapter 4), Habil, being a shepherd, chose one of his best sheep and its products for this purpose, while Qabil, who was a farmer, prepared ears of grain or flour from the worst part of his crop for this purpose.

  2. Regarding how the sons of Adam realized that the deed of one was accepted in the presence of the Lord and the deed of the other was rejected, the Quran again provides no explanation. We only read in some Islamic narrations that the two took their offerings to the top of a mountain; a lightning bolt struck Habil's offering as a sign of acceptance and consumed it, while the other remained as it was. This sign had precedents.

However, some commentators believe that the acceptance of one's deed and the rejection of the other's was announced to them through revelation to Adam. The reason for this was nothing other than that Habil was a man of sincerity, sacrifice, and devotion in the way of God, while Qabil was a dark-hearted, envious, and stubborn man. The words the Quran quotes from these two brothers in the following verses clearly illustrate their respective mindsets.

  1. From these verses, it is clearly understood that the primary source of the first conflicts, murder, aggression, and transgression in the human world was the issue of envy. This subject acquaints us with the significance of this moral vice and its extraordinary impact on social events.

[^1]: "Tabu’a" is from the root "Bawa’," meaning to return. [^2]: The late scholar Sheikh Muhammad Javad Balaghi wrote a treatise on this subject titled "Al-Akadhib al-A’ajib" (The Wondrous Lies), in which lies similar to the one mentioned above are collected. This treatise has been translated into Persian and published.

Ayahs

فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُۥ نَفْسُهُۥ قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ فَقَتَلَهُۥ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٣٠)
So his soul prompted him to kill his brother, and he killed him, and thus became one of the losers. (30)
فَبَعَثَ ٱللَّهُ غُرَابًا يَبْحَثُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُۥ كَيْفَ يُوَٰرِى سَوْءَةَ أَخِيهِ ۚ قَالَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَىٰٓ أَعَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْغُرَابِ فَأُوَٰرِىَ سَوْءَةَ أَخِى ۖ فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ ٱلنَّـٰدِمِينَ (٣١)
Then Allah sent a crow, exploring in the ground, to show him how to bury the corpse of his brother. He said, ‘Woe to me! Am I unable to be [even] like this crow and bury my brother’s corpse?’ Thus he became regretful. (31)

Commentary

Covering Up the Crime

In these verses, the continuation of the story of the sons of Adam is followed. First, it says:

Cain’s rebellious soul made the killing of his brother seem easy to him, and so he killed him.

(فَطَوَّعَتْ لَهُ نَفْسُهُ قَتْلَ أَخِيهِ فَقَتَلَهُ)

Considering that "طوع" (Taw') originally means for something to become submissive or tamed, it can be inferred from this phrase that after the acceptance of Abel’s offering, a storm arose within Cain’s heart. On one hand, the fire of envy blazed more fiercely every moment, inviting him to seek revenge; on the other hand, brotherly affection, human emotion, and the innate abhorrence of sin, oppression, injustice, and murder restrained him from this crime. However, eventually, the rebellious soul gradually overcame the restraining factors, taming and shackling his awakened and aware conscience, preparing him to kill his brother. The word "طوعت" (Tawwa'at), despite its brevity, is a meaningful allusion to all of this, as we know that taming something does not happen in an instant, but rather gradually and after much struggle.

Then it says: "And as a result of this act, he became one of the losers" (فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ الْخاسِرِينَ).

What loss could be greater than this: that he purchased for himself the torment of conscience, divine punishment, and an infamous name until the dawn of Resurrection?

Some have tried to infer from the word "اصبح" (Asbaha) that this murder occurred at night, whereas in the Arabic language, this word is not exclusive to night or day; rather, it indicates the occurrence of something, as in verse 103 of Surah Al-Imran: "فَأَصْبَحْتُمْ بِنِعْمَتِهِ إِخْواناً" ("By the grace of Allah, you became brothers").

As inferred from some narrations transmitted from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, when Cain killed his brother, he cast him in the wilderness and did not know what to do. Before long, predators approached Abel's body, and Cain (who was apparently under intense pressure of conscience) carried his brother's body on his shoulders for a while to save it, but birds still surrounded him, waiting for the moment he would cast the body to the ground so they could attack it! [^1] At this moment, as the Quran states, Allah sent a crow to scratch the ground and, by hiding the lifeless body of another crow, or by hiding part of its prey—as is the habit of crows—show Cain

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse.

how to bury his brother's body.

(فَبَعَثَ اللَّهُ غُراباً يَبْحَثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ لِيُرِيَهُ كَيْفَ يُوارِي سَوْأَةَ أَخِيهِ) [^1] Of course, it is not surprising for a human to learn something from a bird, as both history and experience have shown that many animals possess a series of instinctive knowledge that humans have learned from them throughout history, thereby completing their own knowledge. Some medical books even write that humans owe part of their medical knowledge to animals!

Then the Quran adds that at this moment, Cain became distressed by his own negligence and ignorance and "cried out: 'Woe to me! Am I unable to be even like this crow and bury my brother's body?'"

(قالَ يا وَيْلَتى‌ أَ عَجَزْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِثْلَ هذَا الْغُرابِ فَأُوارِيَ سَوْأَةَ أَخِي)

In any case, he eventually became regretful and remorseful for what he had done, as the Quran says: (فَأَصْبَحَ مِنَ النَّادِمِينَ).

Was his regret because his ugly and shameful act would eventually be revealed to his parents and possibly his other brothers, and they would severely reproach him? Or was it because he had carried his brother's body on his shoulders for a time without burying it? Or was it because, fundamentally, a human feels a kind of distress and regret in their heart after

[^1]: "يبحث" (Yabhathu) is from the root "بحث" (Bahth), which, as stated in Majma' al-Bayan, originally means searching for something in the soil, but later came to mean any kind of search, even in intellectual and rational discussions. "سواة" (Saw'ah) originally means anything that a person finds unpleasant, and thus it is sometimes applied to a corpse and even to private parts. Additionally, it should be noted that the subject in the phrase "ليريه" (Liyuriyahu - to show him) could be Allah, meaning God wanted to teach Cain the method of burial to preserve Abel's dignity, or the subject could be the crow itself, which carried out such a program by Allah's command.

committing any evil deed? However, it is clear that whichever of the three aforementioned possibilities was the motive for his regret, it does not serve as evidence of his repentance for the sin. Repentance is that which occurs out of fear of God and because of the ugliness of the act, compelling one never to return to such deeds in the future. Yet, there is no sign in the Quran of such a repentance being issued by Cain; rather, the following verse perhaps points to the absence of such repentance.

In a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that he said:

"لا تقتل نفس ظلما الا كان على ابن آدم الاول كفل من دمها لانه كان اول من سن القتل" [^1] "No soul is killed unjustly but that a portion of the responsibility for its blood is upon the first son of Adam, for he was the first to establish the custom of killing."

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this hadith that as long as any evil and ominous custom remains in the world, a share of its punishment lies upon its first founder! There is no doubt that the story of the sons of Adam is a real event. In addition to the fact that the literal meaning of the Quranic verses and Islamic reports prove this reality, the expression "بالحق" (in truth) which appears in the first of these verses is also a witness to this matter. Therefore, those who have given these verses a metaphorical, allegorical, or "symbolic" aspect have spoken without evidence.

Yet, at the same time, there is no obstacle to this "real story" being an example of the continuous conflict and war that has always existed in human life: on one side are the pure and faithful men with righteous deeds accepted by God, and on the other side are the defiled

[^1]: Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal, as quoted in Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 2, under the verse, p. 703.

and deviant individuals with a handful of malice, envy, threats, and bullying. How many pure individuals have drunk the nectar of martyrdom at their hands!

But eventually, they become aware of the ugly consequence of their shameful deeds and run in every direction to cover up and bury it. At this moment, long-term desires—of which the crow is a symbol and manifestation—rush to them, inviting them to cover up the traces of their crimes. But in the end, nothing will be gained by them except loss, detriment, and regret!

Ayah

مِنْ أَجْلِ ذَٰلِكَ كَتَبْنَا عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنَّهُۥ مَن قَتَلَ نَفْسًۢا بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسَادٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّمَا قَتَلَ ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا وَمَنْ أَحْيَاهَا فَكَأَنَّمَآ أَحْيَا ٱلنَّاسَ جَمِيعًا ۚ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنَا بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ (٣٢)
That is why We decreed for the Children of Israel that whoever kills a soul, without [its being guilty of] manslaughter or corruption on the earth, is as though he had killed all mankind, and whoever saves a life is as though he had saved all mankind. Our apostles certainly brought them manifest signs, yet even after that, many of them commit excesses on the earth. (32)

Commentary

The Connection Between Human Beings

Following the narration of the story of the sons of Adam, a general and humanitarian conclusion is drawn in this verse. First, it states: "Because of this very matter, We ordained for the Children of Israel that whoever kills a human being—unless it be for murder or for spreading corruption in the land—it is as if he had killed all of humanity; and whoever saves a human life, it is as if he had saved the lives of all humanity."

(مِنْ أَجْلِ [^1] ذلِكَ كَتَبْنا عَلى‌ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ أَنَّهُ مَنْ قَتَلَ نَفْساً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ أَوْ فَسادٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَكَأَنَّما قَتَلَ النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً وَ مَنْ أَحْياها فَكَأَنَّما أَحْيَا النَّاسَ جَمِيعاً)

Here, an important question arises: How can the killing of one person be equal to the killing of all people, and the saving of one person be equal to the saving of all people?

Commentators have provided many answers here. In Tafsir al-Tibyan, six answers are given; in Majma' al-Bayan, five; and in Kanz al-'Irfan, four answers are provided, though some of them are quite distant from the meaning of the verse.

What can be said in response to the above question is that the Quran, in this verse, expresses a social and educational reality because:

Firstly, someone who stains their hands with the blood of an innocent person is, in reality, prepared to attack and kill other innocent people who are equal to the victim in terms of humanity and innocence. He is, in truth, a murderer whose prey is the innocent human, and we know there is no difference among innocent humans in this regard. Similarly, someone who, out of altruism and human compassion, saves another from death is prepared to carry out this humanitarian program for any other human being. He is devoted to saving innocent lives, and from this perspective, there is no difference for him between one person and another. Considering that the Quran says "فَكَأَنَّما..." (as if...), it is understood that while the death and life of one individual are not literally equal to the death and life of a society, they bear a resemblance to it.

Secondly, human society is, in reality, nothing but a single unit, and its individuals are like the limbs of one body. Any injury inflicted upon one of the limbs of this body will, more or less, manifest its effect in the other limbs. This is because a large society is composed of individuals, and the loss of one individual is inevitably a blow to the entire large human society.

His absence causes a void to remain in proportion to the radius of his influence in society, and a loss from this path affects everyone. Likewise, the revival of one soul is the cause for the revival of the other limbs of this body, because every person, to the extent of their own existence, has an effect on the structure of the great human community and the fulfillment of its needs—some more and some less.

And if we read in some narrations that the punishment for such a person on the Day of Resurrection is the punishment of one who has killed all humans, it is an allusion to this very point, not that they are equal in every single respect. Therefore, we read in the continuation of these same narrations that if he kills a larger number, his punishment will be multiplied in that same proportion! From this verse, the importance of the death and life of a human being from the perspective of the Quran becomes completely clear; and considering that these verses were revealed in an environment where human blood had absolutely no value, its greatness becomes even more apparent.

It is noteworthy that it has been mentioned in numerous narrations that although the apparent meaning of the verse refers to physical death and life, more important than that is spiritual death and life—meaning leading someone astray or saving them from misguidance. Someone asked Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام for the commentary of this verse, and the Imam replied:

"Whoever burns or drowns [someone]—then he remained silent—then he said: Its greatest interpretation is if he invites him [to the truth] and he responds to him."

Meaning, the intent of "killing" and "saving from death" mentioned in the verse includes saving from a fire or drowning and the like. Then the Imam remained silent and said:

The greatest interpretation and the larger concept of the verse is that one invites another toward the path of truth or falsehood, and the other accepts his invitation. [^1]

Another question that remains regarding the verse is: Why is the name of the Children of Israel specifically mentioned in this verse? This is while we know the aforementioned ruling is not exclusive to them.

In response, it can be said that the mention of the Children of Israel is because the issue of murder and bloodshed—especially killings rooted in jealousy and a desire for superiority—was frequent among them. Even now, the innocent victims killed at their hands constitute a large number. For this reason, this divine decree was first included in their programs!

At the end of the verse, referring to the law-breaking of the Children of Israel, it says:

"Our messengers came to them with clear proofs for their guidance, but many of them, after that, committed excesses in the land."

(وَ لَقَدْ جاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُنا بِالْبَيِّناتِ ثُمَّ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ بَعْدَ ذلِكَ فِي الْأَرْضِ لَمُسْرِفُونَ‌).

It should be noted that "Israf" (excess) in the literal sense has a broad meaning that includes any kind of transgression or exceeding the limit, although it is most often used regarding gifts, expenses, and expenditures.

[^1]: "Ajl" (أجل) on the weight of "Nakhl" (نخل) was originally in the sense of a crime; then it was applied to any action that has an unpleasant consequence, and after that, to any action that has a consequence. Currently, it is mostly used for reasoning and stating the cause of something.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 620—other narrations with the same theme have also been recorded in this regard.

Ayahs

إِنَّمَا جَزَٰٓؤُا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَارِبُونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا أَن يُقَتَّلُوٓا۟ أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوٓا۟ أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَأَرْجُلُهُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ أَوْ يُنفَوْا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٣٣)
Indeed the requital of those who wage war against Allah and His Apostle, and try to cause corruption on the earth, is that they shall be slain or crucified, or shall have their hands and feet cut off from opposite sides, or be banished from the land. That is a disgrace for them in this world, and in the Hereafter, there is a great punishment for them, (33)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَقْدِرُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٤)
excepting those who repent before you capture them, and know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (34)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse, it has been narrated that: A group of polytheists came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and embraced Islam, but the climate of Medina did not suit them; their complexions turned yellow and their bodies became ill. To aid their recovery, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله ordered them to go outside Medina to a place in the desert with a pleasant climate where the Zakat camels were grazed. He instructed them to benefit from the air and drink sufficient amounts of the camels' fresh milk. They did so and recovered, but instead of thanking the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, they cut off the hands and feet of the Muslim shepherds and gouged out their eyes.

Subsequently, they slaughtered them, plundered the Zakat camels, and apostatized from Islam. The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله ordered their capture, and the same actions they had committed against the shepherds were carried out against them as punishment; that is, they were blinded, their hands and feet were cut off, and they were executed so that others might take heed and not commit such anti-human acts. The above verse was revealed concerning such individuals and explained the Islamic law regarding them. [^1]

Commentary

The Punishment of Those Who Attack the Lives and Property of the People

This verse, in fact, completes the discussion regarding homicide mentioned in the previous verses. It describes, with the utmost severity, the penalty for transgressors who draw weapons against Muslims and plunder their property by threatening death or even committing murder. It states: "The recompense of those who wage war against Allah and His Messenger and strive to spread corruption on earth is that one of four punishments be executed upon them: first, that they be killed; second, that they be crucified; third, that their hands and feet be cut off on opposite sides (right hand and left foot); and fourth, that they be exiled from the land in which they live."

(إِنَّما جَزاءُ الَّذِينَ يُحارِبُونَ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ وَ يَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَساداً أَنْ يُقَتَّلُوا أَوْ يُصَلَّبُوا أَوْ تُقَطَّعَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَ أَرْجُلُهُمْ مِنْ خِلافٍ أَوْ يُنْفَوْا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

Several points must be noted here:

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 353; Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 3, p. 2145.

  1. The meaning of "war against Allah and His Messenger," as stated in the Hadiths of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام and as the occasion of revelation more or less attests, is: When someone assaults the life or property of people by threatening them with a weapon, whether they do so as highway robbers outside cities or within the city. Therefore, thugs who attack the lives, property, and honor of people are also included in this definition. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that waging war and conflict against the servants of Allah is introduced in this verse as waging war against Allah. This proves the extraordinary emphasis Islam places on human rights and the preservation of their security.

  2. The meaning of cutting the hand and foot, according to what is mentioned in books of jurisprudence, is the same amount specified for theft; that is, only cutting four fingers from the hand or foot. [^1]

  3. Are the four aforementioned punishments optional—meaning the Islamic government can implement whichever it deems appropriate for anyone—or are they proportionate to the nature of the crime and atrocity committed? That is, if the combatants (muharib) have killed innocent people, the punishment of execution is chosen for them; if they take people's property by threatening them with weapons, their fingers and feet are cut off; if they have committed both murder and robbery, they are executed and their bodies are crucified for a time as a warning to others; and if they only drew weapons against people without shedding blood or committing robbery, they will be exiled to another city. Undoubtedly, the second meaning is closer to the truth, and this theme has been narrated in several Hadiths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام. [^1]

[^1]: Kanz al-Irfan fi Fiqh al-Quran, Vol. 2, p. 352. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 622.

It is true that in some Hadiths, there is a reference to the Islamic government having a choice in this matter, but considering the previous Hadiths, the meaning of choice is not that the Islamic government may arbitrarily select one of these four punishments without considering the nature of the crime. It seems very unlikely that the matter of killing and crucifixion would be on the same level as exile.

Coincidentally, in many criminal and penal laws of the modern world, this point is clearly seen: several punishments are considered for one type of crime. For example, in some offenses, the law sets the punishment for the criminal as imprisonment from three to ten years, leaving the judge's hand open. This does not mean the judge determines the years of prison according to his own whim; rather, it means he should consider the circumstances of the crime—which are sometimes accompanied by "mitigating factors" and sometimes by "aggravating factors"—and choose the appropriate penalty.

In this important Islamic law regarding combatants (muhariban), because the nature of this crime and atrocity varies greatly and all combatants are certainly not the same, the manner of their punishment is also mentioned differently. It goes without saying that the extraordinary severity Islam has shown regarding combatants is for the protection of the blood of the innocent and to prevent attacks and transgressions by bullies, the powerful, criminals, thugs, and murderers against the lives, property, and honor of innocent people. [^2]

At the end of the verse, He says: "This is their disgrace in this world, and it will not be limited to this punishment; rather, in the Hereafter, they shall have a severe and great punishment."

(ذلِكَ لَهُمْ خِزْيٌ فِي الدُّنْيا وَ لَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذابٌ عَظِيمٌ‌).

From this phrase, it is understood that even the execution of Islamic legal penalties (hudud) and punishments will not prevent the punishments of the Hereafter.

Then, so as not to close the door of return even to such dangerous criminals, and to keep the path of compensation and revision open for them should they intend to reform, He says: "Except for those who repent and return before you gain power over them, for they shall be included in Allah's forgiveness; and know that Allah is Forgiving, Merciful."

(إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تابُوا مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْهِمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

From this phrase, it is understood that the punishment and hadd are removed from them only if they desist from this crime and repent of their own will and volition before being captured. Of course, it needs no reminder that their repentance does not mean that if they have committed murder or stolen property, they will not face the punishment for those; only the punishment for threatening people with weapons (muharabah) will be removed. In other words, his repentance only affects the waiving of the "Right of Allah" (Haqq-Allah), but the "Right of People" (Haqq-an-Nas) will not be waived without the consent of the owners of the right (reflect on this). To put it another way: the punishment for a muharib is more severe than that of an ordinary murderer or thief; by repenting, the specific punishment of muharabah is removed from him, but he will still face the punishment of an ordinary thief, usurper, or murderer.

Question

Repentance is an internal matter; how can it be proven?

Answer

There are many ways to prove this matter, including: Two just witnesses testifying that they heard his repentance in a gathering and that he repented of his own will without anyone forcing him, or that he changes his program and way of life such that the signs of repentance are manifest in it.

[^2]: The rulings mentioned above were as a brief and summarized exegetical discussion; the details and conditions of this Islamic law should be studied in books of jurisprudence.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱبْتَغُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ ٱلْوَسِيلَةَ وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٣٥)
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means of recourse to Him, and wage jihad in His way, so that you may be felicitous. (35)

Commentary

The Reality of Tawassul (Seeking a Means of Nearness)

In this verse, the address is directed toward the believers, and they are given three commands to achieve salvation:

First, it says: "O you who have believed, fear Allah and be mindful of Him." (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ).

Then, it commands them to "seek the means [of nearness] to Him" (وَ ابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوَسِيلَةَ).

And finally, it commands "striving in His cause." (وَ جَاهِدُوا فِي سَبِيلِهِ).

The result of all these is that "you may succeed." (لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ).

The important subject that must be discussed regarding this verse is the instruction given to the believers about choosing a "means" (Wasilah).

"Wasilah" originally means seeking nearness, or something that causes proximity to another out of love and desire.

Therefore, Wasilah in the above verse has a very broad meaning and encompasses every act and every thing that causes closeness to the Holy Presence of the Lord. The most important of these are faith in Allah and the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Jihad, and acts of worship such as prayer (Salat), zakat, fasting, the pilgrimage to the House of Allah (Hajj), as well as maintaining family ties (Silat al-Rahim), and spending in the way of Allah—both secretly and openly—and every good and righteous deed. As Ali عليه السلام has stated in "Nahj al-Balagha":

"The best means by which those seeking nearness to Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, seek proximity is faith in Him and His Messenger, and striving in His way, for it is the peak of Islam; and the word of sincerity (La ilaha illa Allah), for it is the primordial nature (Fitra); and establishing prayer, for it is the creed; and paying zakat, for it is an obligatory duty; and fasting the month of Ramadan, for it is a shield against punishment; and Hajj and Umrah of the House, for they repel poverty and wash away sin; and maintaining family ties, for it increases wealth and extends life; and secret charity, for it expiates sin; and open charity, for it wards off an unpleasant death; and doing good deeds, for they protect against the falls of humiliation." [^1]

Furthermore, the intercession (Shafa'at) of the Prophets, the Imams, and the righteous servants of Allah—which, according to the explicit text of the Quran, causes nearness to the Lord—is included within the broad concept of Tawassul. Likewise, following the Prophet and the Imam and walking in their footsteps is included, as all of these lead to proximity to the Holy Presence of the Lord. Even adjuring Allah by the status of the Prophets, Imams, and the righteous—which is a sign of love for them and valuing their status and school of thought—is part of this broad concept.

Those who have restricted the above verse to only some of these meanings actually have no evidence for such a restriction, because, as we mentioned, "Wasilah" in its literal sense means anything that causes nearness.

It is necessary to mention that the intention is never to request something from the person of the Prophet or the Imam independently; rather, the intention is to ask Allah for something by means of righteous deeds, or by following the Prophet and the Imam, or through their intercession, or by adjuring Allah by their status and school (which is itself a form of respect and attention to their position and a type of worship). This meaning neither reeks of polytheism (Shirk), nor is it contrary to other verses of the Quran, nor is it outside the generality of the above verse. (Reflect carefully).

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 110.

The Quran and Tawassul

From other verses of the Quran, it is clearly understood that using the status of a righteous person as a means before Allah and asking something from Allah for their sake is by no means forbidden and does not conflict with monotheism (Tawhid). In Surah An-Nisa, verse 64, we read:

وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ إِذْ ظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ جَاءُوكَ فَاسْتَغْفَرُوا اللَّهَ وَاسْتَغْفَرَ لَهُمُ الرَّسُولُ لَوَجَدُوا اللَّهَ تَوَّابًا رَحِيمًا:

"And if, when they wronged themselves (and committed a sin), they had come to you and asked forgiveness of Allah and the Messenger had asked forgiveness for them, they would have found Allah Accepting of repentance and Merciful."

We also read in Surah Yusuf, verse 97, that the brothers of Yusuf requested their father to seek forgiveness for them before Allah, and Ya'qub عليه السلام accepted this request.

In Surah At-Tawbah, verse 114, the subject of Ibrahim's عليه السلام seeking forgiveness for his father is mentioned, which confirms the effectiveness of the Prophets' prayers for others, and this subject is reflected in numerous other verses of the Quran as well.

Islamic Traditions and Tawassul

From numerous traditions available through both Shi'a and Sunni sources, it is clearly understood that Tawassul in the sense mentioned above presents no problem; rather, it is considered a good deed. These traditions are very numerous and have been narrated in many books. As an example, we point to a few parts of them found in famous Sunni books:

  1. In the book "Wafa al-Wafa" written by the famous Sunni scholar "Samhudi," we read that seeking help and intercession before Allah through the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and through his status and personality is permissible before his creation, after his birth, after his passing, in the world of Barzakh, and on the Day of Resurrection. He then narrates the famous tradition of Adam's Tawassul to the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله from Umar ibn al-Khattab: Adam, based on the knowledge he had of the future creation of the Prophet of Islam, addressed the presence of Allah saying:

"O Lord, I ask You by the right of Muhammad to forgive me." [^1]

Then he narrates another tradition from a group of traditionists, including "Nasa'i" and "Tirmidhi," the famous Sunni scholars, as evidence for the permissibility of Tawassul to the Prophet during his lifetime. The summary is: "A blind man requested the Prophet to pray for the healing of his illness. The Prophet instructed him to pray thus:

'O Allah, I ask You and turn to You by Your Prophet Muhammad, the Prophet of Mercy. O Muhammad! I have turned by you to my Lord regarding my need so that it may be fulfilled for me. O Allah, make him an intercessor for me.'" [^2]

[^1]: Wafa al-Wafa, Vol. 3, p. 1371. In the book "Al-Tawassul ila Haqiqat al-Tawassul," p. 215, the above tradition is also narrated from "Dala'il al-Nubuwwah" by Bayhaqi. [^2]: Wafa al-Wafa, p. 1372.

Then, regarding the permissibility of Tawassul to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله after his passing, he narrates that a man in need came to the grave of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله during the time of Uthman, performed prayer, and prayed thus:

"O Allah, I ask You and turn to You by our Prophet Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله, the Prophet of Mercy. O Muhammad, I turn by you to your Lord so that my need may be fulfilled."

He then adds that it was not long before his problem was solved. [^1]

  1. The author of the book "Al-Tawassul ila Haqiqat al-Tawassul," who is very strict regarding the subject of Tawassul, narrates 26 traditions from various books and sources in which the permissibility of this subject is reflected. Although he tries to find faults in the chains of narration (Isnad) of these traditions, it is clear that when traditions are numerous and reach the level of Tawatur (consecutive reporting), there remains no room for questioning the chain of a single tradition. The traditions regarding Tawassul in Islamic sources are beyond the level of Tawatur. Among the traditions he narrates is:

"Ibn Hajar al-Makki" in the book "Al-Sawa'iq" narrates from "Imam Shafi'i," the famous Sunni leader, that he used to seek Tawassul through the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet and would say:

The family of the Prophet are my means, And they are my path of nearness to Him. I hope that through them, tomorrow, My record will be given to my right hand. [^2]

He also narrates from "Bayhaqi" that during the caliphate of the second Caliph, there was a year of famine. Bilal, accompanied by a group of companions, came to the grave of the Prophet and said:

"O Messenger of Allah! Ask for rain for your Ummah... for they are perishing..." [^1]

He even narrates from Ibn Hajar in the book "Al-Khayrat al-Hisan" that "Imam Shafi'i," during the days he was in Baghdad, would visit Abu Hanifa and seek Tawassul through him for his needs! [^2]

Furthermore, in the Sahih of "Darimi," it is narrated from "Abi al-Jawza" that one year a severe famine occurred in Medina. Some complained to "Aisha," and she advised them to make an opening in the roof over the grave of the Prophet so that rain might descend from Allah by the blessing of the Prophet's grave. They did so, and abundant rain fell!

In the Tafsir of "Alusi," many of the above traditions are narrated, and after a long analysis and even strictness regarding them, he is ultimately forced to confess, saying:

"After all this discussion, I see no obstacle in seeking Tawassul before Allah through the status of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, whether during the Prophet's life or after his passing." After a relatively detailed discussion on this matter, he adds: "Seeking Tawassul through the status of someone other than the Prophet before Allah is also not prohibited, provided that they truly hold a status before Allah." [^3]

[^1]: Wafa al-Wafa, p. 1373. [^2]: Al-Tawassul..., p. 329. [^1]: Al-Tawassul..., p. 253. [^2]: Al-Tawassul..., p. 331. [^3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 4-6, pp. 114-115.

As for Shi'a sources, the matter is so clear that there is no need to cite traditions.

A Few Necessary Reminders

It is necessary to point out a few points here:

  1. As we have said, the meaning of Tawassul is not that someone asks for their need from the Prophet or the Imams; rather, the meaning is to seek Tawassul through their status before Allah. This is, in reality, turning toward Allah, because the respect for the Prophet is also because he was His messenger and walked in His path. We are surprised by those who consider this type of Tawassul a form of polytheism (Shirk), whereas Shirk is to attribute a partner to Allah in His attributes or actions, and this type of Tawassul bears no resemblance to Shirk whatsoever.

  2. Some insist on distinguishing between the life and death of the Prophet and the Imams, while—aside from the above traditions, many of which relate to after their passing—from the perspective of a Muslim, the Prophets and the righteous have a "Barzakh life" after death; a life more expansive than the world of this life, just as the Quran explicitly states regarding the martyrs, saying do not consider them dead, they are alive. [^1]

  3. Some also insist on distinguishing between requesting a prayer from the Prophet and the expression of adjuring Allah by his status, considering the request for prayer permissible and the other forbidden, while no logical difference is seen between the two.

  4. Some Sunni writers and scholars, especially the "Wahhabis,"

[^1]: Surah Al-Imran, Verse 169.

with a particular stubbornness, try to weaken all the traditions that have been narrated regarding Tawassul or to consign them to oblivion with hollow and baseless objections. They discuss this matter in such a way that any neutral observer feels they have already chosen a belief for themselves and then want to "impose" their belief on the Islamic traditions, pushing aside anything that opposes it. However, a researcher can never accept such illogical and prejudiced discussions.

  1. As we have said, the traditions of Tawassul have reached the level of Tawatur, meaning they are so numerous that they spare us from examining their individual chains of narration. Furthermore, among them are many authentic (Sahih) traditions; thus, no room remains for nitpicking some of their chains.

  2. From what we have said, it becomes clear that the traditions narrated under this verse which say: the Prophet used to tell the people: "Ask Allah for the Wasilah for me," or what is narrated in Kafi from Ali عليه السلام that Wasilah is the highest station located in Paradise, do not conflict at all with what we said in the commentary of the verse. For as we have repeatedly pointed out, "Wasilah" encompasses every kind of nearness to the Lord, and the Prophet's nearness to Allah and the highest degree that exists in Paradise is one of its instances.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُم مَّا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا وَمِثْلَهُۥ مَعَهُۥ لِيَفْتَدُوا۟ بِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ مَا تُقُبِّلَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٣٦)
Indeed if the faithless possessed all that is on the earth, and as much of it besides, to redeem themselves with it from the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, it shall not be accepted from them, and there is a painful punishment for them. (36)
يُرِيدُونَ أَن يَخْرُجُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ وَمَا هُم بِخَـٰرِجِينَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٣٧)
They would long to leave the Fire, but they shall never leave it, and there is a lasting punishment for them. (37)

Commentary

Following the previous verse, which commanded the believers toward piety, Jihad, and seeking a means of approach, these two verses explain the reason for the preceding command by pointing to the fate of the faithless and the corrupt. It says: "Indeed, those who disbelieve—if they should have all that is in the earth and the like of it with it to ransom themselves thereby from the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, it will not be accepted from them, and for them is a painful punishment."

(إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَوْ أَنَّ لَهُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً وَ مِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ لِيَفْتَدُوا بِهِ مِنْ عَذابِ يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ ما تُقُبِّلَ مِنْهُمْ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

The content of this verse also appears in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 47, and it conveys the ultimate emphasis regarding the issue of divine punishments—that one cannot escape them through any wealth or power among all wealth and powers, even if it were all the riches on earth or more. Deliverance can only be found in the light of faith, piety, Jihad, and righteous deeds.

Then, referring to the permanence of this punishment, it says: "They will wish to get out of the Fire, but never are they to emerge therefrom, and for them is a lasting punishment."

(يُرِيدُونَ أَنْ يَخْرُجُوا مِنَ النَّارِ وَ ما هُمْ بِخارِجِينَ مِنْها وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ).

Regarding the eternal punishment and the immortality (khulud) of the disbelievers in Hell, we will discuss this, God willing, under the commentary of verse 108 of Surah Hud.

Ayahs

وَٱلسَّارِقُ وَٱلسَّارِقَةُ فَٱقْطَعُوٓا۟ أَيْدِيَهُمَا جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَسَبَا نَكَـٰلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (٣٨)
As for the thief, man or woman, cut off their hands as a requital for what they have earned. [That is] an exemplary punishment from Allah, and Allah is all-mighty, all-wise. (38)
فَمَن تَابَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِۦ وَأَصْلَحَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٣٩)
But whoever repents after his wrongdoing, and reforms, then Allah shall accept his repentance. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (39)
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤٠)
Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? He punishes whomever He wishes, and forgives whomever He wishes, and Allah has power over all things. (40)

Commentary

The Punishment for Thieves

In the preceding verses, the rulings for the "Muharib" (one who wages war)—that is, someone who "openly" threatens the lives, property, and honor of people with a weapon—were explained. In these verses, by appropriate transition, the ruling for the thief—meaning someone who carries away people's property "secretly" and "covertly"—is set forth:

First, He says: "Cut off the hand of the male and female thief."

(وَ السَّارِقُ وَ السَّارِقَةُ فَاقْطَعُوا أَيْدِيَهُما)

Here, the male thief is mentioned before the female thief, whereas in the verse regarding the punishment for adultery, the female adulteress is mentioned before the male. This difference may be because, in the case of theft, the primary actors are mostly men, while in the commission of adultery, the more significant factor and instigator are often immodest women.

Then He says: "This is a recompense for what they have committed and an exemplary punishment from Allah."

(جَزاءً بِما كَسَبا نَكالًا مِنَ اللَّهِ)

In truth, this phrase points to two things: first, that this punishment is the result of their own actions and something they have earned for themselves; and second, that its purpose is prevention and a return to truth and justice (since "Nakal" refers to a punishment carried out for the purpose of deterrence and the abandonment of sin—this word originally meant a bridle or halter, and was later applied to any action that prevents deviation). At the end of the verse, to dispel the misconception that this punishment is not just, He says: Allah is both "Almighty and Powerful," so He has no reason to take revenge on anyone, and He is "Wise," so He has no reason to punish anyone without cause (وَ اللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ).

In the following verse, He opens the door of return to them, saying: "But whoever repents after his wrongdoing and reforms, indeed Allah will turn to him in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful."

(فَمَنْ تابَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِ وَ أَصلَحَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ)

Does repentance alone forgive the sin, or will the legal punishment (cutting of the hand) also be dropped?

The well-known view among our jurists is that if he repents before the theft is proven in an Islamic court, the legal punishment is removed. However, once his crime is proven through two just witnesses, it is not waived by repentance.

In reality, the true repentance referred to in the verse is that which occurs before the judgment is established in court; otherwise, every thief would claim repentance once they find themselves facing punishment, leaving no room for the execution of justice. In other words, "voluntary repentance" is that which occurs before the crime is proven in court; otherwise, "forced repentance"—similar to the repentance made upon seeing divine punishment or the signs of death—has no value. Following the ruling on the repentance of thieves, the address turns to the great Prophet of Islam, saying:

"Do you not know that to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth? He punishes whom He wills and forgives whom He wills, and He is over all things competent."

(أَ لَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَغْفِرُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ قَدِيرٌ)

Here, several important points must be noted

A- Conditions for the Punishment of a Thief

In this ruling, as with other rulings, the Quran states the root of the matter, and its detailed explanation is left to the Sunnah of the Prophet. What can be gathered from the collective Islamic narrations is that the execution of this Islamic punishment (cutting the hand) has many conditions, without which this action is not permissible. These include:

  1. The stolen goods must be worth at least a quarter of a Dinar [^1].
  2. It must be stolen from a secure place, such as a house, a shop, or inner pockets.
  3. It must not be during a year of famine when people are hungry and have no other recourse.
  4. The thief must be sane and an adult, and must commit the act of their own free will.
  5. A father stealing from his child's property, or a partner stealing from shared property, does not fall under this ruling.
  6. Stealing fruit from garden trees has also been exempted from this ruling.
  7. All cases where there is a possibility of a mistake by the thief—such as potentially confusing their own property with another's—are exempt from this ruling.

And several other conditions whose details are found in books of jurisprudence.

Let there be no misunderstanding: the mention of these conditions does not mean that theft is only forbidden when these conditions are met; rather, it means that the execution of the aforementioned legal punishment is specific to these cases. Otherwise, theft in any form, manner, amount, or quality is forbidden in Islam.

B- The Extent of Cutting the Thief's Hand

The well-known view among our jurists, utilizing the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, is that only four fingers of the right hand are cut, and no more, although the Sunni jurists have prescribed more than that.

C- Is This Islamic Punishment Violent?

This objection has been raised many times by opponents of Islam or some ill-informed Muslims: that this Islamic punishment seems very severe, and if this ruling were to be implemented in today's world, many hands would have to be cut. Furthermore, the execution of this ruling causes a person, besides losing a sensitive limb, to be stigmatized for the rest of their life.

In response to this objection, one must pay attention to these facts:

First—As we mentioned in the conditions of this ruling, not every thief will be subject to it; rather, only a specific group of dangerous thieves are officially subject to it.

Second—Given that the method of proving the crime in Islam has specific conditions, the frequency of this occurrence is further reduced.

Third—Many of the objections raised by those ill-informed about Islamic laws are because they examine a single ruling independently and in isolation from all other rulings. In other words, they imagine that ruling in a 100% non-Islamic society. However, if we realize that Islam is not just this one ruling, but a collection of laws whose implementation in a society leads to social justice, the fight against poverty, proper education, sufficient training, awareness, vigilance, and piety, it becomes clear how few people would actually fall under this ruling. Let there be no mistake: the point is not that this ruling should not be implemented in today's societies, but that all these aspects must be considered when judging and evaluating.

In short, an Islamic government is duty-bound to provide the basic necessities of life for all its citizens, provide them with necessary education, and nurture them morally. It is obvious that in such an environment, deviant individuals will be very few.

Fourth—If we observe that theft is rampant today, it is because such a ruling is not implemented. Therefore, in environments where this Islamic ruling is enforced (such as in Saudi Arabia where this ruling was implemented until recent years), extraordinary security regarding property prevailed everywhere.

Many pilgrims to the House of Allah have seen with their own eyes suitcases or wallets in the streets and alleys of Hijaz that no one dares to touch until the "Lost and Found" officers come and take them to the office, where the owner can come, provide identification, and retrieve them. Most shops at night have no doors or barriers, yet no one commits theft.

Interestingly, although this Islamic ruling was implemented for centuries, and under its protection the early Muslims lived in security and prosperity, this ruling was only carried out on a very small number of individuals—not exceeding a few people—over several centuries.

Is cutting a few criminal hands too high a price to pay for the security of a nation for several centuries?

D- Some Object: [Does this ruling conflict with the respect due to a Muslim?]

They argue that executing this punishment on a thief for a quarter of a Dinar conflicts with all the respect Islam has accorded to the life of a Muslim and their protection from any harm—to the extent that the blood money (Diyah) for cutting four fingers of a human is set at a very high amount.

Coincidentally, this very question—as appears in some historical accounts—was posed to the great scholar of Islam, Alam al-Huda the late Sayyid Murtada, about a thousand years ago. The questioner presented his query in the following poem:

A hand whose blood money is five hundred gold [dinars], Why is it cut for a quarter of a dinar? [^1]

Sayyid Murtada composed this poem in response:

The honor of trustworthiness made it expensive, and it was cheapened By the humiliation of betrayal; so understand the wisdom of the Creator. [^2]

Meaning: the honor of trustworthiness made that hand precious, and the humiliation of betrayal brought its value down. Understand the philosophy of God's ruling.

[^1]: It should be noted that five hundred Dinars is the amount if five fingers are cut; however, as we mentioned, according to the Shia school, only four fingers are cut in theft.

[^2]: This incident is narrated in Tafsir al-Alusi, Vol. 3, p. 6, but instead of Alam al-Huda, it mentions Alam al-Din al-Sakhawi.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرَّسُولُ لَا يَحْزُنكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِأَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَلَمْ تُؤْمِن قُلُوبُهُمْ ۛ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ ۛ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِقَوْمٍ ءَاخَرِينَ لَمْ يَأْتُوكَ ۖ يُحَرِّفُونَ ٱلْكَلِمَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَوَاضِعِهِۦ ۖ يَقُولُونَ إِنْ أُوتِيتُمْ هَـٰذَا فَخُذُوهُ وَإِن لَّمْ تُؤْتَوْهُ فَٱحْذَرُوا۟ ۚ وَمَن يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ فِتْنَتَهُۥ فَلَن تَمْلِكَ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ ۚ لَهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا خِزْىٌ ۖ وَلَهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (٤١)
O Apostle! Do not grieve for those who are active in [promoting] unfaith, such as those who say, ‘We believe’ with their mouths, but whose hearts have no faith, and the Jews who eavesdrop with the aim of [telling] lies [against you] and eavesdrop for other people who do not come to you. They pervert words from their meanings, [and] say, ‘If you are given this, take it, but if you are not given this, beware!’ Yet whomever Allah wishes to mislead, you cannot avail him anything against Allah. They are the ones whose hearts Allah did not desire to purify. For them is disgrace in this world, and there is a great punishment for them in the Hereafter. (41)
سَمَّـٰعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ أَكَّـٰلُونَ لِلسُّحْتِ ۚ فَإِن جَآءُوكَ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُمْ أَوْ أَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ فَلَن يَضُرُّوكَ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَإِنْ حَكَمْتَ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (٤٢)
Eavesdroppers with the aim of [telling] lies, eaters of the unlawful—if they come to you, judge between them, or disregard them. If you disregard them, they will not harm you in any way. But if you judge, judge between them with justice. Indeed Allah loves the just. (42)

Circumstances of Revelation

Multiple narrations have been reported regarding the circumstances of this verse’s revelation. The clearest among them is a narration transmitted from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, the summary of which is as follows:

One of the nobles of the Jews of Khaybar, who was married, committed an act of adultery with a married woman who also belonged to a prominent family of Khaybar. The Jews were distressed by the prospect of executing the ruling of the Torah (stoning) regarding them. They sought a solution that would exempt the two from said ruling while simultaneously appearing to remain committed to Divine laws. Thus, they sent word to their co-religionists in Medina to ask the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله for the ruling on this incident (so that if there were a lighter ruling in Islam, they would adopt it; otherwise, they would ignore it. Perhaps through this, they also intended to draw the Prophet’s attention and present themselves as friends of the Muslims). Consequently, a group of Jewish leaders from Medina hastened to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "Will you accept whatever I rule?" They replied: "That is why we have come to you!" At this moment, the ruling of stoning for those who commit adultery while married (muhsana) was revealed. However, they shirked from accepting this ruling (with the excuse that such a ruling did not exist in their religion!). The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله added: "This is the same ruling that is in your Torah. Do you agree that I call upon one of you to judge, and you accept whatever he relates from the tongue of the Torah?" They said: "Yes."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "What kind of scholar is Ibn Suriya, who lives in Fadak?" They said: "He is the most knowledgeable of all Jews regarding the Torah." They sent for him, and when he came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the Prophet said to him: "I adjure you by the One God who revealed the Torah to Moses عليه السلام, split the sea for your salvation, drowned your enemy Pharaoh, and provided you with His blessings in the wilderness—tell me, was the ruling of stoning in such a case revealed to you in the Torah or not?"

He replied: "You have given me an oath such that I am compelled to say: Yes, such a ruling exists in the Torah."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "Why then do you refuse to implement this ruling?"

He answered: "The truth is that in the past, we would implement this legal punishment (hadd) upon commoners but refrain from it in the case of the wealthy and the nobility. Thus, this sin became prevalent among the affluent classes of our society until the cousin of one of our leaders committed this foul act. As usual, they waived his punishment. Meanwhile, a common person committed the same act. When they wanted to stone him, his relatives protested and said: 'If this ruling is to be implemented, it must be implemented for both.' Therefore, we sat together and enacted a law lighter than stoning, which was to give each forty lashes, blacken their faces, and seat them backwards on a mount to parade them through the streets and markets!" At this point, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered the man and woman to be stoned in front of the mosque [^1] and said: "O Allah, I am the first to revive Your command after the Jews had killed it."

At this moment, the above verses were revealed, concisely describing the aforementioned event.

Commentary

Judging Between Friend and Foe

From this verse and the few verses following it, it is understood that Islamic judges have the right, under specific conditions, to judge the crimes and offenses of non-Muslims as well, the details of which will be explained through these verses.

The verse begins with the address: "O Messenger!" (يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ). This expression is seen in only two places in the Quran: one here, and the other in verse 67 of this same Surah, where the issue of Wilayah (guardianship) and succession is raised. It seems that due to the importance of the subject and the fear or apprehension regarding the enemy, He intends to further stimulate the sense of responsibility in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and strengthen his will, reminding him: "You are the bearer of a message, and that from Us; therefore, you must show steadfastness in conveying the ruling."

Then, He proceeds to comfort the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله as a prelude to the subsequent ruling, saying: "Those who claim faith with their tongues while their hearts have never believed, and who race with one another in disbelief, should never cause you grief (for this situation is not new)."

(لَا يَحْزُنْكَ الَّذِينَ يُسَارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ مِنَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا آمَنَّا بِأَفْوَاهِهِمْ وَلَمْ تُؤْمِنْ قُلُوبُهُمْ)

Some believe that the expression "race in disbelief" (يُسَارِعُونَ فِي الْكُفْرِ) differs from "race toward disbelief" (يسارعون الى الكفر). The first phrase is said regarding those who are already disbelievers and are immersed within disbelief, racing with one another to reach the ultimate stages of it. However, the second phrase is said regarding those who are outside the bounds of disbelief but are moving toward it and racing with one another [^1]. After mentioning the sabotage of the hypocrites and internal enemies, He addresses the state of the external enemies and the Jews, saying: "Also, those among the Jews who follow this path should not cause you grief" (وَمِنَ الَّذِينَ هَادُوا).

Then, He points to some of their hypocritical actions, saying: "They listen much to your words, but this listening is not for the sake of understanding and obedience; rather, it is to find a pretext for denial and slander against you" (سَمَّاعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ).

This sentence has another interpretation: "They listen excessively to the lies of their leaders, but are not ready to accept the word of truth." [^2] Another of their attributes is that they not only attend your assembly to fabricate lies, but they are also spies for others who have not come to you.

(سَمَّاعُونَ لِقَوْمٍ آخَرِينَ لَمْ يَأْتُوكَ)

According to another interpretation, they are attentive to the commands of their own group, and their instruction is that if they hear a ruling from you that matches their desires, they should accept it, and if it is contrary to their desires, they should oppose it. Therefore, they are obedient and attentive to the commands of their elders, not your commands. In such a state, their opposition should not cause you grief, because from the beginning, they did not come to you with the intention of accepting the truth.

Another of their attributes is that they distort the words of Allah (whether literal distortion or semantic distortion). Any ruling they find to be contrary to their interests and whims, they justify, reinterpret, or reject entirely.

(يُحَرِّفُونَ الْكَلِمَ مِنْ بَعْدِ مَوَاضِعِهِ) [^1] More strangely, they have made their decision before even coming to you. Their elders have instructed them: "If Muhammad gives a ruling in accordance with our wishes, accept it; and if it is contrary to our wishes, beware of it."

(يَقُولُونَ إِنْ أُوتِيتُمْ هَذَا فَخُذُوهُ وَإِنْ لَمْ تُؤْتَوْهُ فَاحْذَرُوا)

They are so deeply mired in misguidance and their thoughts have become so fossilized that they reject anything contrary to their distorted beliefs without any thought or study. Thus, there is no hope for their guidance, and Allah intends to punish and disgrace them through this: "And he for whom Allah intends punishment and disgrace, you can never avail him anything against Allah."

(وَمَنْ يُرِدِ اللَّهُ فِتْنَتَهُ فَلَنْ تَمْلِكَ لَهُ مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئاً)

They are so polluted that they are beyond cleansing; for this reason, "They are those whose hearts Allah does not intend to purify."

(أُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُرِدِ اللَّهُ أَنْ يُطَهِّرَ قُلُوبَهُمْ)

Because Allah’s work is always intertwined with wisdom, and for those who have spent a lifetime in deviance by their own will and choice—polluted by hypocrisy, lies, opposition to truth and reality, and the distortion of Divine laws—their return is habitually impossible.
