At the end of the verse, He says: "They shall have disgrace and humiliation in this world, and in the Hereafter, they shall have a great punishment."

(لَهُمْ فِي الدُّنْيَا خِزْيٌ وَلَهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ)

In the second verse, the Quran emphasizes once again that they have ears for listening to your words only to deny them (or they have ears for listening to the lies of their elders).

(سَمَّاعُونَ لِلْكَذِبِ)

This phrase is repeated as an emphasis and confirmation of this foul attribute in them.

Furthermore, "They consume much forbidden and ill-gotten wealth and bribes."

(أَكَّالُونَ لِلسُّحْتِ) [^1] Then, He gives the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله the choice that whenever such persons refer to him for judgment, he may judge between them according to the laws of Islam, or he may turn away from them.

(فَإِنْ جَاءُوكَ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ أَوْ أَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ)

Of course, the intention is not that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله should let personal inclinations influence the choice between these two paths; rather, the intention is that he should consider the conditions and circumstances. If it is in the best interest, he should intervene and judge; if not, he should refrain.

To strengthen the Prophet’s spirit, He adds: "If you see fit to turn away from them, they can never harm you in the least."

(وَإِنْ تُعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ فَلَنْ يَضُرُّوكَ شَيْئاً)

"And if you choose to judge between them, you must certainly observe the principles of justice, for Allah loves those who are equitable and just."

(وَإِنْ حَكَمْتَ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ)

Whether this ruling—namely, the choice of the Islamic government between judging according to Islamic laws regarding non-Muslims or refraining from judgment—is abrogated or remains in force is a matter of discussion among commentators.

Some believe that everyone living within the environment of an Islamic government, whether Muslim or non-Muslim, is subject to Islamic regulations from a legal and penal perspective. Therefore, the ruling of the above verse is either abrogated or specific to non-Dhimmi disbelievers (i.e., disbelievers who do not live as a minority in an Islamic country but have established treaties and interactions with Muslims).

However, others believe that the Islamic government still possesses this choice regarding non-Muslims: to consider the conditions and circumstances and, if deemed appropriate, act toward them according to Islamic laws, or leave them to their own laws. (For further research and clarification on this ruling, refer to the discussion on "Judiciary" in books of jurisprudence).

[^1]: According to a narration reported by al-Bayhaqi in Volume 8 of his Sunan, page 246, when the Jewish scholars came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, they had brought that man and woman with them.

[^1]: Al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 388.

[^2]: In the first case, the "lam" in "lil-kadhib" is the "lam of causality" (lam al-ta'lil), and in the second case, it is the "lam of transition" (lam al-ta'diya).

[^1]: The nature of distortion and its types were discussed under verse 13 of this same Surah.

[^1]: Suht (rhyming with juft) originally means peeling off the skin and also means intense hunger. Subsequently, it was applied to "any illicit wealth," especially "bribes," because such wealth strips away the purity, freshness, and blessing from human society, just as peeling the bark of a tree causes it to wither or dry up. Therefore, the meaning of suht is broad, and if specific instances are mentioned in some narrations, it does not imply exclusivity.

Ayah

وَكَيْفَ يُحَكِّمُونَكَ وَعِندَهُمُ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ فِيهَا حُكْمُ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٤٣)
And how should they make you a judge, while with them is the Torah, in which is Allah’s judgement? Yet in spite of that, they turn their backs [on Him] and they are not believers. (43)

Commentary

This verse follows the discussion regarding the Jews seeking judgment from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, which was mentioned in the previous verse. It asks with astonishment: "How is it that they seek your judgment while they have the Torah with them, containing the judgment of Allah?"

(وَ كَيْفَ يُحَكِّمُونَكَ وَ عِنْدَهُمُ التَّوْراةُ فِيها حُكْمُ اللَّهِ‌)

It should be noted that the aforementioned judgment (i.e., the ruling of stoning for a man and woman who have committed adultery while married) is present in the current Torah in the twenty-second chapter of the Book of Deuteronomy.

The astonishment lies in the fact that they do not consider the Torah to be an abrogated book, and they regard the religion of Islam as false; nevertheless, because the rulings of the Torah do not align with their desires, they abandon it and seek a ruling from a source they fundamentally do not agree with. Even more astonishing is that after choosing you for judgment, they do not accept your ruling—which happens to coincide with the ruling of the Torah—simply because it goes against their inclinations.

(ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ‌)

"The truth is that they essentially have no faith," otherwise they would not play such games with the rulings of Allah.

(وَ ما أُولئِكَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌)

An objection might be raised: How can the above verse state that the judgment of Allah is mentioned in the Torah, while we—inspired by Quranic verses and historical documents—consider the Torah to be corrupted (tahrif), and this same corrupted Torah existed during the time of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله?

However, it must be noted that: Firstly, we do not consider the entire Torah to be corrupted; rather, we believe parts of it are consistent with the truth, and coincidentally, the aforementioned ruling is among these uncorrupted laws. Secondly, whatever the Torah was, it was regarded by the Jews as a divine and uncorrupted book; despite this, is it not a cause for wonder that they refuse to act upon it?!

Ayah

إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَا ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ ۚ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ بِمَا ٱسْتُحْفِظُوا۟ مِن كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ وَكَانُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ شُهَدَآءَ ۚ فَلَا تَخْشَوُا۟ ٱلنَّاسَ وَٱخْشَوْنِ وَلَا تَشْتَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِى ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ (٤٤)
We sent down the Torah containing guidance and light. The prophets, who had submitted, judged by it for the Jews, and so did the rabbis and the scribes, as they were charged to preserve the Book of Allah and were witnesses to it. So do not fear the people, but fear Me, and do not sell My signs for a paltry gain. Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the faithless. (44)

Commentary

This verse and the following verse complete the previous discussion and explain the importance of the Heavenly Book of Musa عليه السلام, namely the Torah, as follows: "We sent down the Torah, in which was guidance and light"—guidance toward the Truth and light to dispel the darkness of ignorance and folly.

(إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَ نُورٌ).

"For this reason, the Divine Prophets, who were submissive to the command of Allah and who came after the revelation of the Torah, all judged for the Jews according to it."

(يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا لِلَّذِينَ هادُوا).

Not only did they do so, but "the great scholars of the Jews and their faithful and pure men of knowledge would judge according to this Heavenly Book which had been entrusted to them, and to which they were witnesses."

(وَ الرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَ الْأَحْبارُ بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتابِ اللَّهِ وَ كانُوا عَلَيْهِ شُهَداءَ) [1] Here, the address is directed toward that group of the scholars of the People of the Book who lived in that era, saying: "Do not fear the people and express the true decrees of Allah; rather, fear My opposition, for if you conceal the truth, you shall be punished."

(فَلا تَخْشَوُا النَّاسَ وَ اخْشَوْنِ‌).

And likewise, do not sell the signs of Allah for a small price.

(وَ لا تَشْتَرُوا بِآياتِي ثَمَناً قَلِيلًا).

In reality, the source of concealing the truth and the decrees of Allah is either fear of the people and populism, or the pursuit of personal interests; whichever it may be, it is a sign of weakness of faith and the downfall of character, and both are pointed out in the above sentences.

[1]: Regarding the meaning of Rabbani and its root word, we discussed it in Volume 2, page 482. Ahbar is the plural of Habr (pronounced like Fikr and also like Abr), meaning a "good trace" or "effect," and it was subsequently applied to scholars who leave a good trace in society. If ink is also called Hibr, it is because of its good traces.

At the end of the verse, He issues a decisive ruling regarding such individuals who judge contrary to the decree of Allah, stating: "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the disbelievers."

(وَ مَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْكافِرُونَ‌).

It is clear that failing to judge according to Allah's decree is broader than just remaining silent and not judging at all—thereby leading people astray through their silence—or speaking and ruling contrary to Allah's decree. It is also clear that disbelief (kufr) has various levels and degrees, starting from the denial of the very existence of Allah and encompassing opposition, disobedience, and sin against Him; for complete faith invites a person to act according to it, and those who do not act do not possess complete faith. This verse clarifies the heavy responsibility of the scholars and men of knowledge of every nation in the face of social storms and events occurring in their environment, and with a decisive tone, it invites them to struggle against deviations and to fear no one.

Ayah

وَكَتَبْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيهَآ أَنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ بِٱلنَّفْسِ وَٱلْعَيْنَ بِٱلْعَيْنِ وَٱلْأَنفَ بِٱلْأَنفِ وَٱلْأُذُنَ بِٱلْأُذُنِ وَٱلسِّنَّ بِٱلسِّنِّ وَٱلْجُرُوحَ قِصَاصٌ ۚ فَمَن تَصَدَّقَ بِهِۦ فَهُوَ كَفَّارَةٌ لَّهُۥ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (٤٥)
In it We prescribed for them: a life for a life, an eye for an eye, a nose for a nose, and an ear for an ear, a tooth for a tooth, and retaliation for wounds. Yet whoever remits it out of charity, that shall be an atonement for him. Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the wrongdoers. (45)

Commentary

Retaliation and Forgiveness

This verse explains another part of the criminal laws and divine limits (Hudud) of the Torah, stating: "We ordained in the Torah the law of retaliation (Qisas), such that if someone intentionally kills an innocent person, the guardians of the deceased may execute the killer in return."

(وَ كَتَبْنا عَلَيْهِمْ فِيها أَنَّ النَّفْسَ بِالنَّفْسِ‌).

And if someone causes injury to another's eye and destroys it, they too may destroy his eye (وَ الْعَيْنَ بِالْعَيْنِ‌).

And likewise, in return for cutting off a nose, it is permissible to cut off the nose of the criminal (وَ الْأَنْفَ بِالْأَنْفِ‌).

And also, in return for cutting off an ear, cutting off the ear of the perpetrator is permitted (وَ الْأُذُنَ بِالْأُذُنِ‌).

And if someone breaks another's tooth, he may break the criminal's tooth in return (وَ السِّنَّ بِالسِّنِ‌).

And generally, for any wound or injury inflicted upon another, retaliation may be carried out in return (وَ الْجُرُوحَ قِصاصٌ‌).

Therefore, the ruling of retaliation is implemented justly and without any distinction regarding race, social class, tribe, or personality, and no discrimination is allowed in these respects (of course, this ruling, like other Islamic rulings, has conditions and regulations mentioned in books of jurisprudence, as this ruling was not exclusive to the Children of Israel; a similar law exists in Islam, as mentioned in the verse of retaliation in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 178).

This verse puts an end to the unjust discriminations that existed in that era. As understood from some commentaries, there was a strange inequality between two Jewish tribes in Medina at that time: if a member of the "Bani al-Nadir" tribe killed someone from the "Bani Qurayza" tribe, he would not be retaliated against. Conversely, if someone from the "Bani Qurayza" killed a member of the "Bani al-Nadir," he would be executed. When Islam came to Medina, the Bani Qurayza questioned the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding this. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said that blood is equal. The Bani al-Nadir tribe began to protest, saying, "You have lowered our status." The above verse was revealed, announcing to them that not only in Islam but also in the Jewish religion, this law existed equally [1]. However, so that the misconception does not arise that Allah has made retaliation mandatory and invited only to "an eye for an eye," He follows this ruling by saying:

"If someone waives his right and forgives, it shall be considered an expiation for his sins, and to the same extent that he has shown forgiveness, Allah will forgive him."

(فَمَنْ تَصَدَّقَ بِهِ فَهُوَ كَفَّارَةٌ لَهُ‌) [2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 3, p. 2188.

[^2]: Many commentators have also suggested another possibility in interpreting the verse: that the pronoun "lehu" (for him) refers to the criminal, meaning: "Whoever waives his right causes the retaliation to be removed from the criminal, and it serves as an expiation for the criminal's deed." However, the apparent meaning of the verse is what was mentioned above.

It should be noted that the pronoun "bihi" (it) refers to retaliation, as if he has made this retaliation a "charity" (gift) bestowed upon the criminal. The use of the term "tasaddaqa" (gave charity) and the promise of forgiveness that Allah has given to such a person are all to encourage pardon and forgiveness. There is no doubt that retaliation can never return what a person has lost; it only provides a kind of temporary peace. However, Allah's promise of forgiveness can completely compensate for what he has lost in another form, thereby removing the remaining distress from his heart and soul. This is the most eloquent encouragement for such individuals.

In a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, he said: "Whoever forgives, Allah forgives his sins to the same extent" [1]. This sentence is, in fact, a crushing response to those who consider the law of retaliation an unjust law that encourages a spirit of murder and mutilation. From the entirety of the verse, it is understood that the permission for retaliation is to create fear and dread in criminals, thereby ensuring the safety of innocent people's lives. At the same time, the path of forgiveness and return is also opened. By creating this state of fear and hope, Islam intends both to prevent crime and, as much as possible and appropriate, to prevent the washing of blood with blood.

At the end of the verse, He says: "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the wrongdoers."

(وَ مَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ‌).

What injustice is greater than being caught up in false emotions and sentiments, completely overlooking the murderer under the pretext that "blood should not be washed with blood,"

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 637.

thereby leaving the hands of murderers free for further killings and, in this way, committing injustice and oppression against innocent individuals?

It should be noted that in the current Torah, in Chapter 21 of the Book of Exodus, this ruling is also mentioned, where it says: "And if any mischief follow, then thou shalt give life for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot, burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe" (Exodus 21:23-25).

Ayah

وَقَفَّيْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِم بِعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْإِنجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدًى وَنُورٌ وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٦)
We followed them with Jesus son of Mary, to confirm that which was before him of the Torah, and We gave him the Evangel containing guidance and light, confirming what was before it of the Torah, and as guidance and advice for the Godwary. (46)

Commentary

Following the verses related to the Torah, these two verses refer to the status of the Gospel (Injil), stating: "Following the previous leaders and prophets, We sent Jesus عليه السلام, while his signs completely corresponded with the signs given by the Torah" (وَ قَفَّيْنا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ بِعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ‌ التورية). This sentence also has another interpretation: "Jesus عليه السلام testified to the truthfulness of the Torah which was revealed to Moses son of Imran, just as all heavenly prophets testified to the truthfulness of the preceding prophets."

Then it says: "We gave him the Gospel, in which was guidance and light" (وَ آتَيْناهُ الْإِنْجِيلَ فِيهِ هُدىً وَ نُورٌ).

In the Holy Quran, the "Torah," the "Gospel," and the "Quran" are all three referred to as "Light":

Regarding the Torah, We read: (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْراةَ فِيها هُدىً وَ نُورٌ) (Al-Ma'idah - 44).

Regarding the Gospel, the term "Light" was applied in the verse above, and regarding the Quran, We read: (قَدْ جاءَكُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ نُورٌ وَ كِتابٌ مُبِينٌ‌) (Al-Ma'idah - 15).

In reality, just as all creatures in the world have a desperate need for light to continue their life and existence, divine religions and the instructions of heavenly books are an absolute necessity for the growth and perfection of human beings.

Fundamentally, it has been proven that all energies, forces, movements, and beauties originate from light; if there were no light, silence and death would prevail everywhere. Similarly, if the teachings of the prophets did not exist, all human values—both individual and social—would fade into darkness, examples of which we clearly see in materialistic societies.

The Quran mentions the Torah and the Gospel as heavenly books in numerous instances. There is no doubt or hesitation that these two books were originally revealed by Allah. However, it is also certain that these two heavenly books were subjected to distortion (tahrif) after the time of the prophets; truths were removed from them, and superstitions were added, causing them to lose their value, or the original books were forgotten and other books containing only a portion of their truths took their place [1]. Therefore, the application of "Light" to these two books refers to the original Torah and Gospel.

Once again, as an emphasis, it stresses the point that "not only did Jesus son of Mary confirm the Torah, but the Gospel, his heavenly book, was also a witness to the truth of the Torah." (مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ).

And at the end, He says: "This heavenly book was a source of guidance and an admonition for the righteous." (وَ هُدىً وَ مَوْعِظَةً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌).

This expression is similar to the expression mentioned at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah regarding the Quran, where it says: هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ‌: "The Quran is a means of guidance for the righteous." Not only the Quran, but all heavenly books are such that they are a means of guidance for the righteous. By "the righteous" (muttaqin), it means those who are in search of the truth and are ready to accept it. It is obvious that those who, out of stubbornness and enmity, close the windows of their hearts to the truth will benefit from no reality.

It is noteworthy that in the above verse regarding the Gospel, first "فِيهِ هُدىً‌" (in it is guidance) is said, and then "هدى" (guidance) is mentioned absolutely. This difference in expression might be because in the Gospel and heavenly books, the evidences of guidance exist for everyone without exception, but for the righteous who contemplate them deeply, they become a cause for guidance, upbringing, and perfection.

[^1]: For more explanation regarding the distortion of the Torah and Gospel and its historical documents, refer to the books "Al-Huda ila Din al-Mustafa" and "Anis al-A'lam."

Ayah

وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ ٱلْإِنجِيلِ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فِيهِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَحْكُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (٤٧)
Let the people of the Evangel judge by what Allah has sent down in it. Those who do not judge by what Allah has sent down—it is they who are the transgressors. (47)

Commentary

Those Who Do Not Judge by Allah’s Law

Following the reference to the revelation of the Gospel in the previous verses, this verse states:

"We commanded the people of the Gospel to judge by what Allah has revealed in it."

(وَلْيَحْكُمْ أَهْلُ الْإِنْجِيلِ بِمَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ فِيهِ)

There is no doubt that the meaning of the above sentence is not that the Quran is commanding the followers of Christ عليه السلام to act according to the rulings of the Gospel right now; for such a statement would be inconsistent with other verses of the Quran and with the very existence of the Quran itself, which announces a new religion and the abrogation of the former one. Rather, the meaning is that after the revelation of the Gospel to Jesus عليه السلام, We commanded his followers to act upon it and to judge according to it [^1]. At the end of the verse, it emphasizes once more: "And whoever does not judge by what Allah has revealed—then it is those who are the rebellious (Fasiqun)."

(وَمَنْ لَمْ يَحْكُمْ بِمَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الْفاسِقُونَ)

It is noteworthy that in these recent verses, such individuals have been referred to in one instance as "disbelievers" (Kafirun), in another as "wrongdoers" (Zalimun), and here as "rebellious" (Fasiqun). This variation in expression may be because every judgment has three aspects: firstly, it relates to the Legislator (Allah); secondly, to the executors of the law (the ruler or judge); and thirdly, to the person against whom the judgment is executed (the convicted).

It seems that each of the three aforementioned expressions refers to one of these three aspects. For someone who judges contrary to Allah’s command has, on one hand, trampled upon Divine law and committed "disbelief" (Kufr); on the other hand, he has committed "wrong" and "oppression" (Zulm) against an innocent person; and on the third hand, he has stepped outside the boundaries of his duty and responsibility and become "rebellious" (Fasiq) (for as we have previously stated, "Fisq" means to step outside the boundaries of servitude and duty).

[^1]: In reality, as many commentators have stated, the word "We said" (Qulna) is implied here, and the meaning of the verse becomes: "And We said: Let the people of the Gospel judge..."

Ayah

وَأَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَمُهَيْمِنًا عَلَيْهِ ۖ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ عَمَّا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنَا مِنكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهَاجًا ۚ وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن لِّيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِى مَآ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٤٨)
We have sent down to you the Book with the truth, confirming what was before it of the Book and as a guardian over it. So judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires against the truth that has come to you. For each [community] among you We had appointed a code [of law] and a path, and had Allah wished He would have made you one community, but [His purposes required] that He should test you in respect to what He has given you. So take the lead in all good works. To Allah shall be the return of you all, whereat He will inform you concerning that about which you used to differ. (48)

Commentary

In this verse, the position of the Quran is mentioned after the discussion of the previous books of the Prophets.

"Muhaymin" (مهيمن) originally means something that is a protector, witness, observer, trustee, and guardian. Since the Quran maintains full oversight in preserving the principles of the previous Divine books and completes them, the term Muhaymin is applied to it. He says: "We have sent down to you this Divine Book in truth, confirming the previous books (as its signs correspond to what appeared in the previous books) and as a guardian and protector over them."

(وَ أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ مِنَ الْكِتابِ وَ مُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ‌).

Fundamentally, all Divine books are in harmony regarding the core principles and pursue a single goal, which is the cultivation and perfection of humanity, even if they differ in secondary matters according to the law of gradual evolution, where each new religion treads a higher stage and possesses a more comprehensive program.

The mention of "مُهَيْمِناً عَلَيْهِ‌" (a guardian over it) after "مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ‌" (confirming what was before it) refers to this very reality; meaning it confirms the principles of the previous books while simultaneously proposing a more comprehensive program.

Then He commands that since this is the case, judge between them according to the rulings that have been revealed to you.

(فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ‌).

This sentence is introduced with the "Fa of derivation" (فاء تفريع) and is the result of the comprehensiveness of Islamic rulings in relation to the rulings of previous religions. This command does not contradict what passed in the previous verses where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was given the choice between judging between them or leaving them to themselves; for this verse says: if you choose to judge between the People of the Book, judge according to the rulings of the Quran.

Afterward, He adds: do not follow their whims and desires—those who wish to adapt Divine rulings to their own inclinations and passions—and do not turn away from the truth that has been revealed to you.

(وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ عَمَّا جاءَكَ مِنَ الْحَقِ‌).

To complete this discussion, He says: "To each of you We prescribed a law, a legal system, a path, and an open way."

(لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَ مِنْهاجاً). "Shir'ah" (شرعة) and "Shari'ah" (شريعة) refer to a path that leads to water, and the reason religion is called Shari'ah is that it leads to truths and teachings that are the source of human purity, sanctity, and life. The words "Nahj" (نهج) and "Minhaj" (منهاج) refer to a clear and open path.

Ragheb in the book Al-Mufradat narrates from Ibn Abbas that he said: the difference between "Shir'ah" and "Minhaj" is that "Shir'ah" refers to what is contained in the Quran, and "Minhaj" refers to matters contained in the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (although this distinction seems interesting, there is no definitive evidence for it). [1] Then He says: "Allah could have made all people a single nation and made everyone follow one religion, but this would not have been consistent with the law of gradual evolution and the progression through different educational stages."

(وَ لَوْ شاءَ اللَّهُ لَجَعَلَكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَ لكِنْ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ فِي ما آتاكُمْ‌).

The phrase "ليبلوكم فيما آتاكم" (that He may test you in what He has given you) refers to what we previously stated: Allah has created talents and merits within human existence and nurtures them through "trials" in the light of the teachings of the Prophets. For this reason, after completing one stage, He places them in a higher stage, and after the end of one educational era, He brings about a more advanced era through another Prophet, exactly like the educational stages of a youth in school.

Finally, He addresses all peoples and nations, inviting them to race with one another in good deeds instead of wasting their energies on disagreement and contention.

(فاستبقوا الخيرات).

For the return of all of you is to Allah, and He is the One who will inform you on the Day of Resurrection regarding that in which you used to differ.

(إِلَى اللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ‌).

[1]: Some prominent commentators believe that "Din" (Religion) and "Shari'ah" (Law) differ from each other; Din consists of the principle of Monotheism and other principles common among all faiths, and therefore Din is always one. However, Shari'ah refers to the laws, rulings, and regulations that differ between faiths. Yet, we have no clear evidence for this distinction, as these two words are used interchangeably in many instances.

Ayahs

وَأَنِ ٱحْكُم بَيْنَهُم بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ وَٱحْذَرْهُمْ أَن يَفْتِنُوكَ عَنۢ بَعْضِ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَٱعْلَمْ أَنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَن يُصِيبَهُم بِبَعْضِ ذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَفَـٰسِقُونَ (٤٩)
Judge between them by what Allah has sent down, and do not follow their desires. Beware of them lest they should beguile you from part of what Allah has sent down to you. But if they turn their backs [on you], then know that Allah desires to punish them for some of their sins, and indeed many of the people are transgressors. (49)
أَفَحُكْمَ ٱلْجَـٰهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ ۚ وَمَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ حُكْمًا لِّقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ (٥٠)
Do they seek the judgement of [pagan] ignorance? But who is better than Allah in judgement for a people who have certainty? (50)

Occasion of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding the occasion of revelation for this verse as follows:

A group of Jewish leaders conspired and said, "Let us go to Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله); perhaps we can divert him from his religion." Following this collusion, they came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and said, "We are the scholars and nobles of the Jews, and if we follow you, surely the rest of the Jews will emulate us. However, there is a dispute between us and another group (concerning a murder or something else); if you judge in this dispute in our favor, we will believe in you." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) refrained from such a judgment (which was not just), and the above verse was revealed. [^1]

Commentary

In this verse, Allah once again emphasizes to His Prophet that he should judge among the People of the Book according to what Allah has revealed and not submit to their whims and desires.

(وَ أَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ وَ لا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ‌) "And judge between them by what Allah has revealed and do not follow their inclinations."

The repetition of this command is either due to the matters mentioned at the end of the verse, or because the subject of this judgment differed from the subject of the judgments in the previous verses. In the preceding verses, the subject was "adultery by a married person," whereas here the subject was "murder or another dispute."

Then He warns the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "They have conspired to divert you from the religion of truth and justice; be wary of them."

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 6, p. 421.

(وَ احْذَرْهُمْ أَنْ يَفْتِنُوكَ عَنْ بَعْضِ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ إِلَيْكَ‌) "And beware of them, lest they tempt you away from some of what Allah has revealed to you."

"And if the People of the Book do not submit to your just judgment, know that this is a sign that their sins have overtaken them and deprived them of success (tawfiq), and Allah intends to punish them for some of their sins."

(فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّما يُرِيدُ اللَّهُ أَنْ يُصِيبَهُمْ بِبَعْضِ ذُنُوبِهِمْ‌) "But if they turn away—then know that Allah only intends to afflict them for some of their sins."

The mention of "some" sins (rather than all of them) may be because the punishment for all sins is not carried out in the life of this world; only a portion of it afflicts man here, while the rest is deferred to the next world.

As for which "punishment" afflicted them, the verse does not explicitly mention it, but it likely refers to the fate that befell the Jews of Medina, who, due to their repeated betrayals, were forced to abandon their homes and leave Medina. Alternatively, their lack of success was itself a type of punishment for their previous sins, as the deprivation of success is considered a form of punishment. In other words, the penalty for continuous sin and stubbornness is being deprived of just laws and becoming lost in the labyrinths of life.

At the end of the verse, He says: "If they persist so much in the path of falsehood, do not be concerned, for many people are rebellious (fasiqun)."

(وَ إِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ لَفاسِقُونَ‌) "And indeed, many of the people are defiantly disobedient."

Question

An objection might be raised that the above verse suggests the possibility of deviating from the truth is conceivable for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and therefore Allah warns him. Is this expression consistent with the status of the infallibility (Ismah) of the Prophets?

Answer

Being infallible never means that sin becomes impossible for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) or the Imam; otherwise, it would not be considered a virtue for them. Rather, it means that while they have the power to sin, they do not commit sin, even if this refraining is due to Divine reminders. In other words, Allah's reminders are part of the factor that protects the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) from sin.

A more detailed explanation regarding the status of the infallibility of the Prophets and Imams will, God willing, be provided under the Verse of Purification (Ayah 33 of Surah Al-Ahzab).

In the next verse, by way of a rhetorical question, He says: "Do these people who claim to follow the Heavenly Books expect you to judge among them with the laws of ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and judgments mixed with various types of discrimination?"

(أَ فَحُكْمَ الْجاهِلِيَّةِ يَبْغُونَ‌) "Then is it the judgement of [the time of] ignorance they desire?"

While no judgment is higher or better for people of faith than the judgment of Allah.

(وَ مَنْ أَحْسَنُ مِنَ اللَّهِ حُكْماً لِقَوْمٍ يُوقِنُونَ‌) "But who is better than Allah in judgement for a people who are certain [in faith]?"

As we mentioned under the previous verses, there were also strange discriminations among the Jewish tribes. For example, if someone from the "Banu Qurayza" tribe killed someone from the "Banu Nadir" tribe, they would be subject to retaliation (qisas), but in the reverse case, they would not perform retaliation. Or when taking blood money (diyah), they would take double the amount. The Quran says such discriminations are signs of the laws of ignorance, and among Divine laws, there is no discrimination whatsoever among the servants of Allah.

In the book Al-Kafi, it is narrated from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), that he said:

(الحكم حكمان حكم اللَّه و حكم الجاهلية فمن اخطا حكم اللَّه حكم بحكم الجاهلية) "Judgment is of only two kinds: the judgment of Allah and the judgment of ignorance (Jahiliyyah). Whoever misses the judgment of Allah has judged by the judgment of ignorance." [^1]

From this, it becomes clear that Muslims who, despite having Heavenly laws, follow the man-made laws of other nations, have in fact stepped onto the path of ignorance.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 640.

Ayahs

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۘ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ وَمَن يَتَوَلَّهُم مِّنكُمْ فَإِنَّهُۥ مِنْهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥١)
O you who have faith! Do not take the Jews and the Christians for allies: they are allies of each other. Any of you who allies with them is indeed one of them. Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot. (51)
فَتَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِيهِمْ يَقُولُونَ نَخْشَىٰٓ أَن تُصِيبَنَا دَآئِرَةٌ ۚ فَعَسَى ٱللَّهُ أَن يَأْتِىَ بِٱلْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ فَيُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَسَرُّوا۟ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٥٢)
Yet you see those in whose hearts is a sickness rushing to them, saying, ‘We fear lest a turn of fortune should visit us.’ Maybe Allah will bring about a victory, or a command from Him, and then they will be regretful for what they kept secret in their hearts, (52)
وَيَقُولُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَهَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ٱلَّذِينَ أَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ إِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ ۚ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ خَـٰسِرِينَ (٥٣)
and the faithful will say, ‘Are these the ones who swore by Allah with solemn oaths that they were with you?!’ Their works have failed, and they have become losers. (53)

Circumstances of Revelation

Many commentators have narrated that after the Battle of Badr, Ubadah ibn al-Samit al-Khazraji came to the Prophet and said: "I have allies among the Jews who are numerous and possess great strength. Now that they are threatening us with war and the account of the Muslims has been separated from the non-Muslims, I declare my dissociation from their friendship and alliance; my only allies are Allah and His Messenger." Abdullah ibn Ubayy said: "But I do not dissociate from the alliance with the Jews, for I fear difficult events and I am in need of them." The Prophet ص told him: "That which I feared for Ubadah regarding friendship with the Jews, I fear for you as well (and the danger of this friendship and alliance is greater for you than for him)." Abdullah replied: "Since it is so, I also accept and will sever ties with them." The above verses were revealed, warning the Muslims against entering into alliances with the Jews and Christians.

Commentary

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 4; p. 409

The verses strictly warn Muslims against cooperation with the Jews and Christians. First, it says: "O you who have believed, do not take the Jews and the Christians as allies and confidants" (meaning that faith in Allah requires that you do not cooperate with them for the sake of gaining material interests).

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالنَّصَارَىٰ أَوْلِيَاءَ)

"Awliya" is the plural of "Wali," derived from the root "Wilayah," meaning extreme closeness between two things, which has been used to mean friendship, as well as alliance and guardianship.

However, considering the circumstances of revelation and other available evidence, the intention here is not that Muslims should have no commercial or social relations with Jews and Christians; rather, it means they should not become their allies or rely on their friendship against enemies.

The issue of "alliance" (Hilf) was fully prevalent among the Arabs at that time and was referred to as "Wala."

It is interesting that here the text does not rely on the title "People of the Book" (Ahl al-Kitab), but rather names them as "Jews and Christians." This perhaps suggests that if they had acted according to their own Heavenly Books, they would have been good allies for you; however, their unity with one another is not based on the commands of the Heavenly Books, but rather on political motives, racial groupings, and the like.

Then, with a short sentence, He states the reason for this prohibition, saying: "Each of those two groups are friends and allies of their own co-religionists."

(بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ بَعْضٍ)

Meaning, as long as their own interests and those of their friends are at stake, they will never attend to you.

In this regard, "whoever among you strikes up a friendship or alliance with them shall be considered one of them in terms of social and religious classification."

(وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ)

And there is no doubt that "Allah will not guide such wrongdoers who betray themselves and their Muslim brothers and sisters and rely on their enemies."

(إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ)

In the next verse, He refers to the excuses that those with diseased hearts choose to justify their illegitimate connections with foreigners, saying:

"Those in whose hearts is a disease insist on choosing them as their support and allies, and their excuse is that they say: 'We fear that a turn of fortune may strike us and we may fall into trouble.'"

(فَتَرَى الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ يُسَارِعُونَ فِيهِمْ يَقُولُونَ نَخْشَىٰ أَنْ تُصِيبَنَا دَائِرَةٌ) [1] The Quran, in response to them, says: "Just as they consider the possibility that one day power might fall into the hands of the Jews and Christians, they must also consider the possibility that 'perhaps eventually, Allah will grant victory to the Muslims and power will fall into their hands, and these hypocrites will become regretful over what they concealed within themselves.'"

(فَعَسَى اللَّهُ أَنْ يَأْتِيَ بِالْفَتْحِ أَوْ أَمْرٍ مِنْ عِنْدِهِ فَيُصْبِحُوا عَلَىٰ مَا أَسَرُّوا فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ نَادِمِينَ)

In reality, this verse responds to them in two ways: First, that such thoughts arise from diseased hearts and from those whose faith is shaky and who harbor ill-suspicions toward Allah; otherwise, a person of faith would not allow such thoughts. Second, even if such a possibility exists, is there no possibility of victory for the Muslims?

Based on what we have said, the word "Asa" (perhaps), which conveys the concept of probability and hope, remains in its original meaning as it is used everywhere. However, commentators usually

[1]: Da'irah is originally from the root dawr, meaning something that revolves. Since powers and governments are constantly in rotation throughout history, they are called "Da'irah," and likewise, the various events of life that revolve around individuals are called Da'irah.

take it here as a definitive promise from Allah to the Muslims, which is not consistent with the literal appearance of the word "Asa."

The meaning of the phrase "or a command from Him" (aw amrin min 'indihi) mentioned after the word "victory" (fath) is that in the future, Muslims may overcome their enemies either through "war and victory" or, without war, they may become so powerful that "the enemy surrenders without a fight." In other words, the word "fath" refers to the military victories of the Muslims, and "a command from Him" refers to social, economic victories, and the like.

However, considering that Allah expresses such a possibility and He is All-Knowing of the future state, this verse serves as an indication of the military, social, and economic victories of the Muslims.

In the final verse, He points to the end of the hypocrites' affair, saying: At that time when victory and triumph are granted to the true Muslims and the affair of the hypocrites is exposed, "the believers will say in astonishment: 'Are these hypocrites the same ones who made such claims and swore by Allah with their most solemn oaths that they were with us? Why did their end come to this?'"

(وَيَقُولُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَهَٰؤُلَاءِ الَّذِينَ أَقْسَمُوا بِاللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَانِهِمْ إِنَّهُمْ لَمَعَكُمْ) [1] And because of this very hypocrisy, "all their good deeds went to waste," because they did not spring from a pure and sincere intention, and "for this reason, they became losers," both in this world and in the hereafter.

(حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فَأَصْبَحُوا خَاسِرِينَ)

[1]: In the above verse, "Ha'ula'i" is the subject (mubtada), "alladhina aqsamu billah" is its predicate (khabar), and "jahda aymanihim" functions as a "cognate accusative" (maf'ul mutlaq).

In fact, the last sentence is like an answer to a hypothetical question; as if someone asks: "Ultimately, where will their end lead?" In response, it is said: "Their deeds went completely to waste and loss and ruin overtook them."

That is, even if they performed good deeds out of sincerity, because they eventually turned toward hypocrisy and polytheism, the results of those deeds also went to waste, just as we explained in Volume 2, page 69, under verse 217 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Relying on Foreigners

Although the circumstances of revelation for the above verses mention two individuals, namely "Ubadah ibn al-Samit" and "Abdullah ibn Ubayy," there is no doubt that they are not intended merely as two historical figures. Rather, they represent two schools of thought and social outlooks. One school says: "One must sever ties with the foreigner, not hand over the reins of one's affairs to him, and not trust in his assistance."

The other says: "In this tumultuous world, every person and nation needs a support, and sometimes expediency dictates that this support be chosen from among foreigners; their friendship is valuable and will one day bear fruit."

The Quran severely strikes down the second school and explicitly and emphatically warns Muslims against this way of thinking. Unfortunately, however, some Muslims have consigned this great command of the Quran to oblivion and have chosen supports for themselves from among foreigners. History shows that many of the misfortunes of Muslims have originated from this very point! "Andalusia" is a living tableau for this subject, showing how Muslims, through their own power, created the most brilliant civilizations in yesterday's Andalusia and today's Spain, yet how easily they lost it because of relying on the foreigner.

The great Ottoman Empire, which melted away completely like snow in the summer season in a short period, is another witness to this claim. In contemporary history as well, the blows that Muslims have suffered due to deviating from this school are not few. But the wonder is how we have not yet awakened! In any case, a foreigner is a foreigner; even if one day they have common interests with us and cooperate in limited steps, eventually, at sensitive moments, they will not only separate their account but will also deal us fatal blows. Today, Muslims must listen to this call of the Quran more than ever and rely on nothing but their own strength.

The Prophet of Islam ص was so vigilant regarding this matter that during the Battle of Uhud, when three hundred Jews declared their readiness to cooperate with the Muslims against the polytheists, the Prophet ص turned them back halfway and did not accept their help, even though this number could have played an effective role in the Battle of Uhud. Why? Because it was not at all improbable that they might cooperate with the "enemy" at sensitive moments of the battle and destroy the remainder of the army of Islam.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مَن يَرْتَدَّ مِنكُمْ عَن دِينِهِۦ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِى ٱللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ يُحِبُّهُمْ وَيُحِبُّونَهُۥٓ أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ يُجَـٰهِدُونَ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَا يَخَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لَآئِمٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ فَضْلُ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٤)
O you who have faith! Should any of you desert his religion, Allah will soon bring a people whom He loves and who love Him, [who will be] humble towards the faithful, stern towards the faithless, waging jihad in the way of Allah, not fearing the blame of any blamer. That is Allah’s grace, which He grants to whomever He wishes, and Allah is all-bounteous, all-knowing. (54)

Commentary

Following the discussion regarding the hypocrites, the text addresses the apostates who, according to the Quranic prediction, would later turn away from this sacred religion. As a general rule, it warns all Muslims: "If any of you turn back from your religion, you shall not harm Allah, His religion, the Muslim community, or the rapid pace of their progress; for in the future, Allah will raise a people to support this faith."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مَنْ يَرْتَدَّ مِنْكُمْ عَنْ دِينِهِ فَسَوْفَ يَأْتِي اللَّهُ بِقَوْمٍ)

Then, He describes the qualities of those who must fulfill this great mission as follows:

  1. They love Allah and think of nothing but His pleasure: "He loves them and they love Him." (يُحِبُّهُمْ وَ يُحِبُّونَهُ).

  2. and 3. "They are humble and kind toward the believers, and stern, tough, and powerful against the enemies and oppressors." (أَذِلَّةٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَعِزَّةٍ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ).

  3. Jihad in the way of Allah is a continuous part of their program. (يُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ).

  4. The final distinction mentioned for them is that in the path of executing Allah's command and defending the truth, they fear the blame of no critic. (وَ لا يَافُونَ لَوْمَةَ لائِمٍ).

In truth, in addition to physical strength, they possess such courage that they have no fear of breaking false traditions or opposing majorities that have taken the path of deviation and mock others by relying on their numerical superiority.

We know many individuals who possess excellent qualities, yet they are very conservative, fearful, and lacking in boldness when faced with the clamor of the environment and the onslaught of the thoughts of the common folk and deviant majorities; they quickly abandon the field before them. However, for a transformative leader and the individuals who enter the field to implement his ideas, such courage is necessary above all else. Being "mass-oriented," "environment-oriented," and the like—all of which are the opposite of this sublime spiritual distinction—are considered obstacles to most reforms.

And at the end, it says: "Attaining these distinctions (in addition to human effort) is indebted to the Divine Grace, which He grants to whomever He wills and deems worthy." (ذلِكَ فَضْلُ اللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

It is He whose circle of grace and generosity is vast, and He is aware of those who possess the merit. (وَ اللَّهُ واسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ).

Regarding whom the above verse refers to and who these helpers of Islam are—whom Allah has praised with these attributes—there is much discussion in Islamic narrations and the words of commentators.

In many narrations transmitted through both Shi'a and Sunni channels, we read that this verse was revealed concerning Ali عليه السلام during the conquest of Khaybar, or his struggle against the "Nakithin," "Qasitin," and "Mariqin" (those who ignited the Battle of Jamal, the army of Mu'awiyah, and the Kharijites). Thus, we see that after several commanders of the Islamic army were unable to conquer Khaybar, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله turned to them one night in the center of the Islamic camp and said:

"I will certainly give the standard tomorrow to a man who loves Allah and His Messenger, and whom Allah and His Messenger love; one who attacks repeatedly and never flees, and he will not return until Allah grants victory at his hands."

"By Allah, I will entrust the flag tomorrow to someone who loves Allah and the Prophet, and Allah and the Prophet also love him. He attacks the enemy continuously and never flees from them, and he will not return from this field unless Allah grants victory to the Muslims through his hand." [1]

In another narration, we read that when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked about this verse,

[1]: In Tafsir al-Burhan and Nur al-Thaqalayn, several narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt have been cited in this regard, and the Sunni scholar "Thalabi" has also narrated these reports (refer to the book Ihqaq al-Haq, Vol. 3, p. 200).

he tapped his hand on the shoulder of "Salman" and said: "It is this man and his companions and his compatriots."

In this way, he prophesied the conversion of the Persians to Islam and their fruitful efforts and endeavors for the progress of Islam in various fields.

Then he said: "If the religion (and in another narration, if knowledge) were suspended at the Pleiades (the star Thurayya), men from the sons of Persia would surely attain it." [1]

In other narrations, we read that this verse was revealed concerning the companions of al-Mahdi عليه السلام, who stand with all their might against those who have apostatized from the religion of truth and justice, and they fill the world with faith, equity, and justice.

There is no doubt that these narrations mentioned in the commentary of the verse do not contradict one another. For this verse, as is the style of the Quran, expresses a general and comprehensive concept of which Ali عليه السلام or Salman al-Farsi are prominent examples, and it also includes others who follow these programs, even if they are not mentioned in the narrations.

However, unfortunately, ethnic prejudices have come into play regarding this verse, and individuals who possess no merit and in whom none of the above attributes existed have been counted as the examples and the reason for the verse's revelation. This went so far that even Abu Musa al-Ash'ari—who,

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 3, p. 208; Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 642. Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani in al-Hilyah, Vol. 6, p. 64, narrated the hadith with the wording: "If knowledge were tied to the Pleiades, men from the sons of Persia would attain it." However, Ibn Abd al-Barr in al-Isti'ab, Vol. 2, p. 577, narrated: "If the religion were at the Pleiades, Salman would have reached it..."

with his rare and historical folly, dragged Islam toward a precipice and placed the standard-bearer of Islam, Ali عليه السلام, in a severe predicament—has been counted among the examples of this verse! [1]

The revision of the latter part of this volume took place in the vicinity of the House of Allah, in Makkah al-Mukarramah, during the honor of performing the magnificent Umrah rites, while I could hardly hold the pen and my hand was uncomfortable.

Interestingly, the same prejudices we see in scholarly books are observed here in an even more intense manner among the common people and even their scholars. It is as if a hand is at work to ensure Muslims never unite. This prejudice has even spread to pre-Islamic history; the street near the Kaaba that draws attention as "Abu Sufyan Street" is currently more magnificent than "Ibrahim al-Khalil Street," the founder of Makkah!

Attributing "shirk" (polytheism) to many Muslims is as easy as drinking water for a group of fanatics in this region. If you move, the cry of "Mushrik! Mushrik!" (Polytheist!) rises. It is as if Islam belongs exclusively to them and they are the sole custodians of the Quran, and the Islam or disbelief of others has been left to their whim, so they may call anyone they wish a polytheist and anyone they wish a Muslim with a single word! This is while we read in the above verses that during the isolation of Islam, Allah raises Salman and his like for the greatness of this grand religion, and this is a glad tiding given by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

[1]: Tafsir al-Tabari, Vol. 6, p. 184. However, in some narrations, only the people of Abu Musa are mentioned, referring to the people of Yemen who strove to aid Islam at critical moments. By implication, Abu Musa himself is excluded from this matter, whereas regarding Salman, we read: he himself and his people are included in this verse.

It is astonishing that the issue of "Tawhid" (Monotheism), which should be the secret to the unity of Muslims, has become a pretext for the fragmentation of Muslim ranks and for accusing Muslims of polytheism and idolatry.

It reached the point where an informed individual told some of their fanatics: "Look at where things have ended up for us and you—if Israel were to dominate us, a group of you would be happy, and if it were to crush you, a group of us would be! Is this not exactly what they want?!"

However, in fairness, through repeated contact with a number of their scholars, it became clear that the enlightened ones are often upset by this situation. Specifically, one of the scholars of Yemen, in the Masjid al-Haram, during a discussion regarding the boundaries of shirk in the presence of some senior teachers of the Haram, said: "The issue of attributing shirk to the People of the Qibla is a very great sin that the predecessors considered very grave. What is this practice where unqualified individuals constantly accuse people of shirk? Do they not know what a great responsibility they are undertaking?"

Ayah

إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُمْ رَٰكِعُونَ (٥٥)
Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful who maintain the prayer and give the zakat while bowing down. (55)

Circumstances of Revelation of the Verse of Wilayah

In Tafsir al-Mizan and other books, it is narrated from "Abdullah ibn Abbas" as follows:

One day, he was sitting by the Well of Zamzam, narrating Hadiths from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to the people. Suddenly, a man wearing a turban who had covered his face approached. Every time Ibn Abbas narrated a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), this man would also narrate another Hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) starting with the phrase "The Messenger of Allah said."

Ibn Abbas adjured him to identify himself. He uncovered his face and called out: "O people! Whoever does not know me, let him know that I am Abu Dharr al-Ghifari. I heard with these two ears of mine from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله)—and may both my ears become deaf if I lie—and I saw with these two eyes of mine—and may both become blind if I lie—that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said:

عَلِيٌّ قَائِدُ الْبَرَرَةِ وَ قَاتِلُ الْكَفَرَةِ مَنْصُورٌ مَنْ نَصَرَهُ مَخْذُولٌ مَنْ خَذَلَهُ.

'Ali (عليه السلام) is the leader of the righteous and the slayer of the disbelievers; victorious is he who helps him, and forsaken is he who abandons him.'

Then Abu Dharr added: 'O people, one day I was praying with the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the mosque. A beggar entered the mosque and asked the people for help, but no one gave him anything. He raised his hands toward the sky and said: "O Allah, be witness that I asked for help in the mosque of Your Messenger, but no one gave me a favorable response." At that moment, Ali (عليه السلام), who was in the state of Ruku' (bowing), signaled with the small finger of his right hand. The beggar approached and took the ring off his finger. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), who was in the state of prayer, observed this incident. When he finished his prayer, he raised his head toward the sky and said: "O Allah! My brother Moses asked You to expand his breast, to make his affairs easy for him, and to untie the knot from his tongue so that people might understand his speech. Moses also requested that You appoint Aaron, his brother, as his vizier and helper, to strengthen his power through him and make him a partner in his affairs.

O Allah! I am Muhammad, Your Prophet and Your chosen one; expand my breast and make my affairs easy for me, and appoint Ali (عليه السلام) from my family as my vizier, so that through him, my back may be strengthened and made firm."'

Abu Dharr says: 'The Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) prayer had not yet ended when Gabriel descended and said to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Read." The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked: "What shall I read?" He said: "Read: إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا..."'

Of course, this circumstance of revelation has been narrated through various chains (as will be mentioned), which sometimes differ in details and specifics, but the essence and core of all of them are the same.

Commentary

This verse begins with the word "Innama" (إِنَّما), which in the Arabic language denotes exclusivity (restriction), and it says: "Your Wali (guardian), master, and the one who has authority over your affairs are only three:

Allah, His Messenger, and those who have believed—those who establish prayer and give Zakat while they are in the state of Ruku'."

(إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَ رَسُولُهُ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ وَ يُؤْتُونَ الزَّكاةَ وَ هُمْ راكِعُونَ).

There is no doubt that "Ruku'" in this verse refers to the bowing in prayer, not to humility (khudu'). This is because in the terminology of the Sharia and the idiom of the Quran, when Ruku' is mentioned, it refers to its well-known meaning, which is the bowing in prayer. Furthermore, in addition to the circumstances of revelation and numerous narrations regarding Ali (عليه السلام) giving the ring while in Ruku'—which we will explain in detail—the mention of the phrase "establish prayer" (يُقِيمُونَ الصَّلاةَ) is also evidence for this subject. We have no instance in the Quran where it is expressed that Zakat should be given with "humility"; rather, it must be given with sincerity of intention and without reminding the recipient of the favor.

Likewise, there is no doubt that the word "Wali" in the verse does not mean "friend" or "helper." This is because Wilayah in the sense of friendship and helping is not exclusive to those who pray and give Zakat while in Ruku'; rather, it is a general ruling that encompasses all Muslims. All Muslims must love and help one another, even those upon whom Zakat is not obligatory and who essentially have nothing to give as Zakat, let alone wanting to pay Zakat while in Ruku'. They too must be friends and helpers to one another.

From this, it becomes clear that the meaning of "Wali" in the above verse is Wilayah in the sense of guardianship, authority, and material and spiritual leadership. This is especially true since this Wilayah is placed in the same rank as the Wilayah of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Wilayah of Allah, and all three are expressed in a single sentence.

Thus, the verse is among those that serve as a Quranic text (Nass) indicating the Wilayah and Imamate of Ali (عليه السلام).

However, there are important discussions here that must be examined separately:

Testimony of Hadiths, Commentators, and Historians

As we mentioned, in many Islamic books and Sunni sources, numerous narrations have been recorded stating that the above verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام). Some of these mention the incident of giving the ring while in Ruku', while others do not, sufficing only with the revelation of the verse regarding Ali (عليه السلام).

This narration has been reported by "Ibn Abbas," "Ammar ibn Yasir," "Abdullah ibn Salam," "Salamah ibn Kuhayl," "Anas ibn Malik," "Utbah ibn Hakim," "Abdullah Ubayy," "Abdullah ibn Ghalib," "Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari," and "Abu Dharr al-Ghifari." [^1] In addition to the ten individuals mentioned above, this narration has also been reported from Ali (عليه السلام) himself in Sunni books. [^2] Interestingly, in the book Ghayat al-Maram, 24 Hadiths are narrated on this subject from Sunni chains and 19 Hadiths from Shia chains. [^3] The well-known books in which this Hadith is narrated exceed thirty, all of which are from Sunni sources. These include Muhibb al-Din al-Tabari in Dhakha'ir al-Uqba (p. 88), Allamah Qadi al-Shawkani in the Tafsir Fath al-Qadir (vol. 2, p. 50), in Jami' al-Usul (vol. 9, p. 478), in Asbab al-Nuzul by al-Wahidi (p. 148), in Lubab al-Nuqul by al-Suyuti (p. 90), in Tadhkirah by Sibt ibn al-Jawzi (p. 18), in Nur al-Absar by al-Shablanji (p. 105), in Tafsir al-Tabari (p. 165), in the book al-Kafi al-Shaf by Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani (p. 56), in Mafatih al-Ghayb by al-Razi (vol. 3, p. 431), in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur (vol. 2, p. 393), in the book Kanz al-Ummal (vol. 6, p. 391), Musnad Ibn Mardawayh, Musnad Ibn al-Shaykh, and in addition to these, in Sahih al-Nasa'i, the book al-Jam' bayna al-Sihah al-Sittah, and numerous other books. [^4]

Given this, how can one ignore all these Hadiths, while for the circumstances of revelation of other verses, they suffice with one or two narrations? It seems, however, that prejudice does not allow all these narrations and the testimonies of scholars regarding the revelation of the above verse to be taken into consideration.

If it were permissible to ignore all these narrations in the interpretation of a Quranic verse, we would essentially have to ignore every narration in the interpretation of Quranic verses, for there are few verses in the Quran regarding whose revelation so many narrations have been recorded.

This matter was so clear and manifest that "Hassan ibn Thabit," the famous poet of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) era, included the content of the above narration in his poems composed about Ali (عليه السلام) as follows:

You are the one who gave, while you were bowing, Zakat—may my soul be sacrificed for you, O best of those who bow. So Allah revealed concerning you the best Wilayah, And clarified it within the firm laws (of the Quran). [^5]

Answer to Eight Objections of Opponents Regarding the Verse of Wilayah

A group of biased Sunni scholars insists on raising several objections to the revelation of this verse regarding Ali (عليه السلام) and to the interpretation of "Wilayah" as "guardianship, authority, and Imamate." We will present and examine the most important of these below:

1- Among the objections raised regarding the revelation of the verse concerning Ali (عليه السلام) is that the verse, given the word "alladhina" (الَّذِينَ) which is for the plural, cannot be applied to a single individual. In other words, the verse says: "Your Wali are those who establish prayer and give Zakat while in Ruku'." How can this expression be applied to one person like Ali (عليه السلام)?

Answer

In Arabic literature, it is frequently seen that a plural term is used to refer to a singular subject. For instance, in the Verse of Mubahala, we see that the word "nisa'ana" (our women) is in the plural form, while according to numerous circumstances of revelation, it refers to Fatima al-Zahra (عليه السلام). Similarly, "anfusana" (ourselves) is plural, yet among the men besides the Prophet, there was no one in that event except Ali (عليه السلام). In verse 172 of Surah Al-Imran, regarding the story of the Battle of Uhud, we read:

الَّذِينَ قالَ لَهُمُ النَّاسُ إِنَّ النَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا لَكُمْ فَاخْشَوْهُمْ فَزادَهُمْ إِيماناً.

And as we mentioned in the commentary of this verse in Volume 3, some commentators consider its revelation to be about Nu'aym ibn Mas'ud, who was but a single individual.

Likewise, in verse 52 of Surah Al-Ma'idah, we read: "يَقُولُونَ نَخْشى‌ أَنْ تُصِيبَنا دائِرَةٌ" while the verse was revealed regarding Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the commentary of which has passed. Similarly, in the first verse of Surah Al-Mumtahanah, verse 8 of Surah Al-Munafiqun, and verses 215 and 274 of Surah Al-Baqarah, expressions are seen that are generally in the plural form, but according to what has been recorded regarding their revelation, the intended subject was a single individual.

This expression is either to highlight the importance of that individual's position and the effective role they played in that action, or it is to present the ruling in a general form, even if its manifestation is limited to one person. In many verses of the Quran, the plural pronoun is used for Allah, who is One and Unique, as a form of glorification (magnification).

Of course, it cannot be denied that using a plural term for a singular subject is, technically, contrary to the literal appearance (dhahir) and is not permissible without a context (qarinah). However, with the existence of all those narrations regarding the revelation of the verse, we have a clear context for such an interpretation, and even in other cases, less evidence than this is deemed sufficient.

2- Fakhr al-Razi and some other biased critics have objected: How could Ali (عليه السلام), with that special focus he had during prayer and being immersed in supplication to the Lord (to the extent that it is famous they pulled an arrowhead from his foot and he did not notice), possibly hear the voice of a beggar and pay attention to him?

Answer: Those who raise this objection overlook the point that hearing the voice of a beggar and attending to his help is not "attention to oneself," but rather it is the very "attention to Allah." Ali (عليه السلام) was oblivious to himself during prayer, not to Allah. We know that being oblivious to Allah's creation is being oblivious to Allah. To put it more clearly: paying Zakat during prayer is performing an act of worship within an act of worship, not performing a permissible (mubah) worldly act within worship. To put it another way: what is incompatible with the spirit of worship is attention to matters related to material and personal life; however, attention to that which is in the path of Allah's pleasure is perfectly compatible with the spirit of worship and reinforces it. It is also necessary to mention that being "immersed in attention to Allah" does not mean that a person involuntarily loses their senses; rather, they voluntarily withdraw their attention from whatever is not in the way of Allah or for Allah.

Interestingly, Fakhr al-Razi's prejudice reached such a point that he considered Ali's (عليه السلام) signal to the beggar to come and take the ring from his finger himself as an instance of "excessive action" (fi'l kathir) which invalidates the prayer. This is while in prayer, there are actions a person is permitted to perform that are far more significant than this signal and yet do not harm the prayer—to the extent that killing insects like snakes and scorpions, or picking up and putting down a child, and even breastfeeding a nursing infant, have not been considered "excessive action." How did a single signal become "excessive action"? But when a scholar is caught in the storm of prejudice, such mistakes are not surprising!

3- Another objection made against the verse concerns the meaning of the word "Wali," which they have taken to mean "friend," "helper," and the like, rather than "guardian," "master," and "one with authority."

Answer: As we mentioned in the commentary of the verse above, the word "Wali" here cannot mean "friend" or "helper," because this attribute is established for all believers, not just the specific believers mentioned in the verse who establish prayer and give Zakat while in Ruku'. In other words, friendship and helping is a general ruling, whereas the verse is concerned with stating a specific ruling; therefore, after mentioning faith, it describes specific attributes that apply to a particular individual.

4- They say: What obligatory Zakat did Ali (عليه السلام) owe, given that he had not accumulated worldly wealth for himself? And if the intention is recommended charity (sadaqah), that is not called "Zakat"?!

Answer: Firstly, by the testimony of history, Ali (عليه السلام) acquired significant wealth through his own labor and gave it in the way of Allah, to the extent that it is written he manumitted a thousand slaves from his own earnings. Furthermore, his share of the spoils of war was also considerable. Therefore, a small saving to which Zakat might apply, or a small palm grove for which it would be obligatory to pay Zakat, was not something significant that Ali (عليه السلام) would lack. We also know that the immediacy of the obligation to pay Zakat is a "customary immediacy" (fawriyyat urfiyyah) which is not incompatible with performing a prayer.

Secondly, the application of the term "Zakat" to "recommended Zakat" (sadaqah) is frequent in the Holy Quran. In many Meccan Surahs, the word "Zakat" appears where the intended meaning is recommended Zakat, because the obligation of Zakat certainly occurred after the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) migration to Medina (Verse 3 of Surah al-Naml, Verse 39 of Surah al-Rum, Verse 4 of Surah Luqman, Verse 7 of Surah Fussilat, and others).

5- They say: If we believe in the immediate caliphate of Ali (عليه السلام), we must ultimately accept that it pertained to the time after the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Therefore, Ali (عليه السلام) was not a "Wali" on that day. In other words, the Wilayah on that day was "potential" (bil-quwwah) for him, not "actual" (bil-fi'l), whereas the literal appearance of the verse suggests an "actual" Wilayah.

Answer: In everyday speech and literary expressions, it is very common for a name or title to be given to individuals who possess it potentially. For example, a person during their lifetime makes a will and appoints someone as their "executor" (wasi) and "guardian" (qayyim) of their children. From that very moment, the title of executor and guardian is applied to that person, even though the party is still alive and has not died. In the narrations regarding Ali (عليه السلام) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) reported through both Shia and Sunni chains, we read that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) addressed him as his "executor" (wasi) and "successor" (khalifah), even though neither of these titles was active during the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) lifetime. In the Holy Quran, such expressions are also seen; for instance, regarding Zechariah, we read that he requested from Allah:

فَهَبْ لِي مِنْ لَدُنْكَ وَلِيًّا يَرِثُنِي وَ يَرِثُ مِنْ آلِ يَعْقُوبَ [^6]

While it is certain that the meaning of "Wali" here was guardianship for after his death. Many people appoint their successor during their lifetime and call them by the name "successor" from that time, even though it has a potential aspect.

6- They say: Why did Ali (عليه السلام) not personally argue using this clear evidence?

Answer: As we read in the discussion regarding the narrations concerning the revelation of the verse, this Hadith has been narrated in numerous books from Ali (عليه السلام) himself, including in Musnad Ibn Mardawayh, Abi al-Shaykh, and Kanz al-Ummal. This is, in fact, equivalent to the Imam's argumentation using this noble verse.

In the precious book al-Ghadir, a detailed Hadith is narrated from the book of Sulaym ibn Qays al-Hilali that Ali (عليه السلام) at the field of Siffin, in the presence of the crowd, brought several proofs to establish his rightfulness, among which was the argument using this very verse. [^7] And in the book Ghayat al-Maram, it is narrated from Abu Dharr that Ali (عليه السلام) also argued using this verse on the day of the Shura (Consultation). [^8]

7- They say: This "interpretation" is not consistent with the preceding and succeeding verses, because in them, Wilayah is used in the sense of "friendship."

Answer: We have said many times that because the verses of the Quran were revealed gradually and during different events, they always have a connection to the incidents in the context of which they were revealed. It is not the case that the verses of a Surah or verses placed one after another always have a close connection in terms of concept and content. Thus, it often happens that two verses were revealed consecutively but regarding two different incidents, and their paths diverge due to their connection to those incidents.

Given that the verse إِنَّما وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ, by the testimony of its circumstances of revelation, was revealed regarding Ali (عليه السلام) giving Zakat while in Ruku', and the past and future verses—as we have read and will read—were revealed in other incidents, we cannot rely too heavily on their connection.

Furthermore, the verse under discussion happens to be consistent with the preceding and succeeding verses as well, because in them the talk is of Wilayah in the sense of helping and support, and in the verse under discussion, the talk is of Wilayah in the sense of leadership and authority. There is no doubt that the person who is the Wali, guardian, and authority will also be the helper and supporter of his followers. In other words, being a helper and supporter is one of the functions of absolute Wilayah.

8- They say: Where did Ali (عليه السلام) get a ring of such an exorbitant price as written in history?! Furthermore, is wearing a ring of such an exceptionally high price not considered extravagance (israf)? Do these not prove the incorrectness of the above interpretation?

Answer: The exaggerations made regarding the price of that ring are completely baseless, and we have no acceptable evidence for that ring being expensive. The mention in a weak narration [^9] that its price was equivalent to the tax (kharaj) of Syria is more akin to a legend than reality, and perhaps it was forged to make the core of the matter appear worthless. In the authentic and reliable narrations mentioned regarding the revelation of the verse, there is no trace of this legend. Therefore, one cannot obscure a historical reality with such talk.

[^1]: Refer to the book Ihqaq al-Haqq, Volume 2, pages 399 to 410. [^2]: Refer to the book al-Muraja'at, page 155. [^3]: Minhaj al-Bara'ah, Volume 2, page 350. [^4]: For more information, refer to the books Ihqaq al-Haqq (Vol. 2), al-Ghadir (Vol. 2), and al-Muraja'at. [^6]: Maryam: 5. [^7]: al-Ghadir, Volume 1, page 196. [^8]: Narrated from Minhaj al-Bara'ah, Volume 2, page 363. [^9]: This weak narration appears in a disconnected (mursal) form in Tafsir al-Burhan, Volume 1, page 485.

Ayah

وَمَن يَتَوَلَّ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٥٦)
Whoever takes for his guardians Allah, His Apostle and the faithful [should know that] the confederates of Allah are indeed the victorious. (56)

Commentary

This verse serves as a completion of the theme of the previous verse, emphasizing and pursuing its objective. It declares to the Muslims: "Those who accept the Wilayah (guardianship), authority, and leadership of Allah, His Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the believers mentioned in the previous verse shall be victorious, for they will be part of the Party of Allah, and the Party of Allah is victorious."

(وَ مَنْ يَتَوَلَّ اللَّهَ وَ رَسُولَهُ وَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فَإِنَّ حِزْبَ اللَّهِ هُمُ الْغالِبُونَ‌).

In this verse, another piece of evidence is found regarding the meaning of Wilayah mentioned in the previous verse—namely "guardianship, leadership, and authority"—because the expression "Party of Allah" (Hizbullah) and its "victory" relates to Islamic governance, not a simple and ordinary friendship. This itself demonstrates that Wilayah in the verse means guardianship, governance, and the leadership of Islam and the Muslims, as the meaning of "party" implies a type of organization, solidarity, and assembly to secure common goals.

It must be noted that the meaning of "those who believe" (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا) in this verse does not refer to all believers, but rather to the specific individual referred to in the previous verse with distinct qualities.

Is the victory of the "Party of Allah" mentioned in this verse limited to spiritual victory, or does it encompass every kind of material and spiritual victory?

There is no doubt that the absolute nature of the verse is evidence of their absolute victory on all fronts. Truly, if a group is part of the Party of Allah—meaning they possess firm faith, piety, righteous deeds, complete unity and solidarity, and sufficient awareness and readiness—they will undoubtedly be victorious in all fields. If we see that Muslims today have not achieved such victory, the reason is clear: the conditions for membership in the Party of Allah mentioned above are not found in many of them. For this very reason, the powers and forces they should utilize to repel the enemy and solve social problems are often used to weaken one another.

In verse 22 of Surah Al-Mujadila, some of the characteristics of the Party of Allah are also mentioned, which, God willing, will be discussed in the commentary of that Surah.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَٱلْكُفَّارَ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٥٧)
