O you who have faith! Do not take those who take your religion in derision and play, from among those who were given the Book before you, and the infidels, as friends, and be wary of Allah, should you be faithful. (57)
وَإِذَا نَادَيْتُمْ إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ٱتَّخَذُوهَا هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لَّا يَعْقِلُونَ (٥٨)
When you call to prayer, they take it in derision and play. That is because they are a people who do not exercise their reason. (58)

Circumstances of Revelation

It has been narrated in the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Abu al-Futuh Razi, and Fakhr Razi that two individuals from the polytheists named "Rifa'ah" and "Suwayd" outwardly professed Islam but subsequently joined the ranks of the hypocrites. Some Muslims continued to associate with them and express friendship toward them. The above verses were revealed, warning them to refrain from such actions. (It becomes clear here that if this verse speaks of Wilayah in the sense of friendship—rather than guardianship and authority as mentioned in the preceding verses—it is because these verses have a separate circumstance of revelation from those, and one cannot be taken as a definitive context for the other).

Regarding the circumstance of revelation for the second verse, which follows the first, it is narrated that a group of Jews and some Christians, upon hearing the voice of the Mu'adhin (caller to prayer) or seeing the Muslims standing for prayer, would begin to mock and ridicule them. The Quran cautioned the Muslims against establishing ties of friendship with such individuals.

Commentary

In this verse, Allah once again commands the believers to avoid choosing hypocrites and enemies as friends. However, to stir their emotions and draw attention to the philosophy behind this ruling, He states: "O you who have believed, do not take as allies those who take your religion in ridicule and amusement—whether from among those who were given the Scripture before you or the disbelievers and hypocrites—do not choose any of them as friends."

(يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَتَّخِذُوا الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا دِينَكُمْ هُزُوًا وَلَعِبًا مِنَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتَابَ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَالْكُفَّارَ أَوْلِيَاءَ).

At the end of the verse, He emphasizes the matter with the phrase: "And fear Allah, if you should be believers" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ), meaning that establishing friendship with them is incompatible with piety (Taqwa) and faith.

It should be noted that Huzu' (mockery) refers to mocking words or gestures performed to portray a subject as worthless. As Raghib states in his book Al-Mufradat, it is mostly applied to joking and ridicule done in the absence of or behind the back of another, although it is occasionally and rarely applied to jokes and mockery performed in one's presence.

La'ib (play/amusement) usually refers to actions performed without a sound purpose or entirely without a goal; the play of children is called La'ib for this very reason.

In the second verse, following the previous discussion regarding the prohibition of friendship with hypocrites and a group of the People of the Book who ridiculed the rulings of Islam, it points to one of their actions as evidence and witness, stating: "And when you call to prayer, they take it in ridicule and amusement" (وَإِذا نادَيْتُمْ إِلَى الصَّلاةِ اتَّخَذُوها هُزُواً وَ لَعِباً). [^1] Then, it explains the reason for their behavior: "That is because they are a people who do not understand" (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَوْمٌ لا يَعْقِلُونَ‌), and they are far from comprehending realities.

[^1]: There is a discussion among commentators as to whether the pronoun in ittakhadhuha (take it) refers to the prayer (Salah) or the call (Munadah/Adhan). From the circumstances of revelation mentioned earlier, both possibilities can be inferred, as the hypocrites and disbelievers mocked both the soul-nourishing call of the Adhan and the prayer itself. However, the apparent wording of the verse more strongly supports the possibility that the pronoun refers to the prayer (Salah).

The Adhan: The Great Anthem of Islam

Every nation in every age and time has had a slogan or anthem to stir the emotions and feelings of its members and to call them to their individual and social duties. This subject is seen even more extensively in today's world.

Christians, in the past and present, call their followers to church by ringing the discordant sound of bells. However, in Islam, the slogan of Adhan is used for this invitation, which is far more eloquent and effective. The attractiveness and pull of this Islamic anthem are such that, according to the author of Tafsir al-Manar, even some fanatical Christians, when they hear the Islamic Adhan, confess to the depth and greatness of its impact on the psyche of the listeners.

The aforementioned author then narrates that in one of the cities of Egypt, a group of Christians was seen gathering at the time of the Muslims' Adhan just to hear this heavenly melody.

What anthem could be more eloquent than this, which begins with the name of the Great God, rises to the Oneness and Uniqueness of the Creator of the world and the testimony to the messengership of His Prophet, and ends with the call to prosperity, success, righteous deeds, prayer, and the remembrance of God? It starts with the name of God, "Allah," and ends with the name of God, "Allah." The sentences are rhythmic, the phrases short, the contents clear, and the message constructive and awakening.

Therefore, in Islamic narrations, great emphasis has been placed on the matter of reciting the Adhan. In a famous hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is narrated that the Mu'adhins (callers to prayer) will be a head and shoulders taller than others on the Day of Resurrection! This height is, in reality, the height of the station of leadership and calling others toward God and toward an act of worship like prayer.

The sound of the Adhan, which resonates from the minarets of Islamic cities at various times of prayer, caresses the ears of true Muslims like a call to freedom and a life-giving breeze of independence and greatness, while it sends tremors and anxiety into the souls of ill-wishers. It is one of the secrets of Islam's survival. A witness to this statement is the explicit confession of a famous British figure who stated before a group of Christians: "As long as the name of Muhammad is raised from the minarets, the Kaaba remains standing, and the Quran is the guide and leader of Muslims, it is impossible for the foundations of our policy to be established and maintained in Islamic lands!" [^1]

But woe and pity to some Muslims who, it is said, have recently removed this great Islamic anthem—which is a testament to the endurance of their religion and culture through the passing of centuries—from their broadcasting stations and replaced it with trivial programs. May Allah guide them and return them to the ranks of the Muslims.

It is obvious that just as the inner essence of the Adhan and its contents are beautiful, efforts must be made to ensure it is performed in a beautiful manner and with a good voice, so that the beauty of the essence is not undermined by an undesirable outward presentation.

[^1]: The speaker of these words was Gladstone, one of the top-tier British politicians of his era.

The Adhan Reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله Through Revelation

In some narrations transmitted through Sunni sources, strange claims are seen regarding the legislation of the Adhan that are inconsistent with Islamic logic. Among these is the narration that: The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, following the request of the companions for a sign to announce the time of prayer, consulted with his friends. Each proposed a suggestion such as raising a special flag, lighting a fire, or ringing a bell, but the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted none of them. This continued until "Abdullah bin Zayd" and "Umar bin Khattab" saw in a dream a person instructing them to recite the Adhan to announce the prayer time and taught them the Adhan, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted it. [^1]

However, this narration is fabricated and considered an insult to the lofty station of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—that instead of relying on revelation, he would rely on the dreams of individuals and base the foundations of his religious instructions on people's dreams. Rather, as stated in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, the Adhan was taught to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله through revelation.

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام says: "When Jibra'il brought the Adhan, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله head was in the lap of Ali عليه السلام. Jibra'il taught the Adhan and Iqamah to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله raised his head, he asked Ali عليه السلام: 'Did you hear the sound of Jibra'il's Adhan?' He replied: 'Yes.' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked again: 'Did you commit it to memory?' He said: 'Yes.' The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'Call Bilal (who had a resonant voice) and teach him the Adhan and Iqamah.' Ali عليه السلام called Bilal and taught him the Adhan." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi.

[^2]: Wasa'il al-Shia, Vol. 4, p. 612.

For further explanation in this regard, you may refer to the book Al-Nass wa al-Ijtihad, page 128.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ هَلْ تَنقِمُونَ مِنَّآ إِلَّآ أَنْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٥٩)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Are you vindictive toward us for any reason except that we have faith in Allah and in what has been sent down to us, and in what was sent down before, and that most of you are transgressors?’ (59)
قُلْ هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكَ مَثُوبَةً عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ مَن لَّعَنَهُ ٱللَّهُ وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْقِرَدَةَ وَٱلْخَنَازِيرَ وَعَبَدَ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضَلُّ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٦٠)
Say, ‘Shall I inform you concerning something worse than that as a requital from Allah? Those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He is wrathful, and turned some of whom into apes and swine, and worshippers of fake deities! Such are in a worse situation and more astray from the right way.’ (60)

Occasion of Revelation

It has been narrated from Abdullah ibn Abbas that a group of Jews came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and requested that he explain his beliefs to them. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said:

"I believe in the Great and Unique God, and I consider as truth whatever was revealed to Abraham, Ishmael, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, Jesus, and all the Divine Prophets, and I make no distinction between them." They replied: "We do not recognize Jesus and do not accept him as a prophet." Then they added: "We know of no religion worse than yours!" Consequently, the above verses were revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

In the first verse, Allah commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to question the People of the Book and "Say: What wrong have we committed that you find fault with us and criticize us, except that we have believed in the One God and are submissive to what has been revealed to us and to the previous Prophets?"

(قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ هَلْ تَنْقِمُونَ مِنَّا إِلَّا أَنْ آمَنَّا بِاللَّهِ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْنا وَ ما أُنْزِلَ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌) [^1] This verse, in fact, points to another aspect of the Jews' obstinacy and unwarranted prejudice, as they accorded no value to anyone other than themselves and their own distorted religion. Due to this intense prejudice, truth appeared as falsehood to them, and falsehood appeared as truth.

At the end of the verse, we see a phrase that is actually an explanation of the reason for the preceding statement. This phrase says: "If you find fault with our pure monotheism and submission to all Heavenly Books, it is because most of you have become rebellious (fasiqun) and tainted by sin. Since you yourselves are tainted and deviated, if some people are pure and on the path of truth, it appears as a flaw to you."

(وَ أَنَّ أَكْثَرَكُمْ فاسِقُونَ‌).

[^1]: "Tanqimun" is derived from the root "niqmah," which originally means to reject something verbally or through action and punishment.

Generally, in contaminated environments where the majority consists of rebellious and sinful individuals, the criteria for truth and falsehood sometimes become so inverted that pure belief and righteous deeds become ugly and subject to criticism, while incorrect beliefs and actions appear beautiful and worthy of praise. This characteristic is the very intellectual metamorphosis that afflicts a human being as a result of sinking into sin and becoming accustomed to it.

However, it should be noted that, as we have previously pointed out, the verse does not criticize all the People of the Book; rather, it carefully separates the account of the righteous minority by using the word "most" (akthar) here as well.

In the second verse, the distorted beliefs and incorrect actions of the People of the Book, along with the punishments that befell them, are compared with the state of the true believers and Muslims, so it may become clear which of these two groups is deserving of criticism and blame. This is a logical response used to draw the attention of obstinate and prejudiced individuals. In this comparison, it says: O Prophet, tell them, is having faith in the One God and the Heavenly Books deserving of blame and objection, or the improper actions of those who were afflicted by all those Divine punishments? "Say: Shall I inform you of those whose recompense with Allah is worse than that?"

(قُلْ هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِشَرٍّ مِنْ ذلِكَ مَثُوبَةً عِنْدَ اللَّهِ‌) [^1] There is no doubt that faith in God and the Heavenly Books is not a bad thing.

[^1]: "Mathubah" and "thawab" originally mean returning to the initial state, and are also applied to any kind of destiny or recompense (reward and punishment). However, they are most often used for good rewards and occasionally for punishment. In the above verse, it can mean destiny or recompense and punishment.

The fact that the verse compares faith with the actions and thoughts of the People of the Book and asks "which is worse" is actually a form of irony. Just as we sometimes see an impure person criticizing a pure person, and the latter responds: "Are the chaste worse or the contaminated?"

Then, it proceeds to elaborate on this matter, saying: "Those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He is angry, and whom He transformed into apes and swine, and those who worshipped Taghut (idols and tyrants); surely such people's position in this world and their place on the Day of Resurrection will be worse, and they are further astray from the right path."

(مَنْ لَعَنَهُ اللَّهُ وَ غَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ وَ جَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ الْقِرَدَةَ وَ الْخَنازِيرَ وَ عَبَدَ الطَّاغُوتَ أُولئِكَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَ أَضَلُّ عَنْ سَواءِ السَّبِيلِ‌) [^1] Regarding the meaning of "maskh" (metamorphosis) and the changing of the appearance of some humans—and whether this refers to a physical transformation or a transformation of the intellectual and moral character—we will speak in detail, God willing, under the commentary of verse 163 of Surah Al-A'raf.

[^1]: "Sawa'" linguistically means equality, moderation, and balance. The reason the straight path is called "Sawa' al-Sabil" in the above verse is that its parts are equal, and its two sides are balanced and level. Any moderate, organized method free from deviation is called the right path.

Additionally, it should be noted that the phrase "abada al-Taghut" (worshipped Taghut) is conjoined to the phrase "man la'anahu Allah" (whom Allah cursed). "Abada" is a past tense verb and not the plural of "abd" (servant), as some have speculated. The attribution of worshipping Taghut to the People of the Book in the above verse either refers to the Jews' worship of the calf or to their unconditional submission to their deviated leaders.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا جَآءُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَقَد دَّخَلُوا۟ بِٱلْكُفْرِ وَهُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْتُمُونَ (٦١)
When they come to you, they say, ‘We believe.’ Certainly, they enter with disbelief and leave with it, and Allah knows best as to what they have been concealing. (61)
وَتَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٦٢)
You see many of them actively engaged in sin and aggression, and consuming illicit gains. Surely, evil is what they have been doing. (62)
لَوْلَا يَنْهَىٰهُمُ ٱلرَّبَّـٰنِيُّونَ وَٱلْأَحْبَارُ عَن قَوْلِهِمُ ٱلْإِثْمَ وَأَكْلِهِمُ ٱلسُّحْتَ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَصْنَعُونَ (٦٣)
Why do not the rabbis and the scribes forbid them from sinful speech and consuming illicit gains? Surely, evil is what they have been working. (63)

Commentary

In the first verse—to complete the discussion regarding the hypocrites among the People of the Book—the Quran pulls back the curtain on their inner hypocrisy and declares to the Muslims: "When they come to you, they say, 'We have believed,' while they entered with hearts full of disbelief and departed in the same state; your logic, reasoning, and words do not leave the slightest impact on their hearts."

(وَ إِذا جاؤُكُمْ قالُوا آمَنَّا وَ قَدْ دَخَلُوا بِالْكُفْرِ وَ هُمْ قَدْ خَرَجُوا بِهِ‌).

Therefore, their righteous appearances, their professions of faith, and the superficial, hypocritical acceptance they display toward your words should not deceive you.

At the end of the verse, He warns them that despite all this concealment, Allah is fully aware and informed of what they hide.

In the following verse, He recounts other signs of their hypocrisy, including: "You see many of them racing one another in sin, transgression, and consuming illicit gain."

(وَ تَرى‌ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ يُسارِعُونَ فِي الْإِثْمِ وَ الْعُدْوانِ وَ أَكْلِهِمُ السُّحْتَ‌) [1] This means they tread the path of sin and oppression so fervently as if they are advancing toward honorable goals, and without any shame or modesty, they strive to outdo one another.

It should be noted that the word "ithm" (sin) has been used to mean both "disbelief" and any kind of "sin." However, since it is placed here in contrast to "udwan" (transgression/aggression), some commentators have interpreted it as sins whose harm only affects the individual himself, unlike udwan, whose harm reaches others. It is also possible that mentioning udwan after ithm is a case of mentioning the general after the specific, and mentioning the consumption of suht (illicit gain) after them is a case of mentioning the most specific.

In this way, He first censures them for every kind of sin, and then points specifically to two major sins due to their significance: one being oppression (udwan) and the other being the consumption of illicit wealth (suht), whether it be bribes or otherwise.

[1]: Discussions regarding the meaning of suht occurred under verse 42 of this same Surah, regarding yusari'un under verse 41 of this Surah, and regarding ithm under verse 219 of Surah Al-Baqarah, Volume 2.

In short: The Quran censures this group of hypocrites among the People of the Book for their audacity, boldness, and recklessness in committing every kind of sin, especially oppression, and most particularly the consumption of illegitimate wealth such as bribes, usury, and the like.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize the ugliness of their deeds, He says: "Evil indeed is what they have been doing."

(لَبِئْسَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌) From the expression kanu ya'malun (they used to do), it is understood that performing these acts was not accidental for them; rather, they persisted in them and committed them repeatedly.

Then, in the third verse, the attack is directed toward their scholars who, through their silence, encouraged them to sin, saying: "Why do the rabbis and the scholars not forbid them from their sinful utterances and their consuming of illicit gain?"

(لَوْ لا يَنْهاهُمُ الرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَ الْأَحْبارُ عَنْ قَوْلِهِمُ الْإِثْمَ وَ أَكْلِهِمُ السُّحْتَ‌) As we have previously noted, Rabbaniyyun is the plural of Rabbani, originally derived from the word Rabb (Lord), referring to scholars who invite people toward God; however, in many instances, this term was applied to Christian religious scholars.

And Ahbar is the plural of Habr, referring to scholars who leave a good impression on society, but in many cases, it is used for Jewish scholars.

Additionally, from the fact that udwan (transgression), which appeared in the previous verse, is not mentioned in this verse, some have inferred that ithm (sin) here carries its broad meaning, which includes transgression.

In this verse, unlike the previous one, the expression "their sinful utterances" (qawlahumu al-ithm) is used. This expression might indicate that scholars are duty-bound to restrain people from both sinful words and sinful deeds. Alternatively, "utterance" (qawl) here might mean "belief," implying that to reform a corrupt society, scholars must first change their incorrect thoughts and beliefs; for as long as a revolution in thought does not occur, one cannot expect deep reforms in practical aspects. In this way, the verse shows scholars the path to reforming a corrupt society, which must begin with an intellectual revolution.

At the end of the verse, the Quran censures the silent scholars who abandoned the duty of "enjoining what is good and forbidding what is evil" in the same manner it censured the primary sinners, saying: "Evil indeed is the work they have been performing." (لَبِئْسَ ما كانُوا يَصْنَعُونَ‌). Thus, it becomes clear that the fate of those who abandon the great duty of enjoining good and forbidding evil—especially if they are scholars and learned men—is the same as the fate of the sinners themselves, and in reality, they are considered accomplices in the crime.

It is narrated from Ibn Abbas, the famous commentator, that he said: "This verse is the severest verse in rebuking and censuring irresponsible and silent scholars."

Naturally, this ruling is not exclusive to the silent scholars of the Jews and Christians; it encompasses all intellectual leaders and scholars who remain silent when people become polluted with sin and accelerate on the path of oppression and corruption, for Allah's ruling is the same for everyone! In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that he said in a sermon:

"Past nations were destroyed only because they committed sins while their scholars remained silent and did not forbid the evil. At that point, divine calamities and punishments descended upon them. So, O people! Enjoin what is good and forbid what is evil, so that you do not meet their fate." [1] The same theme appears in Nahj al-Balagha toward the end of the Qasi'ah sermon (Sermon 192):

"For Allah, the Glorified, did not curse the past generations before you except for their abandonment of enjoining good and forbidding evil. He cursed the foolish for committing sins and the wise for abandoning the prohibition of them."

It is noteworthy that regarding the masses of people in the previous verse, the term yaf'alun (they do) was used, while in this verse regarding the scholars, the term yasna'un (they perform/manufacture) is used. We know that yasna'un comes from the root sun', meaning actions performed with precision and skill, whereas ya'malun comes from amal, which refers to any kind of action, even if it lacks precision. This itself implies a greater censure; for if the ignorant and common people perform bad deeds, part of it is due to ignorance and lack of information, but a scholar who fails to fulfill his duty has committed a violation that is calculated, conscious, and skillful. For this reason, the punishment of the scholar is heavier and harsher than that of the ignorant!

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 649.

Ayah

وَقَالَتِ ٱلْيَهُودُ يَدُ ٱللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ ۚ غُلَّتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَلُعِنُوا۟ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ ۘ بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ يُنفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ ۚ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۚ وَأَلْقَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ كُلَّمَآ أَوْقَدُوا۟ نَارًا لِّلْحَرْبِ أَطْفَأَهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٦٤)
The Jews say, ‘Allah’s hand is tied up.’ Tied up be their hands, and cursed be they for what they say! No, His hands are wide open: He bestows as He wishes. Surely many of them will be increased in rebellion and unfaith by what has been sent to you from your Lord, and We have cast enmity and hatred amongst them until the Day of Resurrection. Every time they ignite the flames of war, Allah puts them out. They seek to cause corruption on the earth, and Allah does not like the agents of corruption. (64)

Commentary

This verse mentions one of the clear examples of the improper speech and sinful discourse of the Jews, which was referred to in general terms in the previous verse. To explain:

History shows that the Jews once lived at the peak of power and ruled over a significant portion of the civilized world of that time; the era of Dawud and Sulayman bin Dawud (peace be upon them) can be cited as an example. In subsequent ages, their power continued with fluctuations. However, with the advent of Islam, especially in the environment of the Hijaz, the star of their power began to set. The Prophet’s (صلى الله عليه وآله) struggle against the Jews of "Bani al-Nadir," "Bani Qurayza," and the "Jews of Khaybar" led to their ultimate weakening. At this time, some of them, considering their former power and grandeur, said mockingly:

God's hand is tied in chains and He grants us no bounty. (According to some commentators, the speaker of these words was "Phineas bin Azura," the leader of "Bani Qaynuqa," or "Nabash bin Qays" according to others).

Since the rest were also pleased with his words, the Quran attributes this statement to all of them, saying: "The Jews said, 'Allah’s hand is tied'"! (قَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ يَدُ اللَّهِ مَغْلُولَةٌ). It should be noted that "Yad" (hand) in the Arabic language is applied to many meanings, one of which is the literal "hand," while others include "blessing," "power," "governance and sovereignty," and "dominance." Of course, the primary meaning is the literal hand.

Since humans perform most important tasks with their hands, it is used metaphorically for other meanings, just as the word "hand" (دست) is used in the Persian language.

From many narrations that have reached us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), it is understood that this verse refers to a belief the Jews held regarding the issue of Divine Decree and Destiny (Qada and Qadar) and delegation (Tafwid). They believed that at the beginning of creation, Allah determined everything, and whatever was to happen has already taken place, and even He Himself cannot practically effect any change in it! [^1] Of course, the end of the verse (بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ), as will be explained, supports the first meaning, but the second meaning can also align with the first. For when the life of the Jews fell into disarray and the star of their fortune set, they believed this was a fixed destiny that could not be changed, because from the beginning all these destinies were determined and practically Allah’s hand is tied!! In response to them, Allah first says by way of reproach and condemnation of this improper belief: "May their own hands be tied, and may they be cursed for what they said." (غُلَّتْ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَلُعِنُوا بِمَا قَالُوا). Then, to invalidate this improper belief, He says: Both of Allah’s hands are outstretched, and He bestows however He wills and to whomever He wills. (بَلْ يَدَاهُ مَبْسُوطَتَانِ يُنْفِقُ كَيْفَ يَشَاءُ). There is neither compulsion in His work, nor is He subject to the determinism of natural factors or historical determinism; rather, His Will is above everything and effective in all things.

It is noteworthy that the Jews used "Yad" (hand) in the singular form, but Allah, in His response, uses "Yad" in the "dual" form (Yadahu) and says: "Both of Allah’s hands are outstretched." In addition to emphasizing the point, this is a subtle metaphor for the ultimate generosity and bounty of Allah, for those who are extremely generous give with both hands. Furthermore, the mention of two hands is a metaphor for perfect power and perhaps an allusion to "material" and "spiritual" blessings, or "worldly" and "hereafter" favors.

Then He says: "Even these verses, which pull back the curtain from their speech and beliefs, instead of having a positive effect on them and making them turn back from their wrong path, cast many of them onto the path of stubbornness, and their rebellion and disbelief increase."

(وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِنْهُمْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ طُغْيَانًا وَكُفْرًا). But in the face of these improper words and beliefs, and their stubbornness and persistence in the path of rebellion and disbelief, Allah has decreed a heavy punishment for them in this world, which is that He has cast enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection. (وَأَلْقَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَالْبَغْضَاءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ).

There is discussion among commentators as to what is meant by this "enmity and hatred," but if we set aside the current exceptional and unstable situation of the Jews and consider the history of their scattered and wandering lives, we will see that a major factor for this specific historical condition has been the lack of unity and sincerity among

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 649, and Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 486.

them on a global level. For if unity and sincerity had existed among them, they would not have witnessed so much fragmentation, misery, and displacement throughout history.

In the commentary of verse 14 of this same Surah, we provided more explanation regarding the issue of continuous enmity and hatred among the People of the Book.

In the final part of the verse, reference is made to the efforts and attempts of the Jews to kindle the fires of wars and Allah’s grace in rescuing the Muslims from these destructive fires, stating: "Whenever they kindle a fire for war, Allah extinguishes it and protects you from it." (كُلَّمَا أَوْقَدُوا نَارًا لِلْحَرْبِ أَطْفَأَهَا اللَّهُ). This is, in fact, one of the miraculous points in the life of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), for the Jews were the most powerful among all the people of the Hijaz, the most familiar with warfare, and possessed the strongest fortresses and strongholds. In addition, they had vast financial power that aided them in battles, to the extent that the Quraysh sought their help, and both the Aws and Khazraj tribes each tried to establish treaties of friendship and military cooperation with them. Yet, despite all this, the scroll of their power was folded in a way that was entirely unpredictable. The Jews of Bani al-Nadir, Bani Qurayza, and Bani Qaynuqa were forced into exile under specific conditions, and the inhabitants of the fortresses of "Khaybar" and the land of "Fadak" surrendered. Even the Jews living in parts of the Hijaz deserts knelt before the grandeur of Islam. Not only were they unable to help the polytheists, but they themselves were removed from the field of struggle.

The Quran adds: "They strive to spread corruption on the earth with persistent and continuous effort." (وَيَسْعَوْنَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَسَادًا) "While Allah does not love the corrupters." (وَاللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُفْسِدِينَ).

Therefore, the Quran never criticizes them from a racial perspective; rather, the scale of the Quran's criticisms and the pattern of its rebukes are the actions committed by any individual or group. In the following verses, we will see that despite all this, the Quran has left the path of return open to them.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ لَكَفَّرْنَا عَنْهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتِهِمْ وَلَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٦٥)
Had the People of the Book believed and been Godwary, We would surely have absolved them of their misdeeds and admitted them into gardens of bliss. (65)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِم مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ لَأَكَلُوا۟ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِم ۚ مِّنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُّقْتَصِدَةٌ ۖ وَكَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ سَآءَ مَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٦٦)
Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel, and what was sent down to them from their Lord, they would surely have drawn nourishment from above them and from beneath their feet. There is an upright group among them, but evil is what many of them do. (66)

Commentary

Following the previous criticisms regarding the plans and methods of the People of the Book, these two verses—as required by the principles of education—aim to return the deviant among the People of the Book to the right path, show them the real trajectory, and appreciate the minority among them who did not participate in their transgressions. First, it says:

"And if the People of the Book had believed and feared Allah, We would surely have expiated their past sins and overlooked them." (وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَكَفَّرْنَا عَنْهُمْ سَيِّئَاتِهِمْ) Not only would We forgive their sins, "but We would surely have admitted them into Gardens of Pleasure, which are the center of all kinds of blessings." (وَلَأَدْخَلْنَاهُمْ جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ) This pertains to spiritual and otherworldly blessings.

Then, it points to the profound effect of faith and piety even on the material life of humans, saying: "If they had upheld the Torah and the Gospel and placed them before their eyes as a manual for life, and generally acted upon all that was revealed to them from their Lord—including previous heavenly books and the Quran—without any discrimination or prejudice, Divine blessings from the heavens and the earth would have encompassed them." (وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ وَمَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ لَأَكَلُوا مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَمِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ).

There is no doubt that "upholding the Torah and the Gospel" refers to those parts of the original Torah and Gospel that were in their hands at that time, not the distorted parts which could more or less be identified through evidence. The phrase "what was revealed to them from their Lord" refers to all heavenly books and Divine commands, as this phrase is absolute. In reality, it indicates that ethnic prejudices should not be mixed with religious and Divine matters; it is not about the heavenly books of the Arabs or the Jews, but rather about Divine commands. By this expression, the Quran intends to reduce their prejudice as much as possible and pave the way for reaching the depths of their souls. Therefore, all the pronouns in this verse refer back to them (إِلَيْهِمْ, ...مِنْ رَبِّهِمْ, ...مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ, ...مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ); all of this is so they may descend from the mount of stubbornness and not imagine that submitting to the Quran means the submission of Jews to Arabs, but rather it is solely submission to their Great God.

Undoubtedly, upholding the regulations of the Torah and the Gospel means acting upon their principles, because as we have stated many times, the principles of the teachings of the Prophets are the same everywhere, and the difference between them is the difference between "complete" and "more complete." This does not contradict the fact that some rulings of a previous religion may be abrogated by rulings of a subsequent religion.

In summary, the above verse once again emphasizes this fundamental principle: following the heavenly teachings of the Prophets is not only for organizing life after death; rather, it has a widespread reflection throughout the material life of humans as well. It makes communities strong, ranks tight-knit, forces concentrated, blessings abundant, possibilities vast, and life prosperous, safe, and secure.

A look at the massive material wealth and vast human resources that are destroyed in today's world due to deviation from these teachings—in the form of deadly weapons, pointless conflicts, and destructive efforts—is a living proof of this truth. Today, the volume of wealth used for the destruction of the world—if we think correctly—is no less than, if not more than, the wealth spent on constructive paths.

Today, the brilliant minds working on the perfection, development, and production of weapons of war and colonial conflicts constitute a significant portion of valuable human resources. How much does mankind need these capitals and these minds, which are being wasted in vain, to meet its needs? How beautiful, desirable, and interesting the face of the world would be if all of these were employed in the path of prosperity.

Furthermore, it should be noted that the meaning of the phrase "from above them" (مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ) and "from beneath their feet" (مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ) is that all the blessings of heaven and earth will encompass them. It is also possible that this phrase is a metaphor for the universality of blessings, just as in Arabic and non-Arabic literature, it is sometimes said: "So-and-so was drowned in blessings from head to toe," meaning in all aspects.

Also, this verse is a response to the statement of the Jews mentioned in previous verses: if you see that Divine blessings have been cut off from you, it is not because stinginess has found its way into the Holy Essence of Allah or that His hand is tied; rather, it is your own actions that have reflected in your material and spiritual life and darkened both. Until you return, these blessings shall not return.

At the end of the verse, it refers to the righteous minority of this group, saying:

"Even though many of them are evildoers, there is a moderate and balanced community among them" (whose account is separate from the account of others before God and in the eyes of God's creation). (مِنْهُمْ أُمَّةٌ مُقْتَصِدَةٌ وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ سَاءَ مَا يَعْمَلُونَ).

Similar expressions regarding the righteous minority of the People of the Book are also seen in verses 159 and 181 of Surah Al-A'raf and verse 75 of Al-Imran.

End of Volume Four of Tafsir Nemouneh.

Ayah

۞ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ ۖ وَإِن لَّمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٧)
O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you from your Lord, and if you do not, you will not have communicated His message, and Allah shall protect you from the people. Indeed Allah does not guide the faithless lot. (67)

Commentary

The Appointment of a Successor as the Culmination of the Mission

This verse has adopted a specific tone that distinguishes it from the verses preceding and following it. In this verse, the address is directed solely to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), outlining only his duty. It begins with the address, "O Messenger!" (يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ) and issues a command with explicit clarity and emphasis to convey what has been revealed to him from his Lord (بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ). [1]

[1]: As Raghib states in the book Al-Mufradat, the word "بلغ" (convey) carries more emphasis than the word "ابلغ".

Then, for further emphasis, He warns him that if he refrains from this task (though he would never refrain), he has not conveyed God's message at all! (وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَمَا بَلَّغْتَ رِسَالَتَهُ).

Next, He consoles and reassures the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), who seemingly felt anxiety and concern regarding a specific event, telling him: "Have no fear of the people in fulfilling this mission, for Allah will protect you from their dangers" (وَاللَّهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ).

At the end of the verse, as a threat and punishment to those who deny this specific mission and stubbornly disbelieve in it, He says:

"Indeed, Allah does not guide the stubborn disbelieving people" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ).

The phrasing of the verse, its unique tone, the successive emphases, and its beginning with the address "O Messenger!"—which appears only twice in the Holy Quran—along with the threat that failure to convey this specific matter is equivalent to not conveying the message at all—a warning found exclusively in this verse—all indicate that the subject was a momentous event. The failure to announce it would have been equal to the failure of the entire prophetic mission.

Furthermore, this matter had fierce opponents, and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was concerned about their opposition, which could have created difficulties for Islam and the Muslims; hence, Allah granted him protection.

Now the question arises: considering the timing of the revelation of this Surah, which was certainly revealed toward the end of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life, what was this important matter that Allah commanded him to convey with such emphasis?

Was it related to Monotheism (Tawhid), polytheism, or the destruction of idols? These issues had been resolved for the Prophet and the Muslims years prior.

Was it related to Islamic laws and rulings? Most of the major ones had already been explained by then.

Was it related to the struggle against the People of the Book—the Jews and Christians? We know that after the events of Banu al-Nadir, Banu Qurayza, Banu Qaynuqa, Khaybar, Fadak, and Najran, the People of the Book no longer posed a significant problem for the Muslims.

Was it related to the hypocrites? We know that after the Conquest of Mecca and the dominance of Islam throughout the Arabian Peninsula, the hypocrites were marginalized from the social scene, their power was broken, and whatever they held was kept hidden.

Truly, what important issue was at stake in these final months of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life that the above verse emphasizes so strongly? It is also beyond doubt that the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) anxiety was not for his own person or life, but rather for the potential sabotage and opposition of the hypocrites, which could have brought dangers or losses to the Muslims.

Could any issue other than the appointment of a successor for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the future destiny of Islam and the Muslims possess such characteristics?

We now turn to the various narrations recorded in numerous Sunni and Shia books regarding the above verse to see how they support the aforementioned possibility. Then, we will examine the objections and criticisms raised by a group of Sunni commentators regarding this interpretation.

The Occasion of Revelation of the Verse of Conveyance (Tabligh)

Although, unfortunately, prejudices and sectarian biases have prevented the truths regarding this verse from being placed at the disposal of all Muslims without concealment, many narrations are found in various books written by Sunni scholars—including works of exegesis, Hadith, and history—which explicitly state: the above verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام).

These narrations have been transmitted by a large group of companions, including Zayd ibn Arqam, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Ibn Abbas, Jabir ibn Abdillah al-Ansari, Abu Hurayrah, Bara' ibn Azib, Hudhayfah, Amir ibn Layla ibn Damrah, and Ibn Mas'ud. They stated that the above verse was revealed concerning Ali (عليه السلام) and the event of the Day of Ghadir.

Some of these Hadiths, like that of Zayd ibn Arqam, are narrated through one chain. Others, like the Hadith of Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, are narrated through eleven chains! Some, like the Hadith of Ibn Abbas, are also narrated through eleven chains, and others, like the Hadith of Bara' ibn Azib, through three chains.

The scholars who have explicitly mentioned these Hadiths in their books are numerous. As an example, we name a few:

  • Hafiz Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani in the book Ma Nazala min al-Quran fi Ali (as cited in Al-Khasa'is, p. 29).
  • Abu al-Hasan al-Wahidi al-Nishapuri in Asbab al-Nuzul, p. 150.
  • Hafiz Abu Sa'id al-Sajistani in the book Al-Wilayah (as cited in the book Al-Tara'if).
  • Ibn Asakir al-Shafi'i (as cited in Al-Durr al-Manthur, vol. 2, p. 298).
  • Fakhr al-Razi in his Tafsir al-Kabir, vol. 3, p. 636.
  • Abu Ishaq al-Hamwayni in Fara'id al-Simtayn.
  • Ibn al-Sabbagh al-Maliki in Al-Fusul al-Muhimmah, p. 27.
  • Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur, vol. 2, p. 298.
  • Qadi al-Shawkani in Fath al-Qadir, vol. 3, p. 57.
  • Shihab al-Din al-Alusi al-Shafi'i in Ruh al-Ma'ani, vol. 6, p. 172.
  • Shaykh Sulayman al-Qunduzi al-Hanafi in Yanabi' al-Mawaddah, p. 120.
  • Badr al-Din al-Hanafi in 'Umdat al-Qari fi Sharh Sahih al-Bukhari, vol. 8, p. 584.
  • Shaykh Muhammad Abduh of Egypt in Tafsir al-Manar, vol. 6, p. 463.
  • Hafiz Ibn Mardawayh (d. 416 AH) (as cited by Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur).

Many others have also narrated this occasion of revelation for the verse.

Make no mistake; the point is not that all the aforementioned scholars and commentators accepted the revelation of this verse regarding Ali (عليه السلام), but rather that they recorded the narrations related to this matter in their books. Although, after quoting this famous narration, some of them—due to fear of the specific conditions of their environment or due to incorrect prejudices that hinder sound judgment in such discussions—refrained from accepting it. In fact, they sometimes tried as much as possible to make it appear faint or insignificant. For instance, Fakhr al-Razi, whose bias in specific sectarian matters is well-known, placed this occasion of revelation as the tenth possibility for the verse, listing nine other possibilities—mostly very weak, hollow, and worthless—before it!

We are not greatly surprised by Fakhr al-Razi, as this is his method everywhere. Our surprise is at enlightened writers such as Sayyid Qutb in the commentary Fi Zilal al-Quran and Muhammad Rashid Rida in Tafsir al-Manar, who either did not mention this occasion of revelation (which fills various books) at all, or portrayed it as so insignificant that it draws no attention. Did their environment not allow for the expression of the truth, or were the sectarian intellectual veils so thick that the light of enlightenment could not penetrate their depths to pull back the curtains? We do not know.

However, another group considered the revelation of the verse regarding Ali (عليه السلام) as certain, but expressed doubt as to whether it indicates the issue of Wilayah (guardianship) and Caliphate. We will soon hear their objections and the responses to them, God willing.

In any case, as stated above, the narrations recorded in this regard in the famous books of the Sunnis—let alone Shia books—are too numerous to be denied or simply overlooked. We do not know why, regarding the occasion of revelation of other Quranic verses, one or two Hadiths are deemed sufficient, yet for this verse, all these narrations are not enough. Does this verse have a characteristic that others do not? Can a logical reason be found for such strictness regarding this verse?

Another point that is necessary to mention here is that the narrations we cited above were only those specifically regarding the revelation of the verse about Ali (عليه السلام) (i.e., the Asbab al-Nuzul). Otherwise, the narrations regarding the event of Ghadir Khumm, the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) sermon, and the introduction of Ali as the legatee (Wasi) and guardian (Wali) are far more numerous. To the extent that the researcher Allamah Amini in Al-Ghadir has narrated the Hadith of Ghadir from 110 companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) with full documentation, from 84 of the Successors (Tabi'un), and from 360 scholars and famous Islamic books. This shows that the said Hadith is one of the most certain "Mutawatir" (frequently narrated) reports. If someone doubts the "Tawatur" of this narration, it must be said that they cannot accept any Mutawatir narration at all.

Since discussing all the narrations regarding the "occasion of revelation of the verse" and all the narrations concerning the Hadith of Ghadir would require writing a thick volume and would take us beyond the style of writing a commentary, we suffice with this much. Those who wish to study further in this field are referred to the books Al-Durr al-Manthur by Suyuti, Al-Ghadir by Allamah Amini, Ihqaq al-Haqq by Qadi Nurullah Shushtari, Al-Muraja'at by Sharaf al-Din, and Dala'il al-Sidq by Muhammad Hassan Muzaffar.

Summary of the Event of Ghadir

In the numerous narrations recorded in this regard, while all follow a single event, there are various expressions. Some narrations are very detailed and long, while others are brief and condensed. Some narrations report one part of the event and others another part. However, from the sum of these narrations, as well as Islamic histories and the consideration of evidence, conditions, environment, and location, the following is understood:

In the final year of the Prophet's life, the Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wida') was completed with the utmost grandeur in the presence of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). Hearts were immersed in an aura of spirituality, and the spiritual pleasure of this great act of worship still resonated in the souls of the people.

The companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), whose number was exceptionally large, were overjoyed at attaining this great grace and fortune. [1] Not only did the people of Medina accompany the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) on this journey, but Muslims from various parts of the Arabian Peninsula were also with him to gain a great historical honor.

The sun of the Hejaz cast fire upon the mountains and valleys, but the sweetness of this unique spiritual journey made everything easy. Noon was approaching; gradually, the land of Juhfah and then the dry and scorching deserts of "Ghadir Khumm" appeared from afar.

[1]: Some have recorded the number of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) companions as 90,000, some 114,000, some 120,000, and some 124,000.

This place is essentially a crossroads where the people of the Hejaz separate. A path leads toward Medina in the north, a path toward Iraq in the east, a path toward the west and the land of Egypt, and a path toward the land of Yemen in the south. It was here that the final memory and the most important chapter of this great journey had to take place, and the Muslims were to part ways after receiving the final instruction, which was indeed the concluding point of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) successful missions.

It was Thursday in the tenth year of the Hijra, exactly eight days after the Eid al-Adha. Suddenly, an order to halt was given by the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) to his companions. The Muslims loudly called back those who were at the front of the caravan and waited for those lagging behind to arrive. The sun passed the meridian. The Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) caller (Mu'adhin) invited the people to the noon prayer with the cry of "Allahu Akbar." People quickly prepared for prayer, but the air was so hot that some were forced to put part of their cloak under their feet and the other part over their heads; otherwise, the hot pebbles of the desert and the sun's rays would have tormented their feet and heads.

No shade was visible in the desert, nor any greenery, plants, or trees, except for a few bare desert trees that stubbornly fought the heat.

A group took refuge under these few trees. They threw a cloth over one of these bare trees and arranged a shade for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), but the hot winds crawled under this shade and spread the scorching heat of the sun beneath it.

The noon prayer ended.

The Muslims intended to immediately take refuge in the small tents they carried with them, but the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) informed them that everyone must prepare to hear a new divine message to be delivered within a detailed sermon. Those who were at a distance from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) could not see his celestial countenance amidst the crowd.

Therefore, a pulpit was fashioned from camel saddles, and the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) stood atop it. First, he praised and thanked the Lord and entrusted himself to God. Then, he addressed the people and spoke thus:

"I shall soon answer God's call and depart from among you. I am responsible, and you too are responsible. How do you testify regarding me?"

The people raised their voices and said: "We testify that you have conveyed the message, given sincere advice, and exerted the utmost effort in the way of our guidance. May Allah reward you with good."

Then he said: "Do you not testify to the oneness of Allah, my messengership, the truth of the Day of Resurrection, and the raising of the dead on that day?" They all said: "Yes, we testify." He said: "O Allah, be witness!"...

Once again he said: "O people! Do you hear my voice?"... They said: "Yes." Following that, silence enveloped the entire desert, and nothing was heard except the whisper of the wind. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "...Now see how you will treat these two weighty and precious things that I leave behind as a legacy among you?"

Someone from the crowd called out: "Which two weighty things, O Messenger of Allah?!"

The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) immediately said: "The first, the 'Greater Weight' (Thaqal al-Akbar), is the Book of Allah, one end of which is in the hand of the Lord and the other end in your hands. Do not let go of it, lest you go astray. And my second precious legacy is my Progeny (Ahl al-Bayt). The Subtle and All-Aware has informed me that these two shall never separate until they join me at the Pond (in Paradise). Do not precede them, for you will perish, and do not lag behind them, for you will also perish."

Suddenly, the people saw the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) looking around as if searching for someone. As soon as his eyes fell upon Ali (عليه السلام), he leaned down, took his hand, and raised it so high that the whiteness of both their armpits became visible, and all the people saw him and recognized that he was the same invincible commander of Islam. At this point, the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) voice became louder and more resonant as he said:

"O people! Who is more entitled to the believers than their own selves?" (أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ مَنْ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنْفُسِهِمْ)

They said: "Allah and His Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) know best." The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "Allah is my Master and Leader, and I am the Master and Leader of the believers, and I am more entitled to them than their own selves (and my will precedes theirs)." Then he said:

"Whomsoever I am his Master, Ali is his Master." (فَمَنْ كُنْتُ مَوْلَاهُ فَعَلِيٌّ مَوْلَاهُ)

He repeated this sentence three times, and according to some narrators, four times. Following that, he raised his head toward the sky and said:

"O Allah! Love those who love him, and be an enemy to those who are his enemies. Love those who love him, and hate those who hate him. Help those who help him, and leave those who abandon him. And turn the truth with him wherever he turns." (اللَّهُمَّ وَالِ مَنْ وَالَاهُ وَعَادِ مَنْ عَادَاهُ وَأَحِبَّ مَنْ أَحَبَّهُ وَأَبْغِضْ مَنْ أَبْغَضَهُ وَانْصُرْ مَنْ نَصَرَهُ وَاخْذُلْ مَنْ خَذَلَهُ وَأَدِرِ الْحَقَّ مَعَهُ حَيْثُ دَارَ)

Then he said: "Behold! Let those present convey this news to those who are absent." (أَلَا فَلْيُبَلِّغِ الشَّاهِدُ الْغَائِبَ)

The Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) sermon ended. Sweat was pouring from the faces of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), Ali (عليه السلام), and the people. Before the ranks of the crowd had dispersed, the Trustee of Revelation descended and recited this verse to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family): "This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favor upon you..." (الْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِي). The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said:

"Allahu Akbar! Allahu Akbar for the perfection of the religion, the completion of the favor, and the Lord's pleasure with my mission and the Wilayah of Ali after me." (اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ، اللَّهُ أَكْبَرُ عَلَى إِكْمَالِ الدِّينِ وَإِتْمَامِ النِّعْمَةِ وَرِضَى الرَّبِّ بِرِسَالَتِي وَالْوِلَايَةِ لِعَلِيٍّ مِنْ بَعْدِي)

At this moment, a commotion arose among the people as they congratulated Ali (عليه السلام) on this position. Among the prominent individuals who congratulated him were Abu Bakr and Umar, who uttered this sentence in the presence of the crowd:

"Bravo, bravo to you, O son of Abu Talib! You have become my Master and the Master of every believing man and woman." (بَخٍ بَخٍ لَكَ يَا بْنَ أَبِي طَالِبٍ أَصْبَحْتَ وَأَمْسَيْتَ مَوْلَايَ وَمَوْلَا كُلِّ مُؤْمِنٍ وَمُؤْمِنَةٍ)

At this moment, Ibn Abbas said: "By Allah, this covenant shall remain upon the necks of all."

And Hassan ibn Thabit, the famous poet, asked the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) for permission to compose verses for this occasion. He then began his famous poem:

Their Prophet calls them on the day of Ghadir at Khumm, And listen to the Messenger as he calls out. He said: "Who is your Master and your Prophet?" They said, showing no blindness there: "Your God is our Master and you are our Prophet, And you shall find no disobedience from us regarding the Wilayah." He said to him: "Rise, O Ali, for I have Chosen you to be the Imam and guide after me." So whomsoever I was his Master, this is his Guardian, So be to him truthful followers and supporters. There he prayed: "O Allah, befriend his friend, And be an enemy to the one who shows enmity to Ali." [1]

This was a summary of the famous Hadith of Ghadir as recorded in the books of Sunni and Shia scholars.

[1]: These verses have been narrated by a group of great Sunni scholars, among whom are: Hafiz Abu Nu'aym al-Isfahani, Hafiz Abu Sa'id al-Sajistani, Khwarizmi al-Maliki, Hafiz Abu Abdillah al-Marzubani, Ganji al-Shafi'i, Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti, Sibt ibn al-Jawzi, and Sadr al-Din Hamuwayni.

Discussions and Objections

There is no doubt that if this verse were about anything other than the Caliphate of Ali (عليه السلام)—as we have said—far fewer narrations and evidences within the verse itself would have sufficed. Just as great Islamic commentators, in explaining other verses of the Quran, sometimes rely on one-tenth of the evidence available for this verse or even less. But unfortunately, the veil of prejudice here has prevented the acceptance of many realities.

Those who have raised the flag of opposition against the interpretation of this verse, the numerous narrations regarding its occasion of revelation, and the beyond-Mutawatir narrations regarding the event of Ghadir itself, fall into two groups:

The first group consists of those who entered this discussion from the beginning with a spirit of animosity and stubbornness, even resorting to insults, defamation, and abuse against the Shia. The second group consists of those who have maintained the spirit of research and truth-seeking to some extent and have pursued the matter through argumentation. For this reason, they have confessed to some of the truths but, following the mention of certain objections—perhaps the result of the specific conditions of their intellectual environment—they have bypassed the verse and its related narrations.

A prominent example of the first group is Ibn Taymiyyah in the book Minhaj al-Sunnah, who is exactly like someone who closes his eyes in broad daylight, plugs his ears firmly with his fingers, and screams, "Where is the sun?" He is neither willing to open the corner of his eye to see a bit of the truth, nor to remove his fingers from his ears to hear a bit of the clamor of Islamic traditionists and commentators. He repeatedly hurls insults and behaves insolently. The excuse for such individuals is their ignorance, lack of awareness, and prejudices mixed with stubbornness and harshness, which go as far as denying self-evident truths and clear matters that anyone can perceive. Therefore, we never trouble ourselves to quote their words nor trouble the readers with hearing the responses. When someone, in the face of all these great Islamic scholars and commentators—the majority of whom are Sunni and have explicitly stated the revelation of the verse regarding Ali (عليه السلام)—has the audacity to say, "Not a single scholar has narrated such a thing in his book!", what can we say to him, and what value do his words have for us to discuss?

Interestingly, to exonerate himself against the numerous reliable books that explicitly state the revelation of the verse regarding Ali (عليه السلام), Ibn Taymiyyah suffices with this ridiculous sentence: "Not a single scholar who knows what he is saying considers this verse to be about Ali (عليه السلام)!"

Apparently, only those scholars "know what they are saying" who echo the extreme, hostile, and stubborn inclinations of Ibn Taymiyyah; otherwise, anyone who does not echo them is a scholar who does not know what he is saying! This is the logic of someone whose thoughts are overshadowed by self-centeredness and stubbornness... Let us move past this group.

However, among the objections mentioned by the second group, several points are worth discussing, which we review below:

1. Does "Mawla" mean "One with more authority"?

The most important objection raised regarding the narration of Ghadir is that "Mawla" can also mean friend, helper, or supporter, and it is not certain that it does not carry that meaning here.

The answer to this is not complicated, for any impartial observer knows that mentioning the friendship of Ali (عليه السلام) does not require all these preliminaries, arrangements, and sermonizing in the middle of a dry and scorching desert, halting the crowd, and taking successive confessions from the people. The friendship of Muslims with one another is one of the most self-evident Islamic issues that has existed since the beginning of Islam.

Furthermore, this was not something the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had failed to convey until then; he had conveyed it many times.

It was not something he would fear expressing such that God would need to reassure him.

It was not an issue about which God would speak to His Prophet with the tone: "If you do not convey it, you have not conveyed the message at all."

All of this testifies that the matter was beyond a simple and ordinary friendship, which was considered part of the alphabet of Islamic brotherhood from the first day of Islam.

Moreover, if the intention was to express a simple friendship, why does the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) first take an admission from the people: "Am I not more entitled to you than your own selves?" (أَلَسْتُ أَوْلَى بِكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ) [1] Does this sentence have any harmony with expressing a simple friendship?

Also, a simple friendship did not warrant the people, even Umar himself, to congratulate Ali (عليه السلام) with the sentence: "You have become my Master and the Master of every believing man and woman," and to count it as a new success. [2]

[1]: This sentence has been recorded in numerous narrations. [2]: This part of the Hadith, known as the "Hadith of Congratulation," has been narrated by many great scholars of Hadith, Tafsir, and history from the Sunnis through multiple chains from a group of companions such as Ibn Abbas, Abu Hurayrah, Bara' ibn Azib, and Zayd ibn Arqam. The late Allamah Amini in the first volume of Al-Ghadir narrated this Hadith from sixty Sunni scholars!

Had Ali (عليه السلام) not been recognized until that day as an ordinary Muslim whose friendship is obligatory upon all? Was the friendship of Muslims with one another something new that required congratulations, especially in the final year of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life?

Furthermore, what connection can there be between the "Hadith of the Two Weighty Things" (Thaqalayn) and the expressions mixed with the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) farewell and the issue of Ali's (عليه السلام) friendship? A simple friendship of Ali (عليه السلام) with the believers does not necessitate the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) placing him alongside the Quran. [1] Does any impartial observer not understand from this expression that the issue of leadership is being raised? For the Quran was the first leader of the Muslims after the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) passing, and therefore the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) were the second leaders.

2. The Connection of the Verses

It is sometimes said that the verses before and after are about the People of the Book and their wrongdoings. Specifically, the author of Tafsir al-Manar in volume 6, page 466, has insisted heavily on this issue.

However, as we said in the commentary of the verse itself, this matter is not significant because:

[1]: The Hadith of Thaqalayn is among the Mutawatir Hadiths recorded in many Sunni books from a group of companions such as Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, Zayd ibn Arqam, Zayd ibn Thabit, Abu Hurayrah, Hudhayfah ibn Asid, Jabir ibn Abdillah al-Ansari, Abdillah ibn Hantab, Abd ibn Humayd, Jubayr ibn Mut'im, Damrah al-Aslami, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, Abu Rafi', and Umm Salamah from the Holy Prophet (peace be upon him and his family).

Firstly, the tone of the verse and its difference from the verses before and after clearly show that the subject of discussion in this verse is different from the preceding and following verses. Secondly, as we have said many times, the Quran is not a "classic" book whose contents are categorized into specific chapters and sections; rather, it was revealed according to various needs, incidents, and events. Thus, we observe the Quran discussing one of the battles while, for example, introducing a secondary legal ruling, or while speaking about the Jews and Christians, it turns the address to the Muslims and recounts one of the Islamic instructions for them. (For more explanation, refer back to the discussion we had at the beginning of the commentary on the verse).

Strangely, some biased individuals insist on saying that this verse was revealed at the beginning of the Prophetic mission, even though Surah al-Ma'idah was revealed at the end of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life. If they say: "This single verse was revealed in Mecca at the beginning of the mission and then appropriately placed among the verses of this Surah," we say: this is exactly the opposite of what you seek and desire. For we know that at the beginning of the mission, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had no struggle with the Jews or the Christians; therefore, the link between this verse and the verses before and after would be severed (reflect on this). All of this shows that this verse has been exposed to the storm of prejudice, and for this reason, possibilities are raised regarding it that are never mentioned in similar verses. Each person tries with a baseless excuse or pretext to divert it from its path!

3. Is this Hadith narrated in all the Sahih books?

Some say: "How can we accept this Hadith when Bukhari and Muslim did not narrate it in their two books?"

This objection is also among the wonders. Firstly, there are many authentic Hadiths that Sunni scholars have accepted which are not in Sahih al-Bukhari or Sahih Muslim; this is not the first Hadith to be in this situation. Secondly, is the reliable literature for them limited to these two books? This Hadith appears in their other trusted sources, even some of the Sihah al-Sittah (the six famous and trusted books of the Sunnis), such as Sunan Ibn Majah [1] and Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal. [2] Scholars like Hakim, Dhahabi, and Ibn Hajar—despite all their fame and the bias they hold—have confessed to the authenticity of many chains of this Hadith. Therefore, it is not at all unlikely that Bukhari and Muslim, in that specific atmosphere and the suffocating environment of their time, were unable or unwilling to explicitly include something in their books that was contrary to the taste of the rulers of their time.

4. Why did Ali (عليه السلام) and the Ahl al-Bayt not argue using this Hadith?

Some say: "If the Hadith of Ghadir existed with such greatness, why did Ali (عليه السلام) himself, his Ahl al-Bayt, his companions, and his devotees not argue using it in times of need? Would it not have been better for them to cite such an important document to prove Ali's (عليه السلام) rightfulness?"

This objection also stems from a lack of comprehensive knowledge of Islamic books, including Hadith, history, and Tafsir. In the books of Sunni scholars, many instances are recorded where Ali (عليه السلام) himself, the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), or the devotees of this school argued using the Hadith of Ghadir:

Among them is Ali (عليه السلام) himself on the Day of the Shura (Consultation), according to the narration of Khatib al-Khwarizmi al-Hanafi in:

[1]: Vol. 1, p. 55 and p. 58. [2]: Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal, vol. 1, pp. 84, 88, 118, 119, 152, 331, 281, 370.

Al-Manaqib from Amir ibn Wathilah, who narrates: "On the Day of the Shura, I was in that house with Ali (عليه السلام) and I heard him saying to the members of the Shura: 'I will present a firm proof to you that neither the Arabs nor the non-Arabs have the power to change: I adjure you by Allah, is there anyone among you who called upon God's oneness before me?' (He then enumerated the spiritual honors of the family of the Message until he reached this point): 'I adjure you by Allah, is there anyone among you besides me for whom the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "Whomsoever I am his Master, Ali is his Master. O Allah, befriend his friend and help his helper; let those present convey it to those absent"?' They all said: 'No.'" [1] This narration was recorded by Hamuwayni in Fara'id al-Simtayn in chapter 58, as well as by Ibn Hatim in Al-Durr al-Nazim, Darqutni, Ibn 'Uqdah, and Ibn Abi al-Hadid in Sharh Nahj al-Balaghah.

We also read that Ali (عليه السلام), according to the narration of Fara'id al-Simtayn in chapter 58, argued using the event of Ghadir in the mosque in the presence of the crowd during the days of Uthman. He also explicitly argued using this narration in Kufa against those who denied the explicit text (Nass) regarding his immediate Caliphate from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family).

According to the report in Al-Ghadir, this Hadith was narrated by four companions and fourteen Successors (Tabi'un) according to famous Sunni sources.

Furthermore, on the Day of the Battle of Jamal, according to the narration of Hakim in the book Al-Mustadrak, vol. 3, p. 371, he argued with it against Talhah.

Also, on the Day of the Battle of Siffin, according to the narration of Sulaym ibn Qays al-Hilali, Ali (عليه السلام) argued using this Hadith in his camp before a group of the Emigrants (Muhajirun), the Helpers (Ansar), and people gathered from all around. Twelve of the "Badriyyun" (those who had participated in the Battle of Badr with the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)) stood up and testified that they had heard this Hadith from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)! After Ali (عليه السلام), the Lady of Islam Fatima al-Zahra (عليها السلام), Imam Hassan, Imam Hussain, Abdullah ibn Ja'far, Ammar Yasir, Qays ibn Sa'd, Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz, and the Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun all cited it. Even Amr ibn al-As, in a letter he wrote to Mu'awiyah to prove that he was well aware of the truths regarding Ali's (عليه السلام) position and Mu'awiyah's situation, explicitly reminded him of the issue of Ghadir, and Khatib al-Khwarizmi al-Hanafi narrated it in the book Al-Manaqib, p. 124. (Those who wish for more details and awareness of the various sources of these narrations regarding the arguments of Ali (عليه السلام), the Ahl al-Bayt, and a group of companions and non-companions using the Hadith of Ghadir can refer to the book Al-Ghadir, vol. 1, pp. 159 to 213. The late Allamah Amini narrated arguments using this Hadith from "22 individuals" among the companions and others in various instances).

5. What is the meaning of the last sentence of the verse?

They say: "If this verse is related to the appointment of Ali (عليه السلام) to the Caliphate and Wilayah and the story of Ghadir Khumm, then what connection can this last sentence—which says: 'Indeed, Allah does not guide the disbelieving people' (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكَافِرِينَ)—have with this issue?"

To answer this objection, it is sufficient to know that "Kufr" (disbelief) in the literal sense, as well as in the language of the Quran, means denial, opposition, and abandonment. Sometimes it is applied to the denial of God or the Prophethood of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), and sometimes to the denial of or opposition to other commands. In Surah Al-Imran, verse 97, regarding Hajj, we read: "And whoever disbelieves (refuses), then indeed Allah is free of need of the worlds" (وَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعَالَمِينَ). This means those who trample upon the command of Hajj and oppose it do not harm God; Allah is independent of all worlds. In Surah al-Baqarah, verse 102, the word "Kufr" is applied to sorcerers and those tainted by sorcery (وَمَا يُعَلِّمَانِ مِنْ أَحَدٍ حَتَّى يَقُولَا إِنَّمَا نَحْنُ فِتْنَةٌ فَلَا تَكْفُرْ). Furthermore, in verse 22 of Surah Ibrahim, we see that Satan, on the Day of Resurrection, explicitly expresses his loathing for those who followed and obeyed him, saying to them: "You made me a partner to God in obeying His commands, and today I disbelieve in what you associated me with before" (إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ). Therefore, applying "Kufr" to the opponents of the issue of Wilayah and leadership is not surprising.

6. Are two Guardians possible at the same time?

Another pretext mentioned for evading this Mutawatir Hadith and the verse under discussion is that if the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) had appointed Ali (عليه السلام) to Wilayah, leadership, and Caliphate at Ghadir Khumm, it would necessitate the existence of two leaders and two guides at the same time! However, attention to the specific conditions and circumstances of the time of the verse's revelation and the Hadith's occurrence, as well as the evidence within the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) speech, completely removes this pretext. For we know that this event occurred in the final months of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) life, while he was conveying the final instructions to the people, especially since he explicitly said:

"I shall soon depart from among you and I leave two weighty things among you."

Someone who speaks thus is clearly seeking to appoint his successor and is planning for the future, not for the present time. Therefore, it is clear that he did not mean the existence of two leaders and two guides at the same time.

An interesting point is that while some Sunni scholars raise this objection, others have raised an objection exactly opposite to it: that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) designated the Wilayah and Caliphate of Ali (عليه السلام) but did not clarify its date; what prevents this Wilayah and Caliphate from being after the other three Caliphs?! Truly, it is astonishing; some fall off one side of the roof and some off the other, and prejudices prevent them from focusing on the text of the matter. Someone must ask them: if the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) wanted to appoint his fourth Caliph and was thinking of the future of the Muslims, then why did he not announce his first, second, and third Caliphs—who preceded him and whose appointment was more necessary—during the ceremony of Ghadir?! Once again, we repeat our previous statement and conclude this discussion: if specific views were not at play, all these objections regarding this verse and this Hadith would not have been made, just as they have not been made in other cases.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۖ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٨)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel and what was sent down to you from your Lord.’ Surely many of them will be increased in rebellion and unfaith by what has been sent down to you from your Lord. So do not grieve for the faithless lot. (68)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـُٔونَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٩)
Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Sabaeans, and the Christians—those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (69)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that a group of Jews came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and first asked: "Do you not acknowledge that the Torah is from Allah?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله replied in the affirmative.

They said: "We also accept the Torah, but we do not believe in anything other than it." (In reality, they meant that the Torah is the common ground between us and you, but the Quran is a book that only you believe in; therefore, it is better that we accept the Torah and reject everything else!) The first verse was revealed and provided them with an answer.

Commentary

As we have read in the commentary of the verses of this Surah so far, a significant portion has revolved around the obstructions, dialogues, questions, and objections of the People of the Book (Jews and Christians). This verse points to another aspect of these discussions and responds to their weak logic—those who wanted to accept the Torah as a book agreed upon by both Muslims and Jews while setting aside the Quran as a book of dispute.

Thus, addressing them, it says: "O People of the Book! You have no standing until you observe the Torah and the Gospel and all the heavenly books that have been sent down to you without discrimination or distinction" (قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَتَّى تُقِيمُوا التَّوْراةَ وَ الْإِنْجِيلَ وَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

This is because, as we have stated, these books all originate from a single Source and their fundamental principles are one, even though the final heavenly book is the most complete and comprehensive of them and is, for this reason, mandatory to follow. Furthermore, in the previous books, there are numerous glad tidings regarding the final book, namely the Quran.
