They claim to accept the Torah and the Gospel; if they are truthful in this claim, they must also accept these glad tidings, and when they find those signs in the Quran, they must bow in submission before it.

The above verse says: Mere claims are not enough; you must practically uphold these heavenly books. Moreover, it is not a matter of "our" book and "your" book; what matters are the heavenly books and what has come from Allah. So how can you, with such weak logic, ignore the final book?

However, the Quran once again points to the condition of the majority of them, saying: "Many of them not only fail to take heed from these verses and find guidance, but because of their spirit of stubbornness, their rebellion and disbelief are increased" (وَ لَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ طُغْياناً وَ كُفْراً). Such is the inverse effect of the verses of truth and balanced words upon diseased minds and hearts filled with stubbornness! At the end of the verse, He comforts His Prophet in the face of the persistence of this deviant majority and says: "Do not grieve over the disbelieving people" (فَلا تَأْسَ عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ‌) [1], for the harm of it will return to them and will not harm you. It is obvious that the contents of this verse are not exclusive to the Jewish people; Muslims too, if they content themselves only with the claim of Islam and do not uphold the principles of the teachings of the Prophets, especially their own heavenly book, will have no standing or value before Allah, nor in their individual and social lives, and will always be abased, subjugated, and defeated.

In the next verse, He emphasizes this truth once again, saying: "All peoples, nations, and followers of all religions without exception—including Muslims, Jews, Sabians [2], and Christians—will be among the saved, and shall have no fear for their future nor grief for their past, provided they have faith in Allah and the Last Day and perform righteous deeds" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ الَّذِينَ هادُوا وَ الصَّابِئُونَ وَ النَّصارى‌ مَنْ آمَنَ بِاللَّهِ وَ الْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ‌).

This verse is, in fact, a crushing response to those who consider salvation to be under the protection of a specific nationality and wish to discriminate between the commands of the Prophets, mixing religious invitations with ethnic prejudice. The verse says: The path to salvation lies exclusively in setting aside such talk.

As we noted under the commentary of verse 62 of Surah Al-Baqarah—the content of which is nearly identical to the above verse—some individuals have tried to use a sophistical argument to take the above verse as evidence for the school of "Universal Peace" (Solh-e Koll), considering all followers of religions to be saved, thereby ignoring the philosophy of the revelation of heavenly books one after another, which aims at advancing the world of humanity on the path of gradual perfection.

However, as we have said, the verse specifies this truth with the expression "performed righteous deeds" (عَمِلَ صالِحاً), indicating that regarding the difference in religions, one must act according to the latest law. This is because acting upon abrogated laws is not a "righteous deed"; rather, a righteous deed is acting according to the existing laws and the final law. (For a more detailed and comprehensive explanation regarding this verse, please refer to Volume 1, pages 191 to 200).

Furthermore, this possibility is also acceptable in the interpretation of the verse: that the phrase "whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day and performs righteous deeds" applies only to the Jews, Christians, and Sabians, because "those who believe" (the Muslims) mentioned at the beginning of the verse do not need this condition [in the same context of conversion]. Thus, the meaning of the verse becomes:

The believers and Muslims—and likewise the Jews, Christians, and Sabians, provided they believe and accept Islam and perform righteous deeds—will all be among the saved and successful, and the religious backgrounds of individuals will have no effect in this regard; the path is open to everyone (reflect carefully).

[^1]: La ta'sa is from the root asa, meaning grief and sorrow. [^2]: Sabians: They are said to be the followers of John (Yahya), or Noah, or Abraham; this was discussed in detail in Volume 1, page 197.

Ayahs

لَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا ۖ كُلَّمَا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا۟ وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ (٧٠)
Certainly We took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We sent apostles to them. Whenever an apostle brought them that which was not to their liking, they would impugn a part of them, and a part they would slay. (70)
وَحَسِبُوٓا۟ أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ ثُمَّ تَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٧١)
They supposed there would be no testing, so they became blind and deaf. Thereafter Allah accepted their repentance, yet [again] many of them became blind and deaf, and Allah watches what they do. (71)

Commentary

In previous verses of Surah Al-Baqarah and the beginning of this very Surah, reference was made to the firm covenant that Allah had taken from the Children of Israel. In this verse, He reminds them of this covenant once again, saying: "We took a covenant from the Children of Israel and We sent Messengers to them" to guide them and demand fulfillment of this covenant (لَقَدْ أَخَذْنا مِيثاقَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ وَ أَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا).

As mentioned in the first volume, it appears that this covenant is the same one referred to in verse 93 of Surah Al-Baqarah—namely, the pledge to act upon what Allah had revealed to them [^1]. He then adds that they not only failed to act upon this covenant, but "whenever a Messenger came to them with what their souls did not desire, they engaged in the most severe struggle against him; a group they denied, and a group they would kill," specifically those whose influence they could not prevent through mere denial (كُلَّما جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ بِما لا تَهْوى‌ أَنْفُسُهُمْ فَرِيقاً كَذَّبُوا وَ فَرِيقاً يقتلون‌).

This is the way and custom of deviated, self-centered individuals: instead of following their leaders, they insist that the leaders follow their whims and desires; otherwise, they grant them no right to leadership or even the right to life.

In the above phrase, "kadhdhabu" (they denied) appears in the past tense, while "yaqtulun" (they kill) appears in the present/future tense. Aside from maintaining the rhythmic harmony with the endings of the preceding and succeeding verses—which are all in the present tense—the reason for this may be that the present tense signifies continuity. It aims to express the persistence of this spirit within them: that denying and killing Prophets was not an accidental occurrence in their history, but had become a systematic program and school of thought [^2]. In the following verse, He points to their misplaced arrogance in the face of all this rebellion and crime, saying:

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 1, p. 246.

[^2]: In reality, the phrase "فَرِيقاً كَذَّبُوا وَ فَرِيقاً يَقْتُلُونَ", as stated in Majma' al-Bayan and other commentaries, was originally "kadhdhabu wa qatalu" (they denied and they killed) or "yukadhdhibuna wa yaqtulun" (they deny and they kill).

"And they thought that no trial or punishment would afflict them." As explicitly stated in other verses, they considered themselves a superior race and referred to themselves as the children of God! (وَ حَسِبُوا أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ).

Ultimately, this dangerous pride and sense of superiority fell like a veil over their eyes and ears, and because of it, "they became blind to seeing the signs of Allah and deaf to hearing the words of truth!" (فَعَمُوا وَ صَمُّوا). However, when they witnessed examples of divine punishment and the grim consequences of their actions, they felt regret and repented. They realized that divine threats are serious and that they are by no means a superior race; thus, Allah accepted their repentance (ثُمَّ تابَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ‌).

But this awakening, regret, and repentance did not last long. Once again, rebellion, defiance, and the rejection of truth and justice began. Once more, the veils of negligence—which are the effects of drowning in sin—were cast over their eyes and ears, "and again they became blind to seeing the signs of truth and deaf to hearing the words of truth," and this state encompassed many of them (ثُمَّ عَمُوا وَ صَمُّوا كَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ‌).

Perhaps placing the word "amu" (they became blind) before "sammu" (they became deaf) indicates that they must first see the signs of Allah and the miracles of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and then listen to his commands.

The mention of "kathirun minhum" (many of them) after the repetition of the phrase "amu wa sammu" serves as an explanation for both phrases. That is, the state of negligence, ignorance, deafness, and blindness toward the truths was not universal; rather, there was always a righteous minority among them. This is clear evidence that the Quranic verses regarding the Jews are by no means racial or ethnic in nature, but are directed solely at their actions.

Is the repetition of the phrase "amu wa sammu" general and for emphasis? Or does it refer to two different events? Some commentators believe these two phrases refer to two distinct historical episodes that befell the Children of Israel: one during the invasion by the people of Babylon and the other during the invasions by the Persians and Romans, to which the Quran briefly alludes at the beginning of Surah Al-Isra (Bani Israel).

It is also possible that they repeatedly fell into this state and, upon seeing the grim results of their actions, would repent, only to break their repentance again—meaning it was not limited to just two occurrences.

At the end of the verse, with a short and meaningful sentence, He says: "Allah is never heedless of their actions and sees all that they do" (وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما يَعْمَلُونَ‌).

Ayahs

لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (٧٢)
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ But the Messiah had said, ‘O Children of Israel! Worship Allah, my Lord, and your Lord. Indeed whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his refuge shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers will not have any helpers.’ (72)
لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ ۘ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’ while there is no god except the One God. If they do not relinquish what they say, there shall befall the faithless among them a painful punishment. (73)
أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧٤)
Will they not repent to Allah and plead to Him for forgiveness? Yet Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (74)

Commentary

Following the discussions regarding the deviations of the Jews in the previous verses, these verses and the ones that follow speak of the deviations of the Christians. First, it addresses the most significant deviation in Christianity, namely the issue of the "Divinity of Christ" and the "Trinity of the Worshiped," stating:

"Certainly they have disbelieved who say, 'Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary'" (لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ‌).

What disbelief could be greater than considering Allah—Who is infinite in every respect—to be one and united with a creature who is limited in every respect, and attributing the qualities of the created to the Creator?! This is while the Messiah عليه السلام explicitly said to the Children of Israel: "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord" (وَ قالَ الْمَسِيحُ يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَ رَبَّكُمْ‌).

In this way, he declared his dissociation from any form of polytheism (shirk) or exaggeration (ghuluww) regarding himself, and introduced himself as a creature among the creatures of Allah, just like others.

Furthermore, to emphasize this point and remove any ambiguity or misunderstanding, the Messiah added: "Indeed, whoever associates others with Allah, Allah has forbidden him Paradise, and his abode is the Fire" (إِنَّهُ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَ مَأْواهُ النَّارُ).

And again, for further emphasis and to prove the fact that polytheism and exaggeration are a form of manifest injustice, he told them: "And for the wrongdoers there will be no helpers" (وَ ما لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنْصارٍ).

As we have previously pointed out, the history of Christianity shows that the Trinity did not exist in the early centuries, especially during the era of the Messiah عليه السلام. Even in the current Gospels, despite all the distortions that have occurred within them, the slightest mention of the Trinity is not found, and Christian researchers themselves admit to this fact. Therefore, what is seen in the above verse regarding the insistence of the Messiah عليه السلام on the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) is a matter that is consistent even with existing Christian sources and is considered one of the proofs of the greatness of the Quran. [^1]

Additionally, it should be noted that what is discussed in this verse is the issue of exaggeration and the unity of the Messiah with God—in other words, "Monotheism within Trinity." However, in the next verse, it refers to the issue of the "plurality of gods" from the Christian perspective—that is, "Trinity within Monotheism"—stating: "Certainly they have disbelieved who say, 'Allah is the third of three'" (لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ).

Many commentators, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Sheikh Tusi in al-Tibyan, Fakhr Razi, and Qurtubi in their respective commentaries, have imagined that the previous verse concerns a sect of Christians called the "Jacobites" who believe God is united with the Messiah عليه السلام, while this verse is about other sects called the "Melkites" and "Nestorians" who believe in three gods. However, as we said before, this perception of Christianity does not align with reality, because the belief in the Trinity is universal among all Christians, just as the belief in the Oneness and Unity of God is definitive and certain among us Muslims. The difference is that while they truly consider the gods to be three, they also consider Him truly One; in their belief, three real units constitute one real unit!

[^1]: For more explanation on this subject and regarding the issue of the Trinity and Unity in Trinity, please refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 4, pages 220 to 230.

[^2]: Uqnum (Hypostasis) means origin and essence, and its plural is Aqanim.

The two verses above apparently refer to two different aspects of these two propositions: the first verse refers to the unity of the three gods, and the second verse refers to their plurality. Placing these two statements back-to-back is, in fact, an allusion to one of the clear reasons for the invalidity of their belief: how can Allah sometimes be truly one with the Messiah (and the Holy Spirit) and sometimes truly three things? Is it rational for three to equal one?

What supports this reality is that we find no group among the Christians who do not believe in three gods. [^1] Then, the Quran decisively says in response to them: "And there is no god except one God" (وَ ما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا إِلهٌ واحِدٌ).

Specifically, the mention of the word "min" (من) before "ilah" (إله) provides even greater emphasis in negating other deities.

Once again, with a severe and emphatic tone, He warns them that "if they do not desist from what they say, there will surely afflict those who disbelieve among them a painful punishment" (وَ إِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهُوا عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ‌).

The word "min" in "minhum" (among them) is, according to some, explanatory (bayaniyyah), but it appears to be for "partitioning" (tab'id), referring in reality to those who persist in their disbelief and polytheism and do not return after the Quran's call to Monotheism, not those who repent and return.

In Tafsir al-Manar, a story is quoted from the book Izhar al-Haqq which is not out of place to mention here, as it shows how the Trinity and Monotheism of the Christians are incomprehensible. The author of that book says: Three people converted to Christianity. A priest taught them the essential beliefs of Christianity, including the doctrine of the Trinity.

One day, an enthusiast of Christianity came to the priest and asked about those who had recently converted. The priest, with great pleasure, pointed to those three. He immediately asked: "Have they learned anything of our essential beliefs?" The priest said with courage and emphasis: "Yes." Then, as an example, he called one of them to test him in the presence of the guest. The priest asked: "What do you know about the Trinity?"

He replied: "You taught me that the gods are three: one is in heaven, another is on earth born from the womb of Mary, and the third descended in the form of a dove upon the second god at the age of thirty!" The priest became angry and threw him out, saying he understood nothing. He then called the second person. In response to the question about the Trinity, he said: "You taught me that the gods were three, but one of them was hanged; therefore, we now have only two gods!" The priest's anger increased, and he threw him out as well. He then called the third one, who was more intelligent and serious in memorizing religious beliefs, and asked him the same question. He said respectfully: "My leader! I have perfectly memorized what you taught me and, by the blessing of the Messiah, I have understood it well. You said: The One God is three, and the three Gods are one. One of them was hanged and died, and therefore they all died because He was one with the rest, and thus, there is no god now!"

In the third verse, He invites them to repent from this blasphemous belief so that Allah may include them in His pardon and forgiveness. Thus He says: "Will they not turn to Allah and ask His forgiveness after all this, while Allah is Forgiving and Merciful?" (أَ فَلا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُ وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

[^1]: It is mentioned in some narrations and some histories that a minority exists among Christians who do not believe in three gods but only in the unity of Jesus with God, but today we see little trace or sign of them.

Ayahs

مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٧٥)
The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly, [other] apostles have passed before him, and his mother was a truthful one. Both of them would eat food. Look how We clarify the signs for them, and yet, look, how they go astray! (75)
قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٧٦)
Say, ‘Do you worship, besides Allah, what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm, while Allah—He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing?!’ (76)
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا۟ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٧٧)
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do not unduly exceed the bounds in your religion and do not follow the fancies of a people who went astray in the past, and led many astray, and [themselves] strayed from the right path.’ (77)

Commentary

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the Christians' exaggeration (ghuluww) concerning Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him) and their belief in his divinity, these verses invalidate their belief in a few short sentences with clear arguments. First, it says: What difference was there between Christ and other prophets that led you to believe in his divinity? Christ, the son of Mary, was also a messenger of Allah, and before him, other messengers and envoys from Allah had come (مَا الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ).

If receiving a mission from Allah is a proof of divinity and shirk (polytheism), then why do you not hold this view regarding the other prophets?

However, we know that deviated Christians are never satisfied with considering Jesus (peace be upon him) merely a messenger of Allah; rather, their general belief currently is that they consider him the son of God and, in a sense, God Himself, who came to redeem the sins of humanity (not for their guidance and leadership). Therefore, they give him the title "Redeemer" (one who is sacrificed for the sins of mankind).

Then, to support this statement, it says: His mother was a very truthful woman (وَأُمُّهُ صِدِّيقَةٌ).

This points to the fact that, firstly, how can someone who has a mother, was nurtured in a woman's womb, and has all these needs be God? Secondly, if his mother is respected, it is because she was aligned with him in the path of the mission of Christ (peace be upon him) and supported his message; thus, she was a special servant of Allah and should not be worshipped, as is common among Christians who bow before her statue to the point of worship.

Next, it points to another reason for denying the Lordship of Christ (peace be upon him), saying:

He and his mother both used to eat food (كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ الطَّعَامَ).

One who is so needy that if food does not reach him for a few days, he is unable to move—how can he be God or in the same rank as God?

At the end of the verse, it points to the clarity of these arguments on one hand, and their stubbornness, persistence, and ignorance in the face of these manifest proofs on the other, saying: Behold how We clarify the signs for them, then behold how they are turned away (انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ الْآيَاتِ ثُمَّ انْظُرْ أَنَّى يُؤْفَكُونَ) [1]. Therefore, the repetition of "Behold" (انظر) in these two phrases indicates that, on one hand, look at these clear signs which are sufficient to alert anyone, and on the other hand, look at their negative and astonishing reactions which are surprising to everyone.

In the next verse, to complete the previous reasoning, it says: You know that Christ (peace be upon him) himself was entirely composed of human needs and did not possess power over his own benefit or harm, let alone possessing power over your benefit or harm. "Say: Do you worship besides Allah that which possesses for you neither harm nor benefit?" (قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا).

For this very reason, he was repeatedly caught in the hands of enemies, or his friends were caught, and if the grace of Allah had not encompassed him, he could not have taken a single step.

Finally, He warns them not to think that Allah does not hear their improper words or is unaware of what is within them: "And Allah is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing."

[1]: Yu'fakun is from the root ifk, which originally means to turn away from something. Ma'fuk is said of someone who has been kept back from the truth, even if it is due to his own negligence. Since a lie turns a person away from the truth, it is called ifk.

(وَاللَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ) It is interesting that the issue of Christ being human and his material and physical needs—which the Quran emphasizes in these and other verses—has become one of the greatest problems for Christians claiming his divinity. They struggle greatly to justify it, sometimes finding themselves forced to posit two aspects for Christ: the aspect of Lahut (divinity) and the aspect of Nasut (humanity). From the perspective of Lahut, he is the son of God and God Himself, and from the perspective of Nasut, he is a body and a creature of Allah, and similar justifications which are the best sign of the weakness and incorrectness of their logic.

Attention should also be paid to the fact that in the verse, the word "ma" (what/that which) is used instead of "man" (who), which is usually mentioned for non-rational beings. This expression might be to include other deities and idols such as stone and wood in the generality of the sentence, saying: If worshipping a creature were permissible, then the idol-worship of idolaters should also be considered permissible, because in being creatures, all are equal. In reality, faith in the divinity of Christ (peace be upon him) is a type of idol-worship, not God-worship.

In the following verse, He commands the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) that, following the clarification of the People of the Book's error regarding exaggeration about Divine prophets, he should invite them with clear arguments to officially return from this path, saying: "Say: O People of the Book, do not exceed the limits in your religion beyond the truth, and do not say anything but the truth" (قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ) [1].

[1]: La taghlu is from the root ghuluww, meaning to exceed the truth. However, when it concerns exceeding someone's status and rank, it is called ghuluww; if it is in price and value, it is called ghala'; and if it is regarding an arrow, they say ghulw (rhyming with dalw). The fact that boiling is called ghaliyan and an exceptionally rebellious animal is called ghulwa' all stem from this same root. Some believe that ghuluww is used for both excess (ifrat) and deficiency (tafrit), while others restrict it to excess and call its opposite taqsir (deficiency).

Of course, the exaggeration of the Christians is clear, but regarding the exaggeration of the Jews—since the address "O People of the Book" includes them as well—it is not unlikely that it refers to what they said about Ezra (Uzair), considering him the son of God. Since the source of exaggeration is often following the whims and desires of the misguided, to complete this statement, He says: Do not follow the desires of a people who went astray before you and led many astray and strayed from the right path (وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا أَهْوَاءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا عَنْ سَوَاءِ السَّبِيلِ). This sentence actually points to something reflected in Christian history: that the issue of the Trinity and exaggeration about Christ (peace be upon him) did not exist among them in the first centuries of Christianity. Rather, when Indian idolaters and their like joined the religion of Christ (peace be upon him), they added remnants of their former religion—which was trinity and polytheism—to Christianity. Thus, we see that the Indian Trinity (faith in the three gods Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva) historically preceded the Christian Trinity, and in reality, this is a reflection of that. In verse 30 of Surah At-Tawbah, after mentioning the exaggeration of the Jews and Christians regarding Ezra and Christ (peace be upon him), we read: يُضَاهِئُونَ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ — "They imitate the saying of those who disbelieved before."

In this phrase, the sentence dallu (they strayed) is repeated twice regarding the disbelievers from whom the People of the Book borrowed their exaggeration. This repetition might be for emphasis, or because they were already astray, and after they led others astray through their propaganda, they fell into a new misguidance. For one who strives to lead others into misguidance is indeed the most misguided of all, as he has wasted his energies in the path of his own misery and that of others, and has also carried the burden of the responsibility for the sins of others. Would someone who is placed on the straight path ever be willing to carry the burden of others' sins in addition to his own?

Ayahs

لُعِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ دَاوُۥدَ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (٧٨)
The faithless among the Children of Israel were cursed on the tongue of David and Jesus son of Mary. That, because they would disobey and they used to commit transgression. (78)
كَانُوا۟ لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَن مُّنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٧٩)
They would not forbid one another from the wrongs that they committed. Surely, evil is what they had been doing. (79)
تَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ أَن سَخِطَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَفِى ٱلْعَذَابِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨٠)
You see many of them fraternizing with the faithless. Surely evil is what they have sent ahead for their souls, as Allah is displeased with them and they shall remain in punishment [forever]. (80)

Commentary

In these verses, in order to prevent the People of the Book from blindly imitating their predecessors, Allah points to their grim fate and says: "Those who disbelieved from among the Children of Israel were cursed by the tongue of David and Jesus, son of Mary; and these two great Prophets asked Allah to remove them from His mercy" (لُعِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى‌ لِسانِ داوُدَ وَ عِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ). Regarding why only the names of these two Prophets صلى الله عليه وآله are mentioned, commentators have offered several possibilities. Sometimes it is said that the reason is that these two were the most prominent Prophets after Moses عليه السلام. At other times, it is said that many of the People of the Book took pride in being the descendants of David; thus, the Quran declares with this phrase that David was disgusted by those who chose the path of disbelief and rebellion. Some have said that this verse refers to two historical events that provoked the anger of these two great Prophets, leading them to curse a group of the Children of Israel: David cursed the inhabitants of the coastal city of Ayla, known as the "Companions of the Sabbath" (whose story will appear in Surah Al-A'raf), and Jesus cursed a group of his followers who continued the path of denial and opposition even after the descent of the Heavenly Table (Ma'idah).

In any case, the verse indicates that being part of the race of the Children of Israel or being among the followers of Christ will not save anyone as long as there is no harmony with their programs; rather, these very Prophets expressed their hatred and loathing toward such individuals.

The final sentence of the verse emphasizes this point, saying: "This declaration of hatred and dissociation was because they were sinful and transgressors" (ذلِكَ بِما عَصَوْا وَ كانُوا يَعْتَدُونَ). Furthermore, they felt no social responsibility whatsoever and did not forbid one another from wrongdoing; even a group of the righteous among them practically encouraged the sinners through silence and compromise (كانُوا لا يَتَناهَوْنَ عَنْ مُنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ). Consequently, the program of their actions was extremely ugly and detestable (لَبِئْسَ ما كانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ).

Regarding the commentary of this verse, narrations have been cited from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام that are very instructive.

In a hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: «لتامرن بالمعروف و لتنهون عن المنكر و لتاخذن على يد السفيه و لتاطرنه على الحق اطرا، او ليضربن اللَّه قلوب بعضكم على بعض و يلعنكم كما لعنهم» "You must certainly enjoin what is good and forbid what is evil, and take the hand of the ignorant and invite them toward the truth; otherwise, Allah will make your hearts like one another and remove you from His mercy just as He removed them from His mercy!" [1]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the commentary of (كانُوا لا يَتَناهَوْنَ عَنْ مُنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ), it is narrated: «اما انهم لم يكونوا يدخلون مداخلهم و لا يجلسون مجالسهم و لكن كانوا اذا لقوهم ضحكوا فى وجوههم و انسوا بهم» [2]: "Indeed, this group whom Allah has criticized never entered the places of the sinners nor sat in their gatherings; rather, it was only that when they met them, they would smile in their faces and be friendly with them."

In the following verse, Allah points to another of their wrongdoings, saying: "You see many of them making alliances of friendship and love with the disbelievers" (تَرى‌ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا). It is obvious that their friendship was not a simple one, but rather a friendship mixed with various sins and the encouragement of their wrong actions and thoughts. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "How evil are the deeds they sent forth for their Resurrection, deeds whose result was the wrath and anger of Allah, and they shall abide eternally in the divine punishment" (لَبِئْسَ ما قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ أَنْ سَخِطَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ فِي الْعَذابِ هُمْ خالِدُونَ).

Regarding who is meant by "those who disbelieved" (alladhina kafaru) in this verse, some have suggested it refers to the polytheists of Mecca with whom the Jews had formed alliances. Others have suggested it refers to the tyrants and oppressors with whom the Jews formed friendships in past ages. A hadith narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام in this regard also supports this meaning, where he says: «يتولون الملوك الجبارين و يزينون لهم اهوائهم ليصيبوا من دنياهم» [1]: "These were the ones who befriended tyrannical kings and beautified their whimsical desires in their eyes so that they might draw near to them and benefit from their worldly wealth!" There is no obstacle to the verse referring to both meanings, or rather, something even more general than them.

[1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan under the verse; and in Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 4, p. 2250, a hadith with the same theme is narrated from Tirmidhi. [2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 492; and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 661.

Ayah

وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلنَّبِىِّ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا ٱتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨١)
Had they believed in Allah and the Prophet and what has been sent down to him, they would not have taken them for allies. But most of them are transgressors. (81)

Commentary

In this verse, He shows them the way of salvation from this wrong and incorrect path.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

If they truly had faith in Allah, the Prophet, and what was revealed to him, they would never have submitted to the friendship of strangers and the enemies of Allah, nor would they have chosen them as their support (وَلَوْ كَانُوا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَالنَّبِيِّ وَمَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا اتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءَ). But unfortunately, those among them who obey Allah's command are few, and many of them have stepped outside the circle of Allah's command and have taken the path of defiance (وَلَكِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ). It is clear that the meaning of "the Prophet" (النبی) here is the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, as this word is used with this meaning in various verses throughout the Holy Quran, and this subject is seen in dozens of Quranic verses.

There is another possibility in the commentary of the verse: that the pronoun "they were" (كانوا) refers to the polytheists and idolaters. That is, if these polytheists—who are liked and trusted by the Jews—had believed in the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Quran, the Jews would never have chosen them as their friends. This would be a clear sign of their [the Jews'] deviation and defiance, for while claiming to follow Heavenly Books, they choose idolaters as friends as long as they remain polytheists, but as soon as they turn toward Allah and the Heavenly Books, they distance themselves from them! However, the first commentary is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verses, and according to it, all pronouns return to a single referent (namely, the Jews).

Beginning of the Seventh Juz' of the Quran

Ayahs

۞ لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ ٱلنَّاسِ عَدَٰوَةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۖ وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُم مَّوَدَّةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٨٢)
Surely You will find the most hostile of all people towards the faithful to be the Jews and the polytheists, and surely you will find the nearest of them in affection to the faithful to be those who say ‘We are Christians.’ That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they are not arrogant. (82)
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ تَرَىٰٓ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٨٣)
When they hear what has been revealed to the Apostle, you see their eyes fill with tears because of the truth that they recognize. They say, ‘Our Lord, we believe; so write us down among the witnesses. (83)
وَمَا لَنَا لَا نُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَا جَآءَنَا مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَنَطْمَعُ أَن يُدْخِلَنَا رَبُّنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٤)
Why should we not believe in Allah and the truth that has come to us, eager as we are that our Lord should admit us among the righteous people?’ (84)
فَأَثَـٰبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٥)
So, for what they said, Allah requited them with gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and that is the reward of the virtuous. (85)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٨٦)
But those who are faithless and deny Our signs—they shall be the inmates of hell. (86)

Occasion of Revelation: The First Migrants of Islam

Many commentators, including "Tabarsi" in Majma' al-Bayan, "Fakhr Razi," and the author of Al-Manar in their respective exegeses, have narrated from earlier commentators that these verses were revealed concerning the Negus (Najashi), the ruler of Abyssinia during the time of the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله, and his companions. This matter is detailed in a hadith cited in Tafsir al-Burhan.

What can be gathered from Islamic narrations, history, and the words of commentators in this regard is as follows:

In the early years of the Prophet’s صلي الله عليه وآله mission and his public invitation, Muslims were in a severe minority. The Quraysh had advised the Arab tribes that each should place their own kin who had believed in the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله under intense pressure. Consequently, every Muslim was severely persecuted by their own tribe and clan.

At that time, the number of Muslims was not sufficient to engage in a "liberation Jihad." To preserve this small group and to establish a base for Muslims outside the Hijaz, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله ordered them to migrate. He chose Abyssinia for this purpose, saying: "There is a righteous ruler there who prevents oppression and tyranny; go there until Allah provides a suitable opportunity for us."

The Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was referring to the Negus (Najashi was a general title, like "Khosrow," given to all kings of Abyssinia, but the name of the Negus contemporary to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله was Ashama, which in the Abyssinian language means 'gift' or 'bestowal').

Eleven men and four women among the Muslims set out for Abyssinia, traveling by sea after hiring a small boat. This occurred in the month of Rajab in the fifth year of the Prophetic mission, and this migration was named the First Migration.

Shortly thereafter, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام and another group of Muslims went to Abyssinia, forming the core of an organized Islamic community consisting of 82 men and a significant number of women and children.

The plan for this migration was very painful for the idolaters, as they clearly saw that it would not be long before they faced a powerful, organized community of Muslims who had gradually accepted Islam and moved to the safe haven of Abyssinia. To disrupt this situation, they took action and chose two clever, active, and deceitful young men—namely, 'Amr ibn al-'As and 'Umarah ibn al-Walid—to undermine the position of the Muslims in Abyssinia, sending them there with many gifts.

These two drank wine on the ship and fell into conflict with each other, but they eventually entered Abyssinia to carry out their plan. Through various means, they gained an audience with the Negus, having previously won over his courtiers with expensive gifts and securing their promise of support.

'Amr ibn al-'As began his speech by saying to the Negus:

"We are the envoys of the elders of Mecca. A number of foolish youths among us have raised the flag of opposition, turned away from the religion of their ancestors, spoken ill of our gods, incited turmoil and sedition, and sown the seeds of discord among the people. They have exploited the situation of your land and taken refuge here. We fear they will engage in sabotage here as well. It is best that you hand them over to us so we may return them to their home..." After saying this, they presented the gifts they had brought.

The Negus replied: "I cannot speak on this matter until I have contacted the representatives of these refugees in my country. Since this is a religious discussion, religious representatives must also be invited to a session in your presence."

On another day, in an important meeting attended by the Negus's courtiers, a group of Christian scholars, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام as the representative of the Muslims, and the representatives of the Quraysh, the Negus—after listening to the words of the Quraysh envoys—turned to Ja'far and asked him to express his view.

Ja'far, after paying his respects, said: "First, ask them: are we runaway slaves belonging to this group?" 'Amr said: "No, you are free."

Ja'far: "And ask them: do we owe them any debt that they are demanding from us?" 'Amr: "No, we have no such claim against you."

Ja'far: "Have we shed any of your blood for which you seek retribution?" 'Amr: "No, there is no such thing."

Ja'far: "Then what do you want from us, after you tortured and harassed us so much that we left your land, which was a center of oppression and injustice?!" Then Ja'far turned to the Negus and said: "We were an ignorant people; we worshipped idols, ate carrion, committed all kinds of shameful and disgraceful acts, severed ties of kinship, mistreated our neighbors, and the strong among us devoured the weak! But Allah sent a Prophet among us who commanded us to distance ourselves from any likeness or partner to God, to abandon lewdness, forbidden acts, oppression, tyranny, and gambling. He commanded us to pray, give zakat, practice justice and kindness, and help our relatives."

The Negus said: "Jesus Christ was also sent for this very purpose!" Then he asked Ja'far: "Do you have memorized any of the verses revealed to your Prophet?"

Ja'far said: "Yes," and then began to recite Surah Maryam.

Ja'far’s excellent choice of the stirring verses of this Surah, which clear Christ and his mother from all unjust accusations, had a profound effect, to the point that tears of joy flowed from the eyes of the Christian scholars. The Negus cried out: "By God, the signs of truth are evident in these verses!" When 'Amr tried to speak and requested that the Muslims be handed over to him, the Negus raised his hand, struck 'Amr hard across the face, and said: "Be silent! By God, if you say another word in disparagement of this group, I will punish you!" After saying this, he turned to his officers and shouted: "Return their gifts to them and expel them from Abyssinia!" And he said to Ja'far and his companions: "Live in peace in my country!"

This event, in addition to its deep missionary impact in introducing Islam to a group of Abyssinians, caused the Muslims of Mecca to seriously count on this secure base and to send new converts there until the day they would find sufficient strength.

Years passed, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله migrated, and the cause of Islam rose. The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah was signed, and the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله turned toward the conquest of Khaybar. On that day, when the Muslims were overjoyed at breaking the greatest center of Jewish threat, they witnessed from afar a group moving toward the Islamic army. It soon became clear that this group was the migrants of Abyssinia returning to their homeland, at a time when the demonic powers of the enemies had been broken and the seedling of Islam had taken sufficient root.

Upon seeing Ja'far and the Abyssinian migrants, the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله uttered this historic sentence: "I do not know whether I am happier with the victory of Khaybar or the arrival of Ja'far!"

It is said that in addition to the Muslims, eight people from Syria, among whom was a Christian monk who had developed a strong inclination toward Islam, came to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله. After hearing the verses of Surah Ya-Sin, they fell into tears, became Muslims, and said: "How much these verses resemble the true teachings of Christ."

According to a narration cited in Tafsir al-Manar from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, the Negus sent thirty of his best companions to Medina as an expression of his affection for the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله and the religion of Islam. It was they who wept upon hearing the verses of Surah Ya-Sin and accepted Islam. The above verses were revealed to honor these believers. (This occasion of revelation does not contradict the fact that Surah al-Ma'idah was revealed toward the end of the Prophet’s صلي الله عليه وآله life, as that statement refers to the majority of the Surah's verses; there is no obstacle to some verses being revealed during earlier events and being placed in this Surah by the Prophet’s صلي الله عليه وآله command due to relevant themes).

Commentary

The Enmity of the Jews and the Gentleness of the Christians

In these verses, a comparison is made between the Jews and the Christians who were contemporaries of the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله.

In the first verse, the Jews and the polytheists are placed in one rank, and the Christians in another. At the beginning, it says: "The most bitter enemies of the believers are the Jews and the polytheists, and the most affectionate toward the believers are those who claim to be Christians" (لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ النَّاسِ عَداوَةً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الْيَهُودَ وَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا وَ لَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُمْ مَوَدَّةً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّا نَصارى‌).

The history of Islam bears witness to this reality, for in many scenes of anti-Islamic battles, the Jews were involved directly or indirectly and did not refrain from any form of sabotage or enmity. Very few of them embraced Islam, whereas in the Islamic battles, we rarely see Muslims facing Christians, and we also observe many of them joining the ranks of the Muslims.

Then the Quran explains the reason for this difference in spirit and social conduct through several sentences, stating that the Christians contemporary to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله possessed qualities that were not found in the Jews.

First: Among them were a group of scholars who did not strive to conceal the truth as much as the worldly Jewish scholars did (ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ) [^1]. Also, among them were a group of ascetics who walked exactly opposite to the greedy Jews; although they suffered from some deviations, they were still at a higher level than the Jews (وَ رُهْباناً).

Many of them were humble in accepting the truth and did not show arrogance, whereas the majority of the Jews, because they considered themselves a superior race, refused to accept the religion of Islam which had not arisen from the Jewish race (وَ أَنَّهُمْ لا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ).

Furthermore, a group of them (like the companions of Ja'far and a group of Abyssinian Christians), when they heard the verses of the Quran, tears of joy would flow from their eyes because they had found the truth (وَ إِذا سَمِعُوا ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ تَرى‌ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ الدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوا مِنَ الْحَقِ).

And with clarity, courage, and impartiality, they would cry out: "Our Lord! We have believed, so record us among the witnesses of the truth and the companions of Muhammad صلي الله عليه وآله and his helpers" (يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنا آمَنَّا فَاكْتُبْنا مَعَ الشَّاهِدِينَ).

They were so moved by the stirring verses of this Heavenly Book that they would say: "How is it possible for us not to believe in the One God and the truths that have come from Him, while we hope that He will place us among the company of the righteous?" (وَ ما لَنا لا نُؤْمِنُ بِاللَّهِ وَ ما جاءَنا مِنَ الْحَقِّ وَ نَطْمَعُ أَنْ يُدْخِلَنا رَبُّنا مَعَ الْقَوْمِ الصَّالِحِينَ).

Of course, as we mentioned above, this comparison is mostly about the Jews and Christians contemporary to the Prophet of Islam صلي الله عليه وآله. For the Jews, despite possessing a Heavenly Book, had placed themselves in the rank of the polytheists—with whom they shared no religious commonality—due to their excessive attachment to material things. This is while initially, the Jews were considered the heralds of Islam and did not have deviations like the Trinity or the exaggeration found in Christianity; however, intense worldliness completely alienated them from the truth, whereas the Christians of that era were not like that.

But past and contemporary history tells us that Christians of later centuries committed crimes against Islam and Muslims that were no less than those of the Jews.

The long and bloody Crusades of the past and the numerous provocations today by the colonialism of Christian countries against Islam and Muslims are not hidden from anyone. Therefore, one should not consider the above verses as a general law for all Christians. The phrases "إِذا سَمِعُوا ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ..." and what follows are evidence that these verses were revealed concerning a group of Christians contemporary to the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله.

In the next two verses, the fate of these two groups and their reward and punishment are mentioned. First, it says: Those who showed affection toward the believers, surrendered to the divine verses, and clearly declared their faith—Allah rewards them for this with gardens of Paradise beneath which rivers flow, to abide therein forever, and this is the reward of the doers of good (فَأَثابَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِما قالُوا جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ) [^2]. In contrast, those who took the path of enmity, disbelieved, and denied Our signs are the companions of Hellfire (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ).

[^1]: Qissis (Priest) is originally a Syriac word meaning a religious leader and guide for Christians; in Arabic, it is called Qissis, and its plural is Qissisin.

[^2]: Athabahum is derived from the root thawab, which originally means "to return," "to do good," and "to bring benefit to someone."

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحَرِّمُوا۟ طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (٨٧)
O you who have faith! Do not prohibit the good things that Allah has made lawful to you, and do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like the transgressors. (87)
وَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
Eat the lawful and good things Allah has provided you, and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith. (88)
لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ فَكَفَّـٰرَتُهُۥٓ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَـٰكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ كَفَّـٰرَةُ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ إِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ ۚ وَٱحْفَظُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٨٩)
Allah shall not take you to task for what is frivolous in your oaths; but He shall take you to task for what you pledge in earnest. The atonement for it is to feed ten needy persons with the average food you give to your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave. He who cannot afford [any of these] shall fast for three days. That is the atonement for your oaths when you vow. But keep your oaths. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may give thanks. (89)

Circumstances of Revelation: Do Not Exceed the Limits!

Multiple narrations have been cited regarding the revelation of the above verses, including the following:

One day, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله delivered a discourse about the Resurrection and the state of people in that great Divine court. These words shook the people, and a group wept. Following this, a group of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله companions decided to forbid themselves certain pleasures and comforts and devote themselves to worship instead. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, took an oath to sleep less at night and engage in worship; Bilal swore to fast every day; and Uthman ibn Maz'un swore to abstain from sexual relations with his wife and dedicate himself to worship.

One day, the wife of Uthman ibn Maz'un came to Aisha. She was a young and beautiful woman, and Aisha was surprised by her appearance and asked: "Why do you not look after yourself and adorn yourself?" She replied: "For whom should I adorn myself? My husband has left me for some time now and has taken up monasticism." This news reached the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. He ordered all Muslims to come to the mosque. When the people gathered in the mosque, He ascended the pulpit, and after praising and glorifying the Lord, He said: "Why have some of you forbidden the pure things to yourselves? I inform you of my Sunnah (practice); whoever turns away from it is not of me. I sleep part of the night, I have relations with my wives, and I do not fast every day.

Be aware, I never command you to renounce the world like Christian priests and monks, for such matters and cloistering are not in my religion. The monasticism of my Ummah is in Jihad (if you wish to renounce the world, it is better to do so in a constructive way like Jihad...). Do not be hard on yourselves, for a group of those before you perished because they were hard on themselves."

Those who had sworn to abandon these matters stood up and said: "O Prophet! We have taken oaths in this regard; what is our duty concerning our oaths?" The above verses were revealed and provided them with the answer. [^1] It is necessary to mention that some of the aforementioned oaths, such as the one narrated from Uthman ibn Maz'un, were not legitimate because they conflicted with the rights of his wife. However, in the case of Ali's عليه السلام oath regarding staying awake at night and engaging in worship, it was a permissible and allowed matter, although it is understood from the verses that it was better and preferable for such a practice not to continue perpetually. This issue in no way contradicts the status of Ali's عليه السلام infallibility (Ismah), just as we read a similar instance regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in Surah At-Tahrim, verse 1: "يَا أَيُّهَا النَّبِيُّ لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ مَا أَحَلَّ اللَّهُ لَكَ تَبْتَغِي مَرْضَاتَ أَزْوَاجِكَ" (O Prophet! Why do you prohibit [yourself from] what Allah has made lawful for you, seeking the approval of your wives?).

Commentary

Oaths and the Expiation for Oaths

In this verse and the subsequent verses, a series of important Islamic rulings are presented—some for the first time, and a significant portion as an emphasis and clarification of rulings previously mentioned in other verses of the Quran. As we have stated, this Surah was revealed toward the end of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, and it was necessary to place greater emphasis on various Islamic rulings within it.

In the first verse, reference is made to the prohibition of some of the Divine blessings by certain Muslims, and it forbids them from repeating this act, saying: "O you who have believed, do not prohibit the good things (Tayyibat) which Allah has made lawful for you" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّبَاتِ مَا أَحَلَّ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ). [^1] In addition to considering the circumstances of revelation, the mention of this ruling may be an indication that if the previous verses praised and commended a group of Christian scholars and monks, it was because of their flexibility and submission to the Truth, not because of their program of renouncing the world and prohibiting pure things. Muslims cannot emulate them in this regard. By stating this ruling, Islam explicitly declares its dissociation from the issue of monasticism and world-renunciation as practiced by Christians and ascetics. You will read a more detailed explanation of this subject under verse 27 of Surah Al-Hadid: "وَرَهْبَانِيَّةً ابْتَدَعُوهَا".

Then, to emphasize this matter, He says: "And do not transgress. Indeed, Allah does not love transgressors" (وَلَا تَعْتَدُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ).

In the following verse, He emphasizes the point again, except that the previous verse was a prohibition against forbidding things, while this verse is a command to utilize Divine blessings in a lawful manner, saying:

"And eat of what Allah has provided for you [which is] lawful and good" (وَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللَّهُ حَلَالًا طَيِّبًا).

The only condition is that you do not forget to observe moderation, piety, and God-wariness in utilizing these blessings; hence He says: "And fear Allah, in whom you are believers" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ). This means your faith in Allah requires you to respect all His commands—both in utilizing blessings and in observing moderation and piety.

There is another possibility in the interpretation of this phrase: that the purpose of the command to piety is that prohibiting permissible and pure things is not consistent with the high and perfect degree of piety. Piety requires that a person does not deviate from the limit of moderation in any direction.

In the next verse, He discusses oaths taken regarding the prohibition of the lawful and other matters in a general sense, dividing oaths into two categories:

First, He says: "Allah will not impose blame upon you for what is meaningless (laghw) in your oaths" (لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ اللَّهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمَانِكُمْ).

As we stated in the commentary of verse 225 of Surah Al-Baqarah, which also discussed the lack of punishment for meaningless oaths: the meaning of a "meaningless oath," as commentators and jurists have said, refers to oaths that do not have a specific goal and do not stem from will and intention. Rather, they are uttered without attention as a figure of speech, such as saying "By Allah" (Wallah, Billah) or "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah," or those said without will and intention in a state of intense excitement or anger.

Some have said that if a person is certain of something and takes an oath based on it, and then it turns out he was mistaken, that is also part of a meaningless oath. For example, if someone, due to the slander of others, becomes certain of his wife's deviation and swears to divorce her, and later it turns out to be a lie, this oath has no validity. We also know that in addition to the necessity of intent, will, and resolve in serious oaths, it is necessary that the content of the oath is not an illegitimate or disliked (makruh) act. Therefore, if a person, of his own volition and with intent, swears to perform a forbidden or disliked act, that oath is also worthless, and fulfilling it is not required—it is possible that the concept of "laghw" (meaningless) in the above verse is broad and even includes this type of oath.

The second category of oaths are those taken with will, intention, and seriousness. Regarding these types of oaths, the Quran says in the above verse:

"But He will impose blame upon you for [breaking] what you intended of oaths" (وَلُكِنْ يُؤَاخِذُكُمْ بِمَا عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمَانَ). The word "aqd" (contract/tie), as we said at the beginning of Surah Al-Ma'idah, originally means to bring the sides of something together firmly. For this reason, tying the two ends of a rope is called "aqd," and sometimes it is used in spiritual matters as well, and any firm covenant is called an "aqd." In the above verse, the meaning of "aqd al-ayman" (binding oaths) is the serious intention for an action that is to be performed according to the oath.

Of course, the seriousness of the oath alone is not sufficient for its validity; rather, as mentioned above, the content of the oath must at least be a permissible matter, and it must be known that an oath is not valid except in the name of Allah.

Therefore, if someone swears by the name of Allah to perform a good deed or at least a permissible deed, it is obligatory to fulfill his oath, and if he breaks it, there is an expiation (kaffarah).

The expiation for an oath is what is explained at the end of the verse under discussion:

"The expiation for such an oath is one of three things:

First, feeding ten needy people (فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَاكِينَ). However, so that some do not infer from the absolute nature of this ruling that any kind of low-quality or cheap food can be used for expiation, He specifies that this food must be at least of an average standard that you normally feed your own families (مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ).

The apparent meaning of this expression is average in terms of quality, but it may also refer to both quality and quantity. As narrated in a tradition from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, "average" refers to quality, and in a tradition from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, it refers to quantity; their meanings converge on the average in both aspects. [^1] It goes without saying that the matter of the "average" in both aspects will vary according to different cities, settlements, and times.

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "awsat" (average) means "best" or "excellent," because one of the meanings of "awsat" is excellent, as we read in verse 28 of Surah Al-Qalam: "قَالَ أَوْسَطُهُمْ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ لَكُمْ لَوْلَا تُسَبِّحُونَ" (The best of them said: Did I not say to you, why do you not glorify Allah?).

Second, clothing ten needy people (أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ). The apparent meaning of the verse is clothing that normally covers the body. Thus, we read in some narrations that Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said the meaning of "kiswah" in the above verse is two pieces of clothing (a shirt and trousers). If we read in some narrations, such as the one from Imam Baqir عليه السلام, that one garment can suffice, it may be because long Arabic shirts alone can cover the entire body. Of course, in the case of women, a single shirt, even if long, is not sufficient; rather, a head-covering (khimar) to cover the head and neck is also necessary, as the minimum clothing required for a woman is not less than this. Nevertheless, it is not unlikely that the clothing given as expiation varies according to seasons [^1], places, and times.

Regarding whether the minimum quality is sufficient or if the average must also be observed here, there are two views among commentators: first, that according to the absolute nature of the verse, any kind of clothing is sufficient; and second, that considering the condition mentioned for feeding, the average must also be observed here. However, the first possibility is more consistent with the absolute wording of the verse.

Third, freeing a slave (أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ).

There is a discussion among jurists as to whether the slave being freed must have faith and be a Muslim, or if freeing any slave is sufficient; the explanation of this should be sought in books of jurisprudence, although the wording of the verse is absolute.

This itself shows that Islam utilized various means to free slaves. In an era like ours, where apparently no slaves exist, one of the other two expiations must be chosen.

There is no doubt that these three options vary greatly in terms of cost, and perhaps this variation is so that everyone is free to choose one according to their ability.

However, there may be people who have the power to do none of these. Therefore, after stating this instruction, He says: those who cannot find [the means for any of these] must fast for three days (فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ).

Thus, fasting for three days is exclusively for those who do not have the ability to perform one of the three aforementioned options.

Then, for emphasis, He says: "That is the expiation for your oaths when you have sworn" (ذُلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمَانِكُمْ إِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ).

But so that no one imagines that by giving expiation, breaking valid oaths is not forbidden, He says: "But guard your oaths" (وَاحْفَظُوا أَيْمَانَكُمْ).

In other words, fulfilling an oath is a mandatory duty (wujub taklifi) and breaking it is forbidden, but after breaking it, one must pay the expiation.

At the end of the verse, He says: "Thus does Allah make clear to you His verses that you may be grateful" (كَذُلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللَّهُ لَكُمْ آيَاتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ)—that you may thank Him for these rulings and instructions which guarantee the happiness and well-being of the individual and society.

[^1]: The above circumstances of revelation are taken partly from the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim and partly from Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan and other commentaries. [^1]: The meaning of "Halal" and "Tayyib" was discussed in Volume 1 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 417. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 666, and Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 496. [^1]: A Hadith has also been narrated in this regard from Imam Baqir or Imam Sadiq عليه السلام (Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 1, p. 496).

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَا ٱلْخَمْرُ وَٱلْمَيْسِرُ وَٱلْأَنصَابُ وَٱلْأَزْلَـٰمُ رِجْسٌ مِّنْ عَمَلِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ فَٱجْتَنِبُوهُ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (٩٠)
O you who have faith! Indeed wine, gambling, idols, and the divining arrows are abominations of Satan’s doing, so avoid them, so that you may be felicitous. (90)
إِنَّمَا يُرِيدُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَن يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْعَدَٰوَةَ وَٱلْبَغْضَآءَ فِى ٱلْخَمْرِ وَٱلْمَيْسِرِ وَيَصُدَّكُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَعَنِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّنتَهُونَ (٩١)
Indeed Satan seeks to cast enmity and hatred among you through wine and gambling, and to hinder you from the remembrance of Allah and from prayer. Will you, then, relinquish? (91)
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ وَٱحْذَرُوا۟ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا عَلَىٰ رَسُولِنَا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٩٢)
And obey Allah and obey the Apostle, and beware; but if you turn your backs, then know that Our Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (92)

Occasion of Revelation

Various occasions of revelation have been mentioned in both Shi'a and Sunni commentaries regarding the first verse, which are largely similar to one another. Among them, it is narrated in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur from Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, who said: "This verse was revealed concerning me. A man from the Ansar prepared some food and invited us. A group participated in his banquet, and in addition to eating, they drank wine—this was before the prohibition of wine in Islam. When their minds became heated from the wine, they began boasting of their honors. Gradually, the situation escalated until one of them took a camel bone and struck my nose, splitting it. I went to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and reported the incident, at which point the above verse was revealed."

It is also narrated from Musnad Ahmad, Sunan Abi Dawud, al-Nasa'i, and al-Tirmidhi that Umar (who, according to the explicit statement in Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 3, p. 33, had a strong inclination toward drinking wine) used to pray, saying: "O Allah, grant us a clear statement regarding wine." When the verse of Surah al-Baqarah (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَ الْمَيْسِرِ ...) (Baqarah: 219) was revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited it to him, but he continued his prayer, saying: "O Allah, grant us a clearer statement in this regard." Then the verse of Surah al-Nisa (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ أَنْتُمْ سُكارى‌) (Nisa: 43) was revealed, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited that to him as well, yet he still continued his prayer! Finally, the verse in Surah al-Ma'idah (the verse under discussion), which is exceptionally explicit on this subject, was revealed. When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the verse to him, he said:

Antahayna, antahayna! "We have desisted, we have desisted!" [1]

[1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 7, p. 50.

Commentary

The Definitive Ruling on Wine and Its Gradual Stages

As we noted in Volume 3 of this commentary, page 396, under verse 43 of Surah al-Nisa, wine-drinking and intoxication were extremely prevalent during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) and before the advent of Islam, becoming a widespread social plague. Some historians even say that the passion of the pre-Islamic Arab was summarized in three things: poetry, wine, and war! Furthermore, it is understood from narrations that even after the prohibition of wine, the issue of its ban was exceptionally heavy and difficult for some Muslims, to the point that they would say:

Ma hurrima 'alayna shay'un ashaddu min al-khamr

"No ruling was heavier upon us than the prohibition of wine." [1] It is clear that if Islam had intended to combat this great public plague without observing psychological and social principles, it would not have been possible. Therefore, it utilized the method of gradual prohibition and the preparation of minds and thoughts to uproot wine-drinking, which had permeated their very being like a second nature. This was done by first making subtle references to the ugliness of this act in some Meccan Surahs, as we read in verse 67 of Surah al-Nahl: وَ مِنْ ثَمَراتِ النَّخِيلِ وَ الْأَعْنابِ تَتَّخِذُونَ مِنْهُ سَكَراً وَ رِزْقاً حَسَناً "And from the fruits of the date-palms and the grapes, you derive intoxicants and wholesome provision."

Here, "sukar" (intoxicant/wine) derived from grapes and dates is placed directly in contrast to "wholesome provision" (rizqan hasana), thereby characterizing it as an impure and tainted drink.

However, the foul habit of wine-drinking was too deep-rooted to be eradicated by such subtle hints. Moreover, wine provided a portion of their economic income. Thus, when the Muslims migrated to Medina and the first Islamic government was formed, the second instruction regarding the prevention of wine-drinking was revealed in a more decisive manner to further prepare minds for the final prohibition. It was at this time that verse 219 of Surah al-Baqarah was revealed: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الْخَمْرِ وَ الْمَيْسِرِ قُلْ فِيهِما إِثْمٌ كَبِيرٌ وَ مَنافِعُ لِلنَّاسِ وَ إِثْمُهُما أَكْبَرُ مِنْ نَفْعِهِما. In this verse, while acknowledging the economic benefits of alcoholic beverages for some societies—like that of the Jahiliyyah—it reminds them of the significance of its great dangers and harms, which are degrees greater than its economic benefits.

Following that, in verse 43 of Surah al-Nisa: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَقْرَبُوا الصَّلاةَ وَ أَنْتُمْ سُكارى‌ حَتَّى تَعْلَمُوا ما تَقُولُونَ, Muslims were explicitly commanded never to pray while intoxicated, so that they might know what they are saying to their Lord.

Of course, the implication of this verse was not that drinking wine was permissible outside of prayer; rather, the plan was one of gradual, stage-by-stage prohibition. In other words, this verse remained silent regarding times other than prayer and said nothing explicitly.

The Muslims' growing familiarity with Islamic rulings and their mental readiness to uproot this great social vice—which had penetrated the depths of their existence—led to the revelation of the final instruction with complete clarity and decisive language, such that even the excuse-seekers could not find fault with it. This is the very verse under discussion.

It is noteworthy that in this verse, the prohibition of this act is emphasized through various expressions:

  1. The verse begins with the address: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ("O you who have believed"), indicating that opposing this ruling is incompatible with the spirit of faith.
  2. Following that, the word "إِنَّمَا" (innama) is used, which serves for restriction and emphasis.
  3. Wine and gambling are mentioned alongside ansab [1] (idols that had no specific shape and were merely pieces of stone), showing that the danger of wine and gambling is so great that they are placed in the same category as idolatry. For this reason, we read in a narration from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: Sharib al-khamr ka 'abid al-wathan ("The drinker of wine is like a worshiper of an idol"). [2]
  4. Wine, gambling, as well as idolatry and azlam (a type of lottery/divining arrows) [3] are all considered rijs (abomination/impurity): (إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَ الْمَيْسِرُ وَ الْأَنْصابُ وَ الْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ).
  5. All these acts are deemed part of Satanic handiwork: (مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ).
  6. Finally, the decisive command to avoid them is issued: (فَاجْتَنِبُوهُ). It should be noted that "avoidance" (ijtinab) is a more expressive concept than "prohibition" (nahy), because avoidance means to distance oneself, to stay away, and not to approach, which is far more eloquent than the phrase "do not drink."
  7. At the end of this verse, it states: This command is so that you may be successful: (لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ).

[1]: Ansab and nasib were discussed in Volume 4 of this commentary, page 260. [2]: In the margins of Tafsir al-Tabari, Vol. 7, p. 31—this same Hadith is also narrated in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 669, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام. [3]: We discussed the nature of azlam in detail in Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 260.

This means that without it, success is impossible.

  1. In the next verse, it addresses some of the manifest harms of wine and gambling. First, it says: Satan only wants to sow the seeds of enmity and hatred among you through wine and gambling, and to turn you away from prayer and the remembrance of Allah: (إِنَّما يُرِيدُ الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ يُوقِعَ بَيْنَكُمُ الْعَداوَةَ وَ الْبَغْضاءَ فِي الْخَمْرِ وَ الْمَيْسِرِ وَ يَصُدَّكُمْ عَنْ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ وَ عَنِ الصَّلاةِ).
  2. At the end of this verse, as a rhetorical question, it asks: "Will you then desist?" (فَهَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُنْتَهُونَ). This means: after all these emphases, is there still room for excuse-seeking or doubt regarding the abandonment of these two great sins?! Thus, we see that even Umar—who did not find the expressions of previous verses sufficient due to his inclination toward wine (as stated by Sunni commentators)—said after the revelation of this verse that this expression is sufficient and convincing.
  3. In the third verse, as an emphasis of this ruling, it first commands the Muslims to obey Allah and His Messenger and to beware of opposing Him: (وَ أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَ احْذَرُوا). Then it threatens the opponents that if they turn away from obeying the command of the Lord, they will deserve punishment, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has no duty except clear delivery of the message: (فَإِنْ تَوَلَّيْتُمْ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّما عَلى‌ رَسُولِنَا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ).

The Deadly Effects of Wine and Gambling

Although concise discussions regarding the harms of these two social plagues were mentioned under verse 219 of Baqarah in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 74 to 79, it is necessary here—following the example of the Holy Quran—to mention additional points for emphasis. These points are a collection of various statistics, each of which alone is as eloquent as a detailed discussion in expressing the depth and magnitude of these harms:

  1. According to statistics published in England regarding alcoholic insanity, comparing it with other forms of insanity, it was found that for every 2,249 alcoholic insane persons, there were only 53 insane persons due to other causes! [1]
  2. According to other statistics obtained from American mental asylums, 85% of their mental patients are alcoholic patients! [2]
  3. An English scientist named Bentham writes: "Alcoholic beverages make people dull and foolish in northern countries, and insane in southern countries." He then adds: "The religion of Islam has prohibited all types of alcoholic drinks, and this is one of the distinctions of Islam." [3]
  4. If statistics were compiled of those who, while intoxicated, committed suicide or crimes, destroyed homes, and ruined families, it would constitute a staggering figure. [4]
  5. In France, 440 people sacrifice their lives to alcohol every day! [5]
  6. According to other statistics, the casualties from mental illnesses in America in one year were double the casualties of that country in World War II, and according to scientists, "alcohol" and "cigarettes" played a fundamental role in America's mental illnesses! [6]
  7. According to statistics presented by a scientist named Hoger on the occasion of the 20th anniversary of a science journal, 60% of intentional murders, 75% of assaults, 30% of moral crimes (including incest!), and 20% of theft crimes were related to alcohol and alcoholic beverages. According to statistics from the same scientist, 40% of juvenile delinquents have a history of alcoholic influence. [7]
  8. From an economic perspective, in England alone, losses resulting from workers' absence from work due to alcoholism were estimated at 50 million dollars a year (approximately 1,750 million Tomans), an amount that alone could fund the establishment of thousands of kindergartens, primary schools, and high schools. [8]
  9. According to statistics published regarding the harms of alcoholic beverages in France: Alcohol imposes 137 billion francs a year on the French budget, excluding personal losses, as follows:
  10. 60 billion francs for the judiciary and prisons.
  11. 40 billion francs for public welfare and charity.
  12. 10 billion francs for hospital expenses for alcoholics.
  13. 70 billion francs for social security costs! Thus, it becomes clear that the number of mental patients, hospitals, murders, bloody conflicts, thefts, assaults, and accidents is directly proportional to the number of taverns. [9]
  14. The largest statistical institutions in America have proven that gambling is directly involved in 30% of crimes. According to other statistics published regarding the crimes of gamblers, we see with great regret that:
  15. 90% of pickpocketing.
  16. 50% of sexual crimes.
  17. 10% of moral corruption.
  18. 30% of divorces.
  19. 40% of assaults.
  20. And 5% of suicides have occurred because of gambling. 10
