[1] & [2]: Alcohol Symposium Book, p. 65. [3]: Tafsir Tantawi, Vol. 1, p. 165. [4]: Encyclopedia of Farid Wajdi, Vol. 3, p. 790. [5]: Social Plagues of Our Century, p. 205. [6]: Nasl-e Javan Publications Collection. [7]: Alcohol Symposium, p. 66. [8]: Nasl-e Javan Publications Collection, Year 2, p. 330. [9]: Journal of the Center for the Study of Iran's Progress (regarding alcohol and gambling).

Ayah

لَيْسَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ جُنَاحٌ فِيمَا طَعِمُوٓا۟ إِذَا مَا ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّءَامَنُوا۟ ثُمَّ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّأَحْسَنُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٩٣)
There will be no sin upon those who have faith and do righteous deeds in regard to what they have eaten [in the past] so long as they are Godwary and faithful and do righteous deeds, and are further Godwary and faithful, and are further Godwary and virtuous. And Allah loves the virtuous. (93)

Occasion of Revelation

In the commentaries of Majma' al-Bayan, Tabari, Qurtubi, and several other exegeses, it is mentioned that after the revelation of the verse prohibiting wine and gambling, some of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked: "If these two acts carry such great sin, then what is the fate of our Muslim brothers who passed away before the revelation of this verse and had not yet abandoned these two practices?" The above verse was revealed to provide them with an answer.

[^10]: Publication of the Center for the Study of Iranian Progress (regarding alcohol and gambling).

Commentary

In response to those concerned about the status of those who passed away before the prohibition of wine and gambling, or those living in remote areas who had not yet heard of this ruling, this verse states: "Those who believed and performed righteous deeds and to whom this ruling had not reached, there is no sin upon them for what they have consumed [of wine or income from gambling]" (لَيْسَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ جُناحٌ فِيما طَعِمُوا) [^1]. It then makes this ruling conditional upon them practicing piety (taqwa), believing, and performing righteous deeds (إِذا مَا اتَّقَوْا وَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ). It repeats this subject once more, saying: "then they practice piety and believe" (ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَ آمَنُوا).

And for the third time, with a slight variation, it repeats the same subject, saying:

"Then they practice piety and do good" (ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَ أَحْسَنُوا).

At the end of the verse, He says: "And Allah loves the doers of good" (وَ اللَّهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُحْسِنِينَ).

There is much discussion among ancient and modern commentators regarding the repetition of these three phrases. Some have interpreted them as emphasis, as the importance of piety, faith, and righteous deeds necessitates that they be stressed seriously and repeatedly.

However, a group of commentators believes that each of these three phrases refers to a distinct reality,

[^1]: It should be noted that "ta'am" (food/consumption) is mostly used for edibles and not drinks, but it is occasionally used for drinks as well, such as in verse 249 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "فَمَنْ شَرِبَ مِنْهُ فَلَيْسَ مِنِّي وَ مَنْ لَمْ يَطْعَمْهُ فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي" (Whoever drinks from it is not of me, and whoever does not taste it is of me).

and they have offered numerous possibilities regarding their differences, many of which lack supporting evidence or indicators.

Perhaps the best explanation in this regard is to say:

The "piety" (taqwa) mentioned the first time refers to that internal sense of responsibility that drives a person toward investigation and research into religion, examining the miracle of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and searching for the truth; the result of this is faith and righteous deeds. In other words, unless a degree of piety exists within a person, they will not think to investigate and seek the truth. Therefore, the first time piety is mentioned in the above verse, it refers to this stage of piety. This does not contradict the beginning of the verse which says, "There is no sin upon those who believe and do righteous deeds...", because the "faith" at the start of the verse might refer to outward submission, whereas the faith that arises after piety is true faith.

The second time piety is mentioned, it refers to the piety that penetrates the depths of a person's soul, its effect becoming deeper, resulting in a stable and established faith of which righteous deeds are an integral part. Thus, in the second phrase, after mentioning faith, there is no mention of righteous deeds; it simply says: "ثُمَّ اتَّقَوْا وَ آمَنُوا" (then they practice piety and believe)... meaning this faith is so influential and firm that there is no need to mention righteous deeds following it.

In the third stage where piety is discussed, it refers to piety that has reached its highest level, such that in addition to inviting one to perform obligatory duties, it invites one to ihsan (excellence/doing good)—that is, performing virtuous acts, even those that are not mandatory.

In summary, each of these three instances of piety refers to a stage of the sense of responsibility and God-wariness: the "initial stage," the "intermediate stage," and the "final stage." Each has a contextual clue within the verse itself upon which one can rely to understand the intent, unlike the possibilities suggested by some commentators regarding the differences between these three pieties and three faiths, which lack such clues and evidence.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلصَّيْدِ تَنَالُهُۥٓ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِمَاحُكُمْ لِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَخَافُهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ فَمَنِ ٱعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٩٤)
O you who have faith! Allah will surely test you with some of the game within the reach of your hands and spears, so that Allah may know those who fear Him in secret. So whoever transgresses after that, there is a painful punishment for him. (94)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَقْتُلُوا۟ ٱلصَّيْدَ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ ۚ وَمَن قَتَلَهُۥ مِنكُم مُّتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَآءٌ مِّثْلُ مَا قَتَلَ مِنَ ٱلنَّعَمِ يَحْكُمُ بِهِۦ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ هَدْيًۢا بَـٰلِغَ ٱلْكَعْبَةِ أَوْ كَفَّـٰرَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسَـٰكِينَ أَوْ عَدْلُ ذَٰلِكَ صِيَامًا لِّيَذُوقَ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِۦ ۗ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ ۚ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَيَنتَقِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِنْهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ (٩٥)
O you who have faith! Do not kill any game when you are in pilgrim sanctity. Should any of you kill it intentionally, its atonement, the counterpart from cattle of what he has killed, as judged by two fair men among you, will be an offering brought to the Ka‘bah, or an atonement by feeding needy persons, or its equivalent in fasting, that he may taste the untoward consequences of his conduct. Allah has excused what is already past; but should anyone resume, Allah shall take vengeance on him, for Allah is all-mighty, avenger. (95)
أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهُۥ مَتَـٰعًا لَّكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ ۖ وَحُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ صَيْدُ ٱلْبَرِّ مَا دُمْتُمْ حُرُمًا ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٩٦)
You are permitted the game of the sea and its food, a provision for you and for the caravans, but you are forbidden the game of the land so long as you remain in pilgrim sanctity, and be wary of Allah toward whom you will be gathered. (96)

Occasion of Revelation

As narrated in the book al-Kafi and many other commentaries, when the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Muslims set out for Umrah in the state of Ihram during the year of Hudaybiyyah, they encountered a vast number of wild animals along the way, such that they could have captured them with their hands or spears! These animals were so numerous that some have written they moved side-by-side with the mounts and passed close to the tents. It was at this time that the first of the above verses were revealed, warning the Muslims against hunting them and cautioning them that this was considered a form of trial for them.

Commentary

The Rulings of Hunting While in Ihram

These verses address one of the rulings of Umrah and Hajj, namely the issue of hunting land and sea animals while in the state of Ihram.

First, referring to the event the Muslims faced in the year of "Hudaybiyyah," it says: "O you who have believed, Allah will surely test you with something of the game that your hands and spears can reach" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَيَبْلُوَنَّكُمُ اللَّهُ بِشَيْءٍ مِنَ الصَّيْدِ تَنَالُهُ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَرِمَاحُكُمْ).

From the phrasing of the verse, it is understood that it intended to inform the people of an upcoming event as a prediction. It also becomes clear that the presence of such abundant game within reach of the people there was an unprecedented matter. This was considered a type of Divine trial for the Muslims, especially considering their need for meat for food, and given that these animals moved around the tents in a tempting manner. Enduring the deprivation of such readily available food, particularly in that era and for those people, could indeed be a great trial.

Some have said that the phrase "that your hands can reach" means they could catch them with traps and nets, but the literal meaning of the verse suggests it was truly possible to catch them with the hands themselves.

Then, as an emphasis, He says: This occurrence was so that those who fear Allah in the unseen (through faith in the Ghayb) may be distinguished from others (لِيَعْلَمَ اللَّهُ مَنْ يَخَافُهُ بِالْغَيْبِ). As we mentioned in Volume 1, page 356, under the commentary of verse 143 of Surah Al-Baqarah, the expression "so that We may know" (لِنَعْلَمَ) or "so that Allah may know" (لِيَعْلَمَ) and the like does not mean that Allah did not know something and wishes to find out through testing and trials. Rather, it means: We wish to bring Our scientific knowledge into the realm of action and external realization. This is because internal intentions and the readiness of individuals alone are not sufficient for perfection, reward, or punishment; rather, they must be implemented in the form of external actions to produce these effects (for more explanation, refer to the commentary of that same verse).

At the end of the verse, He threatens those who oppose this Divine decree with a painful punishment: "So whoever transgresses after that, for him is a painful punishment" (فَمَنِ اعْتَدَىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَهُ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

Although the final sentence of the verse implicitly indicates the prohibition of hunting while in Ihram, the following verse issues the command of prohibition with greater clarity, decisiveness, and generality, saying: "O you who have believed, do not kill game while you are in the state of Ihram" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَقْتُلُوا الصَّيْدَ وَأَنْتُمْ حُرُمٌ).

Does the prohibition of hunting (which, by the context of the next verse, refers to land animals) include all types of land animals, whether their meat is halal (permissible) or haram (forbidden), or is it restricted to halal animals?

There is no consensus among commentators and jurists on this matter, but the famous view among Imami jurists and commentators is the generality of the ruling. The narrations transmitted from the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) support this. As for the Sunni jurists, some like Abu Hanifa agree with us on this, while others like Shafi'i have restricted it to halal animals. In any case, it does not include domestic animals, as domestic animals are not referred to as "game" or "prey." It is noteworthy that in our narrations, not only is hunting while in Ihram prohibited, but even assisting, pointing out, or showing the game while in Ihram is forbidden.

Some might imagine that "game" does not include haram animals, but this is not the case, as animals are hunted for various purposes: sometimes for their meat, sometimes for their skin, and sometimes to remove their nuisance. The famous poem attributed to Ali (عليه السلام) can also serve as evidence for this generalization, where he said: "The prey of kings are rabbits and foxes, But when I ride, my prey are the heroes!" (For more explanation regarding the types and rulings of prohibited hunting while in Ihram, refer to books of jurisprudence).

Then, He refers to the expiation (kaffarah) for hunting while in Ihram, saying: "And whoever among you kills it intentionally, then an expiation [is due]—the like of what he has killed from the livestock" (meaning he should sacrifice it and give its meat to the poor) (وَمَنْ قَتَلَهُ مِنْكُمْ مُتَعَمِّدًا فَجَزَاءٌ مِثْلُ مَا قَتَلَ مِنَ النَّعَمِ).

Here, does "like" (mithl) mean similarity in the shape and size of the animal? For instance, if someone hunts a large wild animal like an ostrich, should he choose a camel for the expiation? Or if he hunts a deer, should he sacrifice a sheep, which is approximately its size? Or does "like" mean similarity in value?

The famous and well-known view among jurists and commentators is the first meaning, and the literal wording of the verse is more consistent with it. Furthermore, considering the generalization of the ruling to both halal and haram animals, many of these animals do not have a fixed and specified price that would allow choosing an equivalent from domestic animals based on value.

In any case, this applies if a likeness in shape and size can be found; otherwise, there is no choice but to determine a price for that game and choose its equivalent in value from halal domestic livestock.

Since the matter of "likeness" might be subject to doubt for some, the Quran has commanded that this matter must be carried out under the supervision of two informed and just individuals (يَحْكُمُ بِهِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ).

Regarding where this expiation should be slaughtered, He commands that it be offered as a sacrifice (hady) reaching the Kaaba (هَدْيًا بَالِغَ الْكَعْبَةِ).

Additionally, it should be noted that the famous view among our jurists is that the expiation for "hunting while in the Ihram of Umrah" must be slaughtered in Mecca, and for "hunting while in the Ihram of Hajj" in Mina and the slaughterhouse. This does not contradict the above verse because, as we said, the verse was revealed regarding the Ihram of Umrah.

Then He adds that it is not necessary for the expiation to be solely in the form of a sacrifice; rather, two other things can each substitute for it. First, he may spend the equivalent of its value on feeding the poor (أَوْ كَفَّارَةٌ طَعَامُ مَسَاكِينَ).

Or he may fast the equivalent of that (أَوْ عَدْلُ ذَٰلِكَ صِيَامًا).

Although the verse does not mention the number of poor people to be fed or the number of days to fast, their placement alongside one another on one hand, and the explicit mention of the need for balance regarding the fasting on the other, shows that it does not mean feeding any number of poor people he wishes. Rather, it means it must be in proportion to the value of the sacrifice. As for how the equivalence between fasting and feeding the poor is established, it is understood from some narrations that for every "mudd" of food (approximately 750 grams of wheat or the like), he should fast one day. From other narrations, it is understood that he should fast one day for every two "mudds." This is, in fact, because in the holy month of Ramadan, those unable to fast give one or two "mudds" of food to the poor for each day (for more explanation on this topic, study books of jurisprudence).

Regarding whether the person who committed hunting while in Ihram has a choice among these three things or must follow a specific order—first sacrificing, and if unable, feeding the poor, and if that is not possible, fasting—there is discussion among commentators and jurists. However, the literal meaning of the verse suggests choice (takhyir).

These expiations are so that he may taste the consequence of his deed (لِيَذُوقَ وَبَالَ أَمْرِهِ) [^1]. But since no ruling usually applies to the past, He explicitly states that Allah has pardoned what occurred in this regard in the past (عَفَا اللَّهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ).

And whenever someone disregards these repeated warnings and the ruling of expiation and commits hunting while in Ihram again, Allah will take retribution from such a person, and Allah is Mighty and Capable of retribution at the right time (وَمَنْ عَادَ فَيَنْتَقِمُ اللَّهُ مِنْهُ وَاللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انْتِقَامٍ).

There is discussion among commentators as to whether the expiation for hunting is repeated with its repetition or not.

The literal meaning of the verse is that in the case of repetition, there is only the threat of Divine retribution. If the expiation were also repeated, it would have been necessary not to settle for mentioning Divine retribution alone, and the repetition of the expiation should have been explicitly stated. This matter is mentioned in narrations transmitted to us through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام).

In the next verse, He speaks about sea game, saying: "Lawful to you is the game of the sea and its food" (أُحِلَّ لَكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَحْرِ وَطَعَامُهُ).

[^1]: Wabal—as Raghib says in al-Mufradat—is originally derived from wabl and wabil, meaning heavy rain, and was later applied to difficult, arduous, and heavy matters. Since punishment and penalty also possess intensity and heaviness, they are called wabal.

Regarding what is meant by "food" (ta'am), some commentators have suggested it refers to fish that die without being caught and remain on the water. However, we know this is incorrect, as eating dead fish is haram, even though some Sunni narrations explicitly state its permissibility.

What is more apparent from the literal meaning of the verse is that "food" refers to the edible items prepared from the caught fish, as the verse intends to permit two things: first, the act of catching, and second, eating the caught food.

Additionally, from the implication of this expression, the famous fatwa among our jurists is also implicitly understood: that in the case of land animals, not only is the act of hunting forbidden, but eating the meat of hunted animals is also not permitted.

Then He refers to the philosophy of this ruling, saying: "as provision for you and the travelers" (مَتَاعًا لَكُمْ وَلِلسَّيَّارَةِ).

That is, so that you do not face hardship for nourishment while in Ihram and can benefit from one type of game, this permission regarding sea game has been given to you.

And since travelers usually, if they want to take caught fish with them, mix it with salt and turn it into "salted fish," some commentators have interpreted the above sentence to mean that "residents" can use fresh fish and "travelers" can use salted fish.

Let there be no mistake: the fact that we read in the above verse that sea game has been made lawful for you does not mean it is a general and universal ruling about sea game, as some have imagined. This is because the verse does not intend to state the original ruling of sea game; rather, the goal of the verse is to allow the muhrim (one in Ihram) to use sea game that was lawful for him before Ihram while he is in Ihram. In other words, the verse does not state the original legislation of the law, but rather looks at the specifics of a law that was previously legislated. In technical terms, it is not in the position of stating the generality of the ruling, but only states the rulings of the muhrim.

But once again, as an emphasis, He returns to the previous ruling, saying: "but forbidden to you is the game of the land as long as you are in the state of Ihram" (وَحُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ صَيْدُ الْبَرِّ مَا دُمْتُمْ حُرُمًا).

At the end of the verse, to emphasize all the rulings mentioned, He says: "And fear Allah, to Whom you will be gathered" in the Hereafter, and do not oppose His command (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ الَّذِي إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ).

The Philosophy of Prohibiting Hunting While in Ihram

We know that Hajj and Umrah are acts of worship that detach a person from the material world and immerse him in an environment full of spirituality. The distinctions of material life, wars and conflicts, hostilities, sexual desires, and material pleasures are completely set aside during the Hajj and Umrah rites, and man engages in a type of legitimate Divine asceticism. It seems that the prohibition of hunting while in Ihram is also for this purpose.

Furthermore, if hunting were a legitimate act for the pilgrims of the House of Allah, considering the vast amount of travel that occurs every year in these holy lands, the species of many animals in that region—which already has few animals due to its dryness and lack of water—would be wiped out. This command is a type of protection and guarantee for the survival of animal species in that region.

Especially considering that even when not in Ihram, hunting in the Haram (Sanctuary), as well as uprooting its trees and plants, is forbidden, it becomes clear that this command is closely related to the issue of environmental protection and preserving the plants and animals of that region from extinction and destruction.

This ruling has been legislated so precisely that not only is hunting animals forbidden, but even assisting, or even showing and presenting the game to a hunter is prohibited. As we read in narrations transmitted through the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said to one of his companions:

"Never deem anything of game lawful while you are in Ihram, nor while you are not in Ihram within the Haram. Do not point it out to one who is not in Ihram nor to one who is in Ihram so that he hunts it, and do not even gesture toward it so that it is deemed lawful because of you, for in that there is a ransom (fida) for the one who does it intentionally." [^1]

[^1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'a, Vol. 5, p. 75.

Ayahs

۞ جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكَعْبَةَ ٱلْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ قِيَـٰمًا لِّلنَّاسِ وَٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ وَٱلْهَدْىَ وَٱلْقَلَـٰٓئِدَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٩٧)
Allah has made the Ka‘bah, the Sacred House, a [means of] sustentation for mankind, and [also] the sacred month, the offering and the garlands, so that you may know that Allah knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth, and that Allah has knowledge of all things. (97)
ٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٩٨)
Know that Allah is severe in retribution, and that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (98)
مَّا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا تَكْتُمُونَ (٩٩)
The Apostle’s duty is only to communicate and Allah knows whatever you disclose and whatever you conceal. (99)

Commentary

Following the previous verses which discussed the prohibition of hunting while in the state of Ihram, this verse points to the importance of "Mecca" and its impact on the social life of Muslims. First, it states: "Allah has made the Kaaba, the Sacred House, a means of standing (sustenance and stability) for the people" (جَعَلَ اللَّهُ الْكَعْبَةَ الْبَيْتَ الْحَرامَ قِياماً لِلنَّاسِ‌).

This holy house is a symbol of the unity of the people and a center for the gathering of hearts, and a great congress for strengthening various bonds. In the light of this holy house and its centrality and spirituality—which stems from deep historical roots—they can organize many of their disorders and establish the palace of their prosperity upon its foundation. Therefore, in Surah Al-Imran, the Kaaba is introduced as the first house established for the benefit of the people [^1]. The truth is that, considering the breadth of the meaning of "قِياماً لِلنَّاسِ‌" (a means of standing for the people), Muslims can organize all their affairs under the protection of this House and the constructive instructions of Hajj.

And since these ceremonies must take place in an environment of peace and security, free from war, conflict, and strife, He refers to the effect of the Sacred Months (months in which war is absolutely forbidden) in this regard, saying: (وَ الشَّهْرَ الْحَرامَ‌) [^2]. Furthermore, considering that the existence of unmarked sacrificial animals (Hady) and marked sacrificial animals (Qala'id)—which provide for the nourishment of the people during the days they are engaged in the Hajj and Umrah rituals and put their minds at ease in this regard—plays a role in completing this program, He mentions them as well, saying: (وَ الْهَدْيَ وَ الْقَلائِدَ).

And since the sum of these calculated programs, laws, and regulations regarding hunting, as well as the Sanctuary of Mecca, the Sacred Month, and others, indicates the depth of planning and the vastness of the knowledge of such a Legislator, He says at the end of the verse: "Allah established these organized programs so that you may know His knowledge is so vast that He knows whatever is in the heavens and the earth, and He is aware of everything—especially the spiritual and physical needs of His servants." (ذلِكَ لِتَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ عَلِيمٌ‌).

Considering what we have said above, the link between the beginning and the end of the verse becomes clear; for only He can organize these deep legislative (tashri'i) instructions who is aware of the depths of the laws of creation (takwini). Unless one is aware of all the details of the earth and the heavens and what is placed in the human soul and body by the decree of creation, one cannot foresee such laws, because a correct and constructive law is one that is harmonious with the law of creation and nature (fitrah).

Then, in the next verse, to emphasize the previous instructions and to encourage people to perform them and to threaten the opponents and sinners, He says: "Know that Allah is severe in retribution and also All-Forgiving, Most Merciful" (اعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ شَدِيدُ الْعِقابِ وَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ‌).

The fact that we see "severe in retribution" mentioned before "All-Forgiving, Most Merciful" in the above verse is perhaps an indication that Allah's punishment, despite all its severity, can be washed away with the water of repentance, allowing one to be encompassed by the forgiveness and mercy of God.

And again, for further emphasis, He says: You yourselves are responsible for your actions, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has no responsibility except the delivery of the message and conveying the commands of Allah (ما عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ‌).

At the same time, Allah is aware of your intentions and of all your open and hidden deeds (وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ ما تُبْدُونَ وَ ما تَكْتُمُونَ‌).

The Importance of the Kaaba

The Kaaba, which is mentioned twice in these and the preceding verses, originally comes from the root Ka'b, meaning the protrusion on the back of the foot; subsequently, it was applied to any kind of height or protrusion. If a "cube" is called muka'ab, it is because it has protrusions from four sides. The reason women whose breasts have just begun to develop are called Ka'ib (plural: Kawa'ib) is also for this same reason.

In any case, this word (Kaaba) refers both to the outward height of the House of Allah and is a symbol of its greatness and the loftiness of its status.

The Kaaba has a very long and eventful history, and in any case, all these events stem from its greatness and importance.

The importance of the Kaaba is such that in Islamic narrations, destroying it is placed in the same rank as killing a Prophet or an Imam. Looking at it is an act of worship, and circumambulating (Tawaf) around it is among the best of deeds. We even read in a narration from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام that he said:

"It is not fitting for anyone to raise his building above the Kaaba." [^1]

However, it must be noted that the importance and sanctity of the Kaaba are never because of its physical structure; for as Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام says in Nahj al-Balagha in the Qasi'ah sermon: "Allah placed His House in one of the dry and scorching lands among harsh mountains and commanded that it be built from very simple materials, from ordinary and common stones." [^2] But since the Kaaba is the oldest and most historical center of monotheism and the worship of God, and a focal point for the attention of various nations and peoples, it has attained all this importance in the presence of Allah.

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 482.

[^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (The Qasi'ah Sermon).

Ayah

قُل لَّا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْخَبِيثُ وَٱلطَّيِّبُ وَلَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ ٱلْخَبِيثِ ۚ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ يَـٰٓأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (١٠٠)
Say, ‘The good and the bad are not equal, though the abundance of the bad should amaze you.’ So be wary of Allah, O you who possess intellect, so that you may be felicitous! (100)

Commentary

Majority is Not a Proof of "Purity"

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the prohibition of intoxicants, gambling, idols, divining arrows, and hunting while in the state of Ihram. Since some individuals might use the actions of the majority in certain environments as a pretext to commit such sins—using excuses such as "the majority of people in such-and-such city drink wine" or "are tainted by gambling," or "the majority of people do not heed the prohibition of hunting under such-and-such conditions"—Allah has expressed a general and fundamental rule in a short phrase to completely eliminate this excuse in this and all similar cases. He says:

"Say [O Prophet!]: The evil and the good are not equal, even though the abundance of the evil and the multitude of the tainted may amaze you!" (قُلْ لا يَسْتَوِي الْخَبِيثُ وَ الطَّيِّبُ وَ لَوْ أَعْجَبَكَ كَثْرَةُ الْخَبِيثِ). Therefore, "evil" (khabith) and "good" (tayyib) in the above verse refer to every type of impure and pure being, including pure and impure foods, as well as pure and impure thoughts.

At the end of the verse, He addresses the "thinkers and possessors of intellect and wisdom," emphasizing: "So fear Allah, O people of understanding, that you may be successful." (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ يا أُولِي الْأَلْبابِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ).

As for the fact that the verse seemingly explains something obvious, it is because someone might imagine that circumstances such as the abundance of supporters of filth—the so-called "majority"—could cause an impure thing to be placed in the same rank as the pure. As you have observed, sometimes people are influenced by large crowds and the inclinations of the majority, imagining that if the majority inclines toward a matter, it is a definitive and unquestionable sign of its correctness. However, this is not the case, and instances where the majority of societies have fallen into clear errors are very numerous. In reality, what is necessary for identifying good from bad ("evil" from "good") is a qualitative majority, not a quantitative one; meaning stronger, loftier, and more sublime thoughts and more capable and purer minds are required, not a multitude of supporting individuals.

This issue might not sit well with the temperament of some people today, who, due to indoctrination and propaganda, have been constantly fed the idea that the inclinations of the majority are a scale for measuring good from bad—to the point that they have come to believe that "truth" is whatever the majority likes, and "good" is whatever the majority inclines toward, whereas this is not so.

Indeed, many of the troubles of the people of the world are due to this very way of thinking.

Yes, if the majority benefits from correct leadership and proper teachings—becoming what is termed a fully "mature" (rashid) majority—then its inclinations might serve as a scale for measuring good and bad, but not unled and immature majorities.

In any case, the Quran, in the verse under discussion, points to this reality and says:

Never let the abundance of the wicked and the impure amaze you. In other places, it has stated more than ten times: "وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ" (But most of the people do not know); meaning the actions of most people are not based on knowledge and wisdom!

Furthermore, it should be noted that if the word "evil" (khabith) precedes "good" (tayyib) in the verse, it is because the discourse in this verse is directed at those who take the abundance of the evil as proof of its importance. They must be answered and reminded that the criterion for good and bad in no case is abundance or scarcity, majority or minority; rather, everywhere and at all times, "purity" is better than "impurity." Possessors of intellect and thought are never deceived by numbers; they always distance themselves from filth even if everyone in their environment is tainted, and they seek out purity even if everyone in their environment opposes it.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْ أَشْيَآءَ إِن تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ وَإِن تَسْـَٔلُوا۟ عَنْهَا حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ عَفَا ٱللَّهُ عَنْهَا ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ (١٠١)
O you who have faith! Do not ask about things, which, if they are disclosed to you, will upset you. Yet if you ask about them while the Quran is being sent down, they shall be disclosed to you. Allah has excused it, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-forbearing. (101)
قَدْ سَأَلَهَا قَوْمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا۟ بِهَا كَـٰفِرِينَ (١٠٢)
Certainly some people asked about them before you and then came to disbelieve in them. (102)

Occasion of Revelation

Regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses, various accounts are found in hadith and commentary sources. However, the one most consistent with the verses and their expressions is the account narrated in Tafsir al-Majma' al-Bayan from Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام: "One day, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله delivered a sermon and explained God's command regarding the Hajj. A person named Ukashah—or according to another narration, Suraqah—asked: 'Is this command for every year? Must we perform Hajj every single year?'

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله did not answer his question, but the man persisted and repeated his question two or three times. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله then said: 'Woe to you! Why do you insist so much? If I were to answer "Yes," Hajj would become obligatory for all of you every year, and if it were obligatory every year, you would not have the capacity to perform it, and if you were to oppose it, you would be sinners. Therefore, as long as I have not told you something, do not insist upon it, for (one of) the matters that caused the destruction of (some) past nations was their persistence, excessive talking, and asking too many questions of their prophets. Therefore, when I command you with something, perform it to the extent of your ability (اذا امرتكم من شى‌ء فاتوا منه ما استطعتم), and when I forbid you from something, refrain from it.'" The above verses were then revealed and restrained them from such actions. [^1] Let there be no misunderstanding; the purpose of this occasion of revelation—as we will explain in the commentary of the verse—is not to close the door of questioning and learning for the people, because

[^1]: Tafsir al-Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, and al-Manar have narrated this occasion of revelation under the verse in question with slight variations.

the Quran explicitly commands in its verses that people should ask those with knowledge about what they do not know: "فَسْئَلُوا أَهْلَ الذِّكْرِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌" (So ask the people of the message if you do not know) [^1]. Rather, the intent is to discourage inappropriate questions, fault-finding, and persistence that often lead to confusing people's minds, bothering the speaker, and disrupting the flow of his speech and program.

Commentary

Inappropriate Questions!

There is no doubt that questioning is the key to understanding truths, and for this reason, those who ask less, know less. In Islamic verses and narrations, Muslims are strictly commanded to ask about whatever they do not know. However, since every law usually has an exception, this fundamental principle of education also has an exception: sometimes it is better for certain matters to remain hidden to preserve social order and ensure the interests of individuals. In such cases, continuous searching and questioning to uncover the reality is not only not a virtue but is also blameworthy and unpleasant. For example, most doctors find it wise to keep severe and terrifying illnesses hidden from the patient; sometimes they only inform the relatives with the condition that they keep it from the patient, because experience has shown that if most people become aware of the depth of their illness, they are seized by a terror that, if not fatal, at least delays recovery.

In such cases, the patient should never resort to questioning and insisting before his compassionate physician. For his repeated insistence sometimes narrows the field for the physician so much

[^1]: Surah An-Nahl, Verse 43.

that, for his own peace of mind and to attend to other patients, he sees no choice but to reveal the truth to this "persistent patient," even if the patient suffers harm as a result.

Similarly, people need optimism in their cooperation, and to preserve this great asset, it is wise that they are not aware of every detail of one another's lives. After all, everyone has a weak point, and the disclosure of all weak points makes cooperation between individuals difficult. For instance, an influential and distinguished individual might have accidentally been born into a lowly family; now, if his background is revealed, his constructive influence in society might be undermined. In such cases, individuals should by no means insist on searching and investigating.

Or, many plans and designs for social struggles must remain hidden until the time of action, and insistence on revealing them is a blow to the success and victory of the community.

These and similar instances are cases where questioning is not correct, and leaders should not answer them unless they are under great pressure.

The Quran refers to this subject in the above verse, explicitly stating: "O you who have believed, do not ask about things which, if they are shown to you, will distress you" (يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَسْئَلُوا عَنْ أَشْياءَ إِنْ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ تَسُؤْكُمْ‌).

However, since continuous questions from individuals and the lack of response to them might cause doubt and hesitation for others and bring about greater corruption, it adds: "And if you ask about them while the Quran is being revealed, they will be shown to you" (وَ إِنْ تَسْئَلُوا عَنْها حِينَ يُنَزَّلُ الْقُرْآنُ تُبْدَ لَكُمْ‌), and you will fall into hardship.

The reason it restricts the disclosure of these matters to the time of the Quran's revelation is that the questions pertained to matters that had to be clarified through revelation.

Then it adds: "Do not imagine that if Allah remained silent about explaining some matters, He was heedless of them; rather, He wanted to grant you latitude and has overlooked them, and Allah is Forgiving and Forbearing" (عَفَا اللَّهُ عَنْها وَ اللَّهُ غَفُورٌ حَلِيمٌ‌).

In a narration from Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"ان اللَّه افترض عليكم فرائض فلا تضيعوها و حد لكم حدودا فلا تعتدوها و نهى عن اشياء فلا تنتهكوها و سكت لكم عن اشياء و لم يدعها نسيانا فلا تتكلفوها"

"Allah has made certain things obligatory for you, so do not neglect them; He has set limits for you, so do not transgress them; He has forbidden you from certain things, so do not violate them; and He has remained silent for you regarding certain things—and He did not leave them out of forgetfulness—so do not burden yourselves with them." [^1]

Question

It might be asked: if revealing these matters is contrary to the people's interest, why are they revealed upon insistence?

Answer

The reason is what we pointed out above: sometimes if a leader remains silent in the face of

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse.

persistent and repeated questions, it brings about other corruptions, arouses suspicions, and causes confusion in people's minds. Just as if a doctor remains silent in the face of a patient's repeated questions, it may sometimes cause the patient to doubt the doctor's diagnosis itself, imagining that his illness has remained unknown, and thus he may not follow the instructions. Here, the doctor has no choice but to reveal the illness, even if the patient encounters troubles because of it.

In the next verse, to emphasize this point, it says: Some people before you asked such questions and, following the answers, they turned to opposition and disobedience (قَدْ سَأَلَها قَوْمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ ثُمَّ أَصْبَحُوا بِها كافِرِينَ‌).

There is a discussion among commentators as to which of the past nations this general reference pertains to:

Some have suggested it refers to the request for the heavenly table (Ma'idah) from Jesus عليه السلام by the disciples, after the realization of which some turned to opposition. Others have suggested it refers to the request for a miracle from Salih عليه السلام. However, it seems all these suggestions are mistaken, because the verse speaks of "asking" in the sense of "inquiring" and uncovering the unknown, not "asking" in the sense of "requesting" or demanding something. It seems the use of the word "su'al" (question/request) in both senses has caused such confusion.

However, it is possible that the intent was the Children of Israel, who, when commanded to slaughter a cow to investigate a crime (the details of which were mentioned in Volume 1, page 209), cross-examined Moses and repeatedly asked about the details of the cow—details for which they never had any specific instruction. Consequently, they made the matter so difficult for themselves that obtaining such a cow became so hard and expensive that they were on the verge of abandoning it.

Regarding the meaning of the phrase "أَصْبَحُوا بِها كافِرِينَ‌" (they became disbelievers therein), two possibilities exist: First, that "kufr" (disbelief) refers to disobedience and opposition, as we mentioned above. Second, that "kufr" is used in its common sense, because sometimes hearing distressing answers that weigh heavily on the listener's mind causes them to rise up in denial of the subject itself and the competence of the speaker. For example, hearing a distressing answer from a doctor sometimes causes the patient to react and deny his competence, attributing the diagnosis, for instance, to the doctor's old age or senility! At the end of this discussion, we find it necessary to repeat the point mentioned at the beginning: that the above verses in no way close the path to logical, educational, and constructive questions for the people, but rather pertain exclusively to inappropriate questions and searching for matters that are not only unnecessary but are better—and sometimes even necessary—to remain hidden.

Ayahs

مَا جَعَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِنۢ بَحِيرَةٍ وَلَا سَآئِبَةٍ وَلَا وَصِيلَةٍ وَلَا حَامٍ ۙ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ ۖ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (١٠٣)
Allah has not prescribed any such thing as Bahirah, Sa’ibah, Waseelah, or Haam; but those who are faithless fabricate lies against Allah, and most of them do not exercise their reason. (103)
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ وَإِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ قَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۚ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ءَابَآؤُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ (١٠٤)
When they are told, ‘Come to what Allah has sent down and [come] to the Apostle,’ they say, ‘Sufficient for us is what we have found our fathers following.’ What, even if their fathers did not know anything and were not guided?! (104)

Commentary

In the first verse, reference is made to four misplaced "innovations" (bid'ah) that were common among the pagan Arabs. They would place marks and names upon certain animals for various reasons, subsequently forbidding the consumption of their meat, or even disallowing the drinking of their milk, the shearing of their wool, and the riding of their backs. Sometimes they would leave these animals free to roam wherever they wished, and no one would interfere with them; in practice, they rendered the animal useless and left it to go to waste.

Therefore, the Holy Quran states: "Allah does not recognize any of these rulings; He has not instituted the Bahirah, nor the Sa'ibah, nor the Wasilah, nor the Ham" (مَا جَعَلَ اللَّهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَ لا سائِبَةٍ وَ لا وَصِيلَةٍ وَ لا حامٍ). Here is an explanation of these four types of animals:

  1. Bahirah: This referred to a female camel that had given birth five times, the fifth of which was a female—and according to some reports, a male. They would make a wide slit in the ear of such an animal, leave it to roam freely, and refrain from slaughtering it. The word Bahirah is derived from the root Bahr, meaning vastness or expansion. The Arabs call the sea Bahr because of its vastness, and they named this animal Bahirah because of the wide slit they made in its ear.

  2. Sa'ibah: This was a camel that had given birth to twelve—and according to some reports, ten—offspring. They would set it free, and no one would even ride it. It was free to enter any pasture and drink from any watering hole or spring without anyone bothering it. Occasionally, they would only milk it to serve guests. (It is derived from the root Sayb, meaning the flow of water and freedom of movement).

  3. Wasilah: This referred to a sheep that had given birth seven times, or according to some reports, a sheep that gave birth to twins (derived from the root Wasl, meaning connection). They also considered the slaughtering of such a sheep to be forbidden.

  4. Ham: This is the active participle from the root Himayah, meaning "protector." It referred to a male camel used for breeding female camels. When it had been used for breeding ten times, and each time an offspring was produced from its seed, they would say: "This animal has protected its back," meaning no one had the right to ride it. (One of the meanings of Hima is protection, prevention, and prohibition).

Among commentators and in various traditions, other possibilities regarding the meanings of these four titles are seen, but the common denominator among all of them is that they referred to animals that had, in fact, provided abundant and repeated service to their owners through reproduction, and in return, the owners granted a type of respect and freedom to these animals.

It is true that in all these cases, glimpses of a spirit of gratitude and appreciation—even toward the service of animals—can be seen, and from this perspective, their action might seem sanctifiable. However, since implementing such respect for these animals was meaningless as they lacked understanding, it was considered a form of wasting wealth, destroying Divine blessings, and leaving them idle. Furthermore, because of this "respect," these animals often suffered agonizing pain and neglect, as practically no one was willing to provide them with proper food or care. Given that these animals were usually of advanced age, they lived in a painful state amidst a multitude of deprivations until they died. For these reasons, Islam strictly prohibited this practice!

Moreover, it is understood from some traditions and commentaries that they performed all or part of this ritual for the sake of "idols" and, in reality, consecrated them to idols. In this case, Islam's struggle against this practice also takes the form of a struggle against idolatry.

Strangely, according to some reports, when some of these animals died a natural death, they would sometimes consume their meat (apparently for blessing and good fortune), which was itself another repulsive act! [1]

[1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 684.

Then He says: "The disbelievers and idolaters attributed these things to Allah and said: 'It is a Divine law'" (وَ لكِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يَفْتَرُونَ عَلَى اللَّهِ الْكَذِبَ).

"And most of them did not exercise the slightest thought or reflection regarding this, nor did they use their intellect; rather, they blindly imitated others" (وَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْقِلُونَ).

In the following verse, the Quran points to their reasoning and logic behind these misplaced and groundless prohibitions, saying: "When it is said to them, 'Come to what Allah has revealed and to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله),' they refuse and say, 'Sufficient for us are the customs and traditions of our forefathers!'" (وَ إِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَعالَوْا إِلى‌ ما أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ وَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ قالُوا حَسْبُنا ما وَجَدْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا).

In reality, their wrongdoings and idolatry stemmed from another form of idolatry: unconditional surrender to the superstitious customs and traditions of their ancestors. It was as if they considered the mere title of "ancestors" and "fathers" sufficient for the validity and correctness of their beliefs, habits, and customs.

The Quran explicitly answers them: "Even though their fathers had no knowledge and were not guided?" (أَ وَ لَوْ كانَ آباؤُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَهْتَدُونَ). That is, if the ancestors upon whom you rely for your beliefs and actions were scholars and guided individuals, your following them would be like the "ignorant" imitating the "learned." But since you yourselves know they knew no more than you—and perhaps were even further behind—your action is a clear example of the "ignorant" imitating the "ignorant," which is highly detestable in the scales of intellect and wisdom.

From the fact that the Quran emphasizes the word "most" (akthar) in the above sentence, it appears that even in that environment of ignorance and darkness, there existed a minority—however weak—of understanding individuals who looked upon such acts with contempt and loathing.

An Idol Named Ancestors!

One of the subjects that was intensely prevalent during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), and is therefore reflected in various verses of the Quran, was the issue of boasting about ancestors and showing unconditional respect—to the point of worship—toward them and their thoughts, habits, and customs. This subject was not exclusive to the Age of Ignorance; it exists today among many nations and is perhaps considered one of the primary factors in the spread and transmission of superstitions from one generation to the next. It is as if "death" creates a kind of immunity and sanctity for the deceased, shrouding them in a halo of respect and piety!

There is no doubt that the spirit of gratitude and the observance of human principles require that fathers, grandfathers, and ancestors be held in respect. However, this does not mean we should consider them infallible or refrain from critiquing and examining their thoughts and customs, nor should we blindly follow their superstitions.

For this act is, in reality, a form of idolatry and pagan logic. Rather, while respecting their rights and useful thoughts and traditions, one must strictly break their erroneous traditions. This is especially true since future generations, through the passage of time and the advancement of science, knowledge, and experience, are usually more knowledgeable and intelligent than previous generations, and no intellect or wisdom permits "blind" imitation of the past.

It is strange to see that some scholars and even university professors are not immune to this weakness, sometimes surprisingly submitting to ridiculous superstitions such as jumping over fire during the final days of the year, wishing to revive the fire-worship of their ancestors in some way. In truth, they have no logic other than the logic of the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance.

Groundless Contradiction

In Tafsir al-Mizan, quoted from Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur from a group of Sunni scholars, it is narrated that a person named Abu al-Ahwas said: "I came to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) while wearing old and tattered clothes. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) asked: 'Do you have wealth?' I said: 'Yes.' He asked: 'What kind of wealth?'

I said: 'I have all kinds of wealth at my disposal: camels, sheep, horses, and the like.' The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: 'When Allah gives you something, He should see its effects upon you (not that you set aside your wealth and live like the destitute).'

Then he asked: 'Are your camel calves born with slit ears or with healthy ears?' I said: 'Certainly with healthy ears; is it possible for a camel to bring forth a calf with slit ears?!' He said: 'Then you must be taking a blade in your hand and slitting the ears of some of them, saying "this is Bahr," and tearing the ears of others, saying "this is Sarm?"' I said: 'Yes, I do such things.' He said: 'Never do such a thing; whatever Allah has given you is lawful for you.' Then he recited:

ما جَعَلَ اللَّهُ مِنْ بَحِيرَةٍ وَ لا سائِبَةٍ وَ لا وَصِيلَةٍ وَ لا حامٍ [1]"

From this tradition, it is understood that they kept a portion of their property idle and unused, but in its place, they practiced a false frugality, wearing old and tattered clothes. This was a groundless contradiction.

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 6, p. 172.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ لَا يَضُرُّكُم مَّن ضَلَّ إِذَا ٱهْتَدَيْتُمْ ۚ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (١٠٥)
O you who have faith! Take care of your own souls. He who strays cannot hurt you if you are guided. To Allah will be the return of you all, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (105)

Commentary

Everyone is Responsible for Their Own Deeds

In the previous verse, there was mention of the blind imitation of the people of the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah) regarding their misguided ancestors, and the Quran explicitly warned them that such imitation is inconsistent with intellect and logic. Following this topic, a question would naturally arise in their minds: if we separate our account from our ancestors in such matters, what will be their fate? Furthermore, if we abandon such imitation, what will be the fate of many people who are under the influence of such traditions? The above verse, in response to such questions, says: "O you who have believed, you are responsible for yourselves. If you are guided, the misguidance of others (whether ancestors or friends and relatives of your own era) will not harm you" (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ مَنْ ضَلَّ إِذَا اهْتَدَيْتُمْ).

Then, referring to the subject of the Resurrection and the reckoning and auditing of everyone's deeds, It says: "To Allah is your return all together, and He will settle the account of each of you separately, and He will inform you of what you used to do." (إِلَى اللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ).

Answer to an Objection

A great deal of commotion has been raised regarding this verse, and some have imagined that there is a kind of contradiction between this verse and the command of "Enjoining the Good" (Amr bi-l-Ma'ruf) and "Forbidding the Evil" (Nahi 'an al-Munkar), which are among the definitive and established Islamic mandates. This is because this verse says you should watch over yourselves, and the deviation of others does not affect your status.

Coincidentally, it appears from the narrations that this type of misunderstanding and error existed even during the era of the verse's revelation for some uninformed individuals. Jubayr ibn Nufayl says: I was sitting in a circle of a group of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and I was the youngest of them all. They brought up the topic of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil. I jumped into their conversation and said: Does Allah not say in the Quran: "O you who have believed, you are responsible for yourselves. If you are guided, the misguidance of others will not harm you" (therefore, what is the necessity of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil?!). Suddenly, they all with one voice rebuked and protested against me, saying: "Are you isolating a verse from the Quran without knowing its meaning or interpretation?!" I deeply regretted my words, and they continued their discussion among themselves. When they wanted to stand up and leave the gathering, they turned to me and said: "You are a young man of few years and you have isolated a verse of the Quran without knowing its meaning; but perhaps you will reach such a time as we describe, when you see that greed has overtaken the people and rules over them, whims and desires are the leaders of the people, and everyone is pleased only with their own opinion. In such a time, watch over yourself; the misguidance of others will not harm you" (meaning the verse pertains to such a time).

Some of the comfort-seekers of our era also, when the mention of performing the two great divine obligations of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil arises, cling to this verse to shirk the burden of responsibility and distort its meaning.

However, with a little reflection, one can realize that there is no contradiction between these two commands, because:

Firstly: The verse under discussion says that everyone's account is separate, and the misguidance of others—whether ancestors or otherwise—does not damage the guidance of those who are guided, even if they are brothers, or father and son. Therefore, do not follow them and save yourselves (reflect carefully).

Secondly: This verse refers to a time when enjoining the good and forbidding the evil is no longer effective, or the conditions for its influence are not met. Sometimes, some individuals become distressed in such a situation, asking: "In this case, what is our duty?" The Quran answers them that there is no cause for concern for you, because you have performed your duty, and they have not accepted it, or there was no receptivity in them. Therefore, no harm will come to you from this direction.

This meaning is mentioned in the hadith we quoted above and also in some other narrations where the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was asked about this verse and He said:

ايتمروا بالمعروف و تناهوا عن المنكر فاذا رايت دنيا مؤثرة و شحا مطاعا و هوى متبعا و اعجاب كل ذى راى برايه فعليك بخويصة نفسك و ذر عوامهم!

"Enjoin the good and forbid the evil, but when you see that people have prioritized the world, and greed and whims rule over them, and everyone is pleased only with their own opinion (and their ears are closed to the words of others), then attend to your own self and leave the commonality!" [1]

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 1, Page 684.

Other narrations have also been cited with this theme, all of which pursue this same truth.

Fakhr al-Razi, as is his habit, mentions several aspects to answer the above question, almost all of which return to one thing—what we mentioned above—and it seems he separated them merely to increase the number!

In any case, there is no doubt that the issue of enjoining the good and forbidding the evil is among the most important pillars of Islam, from which one cannot shirk the burden of responsibility in any way. These two duties are only dropped in cases where there is no hope for their effectiveness and the necessary conditions are not met.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ شَهَـٰدَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ حِينَ ٱلْوَصِيَّةِ ٱثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِّنكُمْ أَوْ ءَاخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ إِنْ أَنتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصَـٰبَتْكُم مُّصِيبَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۚ تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ إِنِ ٱرْتَبْتُمْ لَا نَشْتَرِى بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَىٰ ۙ وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَـٰدَةَ ٱللَّهِ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْـَٔاثِمِينَ (١٠٦)
O you who have faith! The witness between you, when death approaches any of you, while making a bequest, shall be two fair men from among yourselves—or two from among others, if you are journeying in the land and the affliction of death visits you. You shall detain the two of them after the prayer, and, if you have any doubt, they shall vow by Allah, ‘We will not sell it for any gain, even if it were a relative, nor will we conceal the testimony of Allah, for then we would indeed be among the sinners.’ (106)
فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلَىٰٓ أَنَّهُمَا ٱسْتَحَقَّآ إِثْمًا فَـَٔاخَرَانِ يَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْأَوْلَيَـٰنِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِٱللَّهِ لَشَهَـٰدَتُنَآ أَحَقُّ مِن شَهَـٰدَتِهِمَا وَمَا ٱعْتَدَيْنَآ إِنَّآ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠٧)
But if it is found that both of them were guilty of a sin, then two others shall stand up in their place from among those nearest in kinship to the claimants and swear by Allah: ‘Our testimony is surely truer than their testimony, and we have not transgressed, for then we would indeed be among the wrongdoers.’ (107)
ذَٰلِكَ أَدْنَىٰٓ أَن يَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهَآ أَوْ يَخَافُوٓا۟ أَن تُرَدَّ أَيْمَـٰنٌۢ بَعْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۗ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلْفَـٰسِقِينَ (١٠٨)
That makes it likelier that they give the testimony in its genuine form, or fear that other oaths will be taken after their oaths. Be wary of Allah and listen, and Allah does not guide the transgressing lot. (108)

Occasion of Revelation

In Majma' al-Bayan and some other commentaries, it is narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses that a Muslim man named "Ibn Abi Mariyah," accompanied by two Arab Christians named "Tamim" and "Adi" (who were brothers), left Medina for the purpose of trade. During the journey, Ibn Mariyah, the Muslim, fell ill. He wrote a will and hid it among his belongings, then entrusted his wealth to his two Christian traveling companions, instructing them to deliver it to his family, and subsequently passed away.

The companions opened his goods, took the expensive and attractive items, and returned the rest to the heirs. When the heirs opened the goods, they did not find some of the wealth that Ibn Abi Mariyah had taken with him. Suddenly, their eyes fell upon the will, and they saw that a list of all the stolen property was recorded in it. They confronted the two Christian companions with the matter, but they denied it, saying: "Whatever he gave us, we have delivered to you!" Consequently, they complained to the Prophet ﷺ, and the above verses were revealed, explaining the ruling for it.

However, from the occasion of revelation mentioned in the book al-Kafi, it appears that they initially denied the existence of any other goods, and the matter was brought before the Prophet ﷺ. Since there was no evidence against the two men, the Prophet ﷺ required them to take an oath and then acquitted them. But shortly after, some of the disputed property was found with the two men, thereby proving their lie. The matter was reported to the Prophet ﷺ, who waited until the above verses were revealed. He then ordered the guardians of the deceased to take an oath, took the property, and delivered it to them.

Commentary

Among the most important issues emphasized by Islam is the preservation of people's rights and property, and generally, the implementation of social justice. The above verses represent a portion of the instructions related to this area. First, so that the rights of the heirs to the deceased's property are not lost and the rights of survivors, orphans, and minors are not trampled upon, He commands the believers, saying: O you who believe, when death approaches one of you, at the time of making a will, you should call two just persons to witness, and entrust your property to them as a trust to be delivered to the heirs (يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا شَهَادَةُ بَيْنِكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ حِينَ الْوَصِيَّةِ اثْنَانِ ذَوَا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ).

The meaning of "justice" ('adl) here is the same as the general definition of justice—refraining from major sins and the like. However, there is also the possibility in the meaning of the verse that justice refers to "trustworthiness in financial matters" and the absence of treachery, unless it is proven by other evidence that more conditions are required for such a witness.

And the meaning of "مِنْكُمْ" (from among you) is from among you Muslims, in contrast to non-Muslims who are referred to in the subsequent phrase.

Of course, it should be noted that the discussion here is not about ordinary or common testimony, but rather testimony coupled with executorship (wasayah). That is, these two individuals are both "executors" and "witnesses." As for the possibility that, in addition to the two witnesses, the selection of a third person as an executor is necessary here, it is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verse and the occasion of revelation, for we read in the occasion of revelation that Ibn Abi Mariyah had only two companions whom he chose as executors and witnesses over his inheritance.

Then He adds: "If you are on a journey and the calamity of death befalls you (and you do not find a Muslim executor and witness), choose two others from among non-Muslims for this purpose" (أَوْ آخَرَانِ مِنْ غَيْرِكُمْ إِنْ أَنْتُمْ ضَرَبْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَأَصَابَتْكُمْ مُصِيبَةُ الْمَوْتِ).

Although the verse does not explicitly state that choosing an executor and witness from non-Muslims is conditional upon the lack of access to Muslims, it is clear that this is intended for cases where no Muslim is accessible, and the mention of the condition of "travel" is for this very reason. Similarly, although the word "أَوْ" (or) is usually for "choice" (takhyir), here—as in many other cases—it implies "order" (tartib); meaning, first choose from Muslims, and if that is not possible, from non-Muslims.

It is also necessary to mention that by "non-Muslims," only the People of the Book (Jews and Christians) are meant, because Islam has not granted any significance to polytheists and idolaters in any matter.

Then He commands that at the time of giving testimony—in cases of doubt and suspicion—the two should be detained after the prayer and made to swear by the name of Allah (تَحْبِسُونَهُمَا مِنْ بَعْدِ الصَّلَاةِ فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِاللَّهِ إِنِ ارْتَبْتُمْ).

And their testimony must be such that they say: "We are not prepared to sell the truth for material gains and testify falsely, even if it concerns our relatives" (لَا نَشْتَرِي بِهِ ثَمَنًا وَلَوْ كَانَ ذَا قُرْبَى).

"And we never conceal the testimony of Allah, for in that case, we would be among the sinners" (وَلَا نَكْتُمُ شَهَادَةَ اللَّهِ إِنَّا إِذًا لَمِنَ الْآثِمِينَ).

It should be noted that:

Firstly, these formalities for giving testimony are for cases of doubt, suspicion, and accusation.

Secondly, according to the apparent meaning of the verse, there is no difference between a Muslim and a non-Muslim in this regard; in fact, it is a type of precaution to preserve property in the face of accusation. This in no way contradicts the acceptance of the testimony of two just witnesses without an oath, as that ruling pertains to cases where there is no accusation. Therefore, the ruling of the verse is neither abrogated nor restricted to non-Muslims (reflect on this).

Thirdly, the "prayer" mentioned in the case of non-Muslims naturally refers to their own prayers, which create a sense of mindfulness and fear of God in them. As for Muslims, a group believes it refers specifically to the 'Asr prayer, and this is also mentioned in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. However, the apparent meaning of the verse is general and includes any prayer. It is possible that the specific mention of the 'Asr prayer in our narrations has a recommended (mustahabb) aspect, because more people participated in the 'Asr prayer, and furthermore, that was often the time for arbitration and judgment among Muslims.

Fourthly, the choice of the time of prayer for testimony is because the spirit of God-fearingness is awakened in man according to the verse: "Indeed, prayer prohibits immorality and wrongdoing" [^1], and the temporal and situational context creates a focus toward the truth within him. Some jurists have even said that it is good for the testimony to be given—if in Mecca—beside the Kaaba between the "Rukn" and "Maqam" (which is a very holy place), and if in Medina, beside the pulpit of the Prophet ﷺ.

In the next verse, the discussion turns to a case where it is proven that the two witnesses have committed treachery and testified against the truth—just as occurred in the occasion of revelation. In such a case, He commands: "If it is discovered that the two have committed a sin, a crime, and a transgression, and have trampled upon the truth, then two others from among those whom the first witnesses wronged (i.e., the heirs of the deceased) shall take their place and testify to reclaim their right" (فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلَى أَنَّهُمَا اسْتَحَقَّا إِثْمًا فَآخَرَانِ يَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَحَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَوْلَيَانِ).

The late Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan believes that this verse is among the most complex and difficult verses of the Quran in terms of meaning and grammar. However, by considering two points, it becomes clear that the verse is not that complex:

First, the word "اسْتَحَقَّا" (deserved/incurred) here, by the context of the word "إِثْمًا" (sin), refers to the crime and transgression against the rights of another.

Second, "الْأَوْلَيَانِ" (the two nearest/most entitled) here means the "first two" (al-awwalan); that is, those two witnesses who were initially supposed to testify but deviated from the right path.

Therefore, the meaning of the verse becomes: if it is discovered that the first two witnesses committed a violation, two others take their place from among those whom the first two witnesses transgressed against. [^1]

[^1]: Surah Al-Ankabut, Verse 45. [^1]: Therefore, from a grammatical perspective, "آخَرَانِ" is the subject (mubtada'), the phrase "يَقُومَانِ مَقَامَهُمَا" is the predicate (khabar), "الْأَوْلَيَانِ" is the subject (fa'il) for "اسْتَحَقَّ", and "الَّذِينَ" refers to the heirs of the deceased who were wronged, and the prepositional phrase (مِنَ الَّذِينَ) is an adjective for "آخَرَانِ" (reflect on this).

At the end of the verse, He describes the duty of the second two witnesses as follows: "They must swear by Allah that 'Our testimony is truer than the testimony of the first two, and we have not committed any transgression or injustice, and if we have done so, we would indeed be among the wrongdoers'" (فَيُقْسِمَانِ بِاللَّهِ لَشَهَادَتُنَا أَحَقُّ مِنْ شَهَادَتِهِمَا وَمَا اعْتَدَيْنَا إِنَّا إِذًا لَمِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ).

In reality, the guardians of the deceased testify based on information they previously had regarding his property and goods during the journey or otherwise, that the first two witnesses committed injustice and treachery. This testimony becomes based on sensory perception rather than conjecture or circumstantial evidence.

In the final verse under discussion, He explains the philosophy of the rulings regarding testimony mentioned in the previous verses: "If the above instructions are followed—meaning the two witnesses are called to testify after the prayer and in the presence of the congregation, and in the event of their treachery, other individuals from the heirs take their place to reveal the truth—this procedure will cause the witnesses to be careful in their testimony and perform it according to reality, either out of fear of Allah or out of fear of Allah's creation" (ذَلِكَ أَدْنَى أَنْ يَأْتُوا بِالشَّهَادَةِ عَلَى وَجْهِهَا أَوْ يَخَافُوا أَنْ تُرَدَّ أَيْمَانٌ بَعْدَ أَيْمَانِهِمْ).

In fact, this action causes the maximum fear of responsibility before Allah or His servants to be awakened in them, preventing them from deviating from the axis of truth.

At the end of the verse, to emphasize all the preceding rulings, He commands: "Be mindful of Allah and listen (to His commands), and know that Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people" (وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَاسْمَعُوا وَاللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفَاسِقِينَ).

Ayah

۞ يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ مَاذَآ أُجِبْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَآ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (١٠٩)
The day Allah will gather the apostles and say, ‘What was the response to you?’ They will say, ‘We have no knowledge. Indeed You are knower of all that is Unseen.’ (109)

Commentary

This verse is, in fact, a complement to the preceding verses. Since the conclusion of the previous verses—which pertained to the issue of truthful and false testimony—commanded piety and the fear of disobeying Allah's command, this verse states: "Fear that day when Allah shall gather the messengers and question them regarding their mission and mandate, saying: 'What response did the people give to your call?'" (يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللَّهُ الرُّسُلَ فَيَقُولُ ما ذا أُجِبْتُمْ‌).

They will negate knowledge from themselves and entrust all realities to the knowledge of the Lord, saying: "O Allah! We have no knowledge; indeed, You are the Knower of all that is hidden and unseen" (قالُوا لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ‌).

In this manner, your dealings are with such a God who is the Knower of the Unseen and with such a court; therefore, be mindful of truth and justice in your testimonies. [^1] Here, two questions arise: First, it is understood from other verses of the Quran that the Prophets are witnesses and observers over their nations, whereas from the above verse,

[^1]: From what has been mentioned above, it becomes clear that regarding the grammar (i'rab), "The Day..." (Yawma) is the object (maf'ul) for "Fear" (Ittaqu), which is implied and understood from the previous verse.

it appears that they negate knowledge from themselves and leave everything to Allah.

However, there is no contradiction or conflict between these two; rather, they pertain to two different stages. In the first stage, which the verse under discussion refers to, the Prophets express their courtesy and humility in response to the Lord's question, negating knowledge from themselves and deferring everything to Allah's knowledge. But in the subsequent stages, they recount what they know regarding their nations and bear witness. This is exactly like a teacher who asks his student to answer a certain person; the student first shows respect and describes his own knowledge as nothing compared to the teacher's knowledge, and thereafter, he says what he knows.

The second question is: how can the Prophets negate knowledge from themselves when, in addition to ordinary knowledge, they know many hidden realities through the teaching of the Lord?! Although commentators have various discussions in response to this question, in our view, it is clear that the Prophets' intention is to consider their knowledge as nothing in the presence of Allah's knowledge. In reality, it is so: our existence is nothing compared to His infinite existence, and our knowledge is not considered knowledge compared to His knowledge. In short, the "contingent" (mumkin), no matter what it may be, possesses nothing in the presence of the "Necessary" (wajib). In other words: the knowledge of the Prophets, though significant in its own right, is considered nothing when compared to the knowledge of the Lord.

In truth, the real Knower is He who is present and observant in every place and at every time, aware of the connection between all particles of the universe, and cognizant of all the particulars of this world, which is a single interconnected unit; and this attribute belongs exclusively to the Pure Essence of Allah.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that this verse does not serve as evidence for the negation of all types of knowledge of the unseen (ilm al-ghayb) from the Prophets and Imams عليه السلام, as some have imagined. This is because "knowledge of the unseen" essentially (bi-dhat) belongs to Him who is present everywhere and at all times, and anyone other than Him does not possess such knowledge essentially; rather, they know what Allah has taught them of the unseen. The evidence for this

is found in numerous verses of the Quran. For instance, in Surah Al-Jinn, verse 26, we read: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى‌ غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى‌ مِنْ رَسُولٍ‌: "[He is] Knower of the unseen, and He does not disclose His [knowledge of the] unseen to anyone, except whom He has approved of messengers." And in Surah Hud, verse 49, we read: تِلْكَ مِنْ أَنْباءِ الْغَيْبِ نُوحِيها إِلَيْكَ‌: "These are from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you."

From these verses and their like, it is understood that knowledge of the unseen belongs specifically to the Essence of Allah, but He teaches whoever He deems appropriate whatever He sees fit, and its quantity and quality depend on His will and providence.

Ayah

إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ٱذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِى عَلَيْكَ وَعَلَىٰ وَٰلِدَتِكَ إِذْ أَيَّدتُّكَ بِرُوحِ ٱلْقُدُسِ تُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا ۖ وَإِذْ عَلَّمْتُكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ ۖ وَإِذْ تَخْلُقُ مِنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ بِإِذْنِى فَتَنفُخُ فِيهَا فَتَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَتُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ تُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِى ۖ وَإِذْ كَفَفْتُ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَنكَ إِذْ جِئْتَهُم بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (١١٠)
When Allah will say, O Jesus son of Mary, remember My blessing upon you and upon your mother, when I strengthened you with the Holy Spirit, so you would speak to the people in the cradle and in adulthood, and when I taught you the Book and wisdom, the Torah and the Evangel, and when you would create from clay the form of a bird, with My leave, and you would breathe into it and it would become a bird, with My leave; and you would heal the blind and the leper, with My leave, and you would raise the dead, with My leave; and when I held off [the evil of] the Children of Israel from you when you brought them manifest proofs, whereat the faithless among them said, ‘This is nothing but plain magic.’ (110)

Commentary

Divine Favors upon the Messiah

This verse and the subsequent verses until the end of Surah Al-Ma'idah pertain to the life of Prophet Jesus عليه السلام and the favors bestowed upon him and his community, which are mentioned here for the awakening and awareness of Muslims.

First, it says: "Remember when Allah said: 'O Jesus, son of Mary! Remember My favor upon you and upon your mother'" (إِذْ قالَ اللَّهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ اذْكُرْ نِعْمَتِي عَلَيْكَ وَ عَلى‌ والِدَتِكَ‌).

According to this interpretation, the above verses begin an independent discussion that serves an educational purpose for Muslims and pertains to this world. However, a group of commentators such as Tabarsi, Baydawi, and Abu al-Futuh Razi have suggested the possibility that this verse is a continuation of the previous verse and relates to the questions and discourse Allah will have with the prophets on the Day of Resurrection. Consequently, "قال" (He said), which is a past tense verb, would here mean "يقول" (He says), which is present/future tense. However, this possibility contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse, especially since the enumeration of favors is usually intended to revive the spirit of gratitude in a person, whereas this issue is not relevant in the Resurrection.
